The NuScale Power Plant, comprising 12 operating NuScale Power Modules (NPMs), delivers a net total output of approximately 570 MWe. The plant design features no reactor pressure vessel (RPV) or containment penetrations below the top of the reactor core and utilizes modular construction for in-shop fabrication of reactor and containment components. Each NPM operates at full power using natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps. The reactor core consists of 37 fuel assemblies and 16 control rod assemblies (CRAs). The torispherical RPV head supports the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs) and includes 2” to 8” diameter penetrations for pressurizer spray, reactor vent valves, reactor safety valves, degasification instrumentation, I&C instrument channels, and CRDM nozzles. The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) provides passive decay heat removal during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) and consists of three independent reactor vent valves and two independent reactor recirculation valves, all closed during normal operation. The module protection system (MPS) monitors safety-related parameters such as temperature, flow, neutron flux, and pressure, while the neutron monitoring system provides core power indication and safety inputs to the MPS. The power conversion system includes a main steam system, turbine generator set, standard condenser and cooling tower, and condensate and feedwater system. Hoisting and handling equipment, as well as pipe fittings and electrical connections, are located above the reactor pool water level for manual connection/disconnection during NPM installation, refueling, or removal. Refueling operations for each NPM are independent of the status of other modules, and the plant allows for shipment of used NPMs off-site. The cooling system is a closed-loop, two-phase natural circulation system with two trains of decay heat removal equipment—each train, attached to a steam generator (SG) loop and equipped with a passive condenser immersed in the reactor pool, is capable of removing 100% of the decay heat load and cooling the reactor coolant system (RCS).

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) is a modular, movable nuclear steam supply system (NSSS) comprising a reactor core, pressurizer, and two steam generators integrated within a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and housed in a compact steel containment vessel (CNV). The NuScale plant design is scalable, supporting from one to twelve NPMs within a single Reactor Building, with each module rated at 160 MWt (totaling 1,920 MWt for 12 modules) and approximately 50 MWe electrical output per module (600 MWe total), with a net output of about 570 MWe for a 12-module facility after accounting for house loads. The plant includes all necessary primary and secondary systems, such as main steam systems, turbine generator sets, condensate and feedwater systems, shared external cooling water systems, module assembly equipment, fuel handling equipment, and turbine maintenance equipment. The PIM code, developed by NuScale, is used to assess NPM stability during operation, while the NIST-1 is a scaled stainless steel representation of the NPM reactor, containment, and reactor pool systems, with a maximum operating pressure of 1650 psia (11.4 MPa) and temperature of 630°F (605 K). The design and safety bases of the reactor coolant system (RCS) and its major components are closely interrelated, and the facility’s documentation generally describes individual modules, with multi-module information provided where relevant.

results.question: What is the operating pressure of the NuScale Reactor Coolant System?
results.expected_answer: The NuScale RCS operates at 1850 psia.
results.generated_answer:
The operating pressure of the NuScale Reactor Coolant System is 2100 psia.  
(Source: Provided context, paragraph describing pressurizer operation)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale reactor facility features site-specific cooling tower location, orientation, equipment design, and operation, with the reactor core housed in a core support assembly seated in the lower reactor pressure vessel (RPV) assembly and connected to a central hot leg riser. Each Reactor Coolant System (RCS) line enters containment through welded penetrations on the containment upper head, with two containment isolation valves mounted externally, and a single pressurizer spray supply line also entering through the containment head. The pressurizer is integral to the reactor vessel, comprising the upper region above the naturally circulating coolant, separated by a baffle plate. The pressurizer includes the RPV upper shell and head, baffle plate, heater bundles, and two spray nozzles (each 1 inch in diameter). Instrumentation measures the steam-water interface level, providing input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS), including low water level protection for the heaters. The pressurizer heaters maintain RCS pressure during normal operation, supplying at least 785 kW (with a 15% margin), divided into three control groups per bundle, with two heater bundles per pressurizer, each bundle rated at 400 kW. Heater parameters include 480 Vac voltage and 60 Hz frequency, with heater sheath temperature at 800°F. The pressurizer operates at an internal pressure of 2100 psia, external pressure of 1000 psia, and temperature of 650°F. Pressurizer level is maintained within its operating band by operator action to add or reduce coolant inventory via the LRWS.

The NuScale design does not utilize safety-related pumps. In the event of reactor coolant system overpressure, fluid is relieved directly to the containment vessel via the overpressure protection system. Table 1.9-3 documents conformance with NUREG-0800, Standard Review Plan (SRP), and Design Specific Review Standard (DSRS) across multiple sections, including 14.2, 15.1.1–15.1.4, 15.1.5, 15.1.6, 15.2.8, 15.4.2, 15.4.6, 15.6.1, and 15.6.6. The design is noted as conforming to the applicable SRP and DSRS requirements, with no additional comments provided for these sections.

Pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 effective full power years (EFPY) are calculated using the methodology from ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects due to neutron fluence per Regulatory Guide 1.99. The NuScale reactor design and safety analysis methodologies are detailed in topical reports TR-0516-49416 and TR-0516-49422, with pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 EFPY calculated per ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects using Regulatory Guide 1.99 and ASTM E185-82 for surveillance. Fluence and pressure-temperature limits are further addressed in TR-0116-20781-P and TR-1015-18177-P. The design does not require certain systems post-accident and does not credit them in radiological consequence analyses, fully applying the single-failure criterion. NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, justifying departures in a topical report, and applies a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for Chapter 15 events, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models addressing only relevant features per Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30. Boric acid concentrations are selected to bound all operating conditions, with NRC-inspected testing per IP35034.1.5.2 and startup testing detailed in Section 14.2. Hydrogen control is not required, as combustion does not affect CNV integrity. Post-accident assessment relies on radiation monitors and core exit temperature, supporting exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding post-accident sampling. Additional exemptions include 10 CFR 50.46a, 50.34(f)(2)(vi) (high point venting), and portions of 50.34(f)(2)(xx) (pressurizer level indicators). The CVCS does not include holdup tanks under vacuum but provides required venting and draining. The RPV high-point degasification vent is not safety-significant, and high-point vents are not needed. The LTOP system is not connected to a low-pressure system, but guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation applies. NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, for LOCA mass and energy release analysis, and requires no additional ITAAC. The NuScale design was evaluated against DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0, which covers initiating events such as Loss of External Load, Turbine Trip, Loss of Condenser Vacuum, Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve (MSIV), and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed). For the basic objectives regarding initiating events (II.1), the design conforms fully. For specific criteria for events of moderate frequency (II.2), the design partially conforms because NuScale does not have a steam pressure regulator. The design conforms to criteria for Reactor Coolant System and Main Steam System Pressures (II.2.A), Incidents of Moderate Frequency (II.2.C), Instrument Setpoints and their impact on plant response (II.2.D), Most Limiting Plant System Single Failure (II.2.E), and Performance of Nonsafety-Related Systems and Single Failures of Active and Passive Systems (II.2.F). The analyzed Anticipated Operational Occurrences (AOOs) that could lead to Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) overpressure include loss of load, turbine trip (with and without bypass), loss of condenser vacuum, inadvertent MSIV closure, steam pressure regulator failure closed, loss of normal AC power, loss of feedwater, and inadvertent operation of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS). These AOOs are detailed in DCD Chapter 15, including initial conditions, system parameters, analysis assumptions, systems and equipment assumed to operate, reactor trip signals, and sensitivity analyses.

The NuScale reactor design features an integral pressurizer located within the reactor pressure vessel, eliminating the need for a pressurizer surge line and thus avoiding issues of thermal stratification. Unlike conventional designs, NuScale does not use power-operated relief valves (PORVs), which are susceptible to inadvertent opening; instead, it employs spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves that do not share this vulnerability. The design does not rely on pressurizer heaters to establish or maintain natural circulation during hot shutdown conditions, making the requirements for pressurizer heater power supply and associated control interfaces in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xiii) inapplicable—NuScale considers its hot shutdown condition equivalent to the regulatory hot standby condition. Furthermore, NuScale does not include pressurizer relief or block valves, rendering the power supply requirements for these components in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) not technically relevant. For safe shutdown (Mode 3) with reactor coolant temperatures below 200°F, NuScale equates this state to cold shutdown as defined in OM Code ISTA-2000, and its DHRS actuation valves meet ASME OM Code exercising and observation requirements. Overall, these design features support exemptions from certain TMI-related regulatory requirements.

results.question: What type of steam generator does NuScale use?
results.expected_answer: NuScale uses helical coil steam generators integrated within the reactor pressure vessel.
results.generated_answer:
NuScale uses compact helical coil once-through steam generators with reactor pressure on the outside of the tubes.  
(Source: Provided context)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale design incorporates several advanced safety and operational features, including safe shutdown and cooling without reliance on AC or DC power, compact helical coil steam generators with reactor pressure on the tube exteriors, and a high-strength steel containment vessel immersed in a water pool. The containment operates at sub-atmospheric pressure during normal conditions, and the reactor core is small, resulting in a reduced source term. Comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls (I&C) are used for monitoring and control. The plant design is scalable, supporting incremental capacity growth in multi-unit configurations. The passive NuScale system eliminates pumps, sumps, suction strainers, debris interceptors, and trash racks, thereby minimizing debris-related and non-condensable effects on core coolant flow, and complies with regulatory guidance on debris management and chemical effects. Due to the modular and compact nature of the NuScale Power Module, passive separation of redundant safety trains is not always feasible; instead, fire protection measures are implemented to ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational in the event of a fire. Unlike traditional large water reactors, the NuScale design integrates the pressurizer above the reactor core within the reactor vessel, with no connecting piping, ensuring that core uncovery and damage cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint.

The NuScale design employs passive natural circulation of the primary coolant, which eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps. This approach conforms to DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials, specifically regarding the selection, processing, testing, and inspection of materials, with no exceptions noted.

The NuScale Nuclear Steam Supply System (NSSS) is a passive, NuScale-designed small modular pressurized water reactor (PWR), with each NuScale Power Module (NPM) featuring its own compact steel containment vessel mounted to the Reactor Building module compartment walls and at the bottom within the Reactor Building pool. Unlike traditional large light water reactor (LWR) designs, the NuScale containment vessel does not have subcompartments, internal compartments as referenced in GDC 50, or reactor coolant loops, and does not house high-energy piping or fuel storage and handling systems inside containment. Instead, fuel handling operations are performed in the Reactor Building pool where the NPMs reside, making containment design provisions for such operations inapplicable to NuScale’s containment vessel. The NuScale containment vessel’s high-pressure capability eliminates the need for features such as a 3-foot opening for venting, which were requirements for traditional large LWR containment structures.

The NuScale steam generator (SG) design minimizes crevices between SG tubes, tube supports, and tubesheets to limit corrosion product buildup, with tube-to-tubesheet contact fully within the primary coolant environment. Full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore eliminates crevices at the tube-to-tubesheet face, and tubes are expanded into both steam and feed plenum tubesheets. The once-through SG design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet plena, preventing accumulation or concentration of materials and making traditional SG blowdown systems unnecessary and ineffective for removing corrosion products or impurities. Instead, any secondary coolant impurities or corrosion products may deposit as scale or film on interior tube surfaces or be removed by carryover, with low concentrations ensured by material selection and chemistry control. Periodic cleaning during outages, using proven chemical or mechanical methods, removes unacceptable corrosion product films. Secondary side SG surfaces are constructed of corrosion-resistant materials such as nickel alloy, stainless steel, or stainless steel cladding, eliminating concerns about degradation from cleaning solutions and negating the need for invasive sludge removal techniques. The design provides access to both internal (secondary) and external (reactor coolant) sides of tubesheets for inspection and foreign object removal. The tube support structure is located within the primary coolant environment, preventing secondary system corrosion products from depositing on the SG shell. Chemistry control for both primary and secondary water is maintained per industry guidelines, with modifications for the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design, ensuring corrosion protection for stainless steels and nickel alloys in SG components, as detailed in Sections 5.2.3, 6.1, and 10.3.5, with the secondary water quality control program following NEI 97-06.

The NuScale plant design does not include an Auxiliary Feedwater (AFW) system typical of large Light Water Reactors (LWRs), but instead utilizes a Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) that serves a similar function. The NuScale steam generator design eliminates potential water hammer issues without requiring DHRS water to be supplied through an externally mounted top discharge header, as specified by certain acceptance criteria. For spent fuel pool (SFP) mitigation, NuScale is exempt from NEI 06-12 requirements because its SFP is located below grade and cannot be drained. The design does not use power-operated relief valves, and the Anticipated Transient Without Scram (ATWS) provisions are not technically relevant, as NuScale relies on protection system diversity to prevent ATWS rather than on relief and safety valves for mitigation. Consequently, the module response to ATWS is not analyzed in FSAR Section 15.8, and the performance of relief and safety valves is not relied upon to meet ATWS safety criteria. Section 19.2 of the FSAR provides further information on the NuScale Power Plant's response to anticipated transients without scram.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) features two collocated steam generators (SGs) within the reactor pressure vessel, which transfer heat from the primary system to generate steam from feedwater via natural circulation under both normal and off-normal conditions. Thermal-hydraulic performance testing of the SGs was conducted in an electrically heated facility using Joule heating, where a known electrical current was applied to the tube walls to create a constant heat flux boundary condition. Three distinct heating zones were used to simulate subcooled, boiling, and superheat regions. Each SG includes a total heat transfer area with a 10% tube plugging margin, and a fouling factor (as specified in Table 5.4-2) is applied to account for typical end-of-cycle performance based on secondary water chemistry control. The SGs utilize seamless helical tubing, eliminating the need for traditional tube support plates; instead, austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies provide full-circumferential, though not continuous, support to minimize crevice formation and corrosion product buildup. Eight sets of tube support assemblies are positioned between each column of helical tubes, limiting unsupported tube lengths and ensuring sufficiently high modal frequencies to prevent unacceptable flow-induced vibration. The lower SG supports accommodate thermal growth and provide lateral support for the tube supports.

results.question: What is the fuel type used in the NuScale reactor?
results.expected_answer: NuScale uses UO2 (uranium dioxide) fuel in a standard 17x17 lattice configuration.
results.generated_answer:
The NuScale reactor uses UO2 fuel enriched below 4.95% U-235 with Gd2O3 burnable absorber.  
(Source: Context, Paragraph 3)
retrieved_contexts: Preliminary critical heat flux (CHF) tests for the NuScale small modular reactor were conducted at Stern Laboratories in Ontario, Canada, using a high-pressure flow loop and custom 5x5 fuel assembly simulators with a 2-meter heated length, various axial power profiles, and configurations with and without simulated guide tubes. These tests examined the effects of shorter fuel length and low-flow natural circulation, generating data for the development and validation of the NuScale NSP2 CHF correlation. Additional CHF testing at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics (KATHY) facility in Germany, using prototypic fuel designs, provided further data to validate NSP2 and develop the NSP4 CHF correlation for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. CHF occurrence was identified by temperature excursions in the fuel simulators, and data collected included bundle subchannel exit temperatures for mixing coefficient determination and single-phase/two-phase pressure drop characteristics across various bundle powers and hydraulic conditions. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, prepared under 10 CFR 52.47(a), details the plant’s general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, including process effluent radiation monitoring (Section 11.5.1.2.2). The NuScale plant comprises 12 modules, each with a 160 MWth reactor and 50 MWe gross output, for a total net output of 570 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The core uses UO2 fuel enriched below 4.95% with a 24-month refueling interval. Safety features include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps. The containment is designed for high pressure to maintain integrity during worst-case accidents, minimizing radioactive releases. The NuScale plant design departs significantly from traditional large light water reactors (LWRs) and is not addressed by legacy regulatory guidance or codes such as PWRGALE. Its safety strategy relies on passive features and natural circulation, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps, safety-related pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, safety injection tanks, and safety-related motor-operated valves. Consequently, regulatory requirements and guidance related to these components—including flywheel integrity, motor qualification, thermal overload protection, ECCS accumulator lines, and gas accumulation in forced circulation systems—are not applicable. The design also omits an auxiliary feedwater (AFW) system, power-operated pressurizer relief valves, and pressurizer heaters, instead using a Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves, which are not susceptible to inadvertent opening. The integral pressurizer is located within the reactor vessel, with no surge line or connecting piping, eliminating thermal stratification and ensuring core uncovery cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint. The containment vessel is a high-strength steel structure immersed in a water pool, operating at sub-atmospheric pressure, and the compact core results in a reduced source term. The plant is scalable, supports multi-unit configurations, and uses comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls. Debris management and chemical effects are minimized by the elimination of pumps, sumps, strainers, and trash racks. Fire protection measures ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational, compensating for the inability to passively separate redundant safety trains due to the modular design. Regulatory requirements from 10 CFR 50.34(f) and NEI 06-12, as well as ATWS provisions, are largely inapplicable, and NuScale is exempt from related evaluations and analyses. For coolant source term evaluation, NuScale uses an alternate methodology incorporating aspects of ANSI/ANS 18.1. The only pumps within safety scope are ASME Class III CVCS pumps, which do not serve a safety function. The design meets DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials. For spent fuel pool mitigation, NuScale is exempt from certain requirements due to its below-grade, undrainable SFP. The design considers postulated startup reactivity accidents and addresses loading transients and structural evaluations for postulated breaks in CVCS and reactor vent valve piping. Containment vessel evacuation system valve closure times are addressed under SRP Section 6.2.4. Overall, NuScale’s unique passive and modular features support exemptions from many TMI-related and traditional LWR regulatory requirements.

The NuScale Power Plant consists of up to 12 NuScale Power Modules (NPMs), each a modular, integral pressurized water reactor (PWR) rated at 160 MWt (about 50 MWe gross), for a total plant net output of approximately 570 MWe. Each NPM is partially immersed in a below-grade reactor pool, with the water surface about 6 feet below grade, and is enclosed by a high-pressure steel containment vessel (CNV) that operates at a vacuum and is submerged in water for passive fission product control. The reactor pool, spent fuel pool, most primary systems, and some radioactive waste equipment are located below grade, providing multiple barriers for fission product retention beyond the fuel, cladding, reactor pressure vessel (RPV), and containment. The NPM core contains 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, ~2 m rod length, <4.95% U-235 enrichment, Gd2O3 burnable absorber) and 16 control rod assemblies (CRAs), supported by five spacer grids and utilizing low-flow natural circulation for primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps and large-break LOCA scenarios. Each module features passive safety systems, including dedicated Emergency Core Cooling Systems (ECCS) and Decay Heat Removal Systems (DHRS), with three independent reactor vent valves and two independent reactor recirculation valves, capable of removing 100% of decay heat for at least 72 hours without water addition. The design eliminates external piping between major components, and the containment system meets DSRS 6.2.1.1.A requirements without using ice condenser or suppression pool systems. Refueling is independent for each NPM, with bioshields designed for removal and stacking, and the reactor core remains in the lower RPV during refueling. The plant includes a main steam system, turbine generator set, condenser, cooling tower, and condensate/feedwater system, with all hoisting, handling, and electrical connections above the pool water level. The module and plant control systems enable monitoring and control from the main control room, with dedicated panels for each NPM and integrated safety, alarm, and radiation monitoring. Critical heat flux (CHF) testing at Stern Laboratories and AREVA KATHY facility validated the NSP2 and NSP4 CHF correlations for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, supporting safety analysis and licensing. The reactor coolant system operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The containment lacks subcompartments, high-energy piping, or internal fuel handling, and does not require large vent openings. The design supports modular construction, incremental module operation, flexible siting, "island mode" operation, and off-site shipment of used NPMs, with extensive testing and documentation validating safety-related codes and

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) is a modular, movable nuclear steam supply system (NSSS) comprising a reactor core, pressurizer, and two steam generators integrated within a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and housed in a compact steel containment vessel (CNV). The NuScale plant design is scalable, supporting from one to twelve NPMs within a single Reactor Building, with each module rated at 160 MWt (totaling 1,920 MWt for 12 modules) and approximately 50 MWe electrical output per module (600 MWe total), with a net output of about 570 MWe for a 12-module facility after accounting for house loads. The plant includes all necessary primary and secondary systems, such as main steam systems, turbine generator sets, condensate and feedwater systems, shared external cooling water systems, module assembly equipment, fuel handling equipment, and turbine maintenance equipment. The PIM code, developed by NuScale, is used to assess NPM stability during operation, while the NIST-1 is a scaled stainless steel representation of the NPM reactor, containment, and reactor pool systems, with a maximum operating pressure of 1650 psia (11.4 MPa) and temperature of 630°F (605 K). The design and safety bases of the reactor coolant system (RCS) and its major components are closely interrelated, and the facility’s documentation generally describes individual modules, with multi-module information provided where relevant.

For Combined License (COL) applicants referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification, several responsibilities are specified: development of an on-site flood mitigation program (COL Item 3.4-2); justification of fuel performance codes and methods for non-baseload operations (COL Item 4.2-1); demonstration of NuScale Power Module stability during normal and power maneuvering operations for closed-loop module control systems using reactor power as a control input (COL Item 7.0-1); description of processes for handling and receipt of critical loads, including NuScale Power Modules (COL Item 9.1-5); provision of a plan for developing, implementing, and controlling operating procedures, including preliminary schedules, target completion dates, and identification of the responsible operating organization group (COL Item 13.5-5); development of a Startup Administration Manual detailing administrative procedures and requirements for the Initial Test Program (COL Item 14.2-2); and description of a human performance monitoring program in accordance with NUREG-0711 or equivalent criteria (COL Item 18.12-1). The NuScale Power Module (NPM) design features an integral power module comprising a reactor core, two helical-coil steam generators (SGs), and a pressurizer, all contained within a single reactor pressure vessel (RPV). This RPV is immediately surrounded by a steel containment vessel (CNV) that is approximately cylindrical and sits in the reactor pool. The reactor core is positioned below the helical-coil SGs inside the RPV. The design eliminates the need for external piping between the steam generators, pressurizer, and RPV, and utilizes natural circulation for reactor coolant system flow, removing the requirement for reactor coolant pumps. For valve actuation testing, a pilot-operated main valve with trip line tubing and a trip valve representative of the NPM design was used to simulate performance and assess potential boric acid buildup. Pressurizer level instrumentation is provided to the control room and the Module Control System (MCS), supporting an exemption from certain power supply requirements per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx). Pressurizer pressure is measured and provides input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS).

Preliminary critical heat flux (CHF) tests for the NuScale small modular reactor were conducted at Stern Laboratories in Ontario, Canada, using a high-pressure flow loop and custom 5x5 fuel assembly simulators with a 2-meter heated length, various axial power profiles, and configurations with and without simulated guide tubes. These tests examined the effects of shorter fuel length and low-flow natural circulation, generating data for the development and validation of the NuScale NSP2 CHF correlation. Additional CHF testing at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics (KATHY) facility in Germany, using prototypic fuel designs, provided further data to validate NSP2 and develop the NSP4 CHF correlation for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. CHF occurrence was identified by temperature excursions in the fuel simulators, and data collected included bundle subchannel exit temperatures for mixing coefficient determination and single-phase/two-phase pressure drop characteristics across various bundle powers and hydraulic conditions. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, prepared under 10 CFR 52.47(a), details the plant’s general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, including process effluent radiation monitoring (Section 11.5.1.2.2). The NuScale plant comprises 12 modules, each with a 160 MWth reactor and 50 MWe gross output, for a total net output of 570 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The core uses UO2 fuel enriched below 4.95% with a 24-month refueling interval. Safety features include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps. The containment is designed for high pressure to maintain integrity during worst-case accidents, minimizing radioactive releases. The NuScale Power Plant consists of up to 12 NuScale Power Modules (NPMs), each a modular, integral pressurized water reactor (PWR) rated at 160 MWt (about 50 MWe gross), for a total plant net output of approximately 570 MWe. Each NPM is partially immersed in a below-grade reactor pool, with the water surface about 6 feet below grade, and is enclosed by a high-pressure steel containment vessel (CNV) that operates at a vacuum and is submerged in water for passive fission product control. The reactor pool, spent fuel pool, most primary systems, and some radioactive waste equipment are located below grade, providing multiple barriers for fission product retention beyond the fuel, cladding, reactor pressure vessel (RPV), and containment. The NPM core contains 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, ~2 m rod length, <4.95% U-235 enrichment, Gd2O3 burnable absorber) and 16 control rod assemblies (CRAs), supported by five spacer grids and utilizing low-flow natural circulation for primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps and large-break LOCA scenarios. Each module features passive safety systems, including dedicated Emergency Core Cooling Systems (ECCS) and Decay Heat Removal Systems (DHRS), with three independent reactor vent valves and two independent reactor recirculation valves, capable of removing 100% of decay heat for at least 72 hours without water addition. The design eliminates external piping between major components, and the containment system meets DSRS 6.2.1.1.A requirements without using ice condenser or suppression pool systems. Refueling is independent for each NPM, with bioshields designed for removal and stacking, and the reactor core remains in the lower RPV during refueling. The plant includes a main steam system, turbine generator set, condenser, cooling tower, and condensate/feedwater system, with all hoisting, handling, and electrical connections above the pool water level. The module and plant control systems enable monitoring and control from the main control room, with dedicated panels for each NPM and integrated safety, alarm, and radiation monitoring. Critical heat flux (CHF) testing at Stern Laboratories and AREVA KATHY facility validated the NSP2 and NSP4 CHF correlations for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, supporting safety analysis and licensing. The reactor coolant system operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The containment lacks subcompartments, high-energy piping, or internal fuel handling, and does not require large vent openings. The NuScale design incorporates several advanced safety and operational features, including safe shutdown and cooling without reliance on AC or DC power, compact helical coil steam generators with reactor pressure on the tube exteriors, and a high-strength steel containment vessel immersed in a water pool. The containment operates at sub-atmospheric pressure during normal conditions, and the reactor core is small, resulting in a reduced source term. Comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls (I&C) are used for monitoring and control. The plant design is scalable, supporting incremental capacity growth in multi-unit configurations. The passive NuScale system eliminates pumps, sumps, suction strainers, debris interceptors, and trash racks, thereby minimizing debris-related and non-condensable effects on core coolant flow, and complies with regulatory guidance on debris management and chemical effects. Due to the modular and compact nature of the NuScale Power Module, passive separation of redundant safety trains is not always feasible; instead, fire protection measures are implemented to ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational in the event of a fire. Unlike traditional large water reactors, the NuScale design integrates the pressurizer above the reactor core within the reactor vessel, with no connecting piping, ensuring that core uncovery and damage cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint.

The NuScale plant design departs significantly from traditional large light water reactors (LWRs) and is not addressed by legacy regulatory guidance or codes such as PWRGALE. Its safety strategy relies on passive features and natural circulation, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps, safety-related pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, safety injection tanks, and safety-related motor-operated valves. Consequently, regulatory requirements and guidance related to these components—including flywheel integrity, motor qualification, thermal overload protection, ECCS accumulator lines, and gas accumulation in forced circulation systems—are not applicable. The design also omits an auxiliary feedwater (AFW) system, power-operated pressurizer relief valves, and pressurizer heaters, instead using a Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves, which are not susceptible to inadvertent opening. The integral pressurizer is located within the reactor vessel, with no surge line or connecting piping, eliminating thermal stratification and ensuring core uncovery cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint. The containment vessel is a high-strength steel structure immersed in a water pool, operating at sub-atmospheric pressure, and the compact core results in a reduced source term. The plant is scalable, supports multi-unit configurations, and uses comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls. Debris management and chemical effects are minimized by the elimination of pumps, sumps, strainers, and trash racks. Fire protection measures ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational, compensating for the inability to passively separate redundant safety trains due to the modular design. Regulatory requirements from 10 CFR 50.34(f) and NEI 06-12, as well as ATWS provisions, are largely inapplicable, and NuScale is exempt from related evaluations and analyses. For coolant source term evaluation, NuScale uses an alternate methodology incorporating aspects of ANSI/ANS 18.1. The only pumps within safety scope are ASME Class III CVCS pumps, which do not serve a safety function. The design meets DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials. For spent fuel pool mitigation, NuScale is exempt from certain requirements due to its below-grade, undrainable SFP. The design considers postulated startup reactivity accidents and addresses loading transients and structural evaluations for postulated breaks in CVCS and reactor vent valve piping. Containment vessel evacuation system valve closure times are addressed under SRP Section 6.2.4. Overall, NuScale’s unique passive and modular features support exemptions from many TMI-related and traditional LWR regulatory requirements.

The NuScale Power Plant consists of up to 12 NuScale Power Modules (NPMs), each partially immersed in a below-grade reactor pool with the water surface approximately 6 feet below grade. The reactor pool, spent fuel pool (SFP), most primary systems, and some radioactive waste equipment are all located below grade, providing additional barriers for fission product retention beyond the fuel, cladding, reactor pressure vessel (RPV), and containment. Each NPM features passive safety systems, including dedicated Emergency Core Cooling Systems (ECCS) and Decay Heat Removal Systems (DHRS), with three independent reactor vent valves and two independent reactor recirculation valves for passive decay heat removal during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA). The ECCS and DHRS are capable of removing 100% of the decay heat load for at least 72 hours without water addition. The NPM core contains 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, ~2 m rod length, <4.95% U-235 enrichment, Gd2O3 burnable absorber) and 16 control rod assemblies (CRAs) organized into regulating and shutdown banks. The fuel assemblies are supported by five spacer grids and utilize low-flow natural circulation for primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps. The torispherical RPV head supports the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs) with unique features such as a remote disconnect mechanism and extended drive shaft, validated through proof-of-concept and alignment testing. Refueling operations are independent for each NPM, with bioshields designed for removal and stacking, and the reactor core remaining in the lower RPV during refueling. The RBC (Reactor Building Crane) facilitates NPM movement, and specialized tools and dry dock areas support disassembly, inspection, and maintenance. The module control system (MCS) and plant control system enable monitoring and control from the main control room (MCR), which features dedicated panels for each NPM and integrates safety, alarm, and radiation monitoring systems. The power conversion system includes a main steam system, turbine generator set, condenser, cooling tower, and condensate/feedwater system, with all hoisting, handling, and electrical connections located above the pool water level. The plant delivers a net output of approximately 570 MWe and allows for modular construction, incremental module operation, and off-site shipment of used NPMs.

The NuScale reactor design features an integral pressurizer located within the reactor pressure vessel, eliminating the need for a pressurizer surge line and thus avoiding issues of thermal stratification. Unlike conventional designs, NuScale does not use power-operated relief valves (PORVs), which are susceptible to inadvertent opening; instead, it employs spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves that do not share this vulnerability. The design does not rely on pressurizer heaters to establish or maintain natural circulation during hot shutdown conditions, making the requirements for pressurizer heater power supply and associated control interfaces in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xiii) inapplicable—NuScale considers its hot shutdown condition equivalent to the regulatory hot standby condition. Furthermore, NuScale does not include pressurizer relief or block valves, rendering the power supply requirements for these components in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) not technically relevant. For safe shutdown (Mode 3) with reactor coolant temperatures below 200°F, NuScale equates this state to cold shutdown as defined in OM Code ISTA-2000, and its DHRS actuation valves meet ASME OM Code exercising and observation requirements. Overall, these design features support exemptions from certain TMI-related regulatory requirements.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must provide a certified Overpressure Protection Report in accordance with ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Section III Subarticles NB-7200 and NC-7200, demonstrating that the reactor coolant pressure boundary and secondary system have adequate overpressure protection, including low temperature overpressure protection features. The applicant must also establish plant-specific procedures for operator actions to identify, monitor, and trend reactor coolant system leakage when leakage rates are above normal but below technical specification limits, ensuring operators have sufficient time to act before limits are reached. Additionally, measures must be established to control onsite cleaning of the reactor pressure vessel during construction per Regulatory Guide 1.28, and operating procedures must be developed to prevent operational transients more severe than those for which the reactor design has been demonstrated adequate. Finally, the applicant must develop a containment leakage rate testing program specifying whether the prescriptive-based (Option A) or performance-based (Option B) approach under 10 CFR 50, Appendix J will be implemented.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report demonstrates that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) values well above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb throughout the 60-year design life, with initial values of at least 75 ft-lb and predicted end-of-life values of 58.4 ft-lb (lower shell), 62.3 ft-lb (weld), and 62.9 ft-lb (bottom head) at 57 effective full-power years (EFPY). The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. results.question: How many reactor safety valves does the NuScale RCS have?
results.expected_answer: The NuScale RCS has 2 reactor safety valves (RSVs).
results.generated_answer:
The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) has two reactor safety valves (RSVs) per module.  
(Source: Overpressure protection for each NuScale Power Module (NPM) is provided by two pilot-operated, self-actuating Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs)...)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) is designed for natural circulation of the primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps or external piping. The RCS serves as the core neutron moderator and includes reflector blocks to improve neutron economy. It features a degasification line to remove noncondensible gases from the pressurizer (PZR) volume during normal operation. The reactor vessel internals (RVI) support and align the core, control rod assemblies, drive shafts, surveillance capsules, and instrument guide tubes. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core region—89 ft³, steam generator (SG) region—621 ft³, and PZR region—578 ft³ (with the cylindrical portion at 487 ft³). Each integrated NuScale Power Module (NPM) contains a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) housing the reactor, RVI, PZR, two SGs, reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, RCS injection and discharge lines, pressurizer spray and high-point degasification vent lines, and a decay heat removal system (DHRS). The system is depicted in Figure 5.1-2, showing key components and flow paths.

The NuScale reactor facility features site-specific cooling tower location, orientation, equipment design, and operation, with the reactor core housed in a core support assembly seated in the lower reactor pressure vessel (RPV) assembly and connected to a central hot leg riser. Each Reactor Coolant System (RCS) line enters containment through welded penetrations on the containment upper head, with two containment isolation valves mounted externally, and a single pressurizer spray supply line also entering through the containment head. The pressurizer is integral to the reactor vessel, comprising the upper region above the naturally circulating coolant, separated by a baffle plate. The pressurizer includes the RPV upper shell and head, baffle plate, heater bundles, and two spray nozzles (each 1 inch in diameter). Instrumentation measures the steam-water interface level, providing input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS), including low water level protection for the heaters. The pressurizer heaters maintain RCS pressure during normal operation, supplying at least 785 kW (with a 15% margin), divided into three control groups per bundle, with two heater bundles per pressurizer, each bundle rated at 400 kW. Heater parameters include 480 Vac voltage and 60 Hz frequency, with heater sheath temperature at 800°F. The pressurizer operates at an internal pressure of 2100 psia, external pressure of 1000 psia, and temperature of 650°F. Pressurizer level is maintained within its operating band by operator action to add or reduce coolant inventory via the LRWS.

The NuScale reactor design utilizes the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to satisfy GDC 26, with its sole safety-related function being the prevention of inadvertent boron dilution in the reactor coolant system (RCS). The CVCS, including the Boron Recovery System, partially meets DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0 requirements, fulfilling criteria for functional performance, pumping capacity, defense-in-depth RCS makeup, single failure, GDC 5, contamination minimization, system design, leakage detection, and ITAAC, but only partially addresses holdup tank wall buckling/failure and venting/draining due to design differences. The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) departs from conventional PWRs by omitting high- and low-pressure safety injection pumps, containment spray, and a sump, instead relying on steam condensation and natural recirculation of water within the containment vessel for core cooling, meeting ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46. The ECCS does not provide poison addition or makeup as required by GDC 27, and has departures in inservice inspection, operability testing, and combined reactivity control system capability, though actuation signal guidance is applied. The design ensures RCS pressure remains below 110% of design during inadvertent ECCS operation, maintains minimum DNBR, provides high-point venting for noncondensible gases, and includes control room instrumentation for clear indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(vi) and (xviii). The ECCS requires no personnel access, pumps, or containment pressure for recirculation, and lacks refueling water storage tanks; condensate storage is outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building with no safety risk from failure. Instrumentation and Controls review per DSRS 7.0 applies but lacks specific acceptance criteria. Gas accumulation is not expected to impact ECCS performance, and the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) interfaces only with the secondary system. Control room habitability meets SRP 6.4, Rev 3 for the emergency zone, and pre-operational ECCS testing will be performed to meet regulatory guidance.

The RCS, a subsystem of the Nuclear Power Module (NPM) within the containment vessel (CNV), consists of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) with integral pressurizer (PZR), reactor vessel internals (RVI), reactor safety valves (RSVs), RCS piping (injection, discharge, PZR spray supply, high-point degasification), PZR control cabinet, and associated instruments and cables. It transfers heat from the core to two once-through, helical-coil steam generators (SGs) by natural circulation, with SGs located in the annular space between the hot leg riser and RPV inner wall. The RCS interfaces with the CVCS, ECCS (

Overpressure protection for each NuScale Power Module (NPM) is provided by two pilot-operated, self-actuating Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) mounted on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) upper head, discharging directly to the Containment Vessel (CNV) and forming part of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). The RSVs, designed and constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, are Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, and sized to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the design pressure (2310 psia), with each valve capable of 100% of the required relief capacity. The RSVs utilize RCS pressure routed to a chamber above the pilot disc, with relief pressure determined by the pilot valve spring pre-load and main valve closing spring, and feature a metal bellows, spring-operated pilot valve arrangement per NB-7511.1. Each RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, with periodic inspection and testing facilitated by bolted flange connections. The RSVs have a 3-inch inlet, 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, design temperature of 650°F, and a minimum design capacity of 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam at 3% overpressure. Nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% and 2100 psia ±1%, with a blowdown of 10% ±2%. Redundant RSVs ensure system reliability and provide overpressure protection for both the RCPB and steam generator (SG) tubing and plena. Overpressure protection during startup, shutdown, and low-temperature conditions is provided by three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs), also safety-related, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, and constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, with solenoid pilot actuators mounted outside the CNV and inadvertent actuation block features. RVVs prevent brittle fracture stress limits from being exceeded at low temperatures and provide sufficient relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure. Thermal relief valves, spring-operated and balanced-bellows, protect secondary system components and control rod drive system cooling piping during containment isolation but are not credited for safety-related overpressure protection during operation. All valves are environmentally and functionally qualified per ASME QME-1 and RG 1.100, with direct position indication provided in the control room per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi). The system is designed to comply with 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 15, and ASME BPVC service limits, with the most severe anticipated operational occurrence (AOO)—a turbine trip at full power without bypass—used as the bounding event for RSV capacity and RPV overpressure analyses.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is designed to ensure the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) average temperature remains below 420°F within 36 hours following an initiating event, without challenging the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) or uncovering the core. The 420°F threshold is based on the safe shutdown temperature recommended by EPRI for passive plant designs and accepted by the NRC. The DHRS does not require actuation of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) and is shown to be insensitive to a wide range of reactor pool temperatures and to the failure of a Main Steam Isolation Valve (MSIV) to close. Performance analyses demonstrate that the DHRS can remove significant heat across a broad range of loop inventories and accommodate fouling and steam generator (SG) tube plugging. With two DHRS trains operating under nominal conditions, the RCS temperature drops below 420°F in less than 2 hours and stabilizes below 300°F within 36 hours. In low and high DHRS inventory cases, the RCS temperature stabilizes below 350°F and 400°F, respectively, within 36 hours, even with an initial reactor pool temperature of 140°F, consistent with Technical Specifications. The DHRS is also capable of cooling the RCS below 420°F in less than 36 hours with one train operating under limiting off-normal conditions and a single active MSIV failure. The system can remove heat in excess of decay heat after 36 hours, even with the RCS at 420°F and the pool at boiling. Initial testing will use the MHS to bring the RCS to near-normal conditions, then actuate the DHRS and monitor RCS temperature to verify performance against design basis requirements.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is a passive, closed-loop safety system designed to remove decay heat from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) using the steam generators (SGs) and natural circulation. The DHRS operates by circulating water from its condenser through the SG tubes, where it absorbs decay heat, converts to steam, and returns to the condenser to release heat to the reactor pool water. The system is connected to the steam and feedwater piping between the main steam isolation valves (MSIVs) and feedwater isolation valves (FWIVs), forming a closed loop outside containment and providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and reactor pool. Each DHRS train is associated with one of the two NPM SGs, with actuation valves and orifices arranged to promote natural circulation and moderate flow. The DHRS is designed to maintain the RCS average temperature below 420°F within 36 hours of an initiating event, as recommended by EPRI and accepted by the NRC, without challenging the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) or uncovering the core, and without requiring Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) actuation. Performance analyses, including steady-state and transient models using NRELAP5, confirm the DHRS can remove heat across a wide range of loop inventories, accommodate SG tube plugging, fouling, noncondensible gas accumulation, and MSIV failure, and is insensitive to reactor pool temperature variations. With two DHRS trains, RCS temperature drops below 420°F in less than 2 hours and stabilizes below 300°F within 36 hours; with one train and limiting conditions, RCS temperature remains below 420°F within 36 hours. Figures and tables referenced (e.g., Figures 1.2-8, 5.4-10 to 5.4-16; Tables 5.4-5, 5.4-6, 5.4-8) provide detailed design data, failure modes analysis, and temperature cooldown profiles for various DHRS operational scenarios, demonstrating robust decay heat removal capability under both nominal and off-normal conditions. System performance is monitored by RCS temperature and pressure sensors, and initial testing will verify DHRS operation against design basis requirements.

The NuScale design incorporates several advanced safety and operational features, including safe shutdown and cooling without reliance on AC or DC power, compact helical coil steam generators with reactor pressure on the tube exteriors, and a high-strength steel containment vessel immersed in a water pool. The containment operates at sub-atmospheric pressure during normal conditions, and the reactor core is small, resulting in a reduced source term. Comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls (I&C) are used for monitoring and control. The plant design is scalable, supporting incremental capacity growth in multi-unit configurations. The passive NuScale system eliminates pumps, sumps, suction strainers, debris interceptors, and trash racks, thereby minimizing debris-related and non-condensable effects on core coolant flow, and complies with regulatory guidance on debris management and chemical effects. Due to the modular and compact nature of the NuScale Power Module, passive separation of redundant safety trains is not always feasible; instead, fire protection measures are implemented to ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational in the event of a fire. Unlike traditional large water reactors, the NuScale design integrates the pressurizer above the reactor core within the reactor vessel, with no connecting piping, ensuring that core uncovery and damage cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint.

The NuScale Nuclear Steam Supply System (NSSS) is a passive, NuScale-designed small modular pressurized water reactor (PWR), with each NuScale Power Module (NPM) featuring its own compact steel containment vessel mounted to the Reactor Building module compartment walls and at the bottom within the Reactor Building pool. Unlike traditional large light water reactor (LWR) designs, the NuScale containment vessel does not have subcompartments, internal compartments as referenced in GDC 50, or reactor coolant loops, and does not house high-energy piping or fuel storage and handling systems inside containment. Instead, fuel handling operations are performed in the Reactor Building pool where the NPMs reside, making containment design provisions for such operations inapplicable to NuScale’s containment vessel. The NuScale containment vessel’s high-pressure capability eliminates the need for features such as a 3-foot opening for venting, which were requirements for traditional large LWR containment structures.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) features an integral containment system that provides primary containment for the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), as detailed in Section 6.2. The containment design conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, Rev 0 requirements, including design margin for containment pressure, heat removal capability, protection from external pressure, monitoring instrumentation, internal structures, and evaluation of accidents involving generated hydrogen or other modules. For the limiting event, the peak containment pressure remains below the design pressure. The NuScale design does not utilize an ice condenser containment, as specified in SRP 6.2.1.1.B, Draft Rev 3. Additionally, preliminary design information requirements under 10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(v) are not applicable to the design certification application per 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8) and 10 CFR 50.34(f). Further details on containment systems are provided in Section 6.2.

1.1.2 
Containment Type
The NuScale containment vessel (CNV) is a supported, cylindrical vessel-type containment 
that is designed to withstand limiting high-pressure transients.

The NuScale plant design departs significantly from traditional large light water reactors (LWRs) and is not addressed by legacy regulatory guidance or codes such as PWRGALE. Its safety strategy relies on passive features and natural circulation, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps, safety-related pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, safety injection tanks, and safety-related motor-operated valves. Consequently, regulatory requirements and guidance related to these components—including flywheel integrity, motor qualification, thermal overload protection, ECCS accumulator lines, and gas accumulation in forced circulation systems—are not applicable. The design also omits an auxiliary feedwater (AFW) system, power-operated pressurizer relief valves, and pressurizer heaters, instead using a Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves, which are not susceptible to inadvertent opening. The integral pressurizer is located within the reactor vessel, with no surge line or connecting piping, eliminating thermal stratification and ensuring core uncovery cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint. The containment vessel is a high-strength steel structure immersed in a water pool, operating at sub-atmospheric pressure, and the compact core results in a reduced source term. The plant is scalable, supports multi-unit configurations, and uses comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls. Debris management and chemical effects are minimized by the elimination of pumps, sumps, strainers, and trash racks. Fire protection measures ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational, compensating for the inability to passively separate redundant safety trains due to the modular design. Regulatory requirements from 10 CFR 50.34(f) and NEI 06-12, as well as ATWS provisions, are largely inapplicable, and NuScale is exempt from related evaluations and analyses. For coolant source term evaluation, NuScale uses an alternate methodology incorporating aspects of ANSI/ANS 18.1. The only pumps within safety scope are ASME Class III CVCS pumps, which do not serve a safety function. The design meets DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials. For spent fuel pool mitigation, NuScale is exempt from certain requirements due to its below-grade, undrainable SFP. The design considers postulated startup reactivity accidents and addresses loading transients and structural evaluations for postulated breaks in CVCS and reactor vent valve piping. Containment vessel evacuation system valve closure times are addressed under SRP Section 6.2.4. Overall, NuScale’s unique passive and modular features support exemptions from many TMI-related and traditional LWR regulatory requirements.

While the NuScale 
containment vessel design includes an 
evacuation system, it serves a different 
purpose than a purge system, and includes 
features that provide suitable means to 
prevent radiological release to the environs 
(see DSRS 6.2.4, AC II.13).

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) is a modular, movable nuclear steam supply system (NSSS) comprising a reactor core, pressurizer, and two steam generators integrated within a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and housed in a compact steel containment vessel (CNV). The NuScale plant design is scalable, supporting from one to twelve NPMs within a single Reactor Building, with each module rated at 160 MWt (totaling 1,920 MWt for 12 modules) and approximately 50 MWe electrical output per module (600 MWe total), with a net output of about 570 MWe for a 12-module facility after accounting for house loads. The plant includes all necessary primary and secondary systems, such as main steam systems, turbine generator sets, condensate and feedwater systems, shared external cooling water systems, module assembly equipment, fuel handling equipment, and turbine maintenance equipment. The PIM code, developed by NuScale, is used to assess NPM stability during operation, while the NIST-1 is a scaled stainless steel representation of the NPM reactor, containment, and reactor pool systems, with a maximum operating pressure of 1650 psia (11.4 MPa) and temperature of 630°F (605 K). The design and safety bases of the reactor coolant system (RCS) and its major components are closely interrelated, and the facility’s documentation generally describes individual modules, with multi-module information provided where relevant.

Preliminary critical heat flux (CHF) tests for the NuScale small modular reactor were conducted at Stern Laboratories in Ontario, Canada, using a high-pressure flow loop and custom 5x5 fuel assembly simulators with a 2-meter heated length, various axial power profiles, and configurations with and without simulated guide tubes. These tests examined the effects of shorter fuel length and low-flow natural circulation, generating data for the development and validation of the NuScale NSP2 CHF correlation. Additional CHF testing at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics (KATHY) facility in Germany, using prototypic fuel designs, provided further data to validate NSP2 and develop the NSP4 CHF correlation for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. CHF occurrence was identified by temperature excursions in the fuel simulators, and data collected included bundle subchannel exit temperatures for mixing coefficient determination and single-phase/two-phase pressure drop characteristics across various bundle powers and hydraulic conditions. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, prepared under 10 CFR 52.47(a), details the plant’s general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, including process effluent radiation monitoring (Section 11.5.1.2.2). The NuScale plant comprises 12 modules, each with a 160 MWth reactor and 50 MWe gross output, for a total net output of 570 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The core uses UO2 fuel enriched below 4.95% with a 24-month refueling interval. Safety features include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps. The containment is designed for high pressure to maintain integrity during worst-case accidents, minimizing radioactive releases. The FSAR references tables and figures detailing plant and module characteristics (Tables 1.2-1, 1.2-2), system boundaries (Figure 1.2-2), module schematics (Figure 1.2-3), multi-module layouts (Figures 1.2-4, 1.2-5), module cutaways (Figure 1.2-6), steam generator and reactor flow (Figure 1.2-7), technical reports (Table 1.6-2), and I&C diagrams (Table 1.7-1). conditions, the average primary coolant temperature is maintained at a constant value.The 
full power average coolant temperature is higher for higher primary coolant flow 
conditions, and lower for lower primary coolant flow conditions.This phenomenon 
provides for similar hot leg temperatures at full power conditions, which allows for the SG 
to generate sufficient superheat independent of the full power primary coolant flow rate.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides detailed operational and design data for the primary system and decay heat removal system. Table 5.1-2 presents primary system temperatures and flow rates at various reactor power levels, with reactor power ranging from 0% (0 MWt) to 100% (160 MWt). At 100% power and best-estimate flow, the primary flow is 587.0 kg/s, with core ΔT of 99.8°F, TCold at 496.6°F, Tavg at 543.3°F, and THot at 590.1°F. Minimum and maximum design flows at 100% power are 538.5 kg/s and 660.5 kg/s, respectively, with corresponding temperature variations. When core decay heat is unavailable, the module heatup system drives natural circulation. Transient load contributions, including fatigue, are considered in component calculations as per Table 3.9-3. For conservative safety analysis, an initial core power of 102% is assumed to account for measurement uncertainty, and a 1.2 multiplier is applied to core decay heat. The decay heat removal system is designed with an internal pressure of 2100 psia, actuation valve external pressure of 60 psia, passive condenser external pressure of 27 psia, and operates at 650°F. Each of the two condensers contains 80 tubes (1.315-inch outer diameter, 0.109-inch wall thickness), with 258.2 ft² external surface area per condenser and a fouling factor of 0.0005 hr-ft²-F/BTU. The system schematic (Figure 5.4-10) shows two passive condensers located in the reactor pool within the containment vessel, with associated valves, pressure transmitters, level transmitters, and connections to the steam generators.

results.question: What is the RCS primary flow rate at 100% power according to Table 5.1-2?
results.expected_answer: According to Table 5.1-2, the primary flow rate at 100% power is 587 kg/s.
results.generated_answer: At 100% power, the RCS primary flow rate is 587.0 kg/s (best-estimate), 538.5 kg/s (minimum design flow), and 660.5 kg/s (maximum design flow) according to Table 5.1-2. (Source: Table 5.1-2, NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5)
retrieved_contexts: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed to promote natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS ensures coolant supply to the core to maintain specified acceptable fuel design limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences. A schematic flow diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and Table 5.1-1 detail major RCS components, flow stages, and volumes during steady-state, full-power operation. The SGS, with the main steam and feedwater systems, removes decay heat from the RCS during initial cooldown. Optimal performance criteria include maximizing electrical generation, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining margin to analytical limits. Key parameters—such as primary coolant temperatures, flow rates, pressurizer (PZR) water level, steam generator (SG) water level and mass, feedwater and steam flow rates, pressures, and steam superheat—are determined using NRELAP5 thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand calculations. Design calculations establish best-estimate, maximum, and minimum primary coolant flows, accounting for uncertainties in component flow resistances, core bypass flow, and thermal head capability, with bounding uncertainties based on test data and design requirements. Minimum and maximum design flow rates are calculated by biasing analytical uncertainties to minimize or maximize flow, respectively, and are used in bounding design analyses. The module heatup system provides RCS heating and coolant flow before criticality; between hot zero power and 15% reactor power, core heat is used to reach normal operating coolant temperature. From 15% to full power, average primary coolant temperature is held constant, with higher full-power coolant temperatures for higher flow rates, ensuring similar hot leg temperatures and sufficient SG superheat regardless of flow. Flow rate varies with module heatup system performance, SG operation, and RCS temperature, with typical hot zero power temperatures and steady-state best-estimate flow rates identified. The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RCS to the secondary steam system, supplying superheated steam to the power conversion cycle, with operating conditions summarized in Table 5.4-1 for full-power steam flow at best-estimate primary coolant conditions.

A schematic flow diagram of the RCS is provided in Figure 5.1-3, denoting major 
components, and major RCS loop flow stages during normal steady-state, full-power 
operating conditions.In addition, Table 5.1-1 identifies the RCS volumes.The SGS (in conjunction with the main steam and main feedwater systems) are used to 
remove decay heat from the RCS during the initial phase of module cooldown.

Maximum design primary coolant flow rate is used in design 
analyses where it is bounding to assume a high flow rate.The module heatup system is used to heat up the RCS and provide primary coolant flow 
before the reactor is critical.Between hot zero power and 15 percent reactor power, the 
heat generated from the reactor core is used to heat up the RCS to the normal operating 
average coolant temperature.

The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed for natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during both power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps and external piping, with all major components—including the core, pressurizer (PZR), two steam generators (SGs), reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, and decay heat removal system (DHRS)—integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). The core heats the coolant, which rises through the lower and upper riser assemblies, is redirected by the PZR baffle plate, and flows over the SG helical tube bundles, transferring heat to the secondary side and producing superheated steam with a moisture content ≤0.10% by weight at full power. The cooled, denser coolant descends through the annular downcomer and reenters the core, completing the natural circulation loop, with flow driven by density differences and elevation between the core and SGs. The RCS flow path is optimized for low pressure losses, so pressure is primarily determined by static head, and SG tube plugging does not affect natural circulation flow rate. The system includes small bypass flow paths in the upper riser to maintain boron concentration during DHRS-driven riser uncovery after non-LOCA transients. The pressurizer, integral to the RPV above the coolant and separated by a baffle plate, operates at 2100 psia and 650°F, with two 1-inch spray nozzles and heater bundles (each 400 kW, total ≥785 kW with 15% margin, 480 Vac, 60 Hz, 800°F sheath temperature) to maintain RCS pressure. Instrumentation provides control room indication of RCS pressure, pressurizer level, spray flow, and heater output, with the Monitoring and Control System (MCS) automating RCS level and pressure control. The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) maintains pressurizer level (normally 50–60%), adjusts RCS chemistry, and controls boron concentration, supplying continuous spray flow to minimize thermal stresses and maintain chemistry. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core—89 ft³, SG region—621 ft³, and PZR—578 ft³ (487 ft³ cylindrical). During off-normal transients, initial pool temperatures of 100°F or 140°F have minimal effect on RCS cooldown after 36 hours, even with reduced heat removal from fouling, tube plugging, or noncondensible gas accumulation. The system’s design, including intertwined SG tube bundles and elimination of external piping, prevents asymmetric core cooling and large break LOCA events. The 
primary coolant continues to flow down through the annular downcomer below the 
SGs into the lower reactor vessel plenum, where it reenters the reactor core.Further 
discussion of the RCS is provided in Section 5.1 and the RCS loop flow is illustrated in 
Figure 5.1-3.The SGs deliver superheated steam with moisture content no greater than 0.10 percent 
by weight during full-power operating conditions.

Flow rate will vary as a function of module heatup system performance, steam generator operation 
and RCS temperature.A typical hot zero power temperature and range of the steady state best-estimate flow rates are 
identified.

5.4.1.3 
Performance Evaluation
A single RCS natural circulation flow loop is entirely contained within the RPV, thereby 
eliminating distinct RCS piping loops and the associated potential for a large pipe 
break (i.e., large break loss-of-coolant accident [LOCA]) event.This design, combined 
with the intertwined SGs tube bundle configuration, eliminates the potential for 
asymmetric core cooling and temperatures as a result of a loss of a single SG function.

The pressure losses through the RCS flow path are very low, to support the natural 
circulation design of the RCS.Since the pressure losses due to flow are small, the pressure 
at any location in the RCS flow path is primarily a function of the static head.The primary 
coolant flows over the outside of the helical SG tubes; therefore, SG tube plugging 
uncertainties are not applicable to the primary coolant natural circulation flow rate 
determination.

These design bases are listed as follows:
•
The RCS promotes natural circulation to transfer approximately 160 MW thermal power 
from the reactor core to the steam generator system (SGS) during power operation and 
the heat produced when the reactor is subcritical, including the initial phase of plant 
cooldown.•
The RCS provides coolant to the reactor core such that specified acceptable fuel design 
limits are not violated during normal operation including the effects of anticipated 
operational occurrences.

5.2.2.2.1

The main design function of the RSVs is to provide overpressure protection for the
