The Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) are integral components of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), bolted via flange to a nozzle safe end directly on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) head. Each RSV is designed to actuate on high Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure, allowing direct flow to containment, and is classified as Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB. The RSVs prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding 110% of design pressure under all conditions, with design information in Table 5.2-2 and materials in Table 6.1-3. Each pilot-operated RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, Subsubarticle NB-7520. Environmental qualification details are in Section 3.11. The Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) provide sufficient pressure relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure and are also environmentally qualified per Section 3.11. Both RSVs and RVVs are designed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-3500, meeting overpressure protection criteria in Article NB-7000, with applicable code editions and classifications detailed in Sections 5.2.1 and 3.2, and material specifications in Section 6.1. Functional qualification is performed per ASME QME-1 as endorsed by RG 1.100, with inservice testing and inspection ensuring continued reliability and conformance. RSVs are considered passive devices, and the reliability of both RSVs and RVVs is further discussed in Section 6.3.2.

The total pressurizer volume is approximately 23 percent of the total RCS volume.Pressurizer level during full-power operation is controlled between 50 percent and 
60 percent with a minimum pressurizer level of 50 percent at hot zero power (HZP) 
conditions.These programmed values provide margin to the upper and lower 
pressurizer water level analytical limits of 80 percent and 35 percent.

The pressurizer volume is sized, in conjunction with the pressurizer level control band 
and the capabilities of the CVCS, to prevent uncovering of the pressurizer heaters 
following a reactor trip.

The pressurizer is designed with sufficient combined saturated water and steam expansion volumes to ensure the desired pressure response to expected system volume changes without actuating safety systems. Its sizing accommodates surges from operating transients, preventing reactor trips due to RCS low or high pressure, and provides enough steam volume to accept in-surges from loss of load transients without liquid or two-phase flow reaching the Relief and Safety Valves (RSVs). The pressurizer volume, in conjunction with the pressurizer level control band and Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) capabilities, prevents uncovering of the pressurizer heaters following a reactor trip. The total pressurizer volume is approximately 23 percent of the total Reactor Coolant System (RCS) volume. During full-power operation, pressurizer level is controlled between 50 percent and 60 percent, with a minimum of 50 percent at hot zero power (HZP), providing margin to analytical limits of 80 percent (upper) and 35 percent (lower). The nominal pressurizer water level is programmed as a function of reactor power; between HZP and 15 percent power, significant reactor coolant volume changes occur due to heatup or cooldown, which are partially absorbed by adjusting pressurizer water level. However, to maintain sufficient steam volume for pressure control and margin to operating limits, the CVCS is used to add or remove reactor coolant mass as needed. Above 15 percent power, reactor coolant volume changes are much smaller.

The nominal pressurizer water level is provided as a function of reactor power level.Between HZP and 15 percent power, the reactor coolant experiences a heatup or 
cooldown and therefore a large change in volume.

Pressurizer instrumentation provides pressure and level information to the MPS, MCS, and 
control room as described in Chapter 7.5.4.5.1 
Design Basis
The pressurizer is designed to maintain RCS operating pressure so that operating 
transients do not result in a reactor trip or actuation of other safety systems when 
normal support systems are functional.

It also has sufficient combined saturated water 
volume and steam expansion volume to provide the desired pressure response to 
expected system volume changes without actuating safety systems.The pressurizer is sized to accommodate the surge resulting from operating transients 
without causing a reactor trip on RCS low or high pressure.It is also sized to provide 
sufficient steam volume to accept in-surge from a loss of load transient without liquid 
or two-phase flow reaching the RSVs.

The nominal 
pressurizer water level is set to accommodate the expected changes in RCS volume and 
makeup and letdown are not expected to be required to maintain pressurizer water 
level when maneuvering above 15 percent power.Pressure Control 
Pressurizer (and RCS) pressure is regulated by operation of PZR heaters to add steam to 
the PZR steam bubble and PZR spray flow to condense steam from the PZR steam 
bubble.

The pressurizer is a key component designed to maintain stable reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure during operation. Pressure is increased by applying power to two heater bundles located above the pressurizer baffle plate, which heat the water to a temperature higher than the coolant leaving the reactor core, ensuring a saturated water-steam interface. Pressure is decreased by activating pressurizer spray flow, with two spray nozzles on opposite sides providing adequate steam contact; a minimal spray flow is continuously maintained during normal operation to reduce thermal stresses on spray line components. Instrumentation monitors both pressurizer pressure and the steam-water interface level, providing data to the MPS, MCS, and control room. The pressurizer is designed so that operating transients do not cause a reactor trip or actuate other safety systems when normal support systems are functional. The nominal water level is set to accommodate expected RCS volume changes, and makeup or letdown is not required to maintain level above 15 percent power. Control rod drive shafts and instrument lines are located in the central region of the pressurizer.

The pressurizer (PZR) is an integral component of the reactor coolant system (RCS), occupying the volume above the baffle plate within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and containing heater bundles, spray nozzles, and instrument lines. Its primary function is to maintain RCS pressure within the permitted operating range during normal operation and transients, using electrical heaters to increase pressure by generating steam and spray nozzles to decrease pressure by condensing steam. Two heater bundles above the baffle plate heat water to a temperature higher than the core outlet, ensuring a saturated water-steam interface, while two spray nozzles—supplied by the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS)—provide pressure reduction and maintain chemistry equilibrium. Minimal spray flow is maintained during normal operation to reduce thermal stresses and maintain equilibrium. The pressurizer is sized to accommodate expected RCS volume changes, with a total volume approximately 23% of the RCS, and is designed to prevent reactor trips or safety system actuation during transients when support systems are functional. Nominal water level is programmed as a function of reactor power, controlled between 50–60% at full power, with analytical limits of 80% (upper) and 35% (lower), and is adjusted by the CVCS to absorb volume changes, especially between hot zero power (HZP) and 15% power. Instrumentation monitors pressure and level, providing data to the Module Protection System (MPS), Module Control System (MCS), and control room. During shutdown and refueling, decay heat is removed via the steam generator system (SGS) and decay heat removal system (DHRS), with the PZR not relied upon for safe shutdown. Overpressure protection is provided by reactor safety valves (RSVs) and relief valves, with the PZR, CVCS, and automatic isolation features preventing RCS solid conditions and protecting the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). During low-temperature operations, nitrogen is used for initial pressurization and bubble replacement, and the PZR is vented to match containment pressure before opening vent and relief valves. The MPS disconnects heater power if water level drops below the heater elements, using Class 1E circuit breakers for safety isolation. Pressurizer heaters are grouped, with Group 1 providing steady-state control and Groups 2 and 3 serving as backup during pressure drops. The PZR design ensures sufficient surge and steam volume to prevent liquid or two-phase flow to the RSVs during transients, and effective fluid mixing is achieved through thermal effects and flow over SG tube bundles. All PZR regions are constructed for required inspection and tested as part of RPV testing, with compliance to BPVC and seismic qualification addressed in referenced sections.

The pressurizer baffle plate is an integral, safety-related internal structure of the reactor vessel, forming the lower boundary of the pressurizer region, which is located above the baffle plate inside the RPV. It is integrated with the upper steam plenums and serves multiple functions: it acts as a thermal barrier, limits heat transfer between the pressurizer and the RCS coolant flowing from the reactor core to the steam generators, and provides hydraulic communication between the pressurizer and RCS regions. The baffle plate contains eight 4-inch diameter flow holes, allowing low-resistance, low-loss flow paths for rapid pressure equalization and thermal and chemical mixing between the pressurizer and the RCS, especially during heatup, startup, normal, transient, and faulted conditions. It also serves as the tubesheet for the upper termination of steam generator tube bundles, provides penetrations for alignment and support of control rod drive shafts and incore instrument guide tubes, and supports the upper riser assembly. The pressurizer, defined by the volume within the reactor vessel head and a portion of the cylindrical height below the head, maintains a saturated steam-water interface to regulate RCS pressure. The containment vessel (CNV) is a cylindrical steel pressure vessel that houses the RPV, CRDMs, and associated NSSS piping and components. Pressurizer level is controlled by the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) during normal operation.

The Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) are integral components of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), bolted via flange to a nozzle safe end directly on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) head. Each RSV is designed to actuate on high Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure, allowing direct flow to containment, and is classified as Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB. The RSVs prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding 110% of design pressure under all conditions, with design information in Table 5.2-2 and materials in Table 6.1-3. Each pilot-operated RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, Subsubarticle NB-7520. Environmental qualification details are in Section 3.11. The Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) provide sufficient pressure relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure and are also environmentally qualified per Section 3.11. Both RSVs and RVVs are designed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-3500, meeting overpressure protection criteria in Article NB-7000, with applicable code editions and classifications detailed in Sections 5.2.1 and 3.2, and material specifications in Section 6.1. Functional qualification is performed per ASME QME-1 as endorsed by RG 1.100, with inservice testing and inspection ensuring continued reliability and conformance. RSVs are considered passive devices, and the reliability of both RSVs and RVVs is further discussed in Section 6.3.2.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) design features an integral power module comprising a reactor core, two helical-coil steam generators (SGs), and a pressurizer, all contained within a single reactor pressure vessel (RPV). This RPV is immediately surrounded by a steel containment vessel (CNV) that is approximately cylindrical and sits in the reactor pool. The reactor core is positioned below the helical-coil SGs inside the RPV. The design eliminates the need for external piping between the steam generators, pressurizer, and RPV, and utilizes natural circulation for reactor coolant system flow, removing the requirement for reactor coolant pumps. For valve actuation testing, a pilot-operated main valve with trip line tubing and a trip valve representative of the NPM design was used to simulate performance and assess potential boric acid buildup. Pressurizer level instrumentation is provided to the control room and the Module Control System (MCS), supporting an exemption from certain power supply requirements per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx). Pressurizer pressure is measured and provides input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS).

Figure 1.2-6: Cutaway View of NuScale Power Module

The NuScale Power Plant consists of up to 12 NuScale Power Modules (NPMs), each partially immersed in a below-grade reactor pool with the water surface approximately 6 feet below grade. The reactor pool, spent fuel pool (SFP), most primary systems, and some radioactive waste equipment are all located below grade, providing additional barriers for fission product retention beyond the fuel, cladding, reactor pressure vessel (RPV), and containment. Each NPM features passive safety systems, including dedicated Emergency Core Cooling Systems (ECCS) and Decay Heat Removal Systems (DHRS), with three independent reactor vent valves and two independent reactor recirculation valves for passive decay heat removal during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA). The ECCS and DHRS are capable of removing 100% of the decay heat load for at least 72 hours without water addition. The NPM core contains 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, ~2 m rod length, <4.95% U-235 enrichment, Gd2O3 burnable absorber) and 16 control rod assemblies (CRAs) organized into regulating and shutdown banks. The fuel assemblies are supported by five spacer grids and utilize low-flow natural circulation for primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps. The torispherical RPV head supports the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs) with unique features such as a remote disconnect mechanism and extended drive shaft, validated through proof-of-concept and alignment testing. Refueling operations are independent for each NPM, with bioshields designed for removal and stacking, and the reactor core remaining in the lower RPV during refueling. The RBC (Reactor Building Crane) facilitates NPM movement, and specialized tools and dry dock areas support disassembly, inspection, and maintenance. The module control system (MCS) and plant control system enable monitoring and control from the main control room (MCR), which features dedicated panels for each NPM and integrates safety, alarm, and radiation monitoring systems. The power conversion system includes a main steam system, turbine generator set, condenser, cooling tower, and condensate/feedwater system, with all hoisting, handling, and electrical connections located above the pool water level. The plant delivers a net output of approximately 570 MWe and allows for modular construction, incremental module operation, and off-site shipment of used NPMs.

NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report
General Plant Description

components, and major RCS loop flow stages during normal steady-state, full-power

and denoting major RCS components.A simplified RCS diagram is provided in Figure 5.1-2.During normal operation, the RCS transports heat from the reactor core to the SGs by

5.4.1.3 
Performance Evaluation
A single RCS natural circulation flow loop is entirely contained within the RPV, thereby 
eliminating distinct RCS piping loops and the associated potential for a large pipe 
break (i.e., large break loss-of-coolant accident [LOCA]) event.This design, combined 
with the intertwined SGs tube bundle configuration, eliminates the potential for 
asymmetric core cooling and temperatures as a result of a loss of a single SG function.

The 
primary coolant continues to flow down through the annular downcomer below the 
SGs into the lower reactor vessel plenum, where it reenters the reactor core.Further 
discussion of the RCS is provided in Section 5.1 and the RCS loop flow is illustrated in 
Figure 5.1-3.The SGs deliver superheated steam with moisture content no greater than 0.10 percent 
by weight during full-power operating conditions.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) interfaces with several systems, including the chemical and volume control system (CVCS) via the containment system, the emergency core cooling system (ECCS) through reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), the containment vessel (CNV), control rod drive system, steam generator system (SGS), and the primary sampling system (via the CVCS). The RCS piping external to the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) comprises two pressurizer (PZR) spray line branches from a common spray header, an RPV high point degasification line, an RCS injection line, and an RCS discharge line. The RCS injection line includes branch lines connecting to each ECCS valve reset valve. The reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) encompasses the RCS injection and discharge piping interfacing with the CVCS up to the outermost containment isolation valve (CIV) on the top of each nuclear power module (NPM). This piping, including associated isolation valves, is designed as ASME BPVC Section III, Class 1, while the piping from the outermost CIVs to the NPM flange connections is not part of the RCPB and is designed as Class 3. Pressure-retaining piping that penetrates the RCS and forms part of the RCPB includes the PZR spray supply, RCS injection, RCS discharge, and RPV high-point degasification piping, as defined in 10 CFR 50.2. Figures 5.1-2 and 6.6-1 illustrate the RCS piping configuration, including the location and number of RSVs and RVVs relative to the RPV and the routing from the CNV upper head to RPV penetrations. Design, fabrication, and operational provisions to mitigate stress-corrosion cracking are detailed in Sections 3.9, 3.12, and 5.2. The RCS piping supports CVCS functions (see Section 9.3.4), and preservice and inservice inspection (ISI) requirements for ASME Class 1 components, including RCS piping, are summarized in Section 5.2.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed to promote natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS ensures coolant supply to the core to maintain specified acceptable fuel design limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences. A schematic flow diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and Table 5.1-1 detail major RCS components, flow stages, and volumes during steady-state, full-power operation. The SGS, with the main steam and feedwater systems, removes decay heat from the RCS during initial cooldown. Optimal performance criteria include maximizing electrical generation, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining margin to analytical limits. Key parameters—such as primary coolant temperatures, flow rates, pressurizer (PZR) water level, steam generator (SG) water level and mass, feedwater and steam flow rates, pressures, and steam superheat—are determined using NRELAP5 thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand calculations. Design calculations establish best-estimate, maximum, and minimum primary coolant flows, accounting for uncertainties in component flow resistances, core bypass flow, and thermal head capability, with bounding uncertainties based on test data and design requirements. Minimum and maximum design flow rates are calculated by biasing analytical uncertainties to minimize or maximize flow, respectively, and are used in bounding design analyses. The module heatup system provides RCS heating and coolant flow before criticality; between hot zero power and 15% reactor power, core heat is used to reach normal operating coolant temperature. From 15% to full power, average primary coolant temperature is held constant, with higher full-power coolant temperatures for higher flow rates, ensuring similar hot leg temperatures and sufficient SG superheat regardless of flow. Flow rate varies with module heatup system performance, SG operation, and RCS temperature, with typical hot zero power temperatures and steady-state best-estimate flow rates identified. The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RCS to the secondary steam system, supplying superheated steam to the power conversion cycle, with operating conditions summarized in Table 5.4-1 for full-power steam flow at best-estimate primary coolant conditions.

Figure 6.6-1 depicts the 
RCS piping from the CNV upper head to the respective penetrations on the RPV.5.4.2.3 
Performance Evaluation
Section 3.9, Section 3.12, and Section 5.2 provide information regarding the RCS piping 
criteria, methods, and materials, and include the design, fabrication, and operational 
provisions to control those factors that contribute to stress-corrosion cracking.

Figure 5.4-10 provides a simplified 
diagram of the DHRS illustrating the operational flowpath and major system 
components.Table 5.4-5 provides a summary of DHRS design data.Prior to power operations, the feedwater pumps fill the DHRS up to the actuation 
valves.

5.1.3.2 
Reactor Coolant System Piping
The RCS piping external to the RPV consists of the following lines: 
•
two PZR spray line branches from a common spray header 
•
RPV high point degasification line 
•
RCS injection line 
•
RCS discharge line 
The RCS injection line has branch lines that connect to each of the ECCS valve reset 
valves.

5.1.2 
System Description
The RCS is a subsystem of the NPM and is located inside the CNV.The RCS includes the RPV, 
the integral PZR, the RVI, the RSVs, reactor coolant piping inside the CNV and the RCS 
instruments and controls.

The systems and components connected to the RCS that are part of the RCPB are:
•
pressure housing of the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs) described in Section 4.6.•
RCS injection and discharge, PZR spray, and high point degasification piping up to the 
outermost CIVs that connect the NPM to the chemical and volume control system (CVCS), 
described in Section 5.4.2.

The RCS injection line also contains two branch connections to the ECCS lines that 
connect to the reset valves of the five ECCS valves: three RVVs and two RRVs.The RCS discharge line is routed from the containment upper head to a penetration on 
the side of the RPV at an elevation just below the SGs.This penetration takes suction 
from the annular region between the RPV wall and the riser.

the pressurizer is integral (i.e., is located

NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report
Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
Tier 2
5.4-34
Revision 5
5.4.5 
Pressurizer
The pressurizer is an integral part of the reactor vessel and is comprised of the upper region 
of the reactor vessel, above the region of naturally circulating reactor coolant.A baffle plate 
separates the two regions.

The pressurizer baffle plate is an integral, safety-related internal structure of the reactor vessel, forming the lower boundary of the pressurizer region, which is located above the baffle plate inside the RPV. It is integrated with the upper steam plenums and serves multiple functions: it acts as a thermal barrier, limits heat transfer between the pressurizer and the RCS coolant flowing from the reactor core to the steam generators, and provides hydraulic communication between the pressurizer and RCS regions. The baffle plate contains eight 4-inch diameter flow holes, allowing low-resistance, low-loss flow paths for rapid pressure equalization and thermal and chemical mixing between the pressurizer and the RCS, especially during heatup, startup, normal, transient, and faulted conditions. It also serves as the tubesheet for the upper termination of steam generator tube bundles, provides penetrations for alignment and support of control rod drive shafts and incore instrument guide tubes, and supports the upper riser assembly. The pressurizer, defined by the volume within the reactor vessel head and a portion of the cylindrical height below the head, maintains a saturated steam-water interface to regulate RCS pressure. The containment vessel (CNV) is a cylindrical steel pressure vessel that houses the RPV, CRDMs, and associated NSSS piping and components. Pressurizer level is controlled by the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) during normal operation.

The pressurizer is designed with sufficient combined saturated water and steam expansion volumes to ensure the desired pressure response to expected system volume changes without actuating safety systems. Its sizing accommodates surges from operating transients, preventing reactor trips due to RCS low or high pressure, and provides enough steam volume to accept in-surges from loss of load transients without liquid or two-phase flow reaching the Relief and Safety Valves (RSVs). The pressurizer volume, in conjunction with the pressurizer level control band and Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) capabilities, prevents uncovering of the pressurizer heaters following a reactor trip. The total pressurizer volume is approximately 23 percent of the total Reactor Coolant System (RCS) volume. During full-power operation, pressurizer level is controlled between 50 percent and 60 percent, with a minimum of 50 percent at hot zero power (HZP), providing margin to analytical limits of 80 percent (upper) and 35 percent (lower). The nominal pressurizer water level is programmed as a function of reactor power; between HZP and 15 percent power, significant reactor coolant volume changes occur due to heatup or cooldown, which are partially absorbed by adjusting pressurizer water level. However, to maintain sufficient steam volume for pressure control and margin to operating limits, the CVCS is used to add or remove reactor coolant mass as needed. Above 15 percent power, reactor coolant volume changes are much smaller.

The NuScale reactor facility features site-specific cooling tower location, orientation, equipment design, and operation, with the reactor core housed in a core support assembly seated in the lower reactor pressure vessel (RPV) assembly and connected to a central hot leg riser. Each Reactor Coolant System (RCS) line enters containment through welded penetrations on the containment upper head, with two containment isolation valves mounted externally, and a single pressurizer spray supply line also entering through the containment head. The pressurizer is integral to the reactor vessel, comprising the upper region above the naturally circulating coolant, separated by a baffle plate. The pressurizer includes the RPV upper shell and head, baffle plate, heater bundles, and two spray nozzles (each 1 inch in diameter). Instrumentation measures the steam-water interface level, providing input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS), including low water level protection for the heaters. The pressurizer heaters maintain RCS pressure during normal operation, supplying at least 785 kW (with a 15% margin), divided into three control groups per bundle, with two heater bundles per pressurizer, each bundle rated at 400 kW. Heater parameters include 480 Vac voltage and 60 Hz frequency, with heater sheath temperature at 800°F. The pressurizer operates at an internal pressure of 2100 psia, external pressure of 1000 psia, and temperature of 650°F. Pressurizer level is maintained within its operating band by operator action to add or reduce coolant inventory via the LRWS.

NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report

The NuScale reactor design features an integral pressurizer located within the reactor pressure vessel, eliminating the need for a pressurizer surge line and thus avoiding issues of thermal stratification. Unlike conventional designs, NuScale does not use power-operated relief valves (PORVs), which are susceptible to inadvertent opening; instead, it employs spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves that do not share this vulnerability. The design does not rely on pressurizer heaters to establish or maintain natural circulation during hot shutdown conditions, making the requirements for pressurizer heater power supply and associated control interfaces in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xiii) inapplicable—NuScale considers its hot shutdown condition equivalent to the regulatory hot standby condition. Furthermore, NuScale does not include pressurizer relief or block valves, rendering the power supply requirements for these components in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) not technically relevant. For safe shutdown (Mode 3) with reactor coolant temperatures below 200°F, NuScale equates this state to cold shutdown as defined in OM Code ISTA-2000, and its DHRS actuation valves meet ASME OM Code exercising and observation requirements. Overall, these design features support exemptions from certain TMI-related regulatory requirements.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) design features an integral power module comprising a reactor core, two helical-coil steam generators (SGs), and a pressurizer, all contained within a single reactor pressure vessel (RPV). This RPV is immediately surrounded by a steel containment vessel (CNV) that is approximately cylindrical and sits in the reactor pool. The reactor core is positioned below the helical-coil SGs inside the RPV. The design eliminates the need for external piping between the steam generators, pressurizer, and RPV, and utilizes natural circulation for reactor coolant system flow, removing the requirement for reactor coolant pumps. For valve actuation testing, a pilot-operated main valve with trip line tubing and a trip valve representative of the NPM design was used to simulate performance and assess potential boric acid buildup. Pressurizer level instrumentation is provided to the control room and the Module Control System (MCS), supporting an exemption from certain power supply requirements per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx). Pressurizer pressure is measured and provides input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS).

In addition, the NuScale design does not include pressurizer 
relief valves or pressurizer block valves, and the power supply requirements for these 
valves included in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) are not technically relevant.

Additionally, pressurizer level instrumentation is 
provided to the control room to the operating staff and the MCS described in 
Chapter 7.As described in Table 1.9-5, the NuScale design supports an exemption from 
the power supply requirements for pressurizer level indication included in 
10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx).Pressurizer pressure is measured and provides input to the safety-related MPS as 
described in Chapter 7.

The 
NuScale design supports an exemption from the 
portions of 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) related to pressurizer 
level indicators.

results.question: What material is used for the RPV shell, and what chemical composition limits apply to the beltline region according to Table 5.3-2?
results.expected_answer: The RPV shell is fabricated from SA-508 Grade 3 Class 1 low-alloy steel (Table 5.2-4). Table 5.3-2 limits beltline chemistry to: S <= 0.010%, P <= 0.010%, Cu <= 0.06%, Ni <= 0.85% to minimize radiation embrittlement.
results.generated_answer:
The RPV shell is made of SA-508 Grade 3, Class 1 or 2, and the chemical composition limits for the beltline forging material are: sulfur 0.010 wt%, phosphorus 0.010 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, and nickel 0.85 wt% (Table 5.3-2).  
Source: NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Table 5.2-4 and Table 5.3-2.
retrieved_contexts: Measures are established per Criterion XIII of 10 CFR 50, Appendix B to control on-site cleaning of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and pressure-retaining components associated with the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) during construction. The RPV ferritic pressure-retaining and integrally attached materials are tested and meet fracture toughness acceptance criteria per Appendix G to 10 CFR 50. RPV surfaces are designed for examination and conform to ASME BPVC Sections III and XI. Material specifications for the RPV and appurtenances are listed in Table 5.2-4, with materials selected and fabricated to maintain RCPB integrity for the plant design lifetime. The chemical composition of RPV beltline materials is shown in Table 5.3-2. The RPV is fabricated per ASME BPVC Section III, NB-4000, except for steam generator (SG) tube supports (ASME BPVC Section III, NG-4000), RPV supports, and control rod drive mechanism (CRDM) seismic support structure (ASME BPVC Section III, NF-4000). The RPV is designed against non-ductile fracture in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III and XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50, Appendix G. Pressure-temperature limits are developed per ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, and RG 1.99, Revision 2. Specimens are periodically removed and tested to monitor fracture toughness changes per ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50, Appendix H, using material from the actual production forging and weldments. The limiting base metal is expected to be the lower RPV shell forging, but unit-specific limiting base metal is determined by actual forging chemistry and initial RTNDT, and may be the lower RPV shell or bottom head forging. The predicted limiting weld metal is the lower RPV shell to bottom head circumferential weld, and the limiting heat affected zone is adjacent to the limiting base metal. Archive materials fill at least two capsules per ASTM E185-82. RPV design ensures compatibility with inspection requirements. Packaging, shipment, handling, and storage of the RPV comply with ASME BPVC Section III, 2013 edition, subsection NCA-4134.13, and meet Level C requirements per ASME NQA-1-2008/1a-2009 Addenda, subpart 2.2.

The material of the RPV 
upper shell and upper head is described in Section 5.2.The pressurizer spray nozzles 
and pressurizer spray nozzle safe ends are described in Section 4.5 and Section 5.2, 
respectively.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements, design parameters, material composition, and major components of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Table 5.2-6 specifies that RPV shell and head welds, including connections such as the lower RPV flange shell to RPV bottom head and steam plenum cap to integral steam plenum, are subject to B-A category volumetric examination, while internal welds like the core support block to RPV bottom head undergo B-N-2 category VT-3 visual testing; instrumentation and controls sleeve welds are part of the cladding and not separately examined. Table 5.3-1 lists key RPV parameters: design pressure is 2100 psia, design temperature is 650°F, overall height is 778 inches, and diameters range from 96.5 to 115.5 inches depending on section, with inner and outer clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches, respectively. Table 5.3-2 provides maximum allowable concentrations for RPV beltline forging material (e.g., sulfur 0.010 wt%, phosphorus 0.010 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%) and as-deposited weld metal (e.g., sulfur 0.015 wt%, phosphorus 0.012 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, manganese 1.80 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%). Figure 5.3-1 identifies major RPV components, including the lower and upper RPV sections, RPV head section, CRDM latch housing section with 16 latch housings, RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum with 4 access ports, upper and lower RPV SG shells, feed plenum access ports, lower RPV section flange shell, RPV bottom head, upper RPV flanged transition shell, CRDM seismic support structure, and 4 instrument seal assemblies.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report demonstrates that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) values well above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb throughout the 60-year design life, with initial values of at least 75 ft-lb and predicted end-of-life values of 58.4 ft-lb (lower shell), 62.3 ft-lb (weld), and 62.9 ft-lb (bottom head) at 57 effective full-power years (EFPY). The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT for the limiting weld metal is 50°F at 0.55 EFPY, with 28°F due to a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening per 10 CFR 50.61 and RG 1.99 shows RTPTS values at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head), all below regulatory limits. Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The RPV is fabricated and inspected per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, with material specifications and chemical compositions detailed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.3-2. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, and clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. Major components are detailed in Figure 5.3-1, and all packaging, handling, and storage meet ASME and NQA-1 requirements. The RCPB material selections, as specified in Table 5.2-4, include nickel-based Alloy 718 (UNS N07718) for threaded fasteners, SA-508 Grade 3, Class 1 and 2 for RPV sections, SA-533 Type B, Class 2 for support gussets and plates, and austenitic stainless steels (SA-479/SA-240 Type 304/304L, 316/316L) for core barrel guides, support blocks, and instrument assemblies.

A schematic flow diagram of the RCS is provided in Figure 5.1-3, denoting major 
components, and major RCS loop flow stages during normal steady-state, full-power

remove decay heat from the RCS during the initial phase of module cooldown.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed to promote natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS ensures coolant supply to the core to maintain specified acceptable fuel design limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences. A schematic flow diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and Table 5.1-1 detail major RCS components, flow stages, and volumes during steady-state, full-power operation. The SGS, with the main steam and feedwater systems, removes decay heat from the RCS during initial cooldown. Optimal performance criteria include maximizing electrical generation, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining margin to analytical limits. Key parameters—such as primary coolant temperatures, flow rates, pressurizer (PZR) water level, steam generator (SG) water level and mass, feedwater and steam flow rates, pressures, and steam superheat—are determined using NRELAP5 thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand calculations. Design calculations establish best-estimate, maximum, and minimum primary coolant flows, accounting for uncertainties in component flow resistances, core bypass flow, and thermal head capability, with bounding uncertainties based on test data and design requirements. Minimum and maximum design flow rates are calculated by biasing analytical uncertainties to minimize or maximize flow, respectively, and are used in bounding design analyses. The module heatup system provides RCS heating and coolant flow before criticality; between hot zero power and 15% reactor power, core heat is used to reach normal operating coolant temperature. From 15% to full power, average primary coolant temperature is held constant, with higher full-power coolant temperatures for higher flow rates, ensuring similar hot leg temperatures and sufficient SG superheat regardless of flow. Flow rate varies with module heatup system performance, SG operation, and RCS temperature, with typical hot zero power temperatures and steady-state best-estimate flow rates identified. The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RCS to the secondary steam system, supplying superheated steam to the power conversion cycle, with operating conditions summarized in Table 5.4-1 for full-power steam flow at best-estimate primary coolant conditions.

Design

Material Specifications 
The materials and applicable specifications used in the RPV and appurtenances are 
shown in Table 5.2-4.The RPV materials are selected and fabricated to maintain RCPB integrity for the plant 
design lifetime.

The RPV upper section is cylindrical, approximately 9 ft in diameter with slightly 
thicker sections at the feedwater inlet and steam outlet areas.The upper section 
includes penetrations ranging from 2.25” to 25” diameter for main steam piping 
nozzles and main steam access ports, pressurizer heaters, feedwater piping nozzles 
and feedwater access ports, reactor recirculation valves, CVCS, and pressure 
instrumentation.

Piping and Instrumentation Diagram
Figure 5.1-2 provides the RCS piping and instrument diagram and illustrates the design 
configuration of the RSVs and RVVs showing the number and location with respect to 
the RPV.

The RPV lower section is cylindrical, approximately 9 ft in diameter and includes a 
torispherical lower head that is welded in place.There are no penetrations in the 
lower section of the RPV.A steel pressurizer baffle plate integral with the RPV provides a barrier between the 
saturated water in the pressurizer and the RCS.

The Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) is an approximately cylindrical steel vessel with an inside diameter of about 9 ft and an overall height of 58 ft, designed for an operating pressure of 1,850 psia. It consists of three main sections: the head, upper, and lower sections. The torispherical RPV head is welded to the top of the upper section, while the upper and lower sections are flanged together with bolts. The upper section is cylindrical, with thicker areas at the feedwater inlet and steam outlet, and contains penetrations ranging from 2.25” to 25” in diameter for main steam piping nozzles and access ports, pressurizer heaters, feedwater piping and access ports, reactor recirculation valves, Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS), and pressure instrumentation. The lower section is also cylindrical with a welded torispherical lower head and contains no penetrations. An integral steel pressurizer baffle plate separates the saturated water in the pressurizer from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS). The RPV provides steam and feedwater plenums for the steam generators and RSV penetrations, and supports or attaches to the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs), CRDM seismic support structure, pressurizer heater bundles, in-core instrumentation, steam generator system piping, RCS piping, reactor safety valves (RSVs), reactor vent valves, and reactor recirculation valves. The RPV, including the steam generators, is certified and stamped per Article NCA-8000 of ASME BPVC, Section III. Pressure-temperature curves for the RPV comply with 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, ensuring operation below the pressure-temperature limits to prevent non-ductile failure. Two pressurizer heater bundles, each with a nominal capacity of 400 kW, are mounted through the RPV side, 180 degrees apart around the integral steam plenum assembly. The pressurizer baffle plate and its support structure are subject to BPVC and ANSI/ASME NQA-1 requirements, with inspections and testing conducted per BPVC standards. Material specifications for the RPV upper shell and head, as well as details on pressurizer spray nozzles and safe ends, are provided in referenced sections.

In addition, the RPV also provides steam and feedwater plenums 
for the steam generators and RSV penetrations.The RPV and appurtenances are further described in Section 5.3.

The RPV is fabricated in accordance with the requirements of ASME BPVC Section III, 
NB-4000, except for the SG tube supports (shown in Figure 5.4-6), which are fabricated 
in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, NG-4000, and the RPV supports and CRDM 
seismic support structure (shown in Figure 5.3-1), which are fabricated in accordance 
with ASME BPVC Section III, NF-4000.

Surfaces of the RPV are designed to be suitable for examinations and conform to the 
applicable requirements of ASME BPVC Sections III and XI.

Reactor Pressure Vessel
The RPV consists of an approximately cylindrical steel vessel with an inside 
diameter of approximately 9 ft and an overall height of approximately 58 ft that is 
designed for an operating pressure of approximately 1,850 psia.The upper and 
lower heads are torispherical, and the lower portion of the vessel has a flange to 
provide access for refueling.

The RPV consists of three sections: the RPV head section, the upper section, and the 
lower section.The RPV head is welded to the top of the upper section, and the 
upper and lower sections are flanged together using bolts.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements, design parameters, material composition, and major components of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Table 5.2-6 specifies that RPV shell and head welds, including connections such as the lower RPV flange shell to RPV bottom head and steam plenum cap to integral steam plenum, are subject to B-A category volumetric examination, while internal welds like the core support block to RPV bottom head undergo B-N-2 category VT-3 visual testing; instrumentation and controls sleeve welds are part of the cladding and not separately examined. Table 5.3-1 lists key RPV parameters: design pressure is 2100 psia, design temperature is 650°F, overall height is 778 inches, and diameters range from 96.5 to 115.5 inches depending on section, with inner and outer clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches, respectively. Table 5.3-2 provides maximum allowable concentrations for RPV beltline forging material (e.g., sulfur 0.010 wt%, phosphorus 0.010 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%) and as-deposited weld metal (e.g., sulfur 0.015 wt%, phosphorus 0.012 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, manganese 1.80 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%). Figure 5.3-1 identifies major RPV components, including the lower and upper RPV sections, RPV head section, CRDM latch housing section with 16 latch housings, RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum with 4 access ports, upper and lower RPV SG shells, feed plenum access ports, lower RPV section flange shell, RPV bottom head, upper RPV flanged transition shell, CRDM seismic support structure, and 4 instrument seal assemblies.

The RPV is designed against non-ductile fracture in accordance with ASME BPVC, 
Section III, Appendix G, ASME BPVC, Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50,

Section XI, Appendix G, 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, and RG 1.99, Revision 2.

5.2.2.4 
Equipment and Component Description
5.2.2.4.1 
Reactor Safety Valves
The RSVs are redundant, safety-related, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A 
components designed to maintain pressure below 110 percent of design pressure, 
2310 psia.Each valve is sized to provide 100 percent of the required relief capacity.

The Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) are integral components of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), bolted via flange to a nozzle safe end directly on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) head. Each RSV is designed to actuate on high Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure, allowing direct flow to containment, and is classified as Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB. The RSVs prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding 110% of design pressure under all conditions, with design information in Table 5.2-2 and materials in Table 6.1-3. Each pilot-operated RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, Subsubarticle NB-7520. Environmental qualification details are in Section 3.11. The Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) provide sufficient pressure relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure and are also environmentally qualified per Section 3.11. Both RSVs and RVVs are designed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-3500, meeting overpressure protection criteria in Article NB-7000, with applicable code editions and classifications detailed in Sections 5.2.1 and 3.2, and material specifications in Section 6.1. Functional qualification is performed per ASME QME-1 as endorsed by RG 1.100, with inservice testing and inspection ensuring continued reliability and conformance. RSVs are considered passive devices, and the reliability of both RSVs and RVVs is further discussed in Section 6.3.2.

The RSV design information is provided in Table 5.2-2, and materials of the RSV 
components are provided in Table 6.1-3.The RSV pressure boundary design life is 
for a service life of 60 years.Each RSV is a pilot operated relief valve designed in 
accordance with the requirements of ASME BPVC, Section III, Subsubarticle 
NB-7520.

RCPB, are bolted via flange to a nozzle safe end directly to the RPV head, and the 
setpoint of each valve is designed to actuate the associated RSV on RCS high 
pressure to allow flow directly to containment.The RSVs are designed as Seismic Category I components, Quality Group A,

Structural design and valve qualification information related to

temperature conditions and the RPV not vented, LTOP is provided by the RVVs to prevent 
exceeding the maximum allowable pressure.Three RVVs are connected to the RPV above 
the PZR volume and discharge steam and water directly into containment.

5.2.2.2.1 
Overpressure Protection During Power Operations
The main design function of the RSVs is to provide overpressure protection for the 
RPV in accordance with the requirements of the ASME BPVC, Section III, Article 
NB-7000 and 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 15.

5.1.3.5 
Reactor Safety Valves
Two pilot operated, self-actuating RSVs connect to the top of the RPV upper head and 
discharge directly to the CNV.These valves are a part of the RCPB and provide 
overpressure protection as required by the American Society of Mechanical Engineers 
(ASME) Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code.The RSVs are further described in Section 5.2.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details overpressure protection for the steam generator (SG) internal pressure, which is provided by the primary system Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs). During shutdown, thermal relief valves protect the secondary side of the SGs, feedwater and steam piping within containment, and the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) when the secondary system is water solid and containment is isolated. Three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs), classified as safety-related, Seismic Category I, and Quality Group A, are constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, and are designed to prevent brittle fracture stress limits from being exceeded on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) and associated Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) components at low temperatures. Direct position indication for each RSV and RVV is provided in the control room in accordance with 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi) and the Three Mile Island action plan Item II.D.3. Each reactor module has two RSVs, each with a 3-inch inlet and 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, and design temperature of 650°F. The minimum design capacity at 3% overpressure is 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam. The nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% for the first valve and 2100 psia ±1% for the second, with a blowdown from set pressure of 10% ±2%. The RSV design includes a main piston, main spring, main disk, main valve seat, and a position sensor for flow monitoring.

design lifetime.

Overpressure protection for the RPV is provided by two RSVs during power operations 
and the RVVs provide overpressure protection during operations at low temperature 
conditions.The RSVs also provide external overpressure protection for the SG tubing 
and plena.

results.question: Describe the natural circulation mechanism in the NuScale RCS and the role of the riser in establishing flow.
results.expected_answer: The NuScale RCS uses natural circulation without reactor coolant pumps. Heated coolant rises through the hot leg riser due to density differences between hot and cold fluid. The riser directs flow upward from the core to the steam generator region. Heat is transferred to the secondary side in the SG, cooling the primary fluid which then descends through the downcomer back to the core inlet.
results.generated_answer: In the NuScale RCS, natural circulation is driven by density differences between the hot coolant rising from the core through the lower and upper riser assemblies and the cooled, denser coolant descending after passing over the steam generator tubes; the riser provides vertical elevation to maximize this density-driven flow, enabling the coolant to circulate without pumps. (Source: provided context)
retrieved_contexts: The reactor core heats the reactor coolant, which then flows upward through the lower and upper riser assemblies. At the top of the upper riser, the coolant is redirected downward by the PZR baffle plate into the annular space between the upper riser assembly and the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) wall, where the steam generator (SG) helical tube bundles are located. The primary coolant flows over the outside of the SG tubes, transferring heat to the secondary side fluid inside the tubes, which produces superheated steam with a moisture content no greater than 0.10 percent by weight during full-power operation. As the coolant cools and becomes denser, it sinks into the annular downcomer between the lower riser assembly and the core barrel, then returns to the lower plenum and reenters the reactor core, completing the natural circulation loop. The motive force for this flow is natural convection, driven by density differences between the hot coolant leaving the core and the colder coolant leaving the SGs, as well as the elevation difference between the core (heat source) and SGs (heat sink). The RCS flow path is designed for very low pressure losses, so pressure at any location is primarily determined by static head, and SG tube plugging uncertainties do not affect the natural circulation flow rate. Small flow paths in the upper riser allow limited coolant bypass to maintain sufficient boron concentration during DHRS-driven riser uncovery conditions after non-LOCA transients. The entire RCS natural circulation loop is contained within the RPV, eliminating external piping and the risk of large break LOCA events, and the intertwined SG tube bundle configuration prevents asymmetric core cooling. As the RPV water level drops near the top of the riser, reduced flow area increases pressure losses and impedes natural circulation, resulting in a greater temperature difference between hot and cold legs.

The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) is designed for natural circulation of the primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps or external piping. The RCS serves as the core neutron moderator and includes reflector blocks to improve neutron economy. It features a degasification line to remove noncondensible gases from the pressurizer (PZR) volume during normal operation. The reactor vessel internals (RVI) support and align the core, control rod assemblies, drive shafts, surveillance capsules, and instrument guide tubes. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core region—89 ft³, steam generator (SG) region—621 ft³, and PZR region—578 ft³ (with the cylindrical portion at 487 ft³). Each integrated NuScale Power Module (NPM) contains a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) housing the reactor, RVI, PZR, two SGs, reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, RCS injection and discharge lines, pressurizer spray and high-point degasification vent lines, and a decay heat removal system (DHRS). The system is depicted in Figure 5.1-2, showing key components and flow paths.

near the top of the riser due to vapor build up in the top of the core and lower riser.The vapor eventually is discharged into the upper riser and condenses as it rises.During these potential surges, liquid water is pushed over the top of the riser and 
into the downcomer.

The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed for natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during both power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps and external piping, with all major components—including the core, pressurizer (PZR), two steam generators (SGs), reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, and decay heat removal system (DHRS)—integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). The core heats the coolant, which rises through the lower and upper riser assemblies, is redirected by the PZR baffle plate, and flows over the SG helical tube bundles, transferring heat to the secondary side and producing superheated steam with a moisture content ≤0.10% by weight at full power. The cooled, denser coolant descends through the annular downcomer and reenters the core, completing the natural circulation loop, with flow driven by density differences and elevation between the core and SGs. The RCS flow path is optimized for low pressure losses, so pressure is primarily determined by static head, and SG tube plugging does not affect natural circulation flow rate. The system includes small bypass flow paths in the upper riser to maintain boron concentration during DHRS-driven riser uncovery after non-LOCA transients. The pressurizer, integral to the RPV above the coolant and separated by a baffle plate, operates at 2100 psia and 650°F, with two 1-inch spray nozzles and heater bundles (each 400 kW, total ≥785 kW with 15% margin, 480 Vac, 60 Hz, 800°F sheath temperature) to maintain RCS pressure. Instrumentation provides control room indication of RCS pressure, pressurizer level, spray flow, and heater output, with the Monitoring and Control System (MCS) automating RCS level and pressure control. The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) maintains pressurizer level (normally 50–60%), adjusts RCS chemistry, and controls boron concentration, supplying continuous spray flow to minimize thermal stresses and maintain chemistry. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core—89 ft³, SG region—621 ft³, and PZR—578 ft³ (487 ft³ cylindrical). During off-normal transients, initial pool temperatures of 100°F or 140°F have minimal effect on RCS cooldown after 36 hours, even with reduced heat removal from fouling, tube plugging, or noncondensible gas accumulation. The system’s design, including intertwined SG tube bundles and elimination of external piping, prevents asymmetric core cooling and large break LOCA events. The NuScale reactor facility features site-specific cooling tower location, orientation, equipment design, and operation, with the reactor core housed in a core support assembly seated in the lower reactor pressure vessel (RPV) assembly and connected to a central hot leg riser. Each Reactor Coolant System (RCS) line enters containment through welded penetrations on the containment upper head, with two containment isolation valves mounted externally, and a single pressurizer spray supply line also entering through the containment head. The pressurizer is integral to the reactor vessel, comprising the upper region above the naturally circulating coolant, separated by a baffle plate. The pressurizer includes the RPV upper shell and head, baffle plate, heater bundles, and two spray nozzles (each 1 inch in diameter). Instrumentation measures the steam-water interface level, providing input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS), including low water level protection for the heaters. The pressurizer heaters maintain RCS pressure during normal operation, supplying at least 785 kW (with a 15% margin), divided into three control groups per bundle, with two heater bundles per pressurizer, each bundle rated at 400 kW. Heater parameters include 480 Vac voltage and 60 Hz frequency, with heater sheath temperature at 800°F. The pressurizer operates at an internal pressure of 2100 psia, external pressure of 1000 psia, and temperature of 650°F. Pressurizer level is maintained within its operating band by operator action to add or reduce coolant inventory via the LRWS.

results.question: Describe the DHRS function and its passive cooling mechanism.
results.expected_answer: The Decay Heat Removal System provides passive cooling following a reactor trip. Each NPM has two independent DHRS trains connected to the SG secondary side. DHRS condensers are submerged in the reactor pool, transferring decay heat from the RCS through the SG tubes to the pool water by natural circulation on both primary and secondary sides.
results.generated_answer: The DHRS is a passive, closed-loop safety system that removes decay heat from the reactor coolant system (RCS) by circulating water through the steam generators (SGs), where it absorbs heat, converts to steam, and then condenses in the DHRS condenser, transferring heat to the reactor pool and promoting natural circulation without active components. (Source: provided context)

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is a passive, closed-loop safety system designed to remove decay heat from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) using the steam generators (SGs) and natural circulation. The DHRS operates by circulating water from its condenser through the SG tubes, where it absorbs decay heat, converts to steam, and returns to the condenser to release heat to the reactor pool water. The system is connected to the steam and feedwater piping between the main steam isolation valves (MSIVs) and feedwater isolation valves (FWIVs), forming a closed loop outside containment and providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and reactor pool. Each DHRS train is associated with one of the two NPM SGs, with actuation valves and orifices arranged to promote natural circulation and moderate flow. The DHRS is designed to maintain the RCS average temperature below 420°F within 36 hours of an initiating event, as recommended by EPRI and accepted by the NRC, without challenging the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) or uncovering the core, and without requiring Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) actuation. Performance analyses, including steady-state and transient models using NRELAP5, confirm the DHRS can remove heat across a wide range of loop inventories, accommodate SG tube plugging, fouling, noncondensible gas accumulation, and MSIV failure, and is insensitive to reactor pool temperature variations. With two DHRS trains, RCS temperature drops below 420°F in less than 2 hours and stabilizes below 300°F within 36 hours; with one train and limiting conditions, RCS temperature remains below 420°F within 36 hours. Figures and tables referenced (e.g., Figures 1.2-8, 5.4-10 to 5.4-16; Tables 5.4-5, 5.4-6, 5.4-8) provide detailed design data, failure modes analysis, and temperature cooldown profiles for various DHRS operational scenarios, demonstrating robust decay heat removal capability under both nominal and off-normal conditions. System performance is monitored by RCS temperature and pressure sensors, and initial testing will verify DHRS operation against design basis requirements.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) thermal-hydraulic performance analysis assessed the orifice loss coefficient under various initial conditions to confirm that the restriction orifice size ensures consistent heat transfer. Accumulation of noncondensible gas in the DHRS steam lines was identified as a factor that degrades system performance. As a two-phase natural circulation system, DHRS performance depends on factors such as Reactor Coolant System (RCS) temperature, with higher RCS temperatures increasing the driving temperature difference and thus DHRS heat transfer. The analysis, performed using NRELAP5, included three model categories: a Steady State Model (with constant primary side temperature, flow rate, reactor pool temperature, and DHRS inventory), Nominal Transient Models (to demonstrate DHRS cooling for typical initiating events), and Off-Normal Transient Models (to show adequate cooling under the most limiting conditions). The steady state model calculated the DHRS heat transfer rate and evaluated the loss coefficient in the DHRS steam line to ensure acceptable performance across a range of DHRS inventories, including scenarios with additional steam volume due to MSIV failure, DHRS and SG fouling, SG tube plugging, and the presence of noncondensible gas. The limiting mass of dissolved gas that could be released during DHRS operation was also considered. Nominal transient models analyzed DHRS cooldown capability for cases with and without containment isolation, affecting the timing of pressurizer heater isolation. Off-normal transient models assessed DHRS performance under limiting conditions, including heat transfer surface fouling, plugged SG tubes, reactor pool water temperature, and noncondensible gas presence, with conservative biasing of valve timings to simulate worst-case DHRS inventory conditions. The overall analysis confirmed the DHRS capability to remove heat over a range of loop inventories.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is a passive, closed-loop system designed to remove decay heat from the reactor coolant system (RCS) by utilizing the steam generators (SGs). Water from the DHRS condenser circulates through the SG tubes, where it absorbs decay heat and is converted to steam. This steam flows back to the DHRS condenser, where it releases heat to the reactor pool water and condenses, promoting natural circulation in both the RCS and DHRS. The DHRS is connected to the steam and feedwater piping between the main steam isolation valves (MSIVs) and feedwater isolation valves (FWIVs), forming a closed-loop outside the containment and providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and the reactor pool. Each DHRS train is associated with one of the two NPM SGs, with piping connecting to the main steam and feedwater lines upstream of the MSIV and downstream of the main feedwater isolation valve (MFIV). Two DHRS actuation valves, arranged in parallel per train, isolate the system during normal operation. An orifice moderates flow between the actuation valves and the passive condensers, which are located at a higher elevation than the SGs to promote natural circulation. The DHRS remains filled and pressurized via the feedwater system and is in standby during normal operation, with actuation dependent on closure of the MSIVs and FWIVs. The system differs from an auxiliary feedwater system in design, operation, and response to small break LOCA events. The SGs are designed for reliability, minimizing tube corrosion, vibration, and wear. System performance is optimized across power levels by establishing steady-state parameters as a function of reactor power. RCS temperature and pressure sensors confirm DHRS operation, indicated by decreasing values after reactor trip and DHRS actuation.

results.question: Explain how the NuScale containment vessel design differs from conventional containments and its role in safety.
results.expected_answer: The NuScale CNV operates under vacuum conditions during normal operation, unlike conventional pressurized containments. This vacuum reduces heat transfer from RPV to containment and provides a pressure suppression function. During a LOCA, steam released into the CNV condenses on the cool inner wall, maintaining low containment pressure without active spray systems.
results.generated_answer: The NuScale containment vessel is a compact, high-strength steel structure immersed in a water pool, operating at sub-atmospheric pressure, with no subcompartments or pressure suppression systems, and relies on passive safety features rather than active systems like pumps or sprays; its design inherently limits radiological release and ensures core cooling and isolation without AC/DC power, differing from conventional large LWR containments that use larger, compartmentalized structures with active safety systems. (Source: Provided context)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale design incorporates several advanced safety and operational features, including safe shutdown and cooling without reliance on AC or DC power, compact helical coil steam generators with reactor pressure on the tube exteriors, and a high-strength steel containment vessel immersed in a water pool. The containment operates at sub-atmospheric pressure during normal conditions, and the reactor core is small, resulting in a reduced source term. Comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls (I&C) are used for monitoring and control. The plant design is scalable, supporting incremental capacity growth in multi-unit configurations. The passive NuScale system eliminates pumps, sumps, suction strainers, debris interceptors, and trash racks, thereby minimizing debris-related and non-condensable effects on core coolant flow, and complies with regulatory guidance on debris management and chemical effects. Due to the modular and compact nature of the NuScale Power Module, passive separation of redundant safety trains is not always feasible; instead, fire protection measures are implemented to ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational in the event of a fire. Unlike traditional large water reactors, the NuScale design integrates the pressurizer above the reactor core within the reactor vessel, with no connecting piping, ensuring that core uncovery and damage cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint.

The NuScale plant design departs significantly from traditional large light water reactors (LWRs) and is not addressed by legacy regulatory guidance or codes such as PWRGALE. Its safety strategy relies on passive features and natural circulation, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps, safety-related pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, safety injection tanks, and safety-related motor-operated valves. Consequently, regulatory requirements and guidance related to these components—including flywheel integrity, motor qualification, thermal overload protection, ECCS accumulator lines, and gas accumulation in forced circulation systems—are not applicable. The design also omits an auxiliary feedwater (AFW) system, power-operated pressurizer relief valves, and pressurizer heaters, instead using a Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves, which are not susceptible to inadvertent opening. The integral pressurizer is located within the reactor vessel, with no surge line or connecting piping, eliminating thermal stratification and ensuring core uncovery cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint. The containment vessel is a high-strength steel structure immersed in a water pool, operating at sub-atmospheric pressure, and the compact core results in a reduced source term. The plant is scalable, supports multi-unit configurations, and uses comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls. Debris management and chemical effects are minimized by the elimination of pumps, sumps, strainers, and trash racks. Fire protection measures ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational, compensating for the inability to passively separate redundant safety trains due to the modular design. Regulatory requirements from 10 CFR 50.34(f) and NEI 06-12, as well as ATWS provisions, are largely inapplicable, and NuScale is exempt from related evaluations and analyses. For coolant source term evaluation, NuScale uses an alternate methodology incorporating aspects of ANSI/ANS 18.1. The only pumps within safety scope are ASME Class III CVCS pumps, which do not serve a safety function. The design meets DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials. For spent fuel pool mitigation, NuScale is exempt from certain requirements due to its below-grade, undrainable SFP. The design considers postulated startup reactivity accidents and addresses loading transients and structural evaluations for postulated breaks in CVCS and reactor vent valve piping. Containment vessel evacuation system valve closure times are addressed under SRP Section 6.2.4. Overall, NuScale’s unique passive and modular features support exemptions from many TMI-related and traditional LWR regulatory requirements.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) documents conformance with various regulatory criteria, noting that guidance for containment purge systems venting directly to the environment is not applicable, as the NuScale containment does not require or incorporate such systems due to its small size and lack of sub-compartments. The spent fuel pool (SFP) mitigating measure is also not required, as the NuScale SFP is below grade and cannot be drained. Radiation and shielding design reviews are performed for spaces that may contain accident source term radioactive materials, with adequate access provided, and the only vital areas per NUREG-0737 are those for combustible gas monitoring, the main control room, and the technical support center. The containment isolation system conforms to 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xiv)(A)-(D), ensuring automatic isolation of non-essential systems, two isolation barriers for non-essential penetrations, no reopening of isolation valves on signal reset, and a containment set point pressure compatible with normal operation; however, NuScale requests an exemption from (E), which requires automatic closure on high radiation signals for systems venting to the environment. The requirement for redundant dedicated containment penetrations for external hydrogen recombiners is not applicable, as the NuScale design does not use external hydrogen recombiners.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) features an integral containment system that provides primary containment for the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), as detailed in Section 6.2. The containment design conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, Rev 0 requirements, including design margin for containment pressure, heat removal capability, protection from external pressure, monitoring instrumentation, internal structures, and evaluation of accidents involving generated hydrogen or other modules. For the limiting event, the peak containment pressure remains below the design pressure. The NuScale design does not utilize an ice condenser containment, as specified in SRP 6.2.1.1.B, Draft Rev 3. Additionally, preliminary design information requirements under 10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(v) are not applicable to the design certification application per 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8) and 10 CFR 50.34(f). Further details on containment systems are provided in Section 6.2.

results.question: What are the ECCS valve types and their actuation mechanism in the NuScale design?
results.expected_answer: The ECCS consists of reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs). RVVs connect the RPV to the CNV above the waterline, and RRVs connect below. Both valve types are actuated by an inadvertent actuation block (IAB) that prevents spurious opening at normal operating pressure, and they fail open on loss of power.
results.generated_answer:
The NuScale ECCS uses three reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), all pilot operated by remote-mounted trip and reset solenoid valves, with each main valve including an inadvertent actuation block (IAB) feature.  
(Source: Context, Paragraph 1)
retrieved_contexts: The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) utilizes three reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), with the RVVs being larger than the RRVs. Both valve types are pilot operated by remote-mounted trip and reset solenoid valves at the reactor containment boundary, and each main valve includes an inadvertent actuation block (IAB) feature located inside containment. The three RVVs are mounted on the top of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), connecting to the upper head and discharging the pressurizer (PZR) steam space directly to the containment vessel (CNV), thereby venting noncondensible gases during ECCS actuation. The two RRVs connect to the RPV shell just above the main closure flange. The ECCS design, including protection from inadvertent actuation and single failure capability, is detailed in Section 6.3, while the design basis for the RSVs and ECCS valves is provided in Sections 5.2.2 and 6.3. The NuScale design does not require separate safety-related high-point venting in the reactor coolant system (RCS), as the RVVs fulfill this function during and after a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA), ensuring noncondensible gases do not accumulate in the pressurizer space and core cooling is maintained. Mechanical failure of the reactor safety valve (RSV) is bounded by the analysis of inadvertent ECCS valve actuation in Section 15.6.6.

The NuScale ECCS (Emergency Core Cooling System) design differs from traditional systems by not utilizing high-pressure or low-pressure safety injection pumps, a containment spray system, or a sump. Instead, core decay heat removal is achieved through steam condensation and natural reactor coolant recirculation. During ECCS operation, water accumulates inside the NuScale containment vessel and is recirculated back to the reactor core, fulfilling the intent of relevant acceptance criteria. The design does not require personnel access during reactor operation, does not include pumps, and does not rely on containment pressure for ECCS recirculation as in typical PWRs. Consequently, purge or vent capability as specified by 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xv) is not required or included. Pre-operational testing of the ECCS will be conducted to satisfy regulatory guidance.
