The NuScale design employs the chemical and volume control system as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to meet GDC 26 requirements. For containment pressure boundary fracture prevention (SRP 6.2.7, Rev 1) and most Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) criteria (DSRS 6.3, Rev 0), including ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46, single-failure consideration, design of non-safety-related ECCS portions, ECCS interfaces and shared systems, long-term cooling, ECCS outage times and unavailability reporting, and programmatic requirements, the design conforms to regulatory guidance. However, departures are noted for inservice inspection and operability testing, and for combined reactivity control system capability and actuation provisions; specifically, the NuScale ECCS does not perform poison addition or provide a makeup function as required by GDC 27, though actuation signal guidance is applied. Control room habitability system requirements (SRP 6.4, Rev 3) for the emergency zone are met. For inadvertent ECCS operation (DSRS 15.6.6, Rev 0), the reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure remains below 110 percent of design value, and minimum DNBR is maintained. The design provides high point venting capability for noncondensible gases from the RCS and other systems to ensure adequate core cooling (50.34(f)(2)(vi)), and instruments in the control room give unambiguous indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(xviii), with all relevant requirements conformed to unless otherwise noted.

The provided passages detail the inapplicability of several regulatory guidance documents and technical requirements to the NuScale reactor design due to its reliance on passive safety features and natural circulation. Specifically, NuScale does not use reactor coolant pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, or safety-related motor-operated valves, making guidance on flywheel integrity, motor qualification, and thermal overload protection irrelevant. The design also does not employ safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, or safety injection tanks, so requirements for ECCS accumulator lines and related motor-operated valves do not apply. Additionally, NuScale does not use removal of electric power from safety-related valves to meet the single failure criterion, and guidance on gas accumulation in systems dependent on forced circulation pumps is not relevant. However, guidance remains applicable for evaluating loading transients and structural components for postulated breaks in CVCS piping and reactor vent valve piping. Containment vessel evacuation system valve closure times are addressed separately under SRP Section 6.2.4.

The NuScale design incorporates several advanced safety and operational features, including safe shutdown and cooling without reliance on AC or DC power, compact helical coil steam generators with reactor pressure on the tube exteriors, and a high-strength steel containment vessel immersed in a water pool. The containment operates at sub-atmospheric pressure during normal conditions, and the reactor core is small, resulting in a reduced source term. Comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls (I&C) are used for monitoring and control. The plant design is scalable, supporting incremental capacity growth in multi-unit configurations. The passive NuScale system eliminates pumps, sumps, suction strainers, debris interceptors, and trash racks, thereby minimizing debris-related and non-condensable effects on core coolant flow, and complies with regulatory guidance on debris management and chemical effects. Due to the modular and compact nature of the NuScale Power Module, passive separation of redundant safety trains is not always feasible; instead, fire protection measures are implemented to ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational in the event of a fire. Unlike traditional large water reactors, the NuScale design integrates the pressurizer above the reactor core within the reactor vessel, with no connecting piping, ensuring that core uncovery and damage cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint.

results.question: What is the pressurizer design in NuScale, and how does it control RCS pressure?
results.expected_answer: The NuScale pressurizer is an integral part of the upper RPV head, not a separate vessel. It uses electric heaters to increase pressure and a spray system to reduce pressure. The pressurizer volume accommodates RCS inventory changes during transients while maintaining pressure within the operating band.
results.generated_answer: The NuScale pressurizer is integral to the reactor pressure vessel, located above the baffle plate, and uses two 400 kW heater bundles to increase RCS pressure and two 1-inch spray nozzles to decrease pressure, maintaining a saturated water-steam interface and stable RCS pressure during operation. (Source: provided context)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale reactor facility features site-specific cooling tower location, orientation, equipment design, and operation, with the reactor core housed in a core support assembly seated in the lower reactor pressure vessel (RPV) assembly and connected to a central hot leg riser. Each Reactor Coolant System (RCS) line enters containment through welded penetrations on the containment upper head, with two containment isolation valves mounted externally, and a single pressurizer spray supply line also entering through the containment head. The pressurizer is integral to the reactor vessel, comprising the upper region above the naturally circulating coolant, separated by a baffle plate. The pressurizer includes the RPV upper shell and head, baffle plate, heater bundles, and two spray nozzles (each 1 inch in diameter). Instrumentation measures the steam-water interface level, providing input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS), including low water level protection for the heaters. The pressurizer heaters maintain RCS pressure during normal operation, supplying at least 785 kW (with a 15% margin), divided into three control groups per bundle, with two heater bundles per pressurizer, each bundle rated at 400 kW. Heater parameters include 480 Vac voltage and 60 Hz frequency, with heater sheath temperature at 800°F. The pressurizer operates at an internal pressure of 2100 psia, external pressure of 1000 psia, and temperature of 650°F. Pressurizer level is maintained within its operating band by operator action to add or reduce coolant inventory via the LRWS.

The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) is designed for natural circulation of the primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps or external piping. The RCS serves as the core neutron moderator and includes reflector blocks to improve neutron economy. It features a degasification line to remove noncondensible gases from the pressurizer (PZR) volume during normal operation. The reactor vessel internals (RVI) support and align the core, control rod assemblies, drive shafts, surveillance capsules, and instrument guide tubes. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core region—89 ft³, steam generator (SG) region—621 ft³, and PZR region—578 ft³ (with the cylindrical portion at 487 ft³). Each integrated NuScale Power Module (NPM) contains a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) housing the reactor, RVI, PZR, two SGs, reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, RCS injection and discharge lines, pressurizer spray and high-point degasification vent lines, and a decay heat removal system (DHRS). The system is depicted in Figure 5.1-2, showing key components and flow paths.

The pressurizer (PZR) is an integral part of the NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS), located above the baffle plate within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and containing heater bundles, spray nozzles, and instrument lines. Its main function is to maintain RCS pressure within operational limits during normal and transient conditions, using electrical heaters (two bundles, each 400 kW, total ≥785 kW with 15% margin, 480 Vac, 60 Hz, 800°F sheath temperature) to generate steam and increase pressure, and two 1-inch spray nozzles—supplied by the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS)—to condense steam and reduce pressure. The PZR water is heated to a temperature higher than the core outlet, ensuring a saturated water-steam interface, with minimal spray flow maintained to reduce thermal stresses and maintain chemistry equilibrium. The PZR is sized at approximately 23% of the RCS volume (578 ft³ total, 487 ft³ cylindrical) to accommodate expected RCS volume changes and prevent reactor trips or safety system actuation during transients. The nominal water level is controlled between 50–60% at full power, with analytical limits of 80% (upper) and 35% (lower), and is adjusted by the CVCS to absorb volume changes, especially between hot zero power and 15% power. Instrumentation monitors pressure and level, providing data to the Module Protection System (MPS), Module Control System (MCS), and control room, with the MCS automating RCS level and pressure control. During shutdown and refueling, decay heat is removed via the steam generator system (SGS) and decay heat removal system (DHRS), with the PZR not relied upon for safe shutdown. Overpressure protection is provided by reactor safety valves (RSVs) and relief valves, with the PZR, CVCS, and automatic isolation features preventing RCS solid conditions and protecting the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). During low-temperature operations, nitrogen is used for initial pressurization and bubble replacement, and the PZR is vented to match containment pressure before opening vent and relief valves. The MPS disconnects heater power if water level drops below the heater elements, using Class 1E circuit breakers for safety isolation. Heaters are grouped for steady-state control (Group 1) and backup during pressure drops (Groups 2 and 3). The PZR design ensures sufficient surge and steam volume to prevent liquid or two-phase flow to the RSVs during transients, with effective fluid mixing achieved through thermal effects and flow over SG tube bundles. All PZR regions are constructed for inspection and tested as part of RPV testing, with compliance to BPVC and seismic qualification. The RCS is designed for natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW thermal from the core to the SGS, with all major components—including the core, P

The pressurizer is a key component designed to maintain stable reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure during operation. Pressure is increased by applying power to two heater bundles located above the pressurizer baffle plate, which heat the water to a temperature higher than the coolant leaving the reactor core, ensuring a saturated water-steam interface. Pressure is decreased by activating pressurizer spray flow, with two spray nozzles on opposite sides providing adequate steam contact; a minimal spray flow is continuously maintained during normal operation to reduce thermal stresses on spray line components. Instrumentation monitors both pressurizer pressure and the steam-water interface level, providing data to the MPS, MCS, and control room. The pressurizer is designed so that operating transients do not cause a reactor trip or actuate other safety systems when normal support systems are functional. The nominal water level is set to accommodate expected RCS volume changes, and makeup or letdown is not required to maintain level above 15 percent power. Control rod drive shafts and instrument lines are located in the central region of the pressurizer.

The NuScale reactor design features an integral pressurizer located within the reactor pressure vessel, eliminating the need for a pressurizer surge line and thus avoiding issues of thermal stratification. Unlike conventional designs, NuScale does not use power-operated relief valves (PORVs), which are susceptible to inadvertent opening; instead, it employs spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves that do not share this vulnerability. The design does not rely on pressurizer heaters to establish or maintain natural circulation during hot shutdown conditions, making the requirements for pressurizer heater power supply and associated control interfaces in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xiii) inapplicable—NuScale considers its hot shutdown condition equivalent to the regulatory hot standby condition. Furthermore, NuScale does not include pressurizer relief or block valves, rendering the power supply requirements for these components in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) not technically relevant. For safe shutdown (Mode 3) with reactor coolant temperatures below 200°F, NuScale equates this state to cold shutdown as defined in OM Code ISTA-2000, and its DHRS actuation valves meet ASME OM Code exercising and observation requirements. Overall, these design features support exemptions from certain TMI-related regulatory requirements.

results.question: What is the chemical and volume control system (CVCS) role in the NuScale RCS?
results.expected_answer: The CVCS maintains RCS water chemistry by controlling boron concentration, removing corrosion products and fission products via ion exchangers and filters, and managing RCS inventory. It provides makeup water and letdown flow, and adjusts boron concentration for reactivity control during the fuel cycle.
results.generated_answer:
The CVCS in the NuScale RCS maintains coolant cleanliness and chemistry, controls reactor pressure via pressurizer spray, enables chemical addition and removal, and its only safety-related function is to prevent inadvertent boron dilution of the reactor coolant system.  
(Source: Provided context, especially paragraphs 1, 3, and 5)
retrieved_contexts: The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) is a simple system whose operation is not credited during or after an accident. During normal operation, the CVCS recirculates a portion of reactor coolant through demineralizers and filters to maintain coolant cleanliness and chemistry, and supplies pressurizer spray for reactor pressure control. Injection pumps deliver borated or demineralized water into the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), with excess coolant let down to the radioactive waste system. Safety-related protection is provided against unintended RCS dilution due to CVCS equipment failure or operator error. The CVCS is normally connected to the RCS but is isolated following containment isolation actuation. During RCS pressure transients not resulting in isolation, overpressure protection is provided by the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) integral pressurizer (PZR), CVCS design, and relief valves. The CVCS enables chemical addition and removal of chemicals, suspended solids, and impurities via purification systems, and reduces concentrations through dilution with injection flow. It connects to the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) via normally open containment isolation valves (CIVs), with intersystem leakage considered for the boron addition, demineralized water, reactor component cooling water (RCCWS), and process sampling systems. The interface with the RCS is through the steam generators (SGs), which are subject to design, inspection, and testing controls, and primary-to-secondary leakage is monitored per technical specifications. Noncondensible gases collected in degasifiers are processed in the gaseous radioactive waste system. Further design details are provided in Sections 9.3.4, 11.2, and 11.3.

The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) for the PWR, including the Boron Recovery System, partially conforms to DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0 requirements regarding functional performance during adverse environmental phenomena, pumping capacity, and defense-in-depth RCS makeup, as its only safety-related function is to preclude inadvertent boron dilution of the reactor coolant system. The system conforms to single failure criteria and GDC 5, which apply solely to the two safety-related demineralized water isolation valves designed to prevent inadvertent boron dilution. The CVCS also conforms to requirements for minimization of contamination, system design and arrangement, detection of reactor coolant leakage outside containment, and ITAAC, with no additional comments. However, it only partially conforms to criteria for prevention of CVCS holdup tank wall buckling/failure and CVCS venting and draining, as some acceptance criteria reference CVCS designs not applicable to the NuScale design.

The NuScale reactor design utilizes the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to satisfy GDC 26, with its sole safety-related function being the prevention of inadvertent boron dilution in the reactor coolant system (RCS). The CVCS, including the Boron Recovery System, partially meets DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0 requirements, fulfilling criteria for functional performance, pumping capacity, defense-in-depth RCS makeup, single failure, GDC 5, contamination minimization, system design, leakage detection, and ITAAC, but only partially addresses holdup tank wall buckling/failure and venting/draining due to design differences. The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) departs from conventional PWRs by omitting high- and low-pressure safety injection pumps, containment spray, and a sump, instead relying on steam condensation and natural recirculation of water within the containment vessel for core cooling, meeting ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46. The ECCS does not provide poison addition or makeup as required by GDC 27, and has departures in inservice inspection, operability testing, and combined reactivity control system capability, though actuation signal guidance is applied. The design ensures RCS pressure remains below 110% of design during inadvertent ECCS operation, maintains minimum DNBR, provides high-point venting for noncondensible gases, and includes control room instrumentation for clear indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(vi) and (xviii). The ECCS requires no personnel access, pumps, or containment pressure for recirculation, and lacks refueling water storage tanks; condensate storage is outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building with no safety risk from failure. Instrumentation and Controls review per DSRS 7.0 applies but lacks specific acceptance criteria. Gas accumulation is not expected to impact ECCS performance, and the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) interfaces only with the secondary system. Control room habitability meets SRP 6.4, Rev 3 for the emergency zone, and pre-operational ECCS testing will be performed to meet regulatory guidance.

The RCS, a subsystem of the Nuclear Power Module (NPM) within the containment vessel (CNV), consists of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) with integral pressurizer (PZR), reactor vessel internals (RVI), reactor safety valves (RSVs), RCS piping (injection, discharge, PZR spray supply, high-point degasification), PZR control cabinet, and associated instruments and cables. It transfers heat from the core to two once-through, helical-coil steam generators (SGs) by natural circulation, with SGs located in the annular space between the hot leg riser and RPV inner wall. containment system, the emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves consisting of RVVs 
and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), the CNV, control rod drive system, SGS, and the 
primary sampling system (through the chemical and volume control system).

RCS injection line and removal of chemicals, suspended solids, and impurities by

The NuScale reactor design employs the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to meet GDC 26, with its only safety-related function being the prevention of inadvertent boron dilution of the reactor coolant system. The CVCS, including the Boron Recovery System, partially conforms to DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0, meeting requirements for functional performance, pumping capacity, defense-in-depth RCS makeup, single failure criteria, GDC 5, minimization of contamination, system design, leakage detection, and ITAAC, but only partially meets criteria for holdup tank wall buckling/failure and venting/draining due to design differences. The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design diverges from traditional PWRs by omitting high- and low-pressure safety injection pumps, a containment spray system, and a sump; instead, it relies on steam condensation and natural recirculation of water that accumulates inside the containment vessel for core cooling, fulfilling ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46 and related guidance. The ECCS does not perform poison addition or provide a makeup function as required by GDC 27, and departures are noted for inservice inspection, operability testing, and combined reactivity control system capability and actuation provisions, though actuation signal guidance is applied. The design ensures RCS pressure remains below 110% of design value during inadvertent ECCS operation, maintains minimum DNBR, provides high point venting for noncondensible gases, and includes control room instrumentation for unambiguous indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(vi) and (xviii). The ECCS does not require personnel access, pumps, or containment pressure for recirculation, and does not include refueling water storage tanks; condensate storage components are outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building, with no safety risk from their failure. The Instrumentation and Controls review per DSRS 7.0 applies to the DCA but lacks specific acceptance criteria. Gas accumulation is not expected to affect ECCS performance, and the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) interfaces only with the secondary system, not the RCS. Control room habitability system requirements (SRP 6.4, Rev 3) are met for the emergency zone, and pre-operational ECCS testing will be conducted to satisfy regulatory guidance.

results.question: How does the NuScale LTOP system protect the RCS during low-temperature conditions?
results.expected_answer: Low Temperature Overpressure Protection prevents RCS pressure from exceeding limits when the reactor is at low temperatures and vessel ductility is reduced. The LTOP system uses relief valves with temperature-dependent setpoints. Multiple setpoint ranges are defined based on RCS temperature to ensure the pressure-temperature limits are not exceeded during heatup and cooldown.
results.generated_answer:
The NuScale LTOP system protects the RCS during low-temperature conditions by using three reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two pilot-operated reactor safety valves (RSVs) on the reactor pressure vessel head to relieve pressure directly into containment, with temperature-dependent pressure setpoints (ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F) determined by the downcomer temperature, ensuring RCS pressure remains below the maximum allowable value during worst-case transients.  
(Source: Context paragraphs 2, 3, 4, and 5)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale reactor design features an LTOP (Low Temperature Overpressure Protection) system that is not connected to a low-pressure system, but the guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation from the primary system remains applicable. NuScale supports an exemption from selected portions of 10 CFR 50, Appendix K, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, regarding Mass and Energy Release Analysis for Postulated Loss-of-Coolant Accidents (LOCAs), with no additional inspections, tests, analyses, or acceptance criteria (ITAAC) required. In the event of a LOCA, the NuScale design ensures that core uncovery does not occur, and it conforms to DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, and GDC 5 regarding the sharing of structures, systems, and components, with no exceptions. The design does not credit or utilize a suppression pool and conforms to DSRS 6.6, Rev 0, for inservice inspection and testing of Class 2 and 3 components, with all relevant components subject to inspection and no additional requirements.

The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system in the NuScale Power Module (NPM) safeguards the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) during low temperature conditions by using three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) and two pilot-operated Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) mounted on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) upper head, all constructed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A standards. The RSVs, each with a 3-inch inlet, 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, and design temperature of 650°F, are sized to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the 2310 psia design pressure, with a minimum relief capacity of 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam at 3% overpressure. Nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% and 2100 psia ±1%, with a blowdown of 10% ±2%. LTOP is enabled when RCS cold temperature is ≤318°F, with pressure setpoints ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, determined by linear interpolation based on downcomer temperature at the steam generator outlet. The system is actuated by module protection system logic to open RVVs and limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, accounting for worst-case transients such as spurious pressurizer heater actuation (up to 880 kW) and 2 MW core decay heat. The setpoints include margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences, temperature variations, RVV opening delay, sensor response, and processing time. Three RVVs ensure LTOP protection even with a single active component failure, and overpressure protection for secondary side components is integrated to maintain ASME BPVC service limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs), with the most severe AOO being a turbine trip at full power without bypass. RVVs and RSVs are environmentally and functionally qualified per ASME QME-1 and RG 1.100, with direct position indication in the control room per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi). Pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, surveillance capsule locations, and test schedules are detailed in referenced tables and figures, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, 10 CFR 50.61, and GDC 15 for a 60-year plant life. Additional details on valve qualification, design, operation, and testing are provided in Sections 6.3, 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report describes integrated overpressure protection for secondary side components with the same design pressure as the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), ensuring ASME BPVC service limits are not exceeded during normal operations and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs). The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system is designed with sufficient capacity to prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding the pressure-temperature (P-T) limits specified in 10 CFR 50 Appendix G when below the LTOP enable temperature, thereby maintaining reactor pressure vessel (RPV) pressure below brittle fracture limits during all operational, maintenance, testing, or postulated accident conditions. During normal operational transients at full power, the system and core parameters are managed to prevent safety valve lift; if a safety valve does lift, the pressurizer (PZR) steam space is sized to avoid liquid discharge. LTOP analysis assumes a maximum PZR heater output of 880 kW and an additional 2 MW from core decay heat, demonstrating that reactor vent valves (RVVs) open before reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure exceeds the low temperature pressure limit. Table 5.2-10 provides LTOP pressure setpoints as a function of cold temperature, with PZR pressure limits ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, and LTOP not enabled above 318°F. Figure 5.2-4 illustrates the variable LTOP setpoint (PZR pressure) as a function of temperature, in relation to the saturation curve.

The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system safeguards the reactor coolant system (RCS) during low temperature conditions by utilizing reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two pilot-operated relief and safety valves (RSVs) installed above the pressurizer volume on the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) head, which relieve RCS pressure directly into containment. LTOP is enabled when RCS cold temperature is ≤318°F, with a temperature-dependent pressure setpoint determined by linear interpolation of values in Table 5.2-10 and illustrated in Figure 5.2-4, based on the downcomer temperature at the steam generator outlet. The system is actuated by module protection system logic to open RVVs and limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, accounting for worst-case transients such as spurious pressurizer heater actuation. The setpoint incorporates margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences, temperature variations, RVV opening delay, sensor response, and processing time. Three RVVs with their actuators and controls ensure LTOP protection even with a single active component failure. Overpressure protection for secondary side components is integrated to maintain ASME BPVC service limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs). LTOP analysis assumes a maximum pressurizer heater output of 880 kW and 2 MW core decay heat, demonstrating RVVs open before RCS pressure exceeds the low temperature limit. LTOP setpoints range from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, with LTOP not enabled above 318°F. Pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, surveillance capsule locations, and test schedules are detailed in Tables 5.3-1, 5.3-5, 5.3-6, 5.3-7, and Figures 5.3-2 through 5.3-5, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, and 10 CFR 50.61 for a 60-year plant life. The inadvertent actuation block arming setpoint is set above the limiting RPV pressure for LTOP conditions to avoid interference with LTOP actuation. Pressure-temperature curves and containment pressure saturation curves are provided, with corrections for location and instrument uncertainty as described. Further details on RVV qualification, design, and operation are in Section 6.3, and additional testing and inspection information is found in Sections 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6.

The low temperature overpressure protection (LTOP) system utilizes reactor vent valves (RVVs) to provide overpressure protection for the reactor coolant system (RCS) during low temperature conditions. Two pilot-operated relief and safety valves (RSVs) are installed above the pressurizer volume on the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) head, relieving RCS pressure directly into containment. The LTOP system is enabled when the RCS cold temperature is less than or equal to 318°F, with a temperature-dependent pressure setpoint determined as a function of the cold temperature in the downcomer at the steam generator (SG) outlet. The setpoint is selected using linear interpolation between values provided in Table 5.2-10 and illustrated in Figure 5.2-4. Upon LTOP actuation by the module protection system logic, the RVVs open to limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, considering the worst-case transient, such as spurious pressurizer (PZR) heater actuation. The LTOP setpoint incorporates margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences between instrumentation and the RPV bottom, temperature variations in downcomer regions, RVV opening delay, sensor response time, and module protection system processing time. As PZR level approaches 100 percent, analysis shows rapid pressure increase exceeding the LTOP setpoint. The inadvertent actuation block arming setpoint is set above the limiting RPV pressure for LTOP conditions, ensuring it does not prevent LTOP actuation. Three RVVs, along with their actuators and controls, provide LTOP protection even with a single active component failure. Further details on RVV qualification, design, and operation are provided in Section 6.3.

results.question: What corrosion-resistant materials are used for RCS pressure boundary components in contact with primary coolant?
results.expected_answer: Primary coolant-wetted surfaces use corrosion-resistant materials. The RPV interior is clad with stainless steel or nickel-based alloy. Steam generator tubes are Alloy 690, chosen for stress corrosion cracking resistance. Bolting uses Alloy 718 for high-temperature strength and corrosion resistance.
results.generated_answer:
Corrosion-resistant materials used for RCS pressure boundary components in contact with primary coolant are austenitic stainless steel (Type 3XX series with ≤0.03 wt% carbon) and nickel-based alloys (such as Ni-Cr-Fe cladding, e.g., Alloy 52/152); Alloy 600 and Alloy 82/182 are not used.  
(Source: NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report; SRP 5.2.3 context provided)
retrieved_contexts: The Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) surface materials in contact with reactor coolant or pool water during refueling, including welds, are composed of austenitic stainless steel or nickel-based alloys. Alloy 600 base metal and Alloy 82/182 cladding and weld metal are not used in the RCPB design. Reactor Coolant System (RCS) water chemistry is controlled to minimize corrosion and corrosion product transport. Ferritic low alloy steels used in pressure-retaining applications are clad with austenitic stainless steel or Ni-Cr-Fe cladding on surfaces exposed to reactor coolant, and low alloy steel forgings have an average grain size of five or finer per ASTM standards. Cladding of ferritic base materials undergoes post-weld heat treatment as required by ASME BPVC Section III, Subsubarticle NB-4622. Both inside and outside surfaces of the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) low alloy steels, including attachments and appurtenances in contact with reactor coolant, secondary water, or pool water, are clad with austenitic stainless steel or Ni-Cr-Fe. Larger through-holes in the baffle plate (e.g., for CRDM shafts and pressurizer flow, approximately two inches or greater) are also clad with austenitic stainless steel. The use of cobalt-based alloys is minimized, with established limits to reduce cobalt intrusion into the reactor coolant; cobalt-based alloys are used for hard surfacing and wear-resistant parts in the Control Rod Drive Mechanisms (CRDMs), with low cobalt or cobalt-free alloys permitted if qualified by testing. Tools for abrasive work must not be contaminated by prior use on carbon or low alloy steels or other non-corrosion-resistant materials to prevent intergranular or stress-corrosion cracking. The external surfaces of the RPV are clad with austenitic stainless steel. Surfaces of pressure-retaining parts of the Steam Generators (SGs), including weld filler and bolting materials, are made of corrosion-resistant materials such as stainless steel or nickel-based alloys, with all SG materials having a proven history in light water reactor environments and detailed in Table 5.2-4.

Section 5.2.3 of SRP 5.2.3, Rev 3, addresses Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Materials. For II.3.A (Fracture Toughness - 10 CFR 50, Appendix G), the criteria are fully met with no exceptions. For II.4 (Fabrication and Processing of Austenitic Stainless Steel), II.4.A (GDC 4 Compatibility of Components - Measures to Avoid Sensitization), II.4.B (GDC 4 Compatibility of Components - Controls to Avoid Stress Corrosion Cracking), and II.4.D (Control of Welding of Austenitic Stainless Steels), the criteria are only partially met; the exceptions pertain to references and subtier guidance applicable solely to BWRs or large LWRs using nonmetallic thermal insulation, or to Regulatory Guide 1.37 (endorsing NQA-1-1994). NuScale does not use nonmetallic thermal insulation on reactor coolant pressure boundary components, making related guidance not applicable.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details stringent material and fabrication controls for Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) components, emphasizing the use of Type 3XX series austenitic stainless steel with a maximum carbon content of 0.03 wt% to prevent sensitization and intergranular corrosion, particularly when exposed to 800–1500°F. Welding of ferritic and austenitic materials, as well as nickel-based alloys, is performed using procedures qualified per ASME BPVC Section III (notably Subarticles NB-4300, NB-4600, and NB-4622), Section IX, and in accordance with Regulatory Guides (RG) 1.44, 1.43, 1.50, and 1.71. Welders and operators are qualified per ASME Section IX and RG 1.71. Austenitic stainless steel and Ni-Cr-Fe cladding (e.g., Alloy 52/152) are applied to low alloy steel surfaces in contact with reactor coolant, with specific layering (first layer Type 309L, subsequent layers Type 308L) and post-weld heat treatment at 1125°F ± 25°F. Alloy 600 and Alloy 82/182 are excluded from RCPB design. All cladding and weld overlay procedures are qualified per ASME requirements, and electroslag welding is only permitted for austenitic stainless steel cladding of low alloy steel. Nondestructive examination (NDE) of austenitic stainless steel tubular products follows ASME BPVC Section III, NB-2550–NB-2570, and Section XI. Material handling, storage, and cleaning are controlled to prevent contamination and stress corrosion cracking, with written cleanliness plans addressing water purity, foreign material exclusion, and removal of chlorides, fluorides, and low melting point alloys. Acid pickling and abrasive work are minimized, and tools must not be contaminated by prior use on non-corrosion-resistant materials. Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) and Steam Generator (SG) pressure-retaining surfaces, including welds and bolting, are made from corrosion-resistant materials with proven performance in light water reactor environments, as detailed in Table 5.2-4. Cobalt-based alloys are minimized, with alternatives qualified by testing. Preheat and interpass temperature controls for welding comply with ASME BPVC and RG 1.50, and all inspections meet ASME BPVC Section XI and IWE requirements. Sensitization control includes rapid cooling after solution treatment (1800–1850°F) and verification by ASTM A262 corrosion testing when necessary. All fabrication, welding, and inspection processes are designed to ensure long-term corrosion resistance, structural integrity, and regulatory compliance for nuclear safety.

results.question: What inservice inspection capabilities does the NuScale RPV design provide?
results.expected_answer: The NuScale RPV design provides access for volumetric and surface examination of the vessel shell, welds, and nozzles per ASME Section XI requirements. The integral design requires specialized inspection approaches due to the compact configuration. RPV inspections are performed during refueling outages when the module is removed from the reactor pool.

The NuScale RPV design provides inservice inspection capabilities in accordance with ASME BPVC Section XI, including volumetric and visual examinations of welds and pressure-retaining materials, with access for periodic inspection and testing of components such as the RSVs bolted to RPV head flanges.  
(Source: NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Sections 5.2-6, 5.3-1, 5.3.1.3)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements, design parameters, material composition, and major components of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Table 5.2-6 specifies that RPV shell and head welds, including connections such as the lower RPV flange shell to RPV bottom head and steam plenum cap to integral steam plenum, are subject to B-A category volumetric examination, while internal welds like the core support block to RPV bottom head undergo B-N-2 category VT-3 visual testing; instrumentation and controls sleeve welds are part of the cladding and not separately examined. Table 5.3-1 lists key RPV parameters: design pressure is 2100 psia, design temperature is 650°F, overall height is 778 inches, and diameters range from 96.5 to 115.5 inches depending on section, with inner and outer clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches, respectively. Table 5.3-2 provides maximum allowable concentrations for RPV beltline forging material (e.g., sulfur 0.010 wt%, phosphorus 0.010 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%) and as-deposited weld metal (e.g., sulfur 0.015 wt%, phosphorus 0.012 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, manganese 1.80 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%). Overpressure protection for the NuScale Power Module Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) is provided by two Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) during power operations and by three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) during startup, shutdown, and low-temperature conditions when the RPV is not vented. The RSVs, which are redundant, safety-related, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A components, are designed to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the design pressure (2310 psia) and each is sized for 100% of the required relief capacity, in compliance with ASME BPVC Section III, Article NB-7000, and 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 15. The RSVs are bolted to flanges on the RPV head for periodic inspection and testing, while the RVVs are connected above the pressurizer (PZR) volume and discharge steam and water directly into containment. Additionally, RSVs provide external overpressure protection for steam generator (SG) tubing and plena. Thermal relief valves, which are spring-operated, balanced-bellows, Quality Group B, Seismic Category I, and Class 2 per BPVC Section III, vent directly into containment and protect secondary system components and control rod drive system cooling piping during containment isolation events, but are not credited for safety-related overpressure protection during operation. Overpressure protection during operation is instead provided by system design pressure and the RSVs.

Important features of the NPM include the following:
•
a small, modular design
•
an integral pressurized water reactor (PWR) NSSS that combines the reactor 
core, SGs, and pressurizer within the RPV, eliminating the need for external 
piping to connect the SGs and pressurizer to the RPV
•
natural circulation provides the driving force for reactor coolant flow, 
eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps
•
an RPV housed in a steel containment partially immersed in water, providing an 
effective passive heat sink for long-term decay heat removal
•
a steel containment operated at a vacuum, eliminating the need for insulation 
on the RPV
•
passive safety systems that are not reliant on electrical power

The NuScale inspection program is developed through several key steps: identifying the applicable in-service inspection (ISI) or testing requirements for the NuScale design, including the relevant code version and the overall inspections and tests required; determining the structures, systems, and components (SSC) subject to inspection and testing, as well as the specific inspection or test elements for each SSC; and assessing each inspection and test element. A comprehensive ISI and testing plan is then developed. For Class 1 systems and components, the ISI schedule and requirements adhere to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC), Section XI. The documentation also references NuScale testing programs, test and inspection plans, and topical reports, and addresses requirements for nuclear power plants operated on multi-unit sites.

The NuScale reactor design and safety analysis methodologies are detailed in topical reports TR-0516-49416 and TR-0516-49422, with pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 EFPY calculated per ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects using Regulatory Guide 1.99 and ASTM E185-82 for surveillance. Fluence and pressure-temperature limits are further addressed in TR-0116-20781-P and TR-1015-18177-P. The design does not require certain systems post-accident and does not credit them in radiological consequence analyses, fully applying the single-failure criterion. NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, justifying departures in a topical report, and applies a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for Chapter 15 events, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models addressing only relevant features per Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30. Boric acid concentrations are selected to bound all operating conditions, with NRC-inspected testing per IP35034.1.5.2 and startup testing detailed in Section 14.2. The design does not use combustible gas control systems, supporting exemptions to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), 50.34(f)(2)(xiv), and GDC 27, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, for containment isolation valve closure times. Hydrogen control is not required, as combustion does not affect CNV integrity. Post-accident assessment relies on radiation monitors and core exit temperature, supporting exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding post-accident sampling. Additional exemptions include 10 CFR 50.46a, 50.34(f)(2)(vi) (high point venting), and portions of 50.34(f)(2)(xx) (pressurizer level indicators). The CVCS does not include holdup tanks under vacuum but provides required venting and draining. The RPV high-point degasification vent is not safety-significant, and high-point vents are not needed. The LTOP system is not connected to a low-pressure system, but guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation applies. NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, for LOCA mass and energy release analysis, and requires no additional ITAAC. results.question: How does the NuScale secondary side interface with the primary system through the steam generator?
results.expected_answer: Feedwater enters the SG tube side at approximately 300 degrees F and is heated by the primary coolant flowing on the shell side. The helical coil design maximizes heat transfer area in the compact space. Superheated steam exits at approximately 575 degrees F. The secondary side is at a lower pressure than the primary, ensuring any tube leak results in primary-to-secondary leakage direction.
results.generated_answer:
The NuScale secondary side interfaces with the primary system through the steam generator by having preheated feedwater flow upward inside helical tubes, where heat is transferred from the primary reactor coolant across the tube walls, producing superheated steam that exits to the power conversion system, with the primary and secondary fluids separated by the tube walls.  
(Source: Context, paragraphs 3 and 4)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale steam generator (SG) design minimizes crevices between SG tubes, tube supports, and tubesheets to limit corrosion product buildup, with tube-to-tubesheet contact fully within the primary coolant environment. Full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore eliminates crevices at the tube-to-tubesheet face, and tubes are expanded into both steam and feed plenum tubesheets. The once-through SG design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet plena, preventing accumulation or concentration of materials and making traditional SG blowdown systems unnecessary and ineffective for removing corrosion products or impurities. Instead, any secondary coolant impurities or corrosion products may deposit as scale or film on interior tube surfaces or be removed by carryover, with low concentrations ensured by material selection and chemistry control. Periodic cleaning during outages, using proven chemical or mechanical methods, removes unacceptable corrosion product films. Secondary side SG surfaces are constructed of corrosion-resistant materials such as nickel alloy, stainless steel, or stainless steel cladding, eliminating concerns about degradation from cleaning solutions and negating the need for invasive sludge removal techniques. The design provides access to both internal (secondary) and external (reactor coolant) sides of tubesheets for inspection and foreign object removal. The tube support structure is located within the primary coolant environment, preventing secondary system corrosion products from depositing on the SG shell. Chemistry control for both primary and secondary water is maintained per industry guidelines, with modifications for the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design, ensuring corrosion protection for stainless steels and nickel alloys in SG components, as detailed in Sections 5.2.3, 6.1, and 10.3.5, with the secondary water quality control program following NEI 97-06.

The NuScale design employs passive natural circulation of the primary coolant, which eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps. This approach conforms to DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials, specifically regarding the selection, processing, testing, and inspection of materials, with no exceptions noted.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) steam generator (SG) is an integral, once-through, helical tube design located within the reactor pressure vessel, with two SGs per module. Each SG contains 1,380 seamless helical tubes in 21 columns, providing a total heat transfer area of 17,928 ft² and a 10% tube plugging margin. The SG operates at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with inlet and outlet temperatures of 299.7°F and 584.4°F, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. Both primary and secondary sides are rated at 2,100 psia and 650°F, while SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia and 550°F. Tubes have a 0.625-inch outer diameter, 0.050-inch wall thickness, and a minimum bend radius of 6.250 inches. Steam and feed tubesheets are 4.0 and 6.0 inches thick (without clad), increasing to 4.625 and 6.625 inches with clad (0.250 inches secondary, 0.375 inches primary). Eight austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies per column provide full-circumferential, non-continuous support, minimizing crevice formation and corrosion, and ensuring high vibration resistance. The design eliminates traditional tube support plates and uses full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore to minimize crevices, with all tube-to-tubesheet contact in the primary coolant. The once-through design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet, preventing impurity accumulation and eliminating the need for blowdown systems; impurities are managed by chemistry control and periodic cleaning. Secondary side surfaces use corrosion-resistant materials (nickel alloy or stainless steel), allowing safe cleaning and inspection. System monitoring is performed at ≥500 Hz, and preservice inspections include 100% volumetric tube and weld examination per BPVC. The SG tube support structure is within the primary coolant, preventing secondary system corrosion product deposition. Chemistry control follows industry guidelines with NPM modifications, and secondary water quality adheres to NEI 97-06. The fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. SG tube failure is indicated by pressure changes, and actuation valves have position indication.

Secondary side instability evaluation shows no in-phase oscillations and that out-of-phase oscillations cancel, resulting in no net oscillating secondary flow. Acceptable instabilities are limited to tube mass flow fluctuations that do not induce reactor power oscillations exceeding fuel design limits and comply with ASME Code. The vibration assessment program (per Regulatory Guide 1.20, Rev. 3) covers SGs, main steam piping (including MSIV), SG supports, and tube supports within the RPV. Flow-induced vibration (FIV) screening criteria were developed, and low primary coolant flow rates result in low

The reactor coolant returns to the bottom of the vessel via the downcomer and re-enters the reactor core, completing the primary loop. On the secondary side, preheated feedwater is pumped into the tube side of the two independent steam generators (SGs), entering through nozzles into the lower feed plenums. The feedwater flows upward through the helical SG tubes, where heat is transferred from the reactor coolant across the tube walls, causing the feedwater to boil and become superheated steam. The steam is collected in the steam plena at the top of the SGs and exits through steam nozzles, passing through SG piping and containment penetrations to the main steam system and power conversion systems outside the Reactor Building. The superheated steam is directed to a dedicated steam turbine, which drives a generator to produce electricity delivered to the grid via a step-up transformer. A turbine bypass line can route up to 100% of the rated main steam flow directly from the SGs to the main condenser to remove reactor heat following load reductions or loss of electrical load. Steam exiting or bypassing the turbine is condensed in up to six condensers using a shared circulating water loop. The condensate is polished and pumped by variable speed feedwater pumps, with some steam extracted from turbine stages to preheat the feedwater for increased efficiency. Feedwater regulating valves control flow into the SGs. The total SG heat transfer area includes the full tube length from feed to steam plenums, with a 10% tube plugging margin and a fouling factor based on typical nuclear plant operating experience and secondary water chemistry control. The SG configuration maintains separation between the superheated steam at the tube tops and subcooled liquid at the bottoms, minimizing hydraulic instabilities and potential water hammer.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) steam generator (SG) is an integral, once-through, helical tube design located within the reactor pressure vessel, featuring two collocated SGs per module. Each SG contains 1,380 seamless helical tubes arranged in 21 columns, with a total heat transfer area of 17,928 ft² and a 10% tube plugging margin. The SGs operate at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with inlet and outlet temperatures of 299.7°F and 584.4°F, respectively, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. Both primary and secondary sides are rated at 2,100 psia and 650°F, while SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia and 550°F. Tubes have an outer diameter of 0.625 inches, a wall thickness of 0.050 inches, and a minimum transition bend radius of 6.250 inches. The steam and feed tubesheets are 4.0 and 6.0 inches thick (without clad), increasing to 4.625 and 6.625 inches with clad, which is 0.250 inches (secondary) and 0.375 inches (primary) thick. The SGs utilize austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies—eight sets per column—providing full-circumferential, non-continuous support to minimize crevice formation and corrosion product buildup, and to ensure high modal frequencies for vibration resistance. The design eliminates traditional tube support plates and minimizes crevices at tube-to-tubesheet interfaces by full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore, with all tube-to-tubesheet contact in the primary coolant environment. The once-through design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet, preventing impurity accumulation and negating the need for blowdown systems; impurities are managed by chemistry control and periodic cleaning using chemical or mechanical methods. Secondary side surfaces are constructed of corrosion-resistant materials such as nickel alloy or stainless steel, allowing for safe cleaning and inspection access to both sides of the tubesheets. System monitoring is performed at a minimum 500 Hz resolution, and preservice inspections include 100% volumetric, full-length tube examination and weld inspections per BPVC requirements. The SG tube support structure is located within the primary coolant, preventing secondary system corrosion product deposition on the SG shell. Chemistry control for both primary and secondary water follows industry guidelines, with modifications for the NPM, and the secondary water quality program adheres to NEI 97-06. The fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. SG tube failure is indicated by pressure changes, and actuation valves are equipped with position indication.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed to promote natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS ensures coolant supply to the core to maintain specified acceptable fuel design limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences. A schematic flow diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and Table 5.1-1 detail major RCS components, flow stages, and volumes during steady-state, full-power operation. The SGS, with the main steam and feedwater systems, removes decay heat from the RCS during initial cooldown. Optimal performance criteria include maximizing electrical generation, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining margin to analytical limits. Key parameters—such as primary coolant temperatures, flow rates, pressurizer (PZR) water level, steam generator (SG) water level and mass, feedwater and steam flow rates, pressures, and steam superheat—are determined using NRELAP5 thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand calculations. Design calculations establish best-estimate, maximum, and minimum primary coolant flows, accounting for uncertainties in component flow resistances, core bypass flow, and thermal head capability, with bounding uncertainties based on test data and design requirements. Minimum and maximum design flow rates are calculated by biasing analytical uncertainties to minimize or maximize flow, respectively, and are used in bounding design analyses. The module heatup system provides RCS heating and coolant flow before criticality; between hot zero power and 15% reactor power, core heat is used to reach normal operating coolant temperature. From 15% to full power, average primary coolant temperature is held constant, with higher full-power coolant temperatures for higher flow rates, ensuring similar hot leg temperatures and sufficient SG superheat regardless of flow. Flow rate varies with module heatup system performance, SG operation, and RCS temperature, with typical hot zero power temperatures and steady-state best-estimate flow rates identified. The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RCS to the secondary steam system, supplying superheated steam to the power conversion cycle, with operating conditions summarized in Table 5.4-1 for full-power steam flow at best-estimate primary coolant conditions.

A single RCS natural circulation flow loop is entirely contained within the RPV, thereby 
eliminating distinct RCS piping loops and the associated potential for a large pipe 
break (i.e., large break loss-of-coolant accident [LOCA]) event.This design, combined 
with the intertwined SGs tube bundle configuration, eliminates the potential for 
asymmetric core cooling and temperatures as a result of a loss of a single SG function.

results.question: From Tables 5.1-1, what are the core volume and the steam generator volume in the RCS?
results.expected_answer: Table 5.1-1 shows the core volume is 89 ft³ and the steam generator volume is 621 ft³. The SG volume is approximately 7 times larger than the core volume, reflecting the extensive helical coil tube bundle.
results.generated_answer: The core volume is 89 ft³ and the steam generator (SG) region volume is 621 ft³ in the RCS. (Source: Table 5.1-1, NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Tier 2, 5.1-7, Revision 5)
retrieved_contexts:
A schematic flow diagram of the RCS is provided in Figure 5.1-3, denoting major 
components, and major RCS loop flow stages during normal steady-state, full-power 
operating conditions.In addition, Table 5.1-1 identifies the RCS volumes.The SGS (in conjunction with the main steam and main feedwater systems) are used to 
remove decay heat from the RCS during the initial phase of module cooldown.

The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed for natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during both power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps and external piping, with all major components—including the core, pressurizer (PZR), two steam generators (SGs), reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, and decay heat removal system (DHRS)—integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). The core heats the coolant, which rises through the lower and upper riser assemblies, is redirected by the PZR baffle plate, and flows over the SG helical tube bundles, transferring heat to the secondary side and producing superheated steam with a moisture content ≤0.10% by weight at full power. The cooled, denser coolant descends through the annular downcomer and reenters the core, completing the natural circulation loop, with flow driven by density differences and elevation between the core and SGs. The RCS flow path is optimized for low pressure losses, so pressure is primarily determined by static head, and SG tube plugging does not affect natural circulation flow rate. The system includes small bypass flow paths in the upper riser to maintain boron concentration during DHRS-driven riser uncovery after non-LOCA transients. The pressurizer, integral to the RPV above the coolant and separated by a baffle plate, operates at 2100 psia and 650°F, with two 1-inch spray nozzles and heater bundles (each 400 kW, total ≥785 kW with 15% margin, 480 Vac, 60 Hz, 800°F sheath temperature) to maintain RCS pressure. Instrumentation provides control room indication of RCS pressure, pressurizer level, spray flow, and heater output, with the Monitoring and Control System (MCS) automating RCS level and pressure control. The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) maintains pressurizer level (normally 50–60%), adjusts RCS chemistry, and controls boron concentration, supplying continuous spray flow to minimize thermal stresses and maintain chemistry. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core—89 ft³, SG region—621 ft³, and PZR—578 ft³ (487 ft³ cylindrical). During off-normal transients, initial pool temperatures of 100°F or 140°F have minimal effect on RCS cooldown after 36 hours, even with reduced heat removal from fouling, tube plugging, or noncondensible gas accumulation. The system’s design, including intertwined SG tube bundles and elimination of external piping, prevents asymmetric core cooling and large break LOCA events. Key operational parameters—coolant temperatures, flow rates, press

5.1.3.7 
Steam Generators
The SGS is an integral part of the RPV (although not a component of the RCS) 
composed of the SG tubes, SG tube supports, steam and feedwater piping inside 
containment, SG tube inlet flow restrictors, feed plenums, and steam plenums.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed to promote natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS ensures coolant supply to the core to maintain specified acceptable fuel design limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences. A schematic flow diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and Table 5.1-1 detail major RCS components, flow stages, and volumes during steady-state, full-power operation. The SGS, with the main steam and feedwater systems, removes decay heat from the RCS during initial cooldown. Optimal performance criteria include maximizing electrical generation, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining margin to analytical limits. Key parameters—such as primary coolant temperatures, flow rates, pressurizer (PZR) water level, steam generator (SG) water level and mass, feedwater and steam flow rates, pressures, and steam superheat—are determined using NRELAP5 thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand calculations. Design calculations establish best-estimate, maximum, and minimum primary coolant flows, accounting for uncertainties in component flow resistances, core bypass flow, and thermal head capability, with bounding uncertainties based on test data and design requirements. Minimum and maximum design flow rates are calculated by biasing analytical uncertainties to minimize or maximize flow, respectively, and are used in bounding design analyses. The module heatup system provides RCS heating and coolant flow before criticality; between hot zero power and 15% reactor power, core heat is used to reach normal operating coolant temperature. From 15% to full power, average primary coolant temperature is held constant, with higher full-power coolant temperatures for higher flow rates, ensuring similar hot leg temperatures and sufficient SG superheat regardless of flow. Flow rate varies with module heatup system performance, SG operation, and RCS temperature, with typical hot zero power temperatures and steady-state best-estimate flow rates identified. The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RCS to the secondary steam system, supplying superheated steam to the power conversion cycle, with operating conditions summarized in Table 5.4-1 for full-power steam flow at best-estimate primary coolant conditions.

The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system safeguards the reactor coolant system (RCS) during low temperature conditions by utilizing reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two pilot-operated relief and safety valves (RSVs) installed above the pressurizer volume on the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) head, which relieve RCS pressure directly into containment. LTOP is enabled when RCS cold temperature is ≤318°F, with a temperature-dependent pressure setpoint determined by linear interpolation of values in Table 5.2-10 and illustrated in Figure 5.2-4, based on the downcomer temperature at the steam generator outlet. The system is actuated by module protection system logic to open RVVs and limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, accounting for worst-case transients such as spurious pressurizer heater actuation. The setpoint incorporates margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences, temperature variations, RVV opening delay, sensor response, and processing time. Three RVVs with their actuators and controls ensure LTOP protection even with a single active component failure. Overpressure protection for secondary side components is integrated to maintain ASME BPVC service limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs). LTOP analysis assumes a maximum pressurizer heater output of 880 kW and 2 MW core decay heat, demonstrating RVVs open before RCS pressure exceeds the low temperature limit. LTOP setpoints range from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, with LTOP not enabled above 318°F. Pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, surveillance capsule locations, and test schedules are detailed in Tables 5.3-1, 5.3-5, 5.3-6, 5.3-7, and Figures 5.3-2 through 5.3-5, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, and 10 CFR 50.61 for a 60-year plant life. The inadvertent actuation block arming setpoint is set above the limiting RPV pressure for LTOP conditions to avoid interference with LTOP actuation. Pressure-temperature curves and containment pressure saturation curves are provided, with corrections for location and instrument uncertainty as described. Further details on RVV qualification, design, and operation are in Section 6.3, and additional testing and inspection information is found in Sections 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6.

The low temperature overpressure protection (LTOP) system utilizes reactor vent valves (RVVs) to provide overpressure protection for the reactor coolant system (RCS) during low temperature conditions. Two pilot-operated relief and safety valves (RSVs) are installed above the pressurizer volume on the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) head, relieving RCS pressure directly into containment. The LTOP system is enabled when the RCS cold temperature is less than or equal to 318°F, with a temperature-dependent pressure setpoint determined as a function of the cold temperature in the downcomer at the steam generator (SG) outlet. The setpoint is selected using linear interpolation between values provided in Table 5.2-10 and illustrated in Figure 5.2-4. Upon LTOP actuation by the module protection system logic, the RVVs open to limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, considering the worst-case transient, such as spurious pressurizer (PZR) heater actuation. The LTOP setpoint incorporates margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences between instrumentation and the RPV bottom, temperature variations in downcomer regions, RVV opening delay, sensor response time, and module protection system processing time. As PZR level approaches 100 percent, analysis shows rapid pressure increase exceeding the LTOP setpoint. The inadvertent actuation block arming setpoint is set above the limiting RPV pressure for LTOP conditions, ensuring it does not prevent LTOP actuation. Three RVVs, along with their actuators and controls, provide LTOP protection even with a single active component failure. Further details on RVV qualification, design, and operation are provided in Section 6.3.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report confirms that the reactor vessel materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy values well above the 50 ft-lb threshold throughout the vessel's life, with initial values at a minimum of 75 ft-lb, rendering the guidance for lower energies inapplicable. Reactor vessel beltline materials are evaluated to ensure a minimum end-of-life Charpy upper shelf energy of 50 ft-lb. The ΔRTNDT at the 1/4-T location and the methodology for pressure and temperature limits are detailed in NuScale Technical Report TR-1015-18177. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening follows 10 CFR 50.61, using RG 1.99 equations for fluence and temperature factor calculations. At both 32 and 57 effective full-power years (EFPY), the predicted RTPTS values for the lower RPV shell, weld, and bottom head remain below the regulatory screening criteria (270°F for shell and head, 300°F for weld), with maximum adjusted reference temperatures at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head). The material specimen program matrix meets ASTM E185-82 requirements, with specified numbers of Charpy and tension specimens for both unirradiated and irradiated conditions. Operational restrictions are in place, such as prohibiting reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transient conditions and below 185°F during steady-state conditions. The data tables provide detailed fluence, RTNDT, and margin values for key vessel locations, confirming compliance with regulatory safety margins over the 60-year design life.

5.3.2.4 
Upper-Shelf Energy
Reactor vessel beltline materials are evaluated to ensure a minimum end-of-life Charpy 
V-notch upper shelf energy value of 50 ft-lb, as required by 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, at 
57-EFPY fluence, which bounds the NuScale 60-year design life.The initial Charpy 
V-notch upper shelf energy value is 75 ft-lb.

The NuScale reactor vessel beltline materials are evaluated for Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) in accordance with Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.99, Rev. 2, to ensure compliance with the 10 CFR 50, Appendix G requirement of a minimum end-of-life USE of 50 ft-lb at 57 effective full power years (EFPY) fluence, which bounds the 60-year design life. The initial Charpy USE is 75 ft-lb. Table 5.3-9 summarizes the predicted 1/4-T Charpy USE decrease at 57-EFPY fluence: for the lower RPV shell beltline (0.10 wt% Cu, 1.37E+19 n/cm², E > 1 MeV), the USE decreases by 20.5% to 59.6 ft-lb; for the lower RPV weld (0.06 wt% Cu, 3.21E+18 n/cm²), the decrease is 15.3% to 63.5 ft-lb; and for the RPV bottom head (0.10 wt% Cu, 3.21E+18 n/cm²), the decrease is 14.5% to 64.1 ft-lb. Table 5.3-10 provides the final 1/4-T Charpy USE values after adjusting for NuScale reactor pressure vessel irradiation temperature, applying a -1.2 ft-lb correction: lower RPV shell beltline, 58.4 ft-lb; lower RPV weld, 62.3 ft-lb; and RPV bottom head, 62.9 ft-lb. These values meet the regulatory minimum, and the Material Surveillance program monitors changes in fracture toughness properties throughout the vessel’s service life.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report demonstrates that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) values well above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb throughout the 60-year design life, with initial values of at least 75 ft-lb and predicted end-of-life values of 58.4 ft-lb (lower shell), 62.3 ft-lb (weld), and 62.9 ft-lb (bottom head) at 57 effective full-power years (EFPY). The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT for the limiting weld metal is 50°F at 0.55 EFPY, with 28°F due to a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening per 10 CFR 50.61 and RG 1.99 shows RTPTS values at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head), all below regulatory limits. Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The RPV is fabricated and inspected per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, with material specifications and chemical compositions detailed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.3-2. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, and clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. Major components are detailed in Figure 5.3-1, and all packaging, handling, and storage meet ASME and NQA-1 requirements. The RCPB material selections, as specified in Table 5.2-4, include nickel-based Alloy 718 (UNS N07718) for threaded fasteners, SA-508 Grade 3, Class 1 and 2 for RPV sections, SA-533 Type B, Class 2 for support gussets and plates, and austenitic stainless steels (SA-479/SA-240 Type 304/304L, 316/316L) for core barrel guides, support blocks, and instrument assemblies.
