5.4.4.2 
System Design
The NPM design comprises a reactor core, two SGs, and a pressurizer, contained within 
a single RPV, surrounded and contained within a steel CNV.

A NuScale Power Module (NPM) shown in Figure 1.2-6 and Figure 1.2-7, is a collection of 
systems, sub-systems, and components that together constitute a modularized, movable, 
nuclear steam supply system (NSSS).The NPM is composed of a reactor core, a pressurizer, and 
two steam generators integrated within a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and housed in a 
compact steel containment vessel.

A NuScale Power Module (NPM) shown in Figure 1.2-6, is a collection of systems, 
sub-systems, and components that make up a modularized, movable, nuclear steam 
supply system (NSSS).Each NPM is comprised of a reactor core, a pressurizer, and two 
steam generators (SGs) integrated within a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and housed in a 
compact steel containment vessel (CNV).

The NPM (NuScale Power Module) is designed for normal power maneuvers, allowing electric power adjustment via turbine bypass to the condenser, and core power maneuvering through control rods, soluble boron concentration changes, or a combination of both, as detailed in Sections 4.2 and 4.3. In multi-module plants, individual NPMs can be incrementally placed into service to match construction schedules and grid demand, and can be taken offline individually for refueling or maintenance. The refueling pool houses bolting tools for NPM disassembly and reassembly, with the reactor core remaining in the lower head of the RPV during refueling. A fuel handling machine transfers new and used fuel between the refueling pool and spent fuel pool (SFP) via a weir. The dry dock area, separated from the refueling pool by a gate, contains the module inspection rack and allows the water level to be lowered for maintenance on the upper NPM. Inspection and testing equipment are moved to the dry dock during refueling, which also serves for introducing new NPM components and preparing them for assembly. During refueling, the RBC (Reactor Building Crane) moves the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling pool, where it is set into the containment flange tool and the CNV flange is unbolted. The crane then separates the lower CNV from the upper CNV (with RPV attached), moves the upper CNV and RPV to the reactor vessel flange tool, and unbolts the RPV flange. The upper and lower RPV are separated, leaving the lower RPV with the reactor core in the flange tool, while the upper NPM (upper CNV with attached upper RPV) is moved to the module inspection rack in the dry dock for inspection, testing, and maintenance. After these activities and refueling, the upper NPM is returned to the refueling pool and reassembled in reverse order using the flange tools. The reassembled NPM is then moved back to its operating bay by the RBC, where startup tests are performed before the reactor is brought online for steam and power production.

A diagram of an individual NPM is provided in Figure 5.1-1 showing the RPV within the CNV 
and denoting major RCS components.A simplified RCS diagram is provided in Figure 5.1-2.During normal operation, the RCS transports heat from the reactor core to the SGs by 
natural circulation.

The 
crane again lifts the NPM, this time separating the upper and lower RPV, leaving the 
lower RPV including the reactor core, in the reactor vessel flange tool.Finally, the 
crane transports the upper NPM (now consisting of just the upper CNV with 
attached upper RPV) to the module inspection rack in the dry dock.

Chapter 1 provides an overview of the NuScale Power Plant design that includes up to twelve 
individual NPMs.The description in this chapter applies to each of the NPMs individually, 
unless otherwise stated.5.1.1 
Design Basis
The performance and safety design bases of the RCS and its major components are 
interrelated.

To represent the NPM design, a pilot-operated main 
valve was used with an inlet connected to a source able to provide water at reactor 
operating pressures and temperatures, and an outlet that exhausted to atmosphere.Trip line tubing and a trip valve representative of the NPM design was connected to the 
main valve to simulate NPM valve actuation performance and potential for boric acid 
buildup in internal passages.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) design features an integral power module comprising a reactor core, two helical-coil steam generators (SGs), and a pressurizer, all contained within a single reactor pressure vessel (RPV). This RPV is immediately surrounded by a steel containment vessel (CNV) that is approximately cylindrical and sits in the reactor pool. The reactor core is positioned below the helical-coil SGs inside the RPV. The design eliminates the need for external piping between the steam generators, pressurizer, and RPV, and utilizes natural circulation for reactor coolant system flow, removing the requirement for reactor coolant pumps. For valve actuation testing, a pilot-operated main valve with trip line tubing and a trip valve representative of the NPM design was used to simulate performance and assess potential boric acid buildup. Pressurizer level instrumentation is provided to the control room and the Module Control System (MCS), supporting an exemption from certain power supply requirements per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx). Pressurizer pressure is measured and provides input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS).

The RCPB components are designed and provided with access to permit periodic 
inspection and testing of important areas and features to assess their structural and 
leak-tight integrity pursuant GDC 32.The design allows inspection, testing, and 
maintenance of the RSV, PZR heaters, SG primary and secondary sides, instruments, 
electrical connections, and other components located inside of the RCPB of the NPMs.

1.2.2.2.1 
Main Control Room
The MCR contains control panels for all installed NPMs.Each reactor operator 
monitors and controls multiple NPMs from a control room panel.Figure 18.7-1 
provides the layout for the MCR.

The NPMs are partially immersed in a reactor pool and protected by passive safety 
systems.Each NPM has a dedicated emergency core cooling system (ECCS) and 
decay heat removal system (DHRS).

The 
RCPB for each NPM is consistent with the RCPB definition provided in 10 CFR 50.2 and includes 
the pressure retaining components that are part of the reactor coolant system (RCS) up to and 
including:
•
the outermost containment isolation valve (CIV) in system piping that penetrates the 
containment vessel (CNV) 
•
the reactor safety valves (RSVs)
•
the emergency core cooling system (ECCS) reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor 
recirculation valves (RRVs)
For each NPM, the RCS piping that forms the RCPB penetrates both the reactor pressure vessel 
(RPV) and the CNV up to the outermost CIV.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) is a modular, movable nuclear steam supply system (NSSS) comprising a reactor core, pressurizer, and two steam generators integrated within a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and housed in a compact steel containment vessel (CNV). The NuScale plant design is scalable, supporting from one to twelve NPMs within a single Reactor Building, with each module rated at 160 MWt (totaling 1,920 MWt for 12 modules) and approximately 50 MWe electrical output per module (600 MWe total), with a net output of about 570 MWe for a 12-module facility after accounting for house loads. The plant includes all necessary primary and secondary systems, such as main steam systems, turbine generator sets, condensate and feedwater systems, shared external cooling water systems, module assembly equipment, fuel handling equipment, and turbine maintenance equipment. The PIM code, developed by NuScale, is used to assess NPM stability during operation, while the NIST-1 is a scaled stainless steel representation of the NPM reactor, containment, and reactor pool systems, with a maximum operating pressure of 1650 psia (11.4 MPa) and temperature of 630°F (605 K). The design and safety bases of the reactor coolant system (RCS) and its major components are closely interrelated, and the facility’s documentation generally describes individual modules, with multi-module information provided where relevant.

•
PZR heater bundles described in Section 5.4.5.•
RVVs and RRVs and the associated actuator assemblies described in Section 6.3.The RPV, described in Section 5.3, is the primary component of the RCS and RCPB for each NPM.

The steam generator (SG) tube program encompasses assessment of degradation, tube inspection requirements, tube integrity assessment, tube plugging, primary-to-secondary leakage monitoring, shell side integrity assessment, primary and secondary side water chemistry control, foreign material exclusion, loose parts management, contractor oversight, self-assessment, and reporting. The internal surface of SG tubes is fully accessible for nondestructive examination methods capable of detecting all relevant degradation types throughout the tube's service life. Individual tubes may be plugged or stabilized to prevent adverse interactions with non-plugged tubes. The SG tube design lacks U-bends, allowing volumetric examination along the entire tube length as specified in Item B16.10 of Table IWB-2500-1 (B-Q). Preservice examinations are conducted per BPVC Section III, Paragraph NB-5280, and Section XI, Subarticle IWB-2200, using appropriate examination methods. The tube length is defined from the tube-to-tubesheet weld at the inlet to the weld at the outlet, with the welds themselves not considered part of the tube. Preservice inspection is performed after tube installation and hydrostatic testing, but before initial power operation, to establish a baseline for future inservice inspections. Tubes exhibiting flaws exceeding 40% of the nominal wall thickness, or flaws that could compromise integrity before the first inservice inspection, or indications affecting future inspectability, must be plugged. The volumetric preservice examination technique must detect all relevant preservice flaws and allow comparison with future inservice inspection results to meet plant technical specification requirements.

Table 5.1-2 presents primary system temperatures and flow rates, while Table 5.2-1 lists relevant ASME Code Cases. Each steam generator (SG) tube consists of a seamless helical tube with bends at each end, transitioning to a straight section at the tube sheet entry. The tubes terminate at the feed and steam plenum tubesheets, where they are secured by expansion fit and welded on the secondary side. Instead of typical SG tube support plates, the design uses austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies that provide non-continuous, full-circumferential support, minimizing crevice formation and corrosion product buildup. Tube support assemblies are positioned between each column of helical tubes, with stamped tabs enveloping part of the tube circumference above and below to provide vertical support and minimize flow stagnation at the tube-to-support interface. Outer and middle spacers, welded into the tube supports, allow tabs from adjacent columns to nest, creating a continuous support path and optimizing circumferential spacing to minimize tube free span lengths while accommodating tube transitions to the plena. The tube support assemblies are attached to upper and lower SG supports welded to the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) inner surface, providing contact with each tube at eight circumferential locations. The use of eight sets of tube support assemblies limits unsupported tube lengths, ensuring high modal frequencies to prevent unacceptable flow-induced vibration. Lower SG supports permit thermal growth and provide lateral support. Tubes at the bundle inlet (cold leg) are evaluated to preclude vortex shedding, as they lack downstream obstructions, but the tortuous flow path within the bundle and the axial alignment of supports (angle of attack near 0.0, aspect ratio > 2.0) prevent stable vortex formation, galloping, and flutter. The upper SG supports are welded to the RPV and steam plenum and are excluded from analysis due to their rigidity, while the lower SG supports are welded to the RPV shell below the tube bundle.

Figure 5.4-10 provides a simplified 
diagram of the DHRS illustrating the operational flowpath and major system 
components.Table 5.4-5 provides a summary of DHRS design data.Prior to power operations, the feedwater pumps fill the DHRS up to the actuation

The DHRS performance 
analysis assumes fouling factors of 0.0005 hr-ft2-°F/BTU for the DHRS condenser 
and steam piping heat transfer surfaces and 0.0001 hr-ft2-°F/BTU for the SG heat 
transfer surfaces, SG tube plugging of 10 percent, and a noncondensible gas mass 
of 0.422 kg.

The DHRS is 
designed to withstand the effects of natural phenomena without loss of capability to 
perform its safety function.The DHRS is designed to accommodate the effects of, and 
be compatible with, the environmental conditions associated with normal operation, 
maintenance, testing, and postulated accidents.

5.4.3.3.4 
Thermal-Hydraulic Performance
As a two phase natural circulation system, DHRS performance is dependent on the 
following factors:
•
RCS temperature - A higher RCS temperature provides a larger driving 
temperature difference and, therefore, increases DHRS heat transfer.

The safety-related DHRS function is an engineered safety feature of the NPM design.Reliability of DHRS is evaluated using the reliability assurance program described in 
Section 17.4 and risk significance is determined using the guidance described in 
Chapter 19.The DHRS classification and risk categories are included in Table 3.2-1.

The DHRS 
passive condensers are located at a higher elevation relative to the SGs to promote 
natural circulation flow to the SGs.The RCS temperature and pressure sensors provide 
indication that the DHRS is operating normally.The RCS temperature and pressure 
decrease following a reactor trip and DHRS actuation, provide indication that the DHRS 
is working normally.A representative sketch illustrating relative elevation differences is 
provided in Figure 1.2-7.

The provided passages reference multiple figures and tables detailing the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and related reactor safety systems. Figure 1.2-8 and Figure 5.4-10 present diagrams of the DHRS, while Table 5.4-5 provides its design data and Table 5.4-8 offers a failure modes and effects analysis. The system's performance is illustrated in several figures: Figure 5.4-11 shows primary coolant temperature during two-train DHRS operation over 4 hours; Figures 5.4-12 and 5.4-13 depict temperature cooldown with one-train operation at low system inventory for 4 and 36 hours, respectively; Figures 5.4-14 and 5.4-15 show cooldown with one-train operation at high system inventory for 4 and 36 hours. Additional referenced systems include the Emergency Core Cooling System (Figure 1.2-9), Reactor Coolant System High-Point Vents (Section 5.4.4), and Pressurizer Design Data (Table 5.4-6). These materials collectively provide comprehensive technical details on the design, operation, and safety analysis of the DHRS and associated reactor coolant systems.

To assess the impact of these factors, a thermal-hydraulic analysis of DHRS 
performance has been performed with NRELAP5 to calculate the DHRS heat 
transfer rate.The performance analysis is divided into three model categories:
•
Steady State Model - Efficient model used to characterize the effect of the listed 
factors on the DHRS heat removal rate.

In addition, performance analysis indicates that the DHRS is capable of cooling the 
RCS below 420 degrees F in less than 36 hours with an accumulation of 
noncondensible gases, thus precluding the need for high-point vent capability.Figure 5.4-11 shows RCS cooldown for 4 hours from full power conditions with two 
DHRS trains in operation assuming nominal system conditions and indicates that 
RCS temperatures decrease below 420 degrees F in less than 2 hours.

Table 5.4-8 provides the failure modes and effects analysis for the 
DHRS.Each NPM, including the associated DHRS, are located within the Reactor Building, 
which protects the system from physical damage by events and phenomena external 
to the facility as discussed in Section 3.8.The DHRS piping branches from the main steam lines outside and above the CNV and 
upstream of the MSIVs.

•
Nominal Transient Models - Full transient models used to show that the DHRS 
cools the RCS for typical initiating events and conditions.•
Off-Normal Transient Models - Full transient models used to show that the 
DHRS provides adequate cooling even with the most limiting initiating events 
and conditions.The analytical model used for the DHRS performance analysis and the basis for its 
validity is provided in Section 15.

Figure 5.4-10 provides an overview of the DHRS valves, piping, and passive condenser 
arrangement.The DHRS piping and passive condensers are arranged around the exterior of the CNV 
and separated to reduce the potential for a single condition to affect both trains.

The DHRS thermal-hydraulic performance analysis evaluated the 
orifice loss coefficient for a variety of initial conditions to confirm that the size of 
the restriction orifice is adequate to provide consistent heat transfer.5.4.3.2.2 
Instrumentation and Controls
Level 
Accumulation of noncondensible gas in the DHRS steam lines degrades DHRS 
system performance.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) thermal-hydraulic performance analysis assessed the orifice loss coefficient under various initial conditions to confirm that the restriction orifice size ensures consistent heat transfer. Accumulation of noncondensible gas in the DHRS steam lines was identified as a factor that degrades system performance. As a two-phase natural circulation system, DHRS performance depends on factors such as Reactor Coolant System (RCS) temperature, with higher RCS temperatures increasing the driving temperature difference and thus DHRS heat transfer. The analysis, performed using NRELAP5, included three model categories: a Steady State Model (with constant primary side temperature, flow rate, reactor pool temperature, and DHRS inventory), Nominal Transient Models (to demonstrate DHRS cooling for typical initiating events), and Off-Normal Transient Models (to show adequate cooling under the most limiting conditions). The steady state model calculated the DHRS heat transfer rate and evaluated the loss coefficient in the DHRS steam line to ensure acceptable performance across a range of DHRS inventories, including scenarios with additional steam volume due to MSIV failure, DHRS and SG fouling, SG tube plugging, and the presence of noncondensible gas. The limiting mass of dissolved gas that could be released during DHRS operation was also considered. Nominal transient models analyzed DHRS cooldown capability for cases with and without containment isolation, affecting the timing of pressurizer heater isolation. Off-normal transient models assessed DHRS performance under limiting conditions, including heat transfer surface fouling, plugged SG tubes, reactor pool water temperature, and noncondensible gas presence, with conservative biasing of valve timings to simulate worst-case DHRS inventory conditions. The overall analysis confirmed the DHRS capability to remove heat over a range of loop inventories.

The NuScale Power Plant is designed for 1 to 12 NPMs with the associated primary and 
secondary systems and components necessary to produce power and maintain the facility.This includes main steam systems, turbine generator sets, condensate and feedwater 
systems, and shared external cooling water systems (Figure 1.2-3), plus module assembly 
equipment, fuel handling equipment, turbine maintenance equipment, and radioactive

The NuScale Power Plant consists of up to 12 NuScale Power Modules (NPMs), each partially immersed in a below-grade reactor pool with the water surface approximately 6 feet below grade. The reactor pool, spent fuel pool (SFP), most primary systems, and some radioactive waste equipment are all located below grade, providing additional barriers for fission product retention beyond the fuel, cladding, reactor pressure vessel (RPV), and containment. Each NPM features passive safety systems, including dedicated Emergency Core Cooling Systems (ECCS) and Decay Heat Removal Systems (DHRS), with three independent reactor vent valves and two independent reactor recirculation valves for passive decay heat removal during a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA). The ECCS and DHRS are capable of removing 100% of the decay heat load for at least 72 hours without water addition. The NPM core contains 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, ~2 m rod length, <4.95% U-235 enrichment, Gd2O3 burnable absorber) and 16 control rod assemblies (CRAs) organized into regulating and shutdown banks. The fuel assemblies are supported by five spacer grids and utilize low-flow natural circulation for primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps. The torispherical RPV head supports the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs) with unique features such as a remote disconnect mechanism and extended drive shaft, validated through proof-of-concept and alignment testing. Refueling operations are independent for each NPM, with bioshields designed for removal and stacking, and the reactor core remaining in the lower RPV during refueling. The RBC (Reactor Building Crane) facilitates NPM movement, and specialized tools and dry dock areas support disassembly, inspection, and maintenance. The module control system (MCS) and plant control system enable monitoring and control from the main control room (MCR), which features dedicated panels for each NPM and integrates safety, alarm, and radiation monitoring systems. The power conversion system includes a main steam system, turbine generator set, condenser, cooling tower, and condensate/feedwater system, with all hoisting, handling, and electrical connections located above the pool water level. The plant delivers a net output of approximately 570 MWe and allows for modular construction, incremental module operation, and off-site shipment of used NPMs.

Chapter 1 provides an overview of the NuScale Power Plant design that includes up to twelve 
individual NPMs.The description in this chapter applies to each of the NPMs individually, 
unless otherwise stated.5.1.1 
Design Basis
The performance and safety design bases of the RCS and its major components are 
interrelated.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) is a modular, movable nuclear steam supply system (NSSS) comprising a reactor core, pressurizer, and two steam generators integrated within a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and housed in a compact steel containment vessel (CNV). The NuScale plant design is scalable, supporting from one to twelve NPMs within a single Reactor Building, with each module rated at 160 MWt (totaling 1,920 MWt for 12 modules) and approximately 50 MWe electrical output per module (600 MWe total), with a net output of about 570 MWe for a 12-module facility after accounting for house loads. The plant includes all necessary primary and secondary systems, such as main steam systems, turbine generator sets, condensate and feedwater systems, shared external cooling water systems, module assembly equipment, fuel handling equipment, and turbine maintenance equipment. The PIM code, developed by NuScale, is used to assess NPM stability during operation, while the NIST-1 is a scaled stainless steel representation of the NPM reactor, containment, and reactor pool systems, with a maximum operating pressure of 1650 psia (11.4 MPa) and temperature of 630°F (605 K). The design and safety bases of the reactor coolant system (RCS) and its major components are closely interrelated, and the facility’s documentation generally describes individual modules, with multi-module information provided where relevant.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) interfaces with several systems, including the chemical and volume control system (CVCS) via the containment system, the emergency core cooling system (ECCS) through reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), the containment vessel (CNV), control rod drive system, steam generator system (SGS), and the primary sampling system (via the CVCS). The RCS piping external to the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) comprises two pressurizer (PZR) spray line branches from a common spray header, an RPV high point degasification line, an RCS injection line, and an RCS discharge line. The RCS injection line includes branch lines connecting to each ECCS valve reset valve. The reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) encompasses the RCS injection and discharge piping interfacing with the CVCS up to the outermost containment isolation valve (CIV) on the top of each nuclear power module (NPM). This piping, including associated isolation valves, is designed as ASME BPVC Section III, Class 1, while the piping from the outermost CIVs to the NPM flange connections is not part of the RCPB and is designed as Class 3. Pressure-retaining piping that penetrates the RCS and forms part of the RCPB includes the PZR spray supply, RCS injection, RCS discharge, and RPV high-point degasification piping, as defined in 10 CFR 50.2. Figures 5.1-2 and 6.6-1 illustrate the RCS piping configuration, including the location and number of RSVs and RVVs relative to the RPV and the routing from the CNV upper head to RPV penetrations. Design, fabrication, and operational provisions to mitigate stress-corrosion cracking are detailed in Sections 3.9, 3.12, and 5.2. The RCS piping supports CVCS functions (see Section 9.3.4), and preservice and inservice inspection (ISI) requirements for ASME Class 1 components, including RCS piping, are summarized in Section 5.2.

The RCS piping does not terminate inside the CNV.The portions of the RCS that form the RCPB include the RPV shell, integral pressurizer (PZR) 
volume, RPV penetrations, several CNV nozzles and their safe ends, tubes of the steam 
generators (SGs), integral steam plenum, integral steam plenum caps and feed plenum access 
ports, and pressure-retaining portions of the RSVs.

The RCS interfacing systems include the chemical and volume control system via the 
containment system, the emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves consisting of RVVs 
and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), the CNV, control rod drive system, SGS, and the 
primary sampling system (through the chemical and volume control system).

provisions to control those factors that contribute to stress-corrosion cracking.

The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) is designed for natural circulation of the primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps or external piping. The RCS serves as the core neutron moderator and includes reflector blocks to improve neutron economy. It features a degasification line to remove noncondensible gases from the pressurizer (PZR) volume during normal operation. The reactor vessel internals (RVI) support and align the core, control rod assemblies, drive shafts, surveillance capsules, and instrument guide tubes. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core region—89 ft³, steam generator (SG) region—621 ft³, and PZR region—578 ft³ (with the cylindrical portion at 487 ft³). Each integrated NuScale Power Module (NPM) contains a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) housing the reactor, RVI, PZR, two SGs, reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, RCS injection and discharge lines, pressurizer spray and high-point degasification vent lines, and a decay heat removal system (DHRS). The system is depicted in Figure 5.1-2, showing key components and flow paths.

The RCPB include the RCS injection and discharge piping that interfaces with the CVCS up to 
the outermost CIV installed on the top of each NPM.A summary discussion of containment 
isolation system is provided in Section 6.2.4.

5.3.3.1 
Design

Section 5.3.1.The basic design of the RPV establishes compatibility with required 
inspections as described in Section 5.2.4 and Section 5.3.1.

The reactor vessel is shown in Figure 5.3-1 and design parameters are presented in Table 5.3-1.5.3.1 
Reactor Vessel Materials
5.3.1.1 
Material Specifications 
The materials and applicable specifications used in the RPV and appurtenances are 
shown in Table 5.2-4.The RPV materials are selected and fabricated to maintain RCPB integrity for the plant 
design lifetime.

The RPV upper section is cylindrical, approximately 9 ft in diameter with slightly 
thicker sections at the feedwater inlet and steam outlet areas.The upper section 
includes penetrations ranging from 2.25” to 25” diameter for main steam piping 
nozzles and main steam access ports, pressurizer heaters, feedwater piping nozzles 
and feedwater access ports, reactor recirculation valves, CVCS, and pressure 
instrumentation.

The RPV lower section is cylindrical, approximately 9 ft in diameter and includes a 
torispherical lower head that is welded in place.There are no penetrations in the 
lower section of the RPV.A steel pressurizer baffle plate integral with the RPV provides a barrier between the 
saturated water in the pressurizer and the RCS.

The Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) is an approximately cylindrical steel vessel with an inside diameter of about 9 ft and an overall height of 58 ft, designed for an operating pressure of 1,850 psia. It consists of three main sections: the head, upper, and lower sections. The torispherical RPV head is welded to the top of the upper section, while the upper and lower sections are flanged together with bolts. The upper section is cylindrical, with thicker areas at the feedwater inlet and steam outlet, and contains penetrations ranging from 2.25” to 25” in diameter for main steam piping nozzles and access ports, pressurizer heaters, feedwater piping and access ports, reactor recirculation valves, Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS), and pressure instrumentation. The lower section is also cylindrical with a welded torispherical lower head and contains no penetrations. An integral steel pressurizer baffle plate separates the saturated water in the pressurizer from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS). The RPV provides steam and feedwater plenums for the steam generators and RSV penetrations, and supports or attaches to the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs), CRDM seismic support structure, pressurizer heater bundles, in-core instrumentation, steam generator system piping, RCS piping, reactor safety valves (RSVs), reactor vent valves, and reactor recirculation valves. The RPV, including the steam generators, is certified and stamped per Article NCA-8000 of ASME BPVC, Section III. Pressure-temperature curves for the RPV comply with 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, ensuring operation below the pressure-temperature limits to prevent non-ductile failure. Two pressurizer heater bundles, each with a nominal capacity of 400 kW, are mounted through the RPV side, 180 degrees apart around the integral steam plenum assembly. The pressurizer baffle plate and its support structure are subject to BPVC and ANSI/ASME NQA-1 requirements, with inspections and testing conducted per BPVC standards. Material specifications for the RPV upper shell and head, as well as details on pressurizer spray nozzles and safe ends, are provided in referenced sections.

5.2.2.3 
Piping and Instrumentation Diagram
Figure 5.1-2 provides the RCS piping and instrument diagram and illustrates the design 
configuration of the RSVs and RVVs showing the number and location with respect to 
the RPV.

The RPV is fabricated in accordance with the requirements of ASME BPVC Section III, 
NB-4000, except for the SG tube supports (shown in Figure 5.4-6), which are fabricated 
in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, NG-4000, and the RPV supports and CRDM 
seismic support structure (shown in Figure 5.3-1), which are fabricated in accordance 
with ASME BPVC Section III, NF-4000.

The RPV consists of three sections: the RPV head section, the upper section, and the 
lower section.The RPV head is welded to the top of the upper section, and the 
upper and lower sections are flanged together using bolts.

Surfaces of the RPV are designed to be suitable for examinations and conform to the 
applicable requirements of ASME BPVC Sections III and XI.

Reactor Pressure Vessel
The RPV consists of an approximately cylindrical steel vessel with an inside 
diameter of approximately 9 ft and an overall height of approximately 58 ft that is 
designed for an operating pressure of approximately 1,850 psia.The upper and 
lower heads are torispherical, and the lower portion of the vessel has a flange to 
provide access for refueling.

In addition, the RPV also provides steam and feedwater plenums 
for the steam generators and RSV penetrations.The RPV and appurtenances are further described in Section 5.3.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements, design parameters, material composition, and major components of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Table 5.2-6 specifies that RPV shell and head welds, including connections such as the lower RPV flange shell to RPV bottom head and steam plenum cap to integral steam plenum, are subject to B-A category volumetric examination, while internal welds like the core support block to RPV bottom head undergo B-N-2 category VT-3 visual testing; instrumentation and controls sleeve welds are part of the cladding and not separately examined. Table 5.3-1 lists key RPV parameters: design pressure is 2100 psia, design temperature is 650°F, overall height is 778 inches, and diameters range from 96.5 to 115.5 inches depending on section, with inner and outer clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches, respectively. Table 5.3-2 provides maximum allowable concentrations for RPV beltline forging material (e.g., sulfur 0.010 wt%, phosphorus 0.010 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%) and as-deposited weld metal (e.g., sulfur 0.015 wt%, phosphorus 0.012 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, manganese 1.80 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%). Figure 5.3-1 identifies major RPV components, including the lower and upper RPV sections, RPV head section, CRDM latch housing section with 16 latch housings, RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum with 4 access ports, upper and lower RPV SG shells, feed plenum access ports, lower RPV section flange shell, RPV bottom head, upper RPV flanged transition shell, CRDM seismic support structure, and 4 instrument seal assemblies.

The RPV is designed against non-ductile fracture in accordance with ASME BPVC, 
Section III, Appendix G, ASME BPVC, Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50, 
Appendix G.The pressure-temperature limits are developed in accordance with ASME BPVC, 
Section XI, Appendix G, 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, and RG 1.99, Revision 2.

In addition, performance analysis indicates that the DHRS is capable of cooling the

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) thermal-hydraulic performance analysis assessed the orifice loss coefficient under various initial conditions to confirm that the restriction orifice size ensures consistent heat transfer. Accumulation of noncondensible gas in the DHRS steam lines was identified as a factor that degrades system performance. As a two-phase natural circulation system, DHRS performance depends on factors such as Reactor Coolant System (RCS) temperature, with higher RCS temperatures increasing the driving temperature difference and thus DHRS heat transfer. The analysis, performed using NRELAP5, included three model categories: a Steady State Model (with constant primary side temperature, flow rate, reactor pool temperature, and DHRS inventory), Nominal Transient Models (to demonstrate DHRS cooling for typical initiating events), and Off-Normal Transient Models (to show adequate cooling under the most limiting conditions). The steady state model calculated the DHRS heat transfer rate and evaluated the loss coefficient in the DHRS steam line to ensure acceptable performance across a range of DHRS inventories, including scenarios with additional steam volume due to MSIV failure, DHRS and SG fouling, SG tube plugging, and the presence of noncondensible gas. The limiting mass of dissolved gas that could be released during DHRS operation was also considered. Nominal transient models analyzed DHRS cooldown capability for cases with and without containment isolation, affecting the timing of pressurizer heater isolation. Off-normal transient models assessed DHRS performance under limiting conditions, including heat transfer surface fouling, plugged SG tubes, reactor pool water temperature, and noncondensible gas presence, with conservative biasing of valve timings to simulate worst-case DHRS inventory conditions. The overall analysis confirmed the DHRS capability to remove heat over a range of loop inventories.

The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) is a simple system whose operation is not credited during or after an accident. During normal operation, the CVCS recirculates a portion of reactor coolant through demineralizers and filters to maintain coolant cleanliness and chemistry, and supplies pressurizer spray for reactor pressure control. Injection pumps deliver borated or demineralized water into the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), with excess coolant let down to the radioactive waste system. Safety-related protection is provided against unintended RCS dilution due to CVCS equipment failure or operator error. The CVCS is normally connected to the RCS but is isolated following containment isolation actuation. During RCS pressure transients not resulting in isolation, overpressure protection is provided by the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) integral pressurizer (PZR), CVCS design, and relief valves. The CVCS enables chemical addition and removal of chemicals, suspended solids, and impurities via purification systems, and reduces concentrations through dilution with injection flow. It connects to the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) via normally open containment isolation valves (CIVs), with intersystem leakage considered for the boron addition, demineralized water, reactor component cooling water (RCCWS), and process sampling systems. The interface with the RCS is through the steam generators (SGs), which are subject to design, inspection, and testing controls, and primary-to-secondary leakage is monitored per technical specifications. Noncondensible gases collected in degasifiers are processed in the gaseous radioactive waste system. Further design details are provided in Sections 9.3.4, 11.2, and 11.3.

The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) for the PWR, including the Boron Recovery System, partially conforms to DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0 requirements regarding functional performance during adverse environmental phenomena, pumping capacity, and defense-in-depth RCS makeup, as its only safety-related function is to preclude inadvertent boron dilution of the reactor coolant system. The system conforms to single failure criteria and GDC 5, which apply solely to the two safety-related demineralized water isolation valves designed to prevent inadvertent boron dilution. The CVCS also conforms to requirements for minimization of contamination, system design and arrangement, detection of reactor coolant leakage outside containment, and ITAAC, with no additional comments. However, it only partially conforms to criteria for prevention of CVCS holdup tank wall buckling/failure and CVCS venting and draining, as some acceptance criteria reference CVCS designs not applicable to the NuScale design.

The CVCS is connected to the RCPB via normally open CIVs.Intersystem leakage from

The reactor coolant system (RCS) is a subsystem of the Nuclear Power Module (NPM) located within the containment vessel (CNV), comprising the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) with integral pressurizer (PZR), reactor vessel internals (RVI), reactor safety valves (RSVs), RCS piping (injection, discharge, PZR spray supply, high-point degasification), PZR control cabinet, and associated instruments and cables. The RCS transports heat from the reactor core to two once-through, helical-coil steam generators (SGs) by natural circulation, with SGs positioned in the annular space between the hot leg riser and RPV inner wall. The RCS interfaces with the chemical and volume control system (CVCS), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) via reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), CNV, control rod drive system, steam generator system (SGS), and primary sampling system. The RCS piping external to the RPV includes two PZR spray line branches, an RPV high-point degasification line, an RCS injection line (with ECCS branch connections), and an RCS discharge line, all up to the outermost containment isolation valves (CIVs), forming part of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) and designed to ASME BPVC Section III, Class 1. The RCS injection line allows coolant injection above the core and includes ECCS connections, while the discharge line draws suction from the annular region between the RPV wall and riser. RVVs are opened during ECCS operation to discharge PZR steam space to the CNV, removing noncondensible gases and providing overpressure protection at low temperatures; ECCS valves also enable recirculation of water from the CNV to the RPV during emergencies. The CVCS, a nonsafety-related system, maintains coolant chemistry and cleanliness, supplies pressurizer spray, injects borated or demineralized water, and removes excess coolant to the radioactive waste system, with safety-related protection against unintended RCS dilution. The CVCS is normally connected to the RCS but is isolated after containment isolation actuation, and interfaces with the RCPB via normally open CIVs. Reactor coolant inventory is managed by water injection or letdown, with chemistry controlled to minimize deposits, maintain boron concentration, and ensure proper pH using lithium hydroxide (enriched with lithium-7) and boric acid. Hydrogen is injected to suppress oxygen from radiolysis, and hydrazine is used during startup for oxygen scavenging. The RCCWS, reactor pool cooling, and spent fuel pool cooling systems are nonsafety-related, closed-loop systems transferring heat to the site cooling water system, while the circulating water and site cooling water systems are open-loop, supplying cooling to various plant systems. Reactor coolant inventory is managed by injecting additional water when coolant levels are low and letting down excess coolant to the liquid radioactive waste system when inventory is high. The reactor coolant system (RCS) water chemistry is controlled to minimize solid deposits on the reactor core and steam generators, maintain uniform soluble boron concentration for reactivity control during all operating conditions, and ensure adequate chemical and thermal mixing. Steady-state temperatures are maintained to keep the reactor vessel internals (RVI) and reactor pressure vessel (RPV) below design limits. Boric acid is added as a soluble neutron poison and adjusted as needed to compensate for changes in fuel reactivity over the fuel's lifetime. Lithium hydroxide enriched with lithium-7 is introduced via the chemical and volume control system (CVCS) charging flow, and excess lithium is removed using the CVCS delithiating ion exchanger to maintain required pH levels. Lithium hydroxide is selected for its compatibility with boric acid, stainless steel, zirconium alloys, and nickel-base alloys. Hydrogen is injected directly into the CVCS charging flow to suppress oxygen generated by water radiolysis and introduced with makeup water, while hydrazine is added during startup to scavenge dissolved oxygen at low temperatures. Chemical and makeup water quality are controlled to limit contamination, and oxygen, chlorides, and fluorides are controlled to minimize stress corrosion cracking of unstabilized austenitic stainless steels. Hydrogen use inhibits oxygen presence during operation, and gaseous argon may be added at the hydrogen injection tee to support primary-to-secondary leakage controls. The effectiveness of these controls is supported by test and operational experience, and precautions are taken to prevent contaminant intrusion during fabrication, shipping, and storage.

Boric acid concentrations for the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design were selected to bound both refueling and operating boron concentrations, with tests conducted across the full range of reactor operating pressures and temperatures; this test program was inspected by the NRC per Inspection Procedures IP35034.1.5.2, and further details on startup testing are provided in Section 14.2. The NuScale design does not utilize combustible gas control systems and supports an exemption to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), as detailed in DCA Part 7, section 2. Additionally, the design supports an exemption from 50.34(f)(2)(xiv) and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, regarding containment isolation valve closure times, with no deviations noted. Hydrogen control systems within containment are not required, as combustion does not impact containment vessel (CNV) integrity, further supporting the exemption to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2). The design also supports an exemption to GDC 27, complying instead with a NuScale-specific principal design criterion as described in Section 3.1.4. For post-accident assessment, the NuScale design relies on radiation monitors under the bioshield and core exit temperature indications, supporting an exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding the requirement to obtain and analyze post-accident samples of the reactor coolant system and containment without exceeding prescribed radiation dose limits.

For NuScale reactor events involving uncontrolled control rod assembly withdrawal from subcritical or low power startup conditions (SRP 15.4.1), at power (SRP 15.4.2), and control rod misoperation (SRP 15.4.3), critical heat flux (CHF) is used instead of departure from nucleate boiling ratio (DNBR) to assess thermal margin limits. NuScale applies a heat generation rate limit to ensure fuel centerline melting limits are not exceeded during slower reactivity insertions. The uniform cladding strain criterion, specified in the SRP for BWRs, does not apply to NuScale; instead, the 95/95 minimum critical heat flux ratio (MCHFR) approach is used to prevent cladding or fuel failures. All relevant criteria for thermal margin limits and fuel centerline temperatures are met, with no additional requirements for uniform cladding strain under control rod misoperation scenarios. For Anticipated Operational Occurrences (AOOs), guidance is provided in DSRS 15.4.6, which addresses inadvertent decreases in boron concentration in the Reactor Coolant System for PWRs. Review of SRP 15.4.6, Rev 2, confirms conformance with criteria for Reactor Coolant and Main Steam System Pressures (II.1), Fuel Cladding Integrity (II.2)—with CHF as the appropriate term for NuScale—Incidents of Moderate Frequency (II.3), and Analysis Model, Methods, and Assumptions (II.5). The criterion for Minimum Time Intervals for Required Operator Actions (II.4) is not applicable, as operator action is not required to mitigate inadvertent boron dilution events.

Fluence and pressure-temperature limits are further addressed in TR-0116-20781-P and TR-1015-18177-P. The design does not require certain systems post-accident and does not credit them in radiological consequence analyses, fully applying the single-failure criterion. NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, justifying departures in a topical report, and applies a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for Chapter 15 events, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models addressing only relevant features per Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30. Boric acid concentrations are selected to bound all operating conditions, with NRC-inspected testing per IP35034.1.5.2 and startup testing detailed in Section 14.2. Additional exemptions include 10 CFR 50.46a, 50.34(f)(2)(vi) (high point venting), and portions of 50.34(f)(2)(xx) (pressurizer level indicators). The NuScale design features a steel containment vessel without concrete or prestressed tendons, making regulatory guides and requirements related to inservice inspection, prestressing forces, and tendon grouting for prestressed concrete containments inapplicable. Guidance specific to concrete containments, such as SRP 1.136, SRP 3.8.1, and SRP 3.8.3, as well as regulatory positions C.1 and C.14 concerning internal concrete structures, do not apply since the NuScale containment lacks internal concrete or steel structures. The design references the 2006 version of ACI 349 for anchoring components and structural supports, partially conforming to guidance that endorses Appendix B of ACI 349-2001, and uses the 2012 version of ANSI/AISC N690 for barrier design procedures, rather than the referenced 1994 version with Supplement 2 (2004). The steel containment design fully conforms to DSRS 3.8.2, Rev. 0 requirements, including applicable codes, standards, loads, analysis procedures, acceptance criteria, materials, quality control, construction techniques, and inservice surveillance. The description of other Seismic Category I structures conforms to DSRS 3.8.4, Rev. 0. Site-specific evaluations for explosions near transportation routes are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. The NuScale design does not require operator action to mitigate debris accumulation and only partially complies with certain regulatory positions, such as C1.1 (except for the intent of C1.1.1.9 and C1.1.1.10), C.1.3 (intent only), and C.2 (intent of chemical reaction effects in 2.2), but does not comply with positions C.1.2, C.1.3.1, portions of C.1.3.2, C.1.3.7, C.1.3.9, C.1.3.12, or C.3. For conservatism, the design uses 0.10% Cu instead of the maximum 0.06 wt% Cu.

The NuScale Power Plant design certification process utilized 21 main reports, Supplements 1-34, and NRC letter SECY-07-0110 to identify applicable generic issues, with Table 1.9-5 summarizing relevant TMI requirements and NRC positions. Conformance with operational experience, including Generic Letters, Bulletins, and 10 CFR 21 notifications, is evaluated in Table 1.9-6, and NuScale’s QA supplier evaluation program reviews 10 CFR 21 notifications for all Nuclear Safety Related suppliers before approval. The NuScale design partially conforms to RG 1.189 (Fire Protection for Nuclear Power Plants), excluding requirements specific to non-PWR designs or site-specific elements, which are the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. Guidance for near-surface disposal of radioactive waste (Revision 5, 4.18) is not applicable to the NuScale design. Entry/exit control for protected, vital, and material access areas (Revision 5, 5.7) partially conforms, with site-specific and programmatic aspects assigned to the COL applicant or licensee. The standard plant design includes provisions for an onsite technical support center and operational support center per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xxv), partially conforming to DSRS 9.5.2, Rev 0. For TMI requirements under 10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(i), NuScale partially conforms by addressing reliability of core and containment heat removal systems in the design certification, with site-specific updates required from the COL applicant.

The NuScale design supports exemptions from several NRC regulatory requirements, including 10 CFR 50.46a and 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(vi) related to reactor coolant system high point venting, and portions of 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) concerning pressurizer level indicators. The NuScale Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) does not include holdup tanks subject to vacuum conditions as described in Bulletin 80-05, but will provide appropriate venting and draining capability as required by the last sentence of the relevant acceptance criterion. The design's features eliminate the need for high-point vents, and the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) high-point degasification vent does not perform a specific safety function.

results.question: How is the NuScale seismic design described in Ch.01, and what implications does this have for RCS components per Ch.05?
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes the NuScale plant as designed for seismic events with modules submerged in a below-grade reactor pool for added protection. Ch.05 notes that RCS pressure boundary components are designed to Seismic Category I requirements per ASME Section III, ensuring structural integrity during design basis earthquakes.
results.generated_answer: The NuScale seismic design in Ch.01 is described as conforming to regulatory requirements for seismic analysis and design bases, applying to each NPM individually; this ensures that the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) and its major components in Ch.05 are designed and analyzed to meet these seismic performance and safety bases. (Source: Ch.01 overview and Ch.05 design basis passages)
retrieved_contexts: The provided passages detail the NuScale nuclear power plant’s conformance with seismic design and analysis requirements as specified in various regulatory standards and guidance documents. Site-specific ratios of peak ground acceleration (A), velocity (V), and displacement (D)—specifically V/A and AD/V²—are confirmed to be consistent with the controlling seismic events for the site-specific uniform hazard response spectra. Seismic design parameters, including ground motion and damping values, fully conform to DSRS 3.7.1, 3.7.2, and 3.7.3, Rev. 0, with no exceptions. The seismic system and subsystem analyses address analytical modeling, soil-structure interaction, modal response combination, interaction of non-seismic and seismic Category I SSCs, overturning moments, sliding forces, and frictional forces, all in compliance with DSRS 3.7.2 and 3.7.3. The Certified Seismic Design Response Spectra (CSDRS), though not developed using RG 1.60, envelopes the RG 1.60 spectrum anchored to 0.1g and serves as the Safe-Shutdown Earthquake (SSE); the Operating-Basis Earthquake (OBE) is set at one-third of the SSE, decoupling OBE from safety system design evaluation. Seismic design classification generally follows RG 1.29 and SRP 3.2.1, with a noted deviation: SSCs under Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.i are classified as Seismic Category II instead of I. Structural analysis for Seismic Category I structures, including foundations, conforms to DSRS 3.8.4 and 3.8.5, Rev. 0, with no exceptions; masonry walls and concrete dams are not used in the design. Requirements for winter precipitation loads on roofs are met per DC/COL-ISG-7. Seismic instrumentation partially conforms to RG 1.12 and SRP 3.7.4, as detectors cannot be installed inside containment and are instead located in the Reactor Building, with full compliance deferred to the COL applicant. Regulatory Guide 1.93 and draft JLD-ISG-12-04 are not applicable. Overall, the NuScale design demonstrates comprehensive compliance with seismic regulatory requirements, with minor exceptions and COL items noted.

The seismic subsystem analysis for the NuScale design conforms to the requirements of DSRS 3.7.3, Rev. 0, across multiple technical areas. These include seismic analysis methods, determination of the number of earthquake cycles, procedures for analytical modeling, and the basis for selection of frequencies. The analysis also addresses the combination of modal responses, interaction of non-seismic Category I subsystems with seismic Category I SSCs, multiply-supported equipment and components with distinct inputs, and the use of equivalent vertical static factors. Additional considerations include torsional effects of eccentric masses and the seismic analysis of Category I buried piping, conduits, and tunnels. The methods for seismic analysis of above-ground tanks are also addressed and conform to the standard. However, methods for seismic analysis of Category I concrete dams are not applicable, as the NuScale design does not utilize dams.

The NuScale nuclear facility's seismic design conforms to RG 1.61, with an alternative damping value determined for the NPM substructure. Manual initiation of protective actions also conforms, with no additional requirements. The facility includes a metal-lined concrete dike around the PSCS storage tank, eliminating the need for further mitigative analysis. Guidance to operators at the controls and to senior operators in the control room partially conforms, as site-specific guidance is the responsibility of the COL applicant or licensee. The electrical system design meets the passive plant guidance of SECY-94-084, Section G. The facility conforms to positions on the use of generic bounding seismic hazard curves, seismic PRA, and leak-before-break concepts. Consideration of Severe Accident Mitigation Design Alternatives (SAMDAs) is included as part of the final design approval, and generic rulemaking related to design certification is noted as information only.

The NuScale design ensures that the nonsafety-related Secondary Component Water System (SCWS) is constructed to meet the provisions of Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.29, Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.i. For the Reactor Component Cooling Water (RCCW) system, which is not safety-related, NuScale applies RG 1.29 position C.2, ensuring that structures, systems, and components (SSCs) whose structural failure could impact the operability of safety-related SSCs are designed as Seismic Category II. No portion of the NuScale condensate storage system requires design and construction to withstand the safe-shutdown earthquake to prevent adverse effects on Seismic Category I SSCs, consistent with RG 1.29 C.1.i. The plant’s passive design and reduced reliance on AC power align with regulatory guidance for coping with design basis events. The NuScale main steam supply system conforms to RG 1.117, Appendix Positions 2 and 4, for tornado missile protection of important-to-safety SSCs. Unlike B&W reactor designs, which were sensitive to secondary system transients per NUREG-0933, the NuScale design does not exhibit such sensitivity.

The NuScale nuclear power plant design ensures protection of safety-related structures, systems, and components (SSCs) from internal flooding by siting the facility above the probable maximum flood height, including wind-induced wave run-up, making certain flood protection requirements inapplicable. The design conforms or partially conforms to NRC guidance (RG 1.102, SRP 3.4.2, 1.105), consistently assuming the plant is above the most severe flood and groundwater levels. Seismic design adheres to RG 1.61, with an alternative damping value for the NPM substructure, and includes a metal-lined concrete dike around the PSCS storage tank. Seismic parameters, including ground motion and damping, fully conform to DSRS 3.7.1, 3.7.2, and 3.7.3, with site-specific V/A and AD/V² ratios matching controlling seismic events. The Certified Seismic Design Response Spectra (CSDRS) envelopes RG 1.60 anchored to 0.1g and serves as the Safe-Shutdown Earthquake (SSE), with the Operating-Basis Earthquake (OBE) set at one-third SSE. Seismic system and subsystem analyses address analytical modeling, soil-structure interaction, modal response, and forces per DSRS 3.7.2 and 3.7.3. Seismic design classification generally follows RG 1.29 and SRP 3.2.1, except SSCs under Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.i are classified as Seismic Category II. Structural analysis for Seismic Category I structures, including foundations, conforms to DSRS 3.8.4 and 3.8.5; masonry walls and concrete dams are not used. Winter precipitation loads on roofs are addressed per DC/COL-ISG-7. Seismic instrumentation partially conforms to RG 1.12 and SRP 3.7.4, with detectors in the Reactor Building and full compliance deferred to the COL applicant. For turbine missile hazards, the design verifies turbine missile spectrum, rotor design, and building wall integrity, but since no safety-related SSCs are in the Turbine Building, reliance on missile generation probabilities or rotor integrity is not required if RG 1.115 barriers are used. Protective barriers are analyzed using finite element methods for concrete and per SRP 3.5.3 for steel, ensuring physical separation of redundant safety-related equipment and not relying on turbine overspeed protection, per GDC 4. Tornado and hurricane hazards are addressed using bounding Region 1 characteristics (RG 1.76, RG 1.221) with a maximum tornado wind speed of 230 mph, conforming to SRP 3.3.2, 3.5.1.4, and DC/COL-ISG-24, with site-specific confirmation left to the COL applicant. Specifically, NuScale does not use reactor coolant pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, or safety-related motor-operated valves, making guidance on flywheel integrity, motor qualification, and thermal overload protection irrelevant. The design also does not employ safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, or safety injection tanks, so requirements for ECCS accumulator lines and related motor-operated valves do not apply. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, and supporting documentation emphasize that many regulatory criteria and guidance documents—especially those tailored to Boiling Water Reactors (BWRs) and large Pressurized Water Reactors (PWRs)—are inapplicable to NuScale due to its unique, passive design. The plant eliminates forced reactor coolant flow, coolant loops, pumps, and several auxiliary systems, rendering guidance such as RG 1.79.1, BTP 3-2, SRP 4.6 II.8, BTP 10-1, SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5, and requirements for core spray and low pressure coolant injection systems (10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(viii)) non-applicable. The Strategic Water Chemistry Plan aligns with EPRI PWR Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines (e.g., EPRI #3002000505) and fuel vendor recommendations, specifying impurity limits (chloride, fluoride, sulfate ≤ 0.05 ppm; hydrogen 25–50 cc/kg; boron 0–2000 ppm), sampling frequencies, corrective actions, and chemistry parameters per RG 1.44, Rev 1. The plan ensures reactor pressure boundary and fuel integrity through alkaline-reducing chemistry, controlled pH, lithium, dissolved hydrogen, and zinc addition to mitigate PWSCC and radiation fields. Effluent release and coolant source term calculations follow NUREG-0017, PWR-GALE86, ANSI/ANS 18.1-1999, and NuScale technical reports, with decontamination factors and failed fuel fractions well below 0.25%. Partial conformance is noted with RG 1.110, RG 1.112, and 10 CFR 50.34(b)(3), 50.34a, and 52.79(a)(3), while DC/COL-ISG-5 and guidance for CAOC, SG blowdown, and once-through/top-feed/preheat steam generators (BTP 4-1, SRP 10.4.8, SRP 10.4.6, BTP 10-2) are not applicable; only OTSGD B.2 test acceptance criteria for once-through SGs are relevant. Flow Accelerated Corrosion is addressed per DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0. For LOCA and recirculation cooling (RG 1.82), NuScale meets the intent but differs in system design, supporting exemptions for 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) and portions of Appendix K, and partially conforming to radiological consequences of main steam line failures. results.question: What does Ch.01 state about the plant control room, and how does Ch.05 describe the interface with RCS instrumentation?
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes a single control room that monitors and controls all 12 modules with a modular control system. Ch.05 describes RCS instrumentation including temperature, pressure, and level measurements that feed to the control room for operator monitoring and automatic protection system actuation.
results.generated_answer:
Ch.01 states that the main control room (MCR) contains control panels for all installed NPMs, allowing operators to monitor and control multiple NPMs from dedicated panels, with displays designed using human factors analysis; Ch.05 describes the interface with RCS instrumentation as including indications of RCS pressure, spray flow into the pressurizer, and pressurizer heater output, all monitored and controlled via the module control system (MCS) in the control room.  
(Source: Ch.01, Section 1.2.2.2.1; Ch.05, Section 5.1.2 and related context)

It also is indicated in the control room to the operating staff to 
indicate RCS pressure and the MCS described in Chapter 7.Instrumentation to indicate the spray flow into the pressurizer by the CVCS, and 
pressurizer heaters output are also provided to the MCS.The MCS provides automated 
assistance to control level and pressure in the RCS.

The control room provides indications of Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure and the Monitoring and Control System (MCS) as described in Chapter 7. Instrumentation is available to display spray flow into the pressurizer via the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) and the output of pressurizer heaters, both monitored by the MCS, which also offers automated assistance for controlling RCS level and pressure. The CVCS supplies a small continuous flow to the pressurizer through spray nozzles to maintain pressurizer chemistry consistent with the RCS and to minimize thermal stresses during full spray flow initiation. Instrumentation in the CVCS pressurizer spray line provides control room indication of spray bypass flow. The CVCS is responsible for maintaining pressurizer level, adjusting RCS chemistry, and controlling boron concentration. During normal operations, pressurizer level is maintained between 50% and 60% of total capacity, with the programmed level increasing as reactor power increases.

The Security Buildings (SCBs) are nonsafety-related structures, including the primary access control building, main security building, and vehicle barrier system, that provide controlled access into the Site Outer Controlled Area (SOCA) and the Protected Area (PA) of the plant, controlling both personnel and vehicle entry and screening for unescorted access. The Central Utility Building (CUB) houses equipment for the chilled water system and other ancillary balance of plant systems. The Main Control Room (MCR) is supported by associated spaces such as a conference room for shift turnover, open office area (auxiliary operator room), two offices, storage room, janitor closet, three air locks, viewing area, shift manager’s office, reference room, emergency equipment room, lavatories, break room, and telecommunication room. The Technical Support Center (TSC) and its associated spaces—including records storage, three offices, two conference rooms, data equipment room, lavatories, data maintenance room, and break room—are located at grade level in the Control Room Building (CRB), which also contains the control room HVAC system (CRVS) equipment, chilled water system equipment supporting the CRVS, and an elevator machine room. The Access and Nonradiological Building (ANB) controls access to both radiologically-controlled and nonradiologically-controlled areas of the Reactor Building (RXB), and houses personnel support services (locker rooms, showers, toilets, lunch and conference rooms, first aid), space for decontamination equipment and dosimeter processing, and plant security support facilities (secondary alarm station, security briefing room, armory, security manager's office). If AC power is unavailable, TSC functions are transferred to the main control room.

The module control system (MCS) is a distributed control system enabling monitoring and control of module-specific plant components, while the plant control system provides nonsafety inputs to human system interfaces in the main control room (MCR), remote shutdown station, and other necessary locations. The fixed area radiation monitoring system continuously tracks in-plant radiation and airborne radioactivity levels. The safety display and indication system delivers visual displays and indications in the MCR from the module protection system (MPS) and plant protection system, with the latter monitoring and controlling systems common to all NuScale Power Modules (NPMs). The health physics network supplies the permanent communications infrastructure for the radiation protection program. The MCR contains control panels for all installed NPMs, allowing reactor operators to monitor and control multiple NPMs from dedicated panels, with the control room supervisor station providing an overview of all NPMs via multiple monitors. All displays are designed using human factors analysis and graphical representations to enhance simplicity. Typical MCR functions include initiating NPM startup and shutdown, adjusting set points, and taking corrective actions if systems do not operate as intended. Alarm annunciation is provided on the plant group-view overview display monitor as part of the alarm management system, integrating information from individual NPMs via the MPS, MCS, and shared I&C systems. A Technical Support Center (TSC) is provided in compliance with NUREG-0696. The reactor coolant system (RCS) is a subsystem of the NPM.

The systems and components connected to the RCS that are part of the RCPB are:
•
pressure housing of the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs) described in Section 4.6.•
RCS injection and discharge, PZR spray, and high point degasification piping up to the 
outermost CIVs that connect the NPM to the chemical and volume control system (CVCS), 
described in Section 5.4.2.

The RCPB include the RCS injection and discharge piping that interfaces with the CVCS up to 
the outermost CIV installed on the top of each NPM.A summary discussion of containment 
isolation system is provided in Section 6.2.4.

Preliminary critical heat flux (CHF) tests for the NuScale small modular reactor were conducted at Stern Laboratories in Ontario, Canada, using a high-pressure flow loop and custom 5x5 fuel assembly simulators with a 2-meter heated length, various axial power profiles, and configurations with and without simulated guide tubes. These tests examined the effects of shorter fuel length and low-flow natural circulation, generating data for the development and validation of the NuScale NSP2 CHF correlation. Additional CHF testing at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics (KATHY) facility in Germany, using prototypic fuel designs, provided further data to validate NSP2 and develop the NSP4 CHF correlation for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. CHF occurrence was identified by temperature excursions in the fuel simulators, and data collected included bundle subchannel exit temperatures for mixing coefficient determination and single-phase/two-phase pressure drop characteristics across various bundle powers and hydraulic conditions. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, prepared under 10 CFR 52.47(a), details the plant’s general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, including process effluent radiation monitoring (Section 11.5.1.2.2). The NuScale plant comprises 12 modules, each with a 160 MWth reactor and 50 MWe gross output, for a total net output of 570 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The core uses UO2 fuel enriched below 4.95% with a 24-month refueling interval. Safety features include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps. The NuScale reactor design incorporates several unique features and regulatory approaches, including a single-failure-proof crane that eliminates the need for load drop accident analyses for spent fuel casks or Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs). Containment atmosphere management minimizes oxygen during normal operations and uses continuous hydrogen and oxygen monitoring post-accident, meeting the intent of RG 1.7 without active combustible gas control systems; the design partially meets DSRS 6.2.5 for gas concentration analysis but fully meets requirements for equipment survivability, containment integrity, and atmosphere mixing, supporting exemptions from containment leakage rate testing (GDC 52, 10 CFR 50 Appendix J Type A). The Decay Heat Removal (DHR) System generally conforms to GDC 5 and related requirements, with exemptions for GDC 19 and 34, and supports NuScale-specific principal design criteria; for containment heat removal (DSRS 6.2.2), the design meets GDC 39 but departs from GDC 40, and for combustible gas control (DSRS 6.2.5), it supports an exemption from GDC 41 power provisions. Equipment survivability ensures radiation protection, and the design does not rely on prompt sampling for accident source term assessment (10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii)). The Control Rod Drive System (CRDS) conforms to GDCs 4, 23, 25, 28, and 29, clarifies GDC 26 does not require two safety-related reactivity controls, and departs from GDC 27 in favor of a design-specific PDC. The design does not use exclusion zone/buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability and is similar to large PWRs for effluent release calculations, making certain interim guidance inapplicable. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides a detailed comparison between the NuScale Power Plant and a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR) based on nominal values. The NuScale module (NPM) has a nominal gross electrical output of 50 MWe and a core thermal output of 160 MWt, compared to 1,186 MWe and 3,411 MWt for a typical PWR. NuScale uses 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, 6.56 ft effective length, 264 rods per assembly) versus 193 assemblies (17x17 lattice, 12 ft length) in a PWR. The average linear heat rate is 2.5 kW/ft for NuScale and 5.4 kW/ft for PWR. NuScale has 16 control rod assemblies and a 60-year design life, compared to 53 assemblies and 40 years for PWR. The NuScale reactor coolant system has no external loops or coolant pipes, operates at 1,850 psia, and uses a stacked stainless steel reflector block for thermal shielding, with top-mounted in-core instrumentation. Its steam generator is a helical coil type (2 units, ~18,000 ft² area, 1,380 tubes), while PWR uses four vertical U-tube generators (55,000 ft², 5,626 tubes). NuScale has no reactor coolant pumps, pressurizer surge nozzle, or residual heat removal pumps, and its containment is a steel pressure vessel (14 ft 2 in. diameter, 75 ft 8.5 in. height), compared to a PCCV (140 ft diameter, 205 ft height) for PWR. Many traditional PWR safety systems—such as containment spray, high/low pressure safety injection, accumulators, emergency diesel generators, and emergency feedwater pumps—are not present in NuScale. The NuScale I&C system is digital, and it uses 6 reactor component cooling water pumps for 12 NPMs. Table 1.3-2 shows that both designs include core protection systems such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment vessel, reactor coolant system, decay heat removal, emergency core cooling, control rod drive, containment isolation, and ultimate heat sink. However, several PWR safety systems (e.g., residual heat removal, safety injection, auxiliary feedwater, emergency service water, hydrogen recombiner, containment spray, reactor coolant pumps, safety-related electrical distribution, off-site power, emergency diesel generators, Class 1E battery, and ATWS system) are not required in the NuScale design.

The NuScale reactor design features an integral pressurizer located within the reactor pressure vessel, eliminating the need for a pressurizer surge line and thus avoiding issues of thermal stratification. Unlike conventional designs, NuScale does not use power-operated relief valves (PORVs), which are susceptible to inadvertent opening; instead, it employs spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves that do not share this vulnerability. The design does not rely on pressurizer heaters to establish or maintain natural circulation during hot shutdown conditions, making the requirements for pressurizer heater power supply and associated control interfaces in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xiii) inapplicable—NuScale considers its hot shutdown condition equivalent to the regulatory hot standby condition. Furthermore, NuScale does not include pressurizer relief or block valves, rendering the power supply requirements for these components in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) not technically relevant. For safe shutdown (Mode 3) with reactor coolant temperatures below 200°F, NuScale equates this state to cold shutdown as defined in OM Code ISTA-2000, and its DHRS actuation valves meet ASME OM Code exercising and observation requirements. Overall, these design features support exemptions from certain TMI-related regulatory requirements.

Figures 1.2-4 and 1.2-5 illustrate the layout of a multi-module NuScale plant and a cutaway of the 12-module configuration, respectively. Figure 1.2-6 offers a cutaway view of a NuScale Power Module, and Figure 1.2-7 depicts the steam generator and reactor flow. Table 1.6-2 lists referenced technical reports for NuScale, and Table 1.7-1 provides instrumentation and controls functional and electrical one-line diagrams. Additionally, NuScale Power, LLC’s report TR-0516-49417-P-A Revision 1 describes the evaluation methodology for stability analysis of the NuScale Power Module.
