Each SG tube is comprised of a helix with bends at each end that transition from the 
helix to a straight configuration at the entry to the tubes sheets as shown in 
Figure 5.4-1.The helical tubes are seamless with no intermediate welds.The helical 
tubes terminate at the feed and steam plenum tubesheets, where the tubes are 
secured to the tubesheet by expansion fit and are welded to the tubesheet on the 
secondary side.

The helical shape of the SG tubing itself does not represent risk of 
degradation based on the minimum bend radius of the helical tubing being within the 
experience base of operating PWR SG designs.The SG tube support design is novel.However, as discussed in Section 5.4.1.2, it preserves attributes of the existing tube 
support (plate) designs.

The feed and steam plena comprising a 
single SG are configured 180 degrees apart.As shown in Figure 5.4-1 and Figure 5.4-2, 
the configuration of the helical tube columns of the two SGs form an intertwined 
bundle of tubes around the upper riser assembly with a total of four feed and four 
steam plena located 90 degrees apart around the RPV.

Each steam generator (SG) tube consists of a seamless helical tube with bends at each end, transitioning to a straight section at the tube sheet entry. The tubes terminate at the feed and steam plenum tubesheets, where they are secured by expansion fit and welded on the secondary side. Instead of typical SG tube support plates, the design uses austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies that provide non-continuous, full-circumferential support, minimizing crevice formation and corrosion product buildup. Tube support assemblies are positioned between each column of helical tubes, with stamped tabs enveloping part of the tube circumference above and below to provide vertical support and minimize flow stagnation at the tube-to-support interface. Outer and middle spacers, welded into the tube supports, allow tabs from adjacent columns to nest, creating a continuous support path and optimizing circumferential spacing to minimize tube free span lengths while accommodating tube transitions to the plena. The tube support assemblies are attached to upper and lower SG supports welded to the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) inner surface, providing contact with each tube at eight circumferential locations. The use of eight sets of tube support assemblies limits unsupported tube lengths, ensuring high modal frequencies to prevent unacceptable flow-induced vibration. Lower SG supports permit thermal growth and provide lateral support. Tubes at the bundle inlet (cold leg) are evaluated to preclude vortex shedding, as they lack downstream obstructions, but the tortuous flow path within the bundle and the axial alignment of supports (angle of attack near 0.0, aspect ratio > 2.0) prevent stable vortex formation, galloping, and flutter. The upper SG supports are welded to the RPV and steam plenum and are excluded from analysis due to their rigidity, while the lower SG supports are welded to the RPV shell below the tube bundle.

The SG tube support assemblies span the full height of the helical tube bundle and are 
anchored at the top by the upper SG supports and at the bottom by connection to the 
lower SG supports.The confinement of the tube supports within the tube bundle, 
where the tortuous flow path creates continuous turbulence, ensures formation of 
stable vortices does not occur.

The 
design contains two independent, but intertwined, SGs located inside the RPV that 
facilitate heat transfer to the secondary coolant system and provide redundancy for the 
DHRS.The SGs are a once-through, helical-coil design, with primary-side reactor 
coolant outside the tubes and secondary-side fluid inside the tubes.On the primary 
side, the reactor coolant flows downward across the outside of the tubes, transferring 
heat to the fluid inside the SG tubes.

As shown on Figure 5.4-6, 
the tube support assemblies are attached to upper SG supports that are welded to the 
inner surface of the RPV and also interface with lower SG supports that are welded to 
the inner surface of the RPV.The SG tube support assemblies in the SG provides contact 
with each tube at eight separate circumferential locations.

Because these design aspects of the helical SGs are different from those used in 
the nuclear fleet, operational experience is not available and large-scale experimental 
data were needed for validation of NuScale thermal-hydraulic systems and design 
computer codes, as well as determination of SG performance characteristics.The objective of this testing was to determine the secondary side (inside tube) 
thermal-hydraulic performance of individual helical tubes representative of those used 
in the NPM steam generator design.

Likewise, the 
tube support structure is located within the primary coolant environment; therefore, 
no ingress path exists for general corrosion products from the secondary system to 
deposit on the shell side of the SG as may occur in traditional SG designs.

The axial alignment of the tube supports to the 
prevailing flow through the bundle ensures that the angle of attack is close to 0.0 and 
the aspect ratio is > 2.0, thus precluding galloping and flutter.The upper SG supports are welded to the RPV and the integral steam plenum, and 
excluded from analysis due to their rigidity.The lower SG supports are welded to the 
RPV shell below the SG tube bundle.

Because the SG tube design does not contain U-bends, a 
volumetric examination is performed on the entire length of the SG tubing as specified 
in Item B16.10 of Table IWB-2500-1 (B-Q).Preservice examinations are performed in accordance with the BPVC, Section III, 
Paragraph NB-5280 and Section XI, Subarticle IWB-2200 using examination methods of

The use of 8 sets of tube 
support assemblies limits the unsupported tube lengths, which ensures SG tube modal 
frequencies are sufficiently high to preclude unacceptable flow-induced vibration.As shown in Figure 5.4-6, the lower SG supports permit thermal growth and provide 
lateral support of the tube supports.

Figure 5.4-3 shows the 
cross-sectional arrangement of the integral steam plena and feed plena, while 
Figure 5.4-4 and Figure 5.4-5 show individual cross-sectional views of an individual 
steam and feed plenum.The main steam supply nozzles and the feedwater supply 
nozzles are also part of the SGS.Each SG has a pair of feedwater and main steam supply 
nozzles.

With respect to the risk of 
introduction of foreign objects, the NPM is at no greater risk than existing designs, 
therefore no deviations from existing SG program guidelines are warranted.From the 
standpoint of SG tube design, the two significant differences between the NuScale SG 
design and existing designs is the helical shape of the SG tubing and the SG tube 
support structure.

Unlike current PWR 
designs, the reactor coolant flows around the outside of the SG tubes (primary side) 
and the feedwater and main steam flow through the inside of the tubes (secondary 
side).

Isolation or other loss-of-heat transfer capability by either of the two intertwined SGs 
does not introduce asymmetrical cooling in the reactor coolant vessel or system 
because the tube configuration of the remaining functional SG continues to provide 
symmetrical heat removal from the reactor coolant flowing in the downcomer of the 
reactor vessel.The primary coolant system operates at a higher pressure than the secondary system 
resulting in the SG tubes being in compression.

As it rises through the tubes, it 
undergoes a phase change and is heated above saturation temperature before exiting 
the SG tubes as superheated steam.The configuration keeps the steam-water interface 
fluid and the superheated steam at the top of the tubes separated from the subcooled 
liquid at their bottoms.This configuration minimizes the hydraulic instabilities that 
could introduce potential sources of water hammer.

On the secondary side, preheated feedwater 
enters the two feed plenums at the bottom of each SG, flows up the helical tubes 
where it is heated, boiled, and superheated, and exits the two steam plenums at theNuScale Final Safety Analysis Report

results.question: Using Table 1.3-2 and the text description of NuScale safety systems, compare the systems NuScale eliminates versus those retained.
results.expected_answer: Table 1.3-2 lists eliminated systems: RHR, SIS, RWST, AFW, EDG, and large containment spray. NuScale retains and simplifies: ECCS (passive valves instead of pumps), DHRS (passive condensers instead of AFW), and the containment (vacuum CNV instead of large dry with sprays). The passive approach eliminates more systems than it retains.
results.generated_answer: NuScale eliminates the Residual Heat Removal System, Safety Injection System, Refueling Water Storage Tank, Condensate Storage Tank, Auxiliary Feedwater System, Emergency Service Water System, Hydrogen Recombiner or Ignition System, Containment Spray System, Reactor Coolant Pumps, Safety-Related Electrical Distribution System, Alternative Off-Site Power, Emergency Diesel Generators, Safety-Related Class 1E Battery System, and ATWS System, while retaining the Reactor Pressure Vessel, Containment Vessel, Reactor Coolant System, Decay Heat Removal System, Emergency Core Cooling System, Control Rod Drive System, Containment Isolation System, and Ultimate Heat Sink (Table 1.3-2, NuScale FSAR Rev. 5, Tier 2, 1.3-3).

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, details the design’s validation, regulatory conformance, and tailored application of industry standards for safety-related systems. NuScale partially conforms to several regulatory criteria, including accident monitoring instrumentation (IEEE Std 497-2002), use of computers in safety systems (RG 1.152), and software lifecycle processes (IEEE 1074-2006), adapting requirements to its I&C development lifecycle, which differs from the traditional waterfall model. Applicable tasks from IEEE standards such as 1012-2004, 1028-2008, 828-2005, 829-2008, 1008-1987, 830-1998, and 1074-2006 are mapped to NuScale’s processes, with exceptions to certain administrative requirements conflicting with established documentation practices. The design fully conforms to aircraft threat response strategies and environmental qualification for safety-related computer-based I&C systems in mild environments (RG 1.209), and addresses environmental effects on important-to-safety equipment. For electromagnetic and radio-frequency interference (RG 1.180) and environmental qualification (DSRS 3.11, RG 1.89), NuScale partially conforms, with some requirements designated for COL applicants. The Instrument Air Supply (IAS) system meets ANS/ISA S7.3-R1981 and Generic Letter 88-14, while setpoint methodology conforms to ISA-67.04.01-2006, addressing NRC RIS 2006-17. Safety classification of SSCs integrates deterministic and probabilistic methods, combining traditional definitions (10 CFR 50.2, RG 1.26, SRP 3.2.2) with an alternative risk-informed framework (10 CFR 50.69, RG 1.201, NEI 00-04), consistent with SECY-03-0047 and SECY-10-0034. The potable and sanitary water systems are designed to prevent flooding or adverse impacts on essential SSCs. NuScale’s electrical system partially conforms to NRC Generic Letter 88-15 and addresses SECY-91-078 by providing alternate power sources and direct offsite circuit connections to redundant safety divisions. The Fire Protection System contains no RTNSS equipment, and RTNSS guidance is partially incorporated into current regulations. Qualification of safety-related motor control centers and development of a risk-informed, performance-based inservice testing program (SRP 3.9.7) are not applicable, as these are either not used or are the responsibility of COL applicants.

The NuScale reactor design incorporates several unique features and regulatory approaches, including a single-failure-proof crane that eliminates the need for load drop accident analyses for spent fuel casks or Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs). Containment atmosphere management minimizes oxygen during normal operations and uses continuous hydrogen and oxygen monitoring post-accident, meeting the intent of RG 1.7 without active combustible gas control systems; the design partially meets DSRS 6.2.5 for gas concentration analysis but fully meets requirements for equipment survivability, containment integrity, and atmosphere mixing, supporting exemptions from containment leakage rate testing (GDC 52, 10 CFR 50 Appendix J Type A). The Decay Heat Removal (DHR) System generally conforms to GDC 5 and related requirements, with exemptions for GDC 19 and 34, and supports NuScale-specific principal design criteria; for containment heat removal (DSRS 6.2.2), the design meets GDC 39 but departs from GDC 40, and for combustible gas control (DSRS 6.2.5), it supports an exemption from GDC 41 power provisions. Equipment survivability ensures radiation protection, and the design does not rely on prompt sampling for accident source term assessment (10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii)). The Control Rod Drive System (CRDS) conforms to GDCs 4, 23, 25, 28, and 29, clarifies GDC 26 does not require two safety-related reactivity controls, and departs from GDC 27 in favor of a design-specific PDC. Testing confirmed CRA drop time and impact force, with partial conformance to RG 1.77, SRP 6.5.3, BTP 6-1, SRP 9.2.4, and SRP 9.3.2, and full conformance to SRP 4.2, 9.2.6, 15.4.2, and 15.4.7; the condensate storage system is non-safety-related, and potable/sanitary water systems do not interface with radioactive systems. The FSAR documents non-applicability or non-conformance with several regulatory positions (e.g., C1.1.3, C1.1.4, C1.3.12, C.2 except 2.2, C.3), partial conformance to GDC 54 for DHRS, and exemptions from GDC 38, 42, and 43 due to design features. The design does not use exclusion zone/buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability and is similar to large PWRs for effluent release calculations, making certain interim guidance inapplicable. Containment purge systems vent directly to the environment, and SFP mitigating measures are not required due to design features (small containment, below-grade SFP). The Ultimate

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents several departures and conformances with regulatory criteria. Positions C1.1.3 and C1.1.4 are not applicable because the NuScale design does not rely on operator action or active devices to mitigate or prevent debris accumulation. The design does not comply with regulatory position C1.3.12 (prototypical head loss testing), nor with position C.2 except for meeting the intent of chemical reaction effects (position 2.2), and does not comply with position C.3. For DSRS 5.4.7 (Decay Heat Removal System), the design partially conforms to GDC 54, featuring a closed-loop DHRS outside containment directly connected to the closed-loop SG system within the RPV, providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and the reactor pool outside the NPM. Regarding DSRS 6.2.2 (Containment Heat Removal Systems), the design supports an exemption from the power provisions of GDC 38. For DSRS 6.2.5 (Combustible Gas Control in Containment), GDC 42 and 43 are not applicable as the design lacks a containment atmospheric cleanup system. In DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, the design conforms to fuel cladding integrity requirements and does not include a steam pressure regulator. The exclusion zone and buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability review (DSRS 15.9.A) is not used in the NuScale design. Finally, for DC/COL-ISG-5, the NuScale design is similar to large PWRs in the existing fleet regarding effluent release calculations, rendering the specific interim staff guidance not applicable.

retrieved_contexts: The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report describes integrated overpressure protection for secondary side components with the same design pressure as the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), ensuring ASME BPVC service limits are not exceeded during normal operations and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs). The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system is designed with sufficient capacity to prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding the pressure-temperature (P-T) limits specified in 10 CFR 50 Appendix G when below the LTOP enable temperature, thereby maintaining reactor pressure vessel (RPV) pressure below brittle fracture limits during all operational, maintenance, testing, or postulated accident conditions. During normal operational transients at full power, the system and core parameters are managed to prevent safety valve lift; if a safety valve does lift, the pressurizer (PZR) steam space is sized to avoid liquid discharge. LTOP analysis assumes a maximum PZR heater output of 880 kW and an additional 2 MW from core decay heat, demonstrating that reactor vent valves (RVVs) open before reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure exceeds the low temperature pressure limit. Table 5.2-10 provides LTOP pressure setpoints as a function of cold temperature, with PZR pressure limits ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, and LTOP not enabled above 318°F. Figure 5.2-4 illustrates the variable LTOP setpoint (PZR pressure) as a function of temperature, in relation to the saturation curve.

The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system in the NuScale Power Module (NPM) safeguards the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) during low temperature conditions by using three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) and two pilot-operated Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) mounted on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) upper head, all constructed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A standards. The RSVs, each with a 3-inch inlet, 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, and design temperature of 650°F, are sized to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the 2310 psia design pressure, with a minimum relief capacity of 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam at 3% overpressure. Nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% and 2100 psia ±1%, with a blowdown of 10% ±2%. LTOP is enabled when RCS cold temperature is ≤318°F, with pressure setpoints ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, determined by linear interpolation based on downcomer temperature at the steam generator outlet. The system is actuated by module protection system logic to open RVVs and limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, accounting for worst-case transients such as spurious pressurizer heater actuation (up to 880 kW) and 2 MW core decay heat. The setpoints include margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences, temperature variations, RVV opening delay, sensor response, and processing time. Three RVVs ensure LTOP protection even with a single active component failure, and overpressure protection for secondary side components is integrated to maintain ASME BPVC service limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs), with the most severe AOO being a turbine trip at full power without bypass. RVVs and RSVs are environmentally and functionally qualified per ASME QME-1 and RG 1.100, with direct position indication in the control room per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi). Pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, surveillance capsule locations, and test schedules are detailed in referenced tables and figures, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, 10 CFR 50.61, and GDC 15 for a 60-year plant life. Additional details on valve qualification, design, operation, and testing are provided in Sections 6.3, 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6.

The NuScale reactor design features an LTOP (Low Temperature Overpressure Protection) system that is not connected to a low-pressure system, but the guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation from the primary system remains applicable. NuScale supports an exemption from selected portions of 10 CFR 50, Appendix K, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, regarding Mass and Energy Release Analysis for Postulated Loss-of-Coolant Accidents (LOCAs), with no additional inspections, tests, analyses, or acceptance criteria (ITAAC) required. In the event of a LOCA, the NuScale design ensures that core uncovery does not occur, and it conforms to DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, and GDC 5 regarding the sharing of structures, systems, and components, with no exceptions. The design does not credit or utilize a suppression pool and conforms to DSRS 6.6, Rev 0, for inservice inspection and testing of Class 2 and 3 components, with all relevant components subject to inspection and no additional requirements.

The NuScale reactor design and safety analysis methodologies are detailed in topical reports TR-0516-49416 and TR-0516-49422, with pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 EFPY calculated per ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects using Regulatory Guide 1.99 and ASTM E185-82 for surveillance. Fluence and pressure-temperature limits are further addressed in TR-0116-20781-P and TR-1015-18177-P. The design does not require certain systems post-accident and does not credit them in radiological consequence analyses, fully applying the single-failure criterion. NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, justifying departures in a topical report, and applies a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for Chapter 15 events, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models addressing only relevant features per Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30. Boric acid concentrations are selected to bound all operating conditions, with NRC-inspected testing per IP35034.1.5.2 and startup testing detailed in Section 14.2. The design does not use combustible gas control systems, supporting exemptions to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), 50.34(f)(2)(xiv), and GDC 27, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, for containment isolation valve closure times. Hydrogen control is not required, as combustion does not affect CNV integrity. Post-accident assessment relies on radiation monitors and core exit temperature, supporting exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding post-accident sampling. Additional exemptions include 10 CFR 50.46a, 50.34(f)(2)(vi) (high point venting), and portions of 50.34(f)(2)(xx) (pressurizer level indicators). The CVCS does not include holdup tanks under vacuum but provides required venting and draining. The RPV high-point degasification vent is not safety-significant, and high-point vents are not needed. The LTOP system is not connected to a low-pressure system, but guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation applies. NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, for LOCA mass and energy release analysis, and requires no additional ITAAC. results.question: Compare the NuScale DHRS passive cooling mechanism with conventional auxiliary feedwater systems.
results.expected_answer: NuScale DHRS uses passive condensers submerged in the reactor pool, requiring no pumps, power, or operator action. Conventional AFW uses motor-driven and turbine-driven pumps with a separate water source. The DHRS eliminates multiple failure modes: pump failure, valve misposition, and loss of water supply.

The NuScale Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) serves a function similar to the Auxiliary Feedwater (AFW) system in large pressurized water reactors (PWRs), but operates via passive natural circulation without pumps. While the DHRS performs some AFW functions, it is specifically designed for NuScale reactor transients and system characteristics, with tailored actuation and indication features. Key monitored parameters include DHRS pressure, valve position indication, and reactor coolant system pressure and temperature. Regulatory requirements such as 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xii) and 10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(ii) are not considered applicable to the NuScale DHRS due to differences in design intent and system operation.

The NuScale Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) is a passive system that supplies clean, compressed, breathable air to the Main Control Room (MCR) during a radioactive release or loss of AC power, maintaining positive pressure relative to adjacent areas, with sufficient air storage for at least 72 hours post-initiating event. The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is a passive, safety-related system that provides secondary side reactor cooling for non-LOCA events when normal feedwater or secondary-side cooling is unavailable, using redundant, single active failure-proof trains and natural circulation without pumps. The DHRS is analogous to the Auxiliary Feedwater (AFW) system in large PWRs but is specifically designed for NuScale reactor transients, with tailored actuation and indication features such as DHRS pressure, valve position, and reactor coolant system (RCS) parameters. The DHRS interfaces with the secondary system via the steam generators (SGs) and does not directly connect to the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), making GDC 14 and certain regulatory requirements inapplicable. The system is actuated automatically by the Module Protection System (MPS), with manual initiation possible from the control room or MPS equipment rooms, but remote access is restricted for security. The DHRS can be actuated without reducing RCS pressure or temperature and is designed to prevent flow disruption from noncondensible gas accumulation, with analyses accounting for heat transfer surface fouling, SG tube plugging, and maximum gas accumulation at 60°F. The system is capable of maintaining core cooling for 72 hours during a station blackout, in compliance with 10 CFR 50.63, and can transition the NuScale Power Module (NPM) to safe shutdown without external power, maintaining fuel and RCPB design limits. The DHRS is designed with leakage detection and control to minimize radioactive contamination, which originates indirectly from the feedwater and main steam systems, and utilizes an all-welded design and facility programs to limit contamination and waste. In the event of extended DHRS cooldown with low boron concentration and unavailable CVCS, there is a potential for reestablishing critical neutron power if the most reactive control rod remains stuck out. The DHRS has no direct interface or shared components with the RCPB, and its operation is monitored via system parameters rather than direct flow measurement due to its passive natural circulation design.

The NuScale Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) is a passive system that supplies clean, compressed air to the Main Control Room (MCR) during radioactive releases or loss of AC power, maintaining positive pressure and providing at least 72 hours of breathable air. The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is a passive, safety-related system designed for secondary side reactor cooling during non-LOCA events when normal feedwater or secondary cooling is unavailable. The DHRS uses redundant, single active failure-proof trains and operates via natural circulation without pumps, analogous to the Auxiliary Feedwater (AFW) system in large PWRs but tailored for NuScale reactor transients. It interfaces with the secondary system through the steam generators (SGs) and does not connect directly to the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), making certain regulatory requirements inapplicable. DHRS actuation is automatic via the Module Protection System (MPS), with manual initiation possible from the control room or MPS equipment rooms, though remote access is restricted. The system can be actuated without reducing RCS pressure or temperature and is designed to prevent flow disruption from noncondensible gas accumulation, with analyses considering heat transfer surface fouling, SG tube plugging, and maximum gas accumulation at 60°F. The DHRS maintains core cooling for 72 hours during a station blackout, meeting 10 CFR 50.63, and can transition the NuScale Power Module (NPM) to safe shutdown without external power, maintaining fuel and RCPB design limits. Leakage detection and control minimize radioactive contamination, with an all-welded design and facility programs to limit contamination and waste. In extended cooldown scenarios with low boron and unavailable CVCS, there is a potential for reestablishing criticality if the most reactive control rod remains stuck out. The DHRS has no direct interface or shared components with the RCPB and is monitored via system parameters rather than direct flow measurement. Key DHRS actuation valves (DHR-HOV-0101A/B, DHR-HOV-0201A/B) are normally closed and fail open upon actuation or loss of control power, using nitrogen pressure for operation without electrical power. Redundant parallel valves per train ensure continued function in case of single valve failure. Main steam and feedwater isolation valves (MS-HOV-0101, MS-HOV-0201, FW-HOV-0137, FW-HOV-0237) are normally open and fail closed to establish a closed DHRS loop, with downstream valves or temporary inventory addition mitigating failure to close. These valves are located outside the NPM atop the containment vessel and actuated automatically via ESFAS or manually via MPS, though manual actuation is not credited for design basis events. DHRS actuation valves are designed to fully open or close within 30 seconds at ≤50 psid differential pressure. The DHRS operates by natural circulation, driven by density differences

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report describes the operation and design of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and its integration with the secondary system. During wet layup, feedwater is recirculated through the DHRS heat exchangers, and secondary water chemistry is controlled before the steam generators (SGs) are isolated from the main steam and feedwater systems. The DHRS connects to the secondary system without direct interface to the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), with SGs detailed in Section 5.4.1 and the containment system piping coupling the DHRS to the SGs described in Section 6.2.4. To ensure DHRS safety function, the accumulation of noncondensible gases is evaluated, assuming a gas temperature of 60°F based on a minimum pool temperature of 65°F. Analyses include the effects of heat transfer surface fouling, SG tube plugging, and noncondensible gas accumulation on DHRS performance across the DHRS loop inventory range. The report also calculates the maximum mass of noncondensible gas that may accumulate within the secondary system before, during, and after DHRS actuation.

The NuScale design employs passive natural circulation of the primary coolant, which eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps. This approach conforms to DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials, specifically regarding the selection, processing, testing, and inspection of materials, with no exceptions noted.

The NuScale plant design does not include an Auxiliary Feedwater (AFW) system typical of large Light Water Reactors (LWRs), but instead utilizes a Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) that serves a similar function. The NuScale steam generator design eliminates potential water hammer issues without requiring DHRS water to be supplied through an externally mounted top discharge header, as specified by certain acceptance criteria. For spent fuel pool (SFP) mitigation, NuScale is exempt from NEI 06-12 requirements because its SFP is located below grade and cannot be drained. The design does not use power-operated relief valves, and the Anticipated Transient Without Scram (ATWS) provisions are not technically relevant, as NuScale relies on protection system diversity to prevent ATWS rather than on relief and safety valves for mitigation. Consequently, the module response to ATWS is not analyzed in FSAR Section 15.8, and the performance of relief and safety valves is not relied upon to meet ATWS safety criteria. Section 19.2 of the FSAR provides further information on the NuScale Power Plant's response to anticipated transients without scram.

results.question: Compare the primary-to-secondary pressure relationship in NuScale vs conventional PWRs and its safety implications.
results.expected_answer: In both NuScale and conventional PWRs, primary pressure exceeds secondary pressure, so any SG tube leak results in primary-to-secondary leakage. However, NuScale's integral design means the SG is inside the RPV, so a tube leak does not result in RCS leakage outside containment. In conventional PWRs, a tube rupture releases radioactive primary coolant to the secondary side outside containment.

(Source: NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Table 1.3-2)

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides a detailed comparison between the NuScale Power Plant and a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR) based on nominal values. The NuScale module (NPM) has a nominal gross electrical output of 50 MWe and a core thermal output of 160 MWt, compared to 1,186 MWe and 3,411 MWt for a typical PWR. NuScale uses 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, 6.56 ft effective length, 264 rods per assembly) versus 193 assemblies (17x17 lattice, 12 ft length) in a PWR. The average linear heat rate is 2.5 kW/ft for NuScale and 5.4 kW/ft for PWR. NuScale has 16 control rod assemblies and a 60-year design life, compared to 53 assemblies and 40 years for PWR. The NuScale reactor coolant system has no external loops or coolant pipes, operates at 1,850 psia, and uses a stacked stainless steel reflector block for thermal shielding, with top-mounted in-core instrumentation. Its steam generator is a helical coil type (2 units, ~18,000 ft² area, 1,380 tubes), while PWR uses four vertical U-tube generators (55,000 ft², 5,626 tubes). NuScale has no reactor coolant pumps, pressurizer surge nozzle, or residual heat removal pumps, and its containment is a steel pressure vessel (14 ft 2 in. diameter, 75 ft 8.5 in. height), compared to a PCCV (140 ft diameter, 205 ft height) for PWR. Many traditional PWR safety systems—such as containment spray, high/low pressure safety injection, accumulators, emergency diesel generators, and emergency feedwater pumps—are not present in NuScale. The NuScale I&C system is digital, and it uses 6 reactor component cooling water pumps for 12 NPMs. Table 1.3-2 shows that both designs include core protection systems such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment vessel, reactor coolant system, decay heat removal, emergency core cooling, control rod drive, containment isolation, and ultimate heat sink. However, several PWR safety systems (e.g., residual heat removal, safety injection, auxiliary feedwater, emergency service water, hydrogen recombiner, containment spray, reactor coolant pumps, safety-related electrical distribution, off-site power, emergency diesel generators, Class 1E battery, and ATWS system) are not required in the NuScale design.

The NuScale reactor design and safety analysis methodologies are detailed in topical reports TR-0516-49416 and TR-0516-49422, with pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 EFPY calculated per ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects using Regulatory Guide 1.99 and ASTM E185-82 for surveillance. Fluence and pressure-temperature limits are further addressed in TR-0116-20781-P and TR-1015-18177-P. The design does not require certain systems post-accident and does not credit them in radiological consequence analyses, fully applying the single-failure criterion. NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, justifying departures in a topical report, and applies a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for Chapter 15 events, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models addressing only relevant features per Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30. Boric acid concentrations are selected to bound all operating conditions, with NRC-inspected testing per IP35034.1.5.2 and startup testing detailed in Section 14.2. The design does not use combustible gas control systems, supporting exemptions to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), 50.34(f)(2)(xiv), and GDC 27, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, for containment isolation valve closure times. Hydrogen control is not required, as combustion does not affect CNV integrity. Post-accident assessment relies on radiation monitors and core exit temperature, supporting exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding post-accident sampling. Additional exemptions include 10 CFR 50.46a, 50.34(f)(2)(vi) (high point venting), and portions of 50.34(f)(2)(xx) (pressurizer level indicators). The CVCS does not include holdup tanks under vacuum but provides required venting and draining. The RPV high-point degasification vent is not safety-significant, and high-point vents are not needed. The LTOP system is not connected to a low-pressure system, but guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation applies. NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, for LOCA mass and energy release analysis, and requires no additional ITAAC. The NuScale design was evaluated against DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0, which covers initiating events such as Loss of External Load, Turbine Trip, Loss of Condenser Vacuum, Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve (MSIV), and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed). For the basic objectives regarding initiating events (II.1), the design conforms fully. For specific criteria for events of moderate frequency (II.2), the design partially conforms because NuScale does not have a steam pressure regulator. The design conforms to criteria for Reactor Coolant System and Main Steam System Pressures (II.2.A), Incidents of Moderate Frequency (II.2.C), Instrument Setpoints and their impact on plant response (II.2.D), Most Limiting Plant System Single Failure (II.2.E), and Performance of Nonsafety-Related Systems and Single Failures of Active and Passive Systems (II.2.F). The analyzed Anticipated Operational Occurrences (AOOs) that could lead to Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) overpressure include loss of load, turbine trip (with and without bypass), loss of condenser vacuum, inadvertent MSIV closure, steam pressure regulator failure closed, loss of normal AC power, loss of feedwater, and inadvertent operation of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS). These AOOs are detailed in DCD Chapter 15, including initial conditions, system parameters, analysis assumptions, systems and equipment assumed to operate, reactor trip signals, and sensitivity analyses.

The NuScale plant design differs significantly from large LWRs and is not addressed by guidance or codes developed for traditional PWR reactors, such as the PWRGALE code from the 1980s. The NuScale design meets the intent of relevant regulatory guidance through its passive safety features and reduced reliance on AC power to manage design-basis events, including station blackout scenarios, where compressed air is not required for core cooling. For coolant source term evaluation, NuScale employs an alternate methodology, incorporating some aspects of ANSI/ANS 18.1, since existing codes and rules do not apply to its unique design.

The analytical model for the overpressure protection system analysis is detailed in NuScale Topical Reports TR-0516-49416, "Non-Loss-of-Coolant Accident Analysis Methodology," and TR-0516-49422, "Loss-of-Coolant Accident Evaluation Model" (Revision 2). Pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 effective full power years (EFPY) are calculated using the methodology from ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects due to neutron fluence per Regulatory Guide 1.99. Additional references include ASTM E185-82 for surveillance testing of light-water reactor vessels, NuScale's "Fluence Calculational Methodology and Results" (TR-0116-20781-P, Revision 1), and "Pressure and Temperature Limits Methodology" (TR-1015-18177-P, Revision 2).

results.question: Compare the NuScale RPV materials with those used in conventional PWR vessels.
results.expected_answer: NuScale uses SA-508 Grade 3 Class 1 forgings and SA-533 Type B plates for the RPV, similar to conventional PWRs. However, NuScale imposes stricter chemistry limits on beltline materials (Cu <= 0.06%, Ni <= 0.85%) to minimize irradiation embrittlement over the 60-year design life.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report demonstrates that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) values well above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb throughout the 60-year design life, with initial values of at least 75 ft-lb and predicted end-of-life values of 58.4 ft-lb (lower shell), 62.3 ft-lb (weld), and 62.9 ft-lb (bottom head) at 57 effective full-power years (EFPY). The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT for the limiting weld metal is 50°F at 0.55 EFPY, with 28°F due to a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening per 10 CFR 50.61 and RG 1.99 shows RTPTS values at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head), all below regulatory limits. Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The RPV is fabricated and inspected per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, with material specifications and chemical compositions detailed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.3-2. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, and clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. results.question: Compare the NuScale pressurizer design with a conventional separate pressurizer vessel.
results.expected_answer: NuScale's pressurizer is integral to the upper RPV head, sharing the same vessel as the core and SG. Conventional PWRs have a separate pressurizer vessel connected via a surge line. The integral design eliminates the surge line, associated piping, and potential surge line LOCA. Both use heaters and spray for pressure control.
results.generated_answer: The NuScale pressurizer is integral to the reactor pressure vessel, located above the core with no connecting piping or surge line, whereas a conventional pressurizer is a separate vessel connected by piping, which can experience thermal stratification and requires additional components like surge lines and relief valves. The NuScale reactor design features an integral pressurizer located within the reactor pressure vessel, eliminating the need for a pressurizer surge line and thus avoiding issues of thermal stratification. Unlike conventional designs, NuScale does not use power-operated relief valves (PORVs), which are susceptible to inadvertent opening; instead, it employs spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves that do not share this vulnerability. The design does not rely on pressurizer heaters to establish or maintain natural circulation during hot shutdown conditions, making the requirements for pressurizer heater power supply and associated control interfaces in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xiii) inapplicable—NuScale considers its hot shutdown condition equivalent to the regulatory hot standby condition. Furthermore, NuScale does not include pressurizer relief or block valves, rendering the power supply requirements for these components in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) not technically relevant. For safe shutdown (Mode 3) with reactor coolant temperatures below 200°F, NuScale equates this state to cold shutdown as defined in OM Code ISTA-2000, and its DHRS actuation valves meet ASME OM Code exercising and observation requirements. Overall, these design features support exemptions from certain TMI-related regulatory requirements.

The NuScale design incorporates several advanced safety and operational features, including safe shutdown and cooling without reliance on AC or DC power, compact helical coil steam generators with reactor pressure on the tube exteriors, and a high-strength steel containment vessel immersed in a water pool. The containment operates at sub-atmospheric pressure during normal conditions, and the reactor core is small, resulting in a reduced source term. Comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls (I&C) are used for monitoring and control. The plant design is scalable, supporting incremental capacity growth in multi-unit configurations. The passive NuScale system eliminates pumps, sumps, suction strainers, debris interceptors, and trash racks, thereby minimizing debris-related and non-condensable effects on core coolant flow, and complies with regulatory guidance on debris management and chemical effects. Due to the modular and compact nature of the NuScale Power Module, passive separation of redundant safety trains is not always feasible; instead, fire protection measures are implemented to ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational in the event of a fire. Unlike traditional large water reactors, the NuScale design integrates the pressurizer above the reactor core within the reactor vessel, with no connecting piping, ensuring that core uncovery and damage cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) design features an integral power module comprising a reactor core, two helical-coil steam generators (SGs), and a pressurizer, all contained within a single reactor pressure vessel (RPV). This RPV is immediately surrounded by a steel containment vessel (CNV) that is approximately cylindrical and sits in the reactor pool. The reactor core is positioned below the helical-coil SGs inside the RPV. The design eliminates the need for external piping between the steam generators, pressurizer, and RPV, and utilizes natural circulation for reactor coolant system flow, removing the requirement for reactor coolant pumps. For valve actuation testing, a pilot-operated main valve with trip line tubing and a trip valve representative of the NPM design was used to simulate performance and assess potential boric acid buildup. Pressurizer level instrumentation is provided to the control room and the Module Control System (MCS), supporting an exemption from certain power supply requirements per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx). Pressurizer pressure is measured and provides input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS).

In addition, the NuScale design does not include pressurizer 
relief valves or pressurizer block valves, and the power supply requirements for these 
valves included in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) are not technically relevant.

The NuScale design does not utilize safety-related pumps. In the event of reactor coolant system overpressure, fluid is relieved directly to the containment vessel via the overpressure protection system. Table 1.9-3 documents conformance with NUREG-0800, Standard Review Plan (SRP), and Design Specific Review Standard (DSRS) across multiple sections, including 14.2, 15.1.1–15.1.4, 15.1.5, 15.1.6, 15.2.8, 15.4.2, 15.4.6, 15.6.1, and 15.6.6. The design is noted as conforming to the applicable SRP and DSRS requirements, with no additional comments provided for these sections.

The NuScale plant design departs significantly from traditional large light water reactors (LWRs) and is not addressed by legacy regulatory guidance or codes such as PWRGALE. Its safety strategy relies on passive features and natural circulation, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps, safety-related pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, safety injection tanks, and safety-related motor-operated valves. Consequently, regulatory requirements and guidance related to these components—including flywheel integrity, motor qualification, thermal overload protection, ECCS accumulator lines, and gas accumulation in forced circulation systems—are not applicable. The design also omits an auxiliary feedwater (AFW) system, power-operated pressurizer relief valves, and pressurizer heaters, instead using a Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves, which are not susceptible to inadvertent opening. The integral pressurizer is located within the reactor vessel, with no surge line or connecting piping, eliminating thermal stratification and ensuring core uncovery cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint. The containment vessel is a high-strength steel structure immersed in a water pool, operating at sub-atmospheric pressure, and the compact core results in a reduced source term. The plant is scalable, supports multi-unit configurations, and uses comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls. Debris management and chemical effects are minimized by the elimination of pumps, sumps, strainers, and trash racks. Fire protection measures ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational, compensating for the inability to passively separate redundant safety trains due to the modular design. Regulatory requirements from 10 CFR 50.34(f) and NEI 06-12, as well as ATWS provisions, are largely inapplicable, and NuScale is exempt from related evaluations and analyses. For coolant source term evaluation, NuScale uses an alternate methodology incorporating aspects of ANSI/ANS 18.1. The only pumps within safety scope are ASME Class III CVCS pumps, which do not serve a safety function. The design meets DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials. For spent fuel pool mitigation, NuScale is exempt from certain requirements due to its below-grade, undrainable SFP. The design considers postulated startup reactivity accidents and addresses loading transients and structural evaluations for postulated breaks in CVCS and reactor vent valve piping. Containment vessel evacuation system valve closure times are addressed under SRP Section 6.2.4. Overall, NuScale’s unique passive and modular features support exemptions from many TMI-related and traditional LWR regulatory requirements.

results.question: How do the NuScale ECCS valve design and conventional ECCS injection systems differ in their response to a LOCA?
results.expected_answer: NuScale ECCS valves (RVVs/RRVs) open to connect RPV to CNV, allowing steam to vent and condensed water to recirculate by gravity. Conventional ECCS activates high-pressure injection, then transitions to low-pressure injection and recirculation using pumps. NuScale's approach is fully passive while conventional systems require AC power for pump operation.
results.generated_answer:
The NuScale ECCS uses passive steam condensation and natural recirculation via reactor vent and recirculation valves without pumps, containment spray, or a sump, while conventional ECCS injection systems rely on high- and low-pressure safety injection pumps, containment spray, and a sump for core cooling during a LOCA.  
(Source: Provided context, paragraphs 1, 2, and 6)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale ECCS (Emergency Core Cooling System) design differs from traditional systems by not utilizing high-pressure or low-pressure safety injection pumps, a containment spray system, or a sump. Instead, core decay heat removal is achieved through steam condensation and natural reactor coolant recirculation. During ECCS operation, water accumulates inside the NuScale containment vessel and is recirculated back to the reactor core, fulfilling the intent of relevant acceptance criteria. The design does not require personnel access during reactor operation, does not include pumps, and does not rely on containment pressure for ECCS recirculation as in typical PWRs. Consequently, purge or vent capability as specified by 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xv) is not required or included. Pre-operational testing of the ECCS will be conducted to satisfy regulatory guidance.

The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) utilizes three reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), with the RVVs being larger than the RRVs. Both valve types are pilot operated by remote-mounted trip and reset solenoid valves at the reactor containment boundary, and each main valve includes an inadvertent actuation block (IAB) feature located inside containment. The three RVVs are mounted on the top of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), connecting to the upper head and discharging the pressurizer (PZR) steam space directly to the containment vessel (CNV), thereby venting noncondensible gases during ECCS actuation. The two RRVs connect to the RPV shell just above the main closure flange. The ECCS design, including protection from inadvertent actuation and single failure capability, is detailed in Section 6.3, while the design basis for the RSVs and ECCS valves is provided in Sections 5.2.2 and 6.3. The NuScale design does not require separate safety-related high-point venting in the reactor coolant system (RCS), as the RVVs fulfill this function during and after a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA), ensuring noncondensible gases do not accumulate in the pressurizer space and core cooling is maintained. Mechanical failure of the reactor safety valve (RSV) is bounded by the analysis of inadvertent ECCS valve actuation in Section 15.6.6.

The NuScale reactor design features an LTOP (Low Temperature Overpressure Protection) system that is not connected to a low-pressure system, but the guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation from the primary system remains applicable. NuScale supports an exemption from selected portions of 10 CFR 50, Appendix K, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, regarding Mass and Energy Release Analysis for Postulated Loss-of-Coolant Accidents (LOCAs), with no additional inspections, tests, analyses, or acceptance criteria (ITAAC) required. In the event of a LOCA, the NuScale design ensures that core uncovery does not occur, and it conforms to DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, and GDC 5 regarding the sharing of structures, systems, and components, with no exceptions. The design does not credit or utilize a suppression pool and conforms to DSRS 6.6, Rev 0, for inservice inspection and testing of Class 2 and 3 components, with all relevant components subject to inspection and no additional requirements.

The NuScale design employs the chemical and volume control system as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to meet GDC 26 requirements. For containment pressure boundary fracture prevention (SRP 6.2.7, Rev 1) and most Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) criteria (DSRS 6.3, Rev 0), including ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46, single-failure consideration, design of non-safety-related ECCS portions, ECCS interfaces and shared systems, long-term cooling, ECCS outage times and unavailability reporting, and programmatic requirements, the design conforms to regulatory guidance. However, departures are noted for inservice inspection and operability testing, and for combined reactivity control system capability and actuation provisions; specifically, the NuScale ECCS does not perform poison addition or provide a makeup function as required by GDC 27, though actuation signal guidance is applied. Control room habitability system requirements (SRP 6.4, Rev 3) for the emergency zone are met. For inadvertent ECCS operation (DSRS 15.6.6, Rev 0), the reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure remains below 110 percent of design value, and minimum DNBR is maintained. The design provides high point venting capability for noncondensible gases from the RCS and other systems to ensure adequate core cooling (50.34(f)(2)(vi)), and instruments in the control room give unambiguous indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(xviii), with all relevant requirements conformed to unless otherwise noted.

The NuScale reactor design omits a containment spray system and sump, but during Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) operation, water accumulates inside the containment vessel for recirculation to the reactor core, making the pH guideline in the acceptance criterion applicable. The ECCS design does not utilize pumps or refueling water storage tanks, and operates via natural circulation without the need for pumps. The condensate storage systems and components are located outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building, and failure of these facilities poses no reasonable risk to safety-related systems or plant operation. The Instrumentation and Controls (I&C) review process described in DSRS 7.0 applies to the Design Certification Application (DCA), but lacks specific acceptance criteria. Regarding gas accumulation, NuScale has determined that gas buildup will not affect ECCS performance under accident conditions; the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) does not interface with the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) and is instead connected to the secondary system.

The NuScale reactor design employs the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to meet GDC 26, with its only safety-related function being the prevention of inadvertent boron dilution of the reactor coolant system. The CVCS, including the Boron Recovery System, partially conforms to DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0, meeting requirements for functional performance, pumping capacity, defense-in-depth RCS makeup, single failure criteria, GDC 5, minimization of contamination, system design, leakage detection, and ITAAC, but only partially meets criteria for holdup tank wall buckling/failure and venting/draining due to design differences. The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design diverges from traditional PWRs by omitting high- and low-pressure safety injection pumps, a containment spray system, and a sump; instead, it relies on steam condensation and natural recirculation of water that accumulates inside the containment vessel for core cooling, fulfilling ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46 and related guidance. The ECCS does not perform poison addition or provide a makeup function as required by GDC 27, and departures are noted for inservice inspection, operability testing, and combined reactivity control system capability and actuation provisions, though actuation signal guidance is applied. The design ensures RCS pressure remains below 110% of design value during inadvertent ECCS operation, maintains minimum DNBR, provides high point venting for noncondensible gases, and includes control room instrumentation for unambiguous indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(vi) and (xviii). The ECCS does not require personnel access, pumps, or containment pressure for recirculation, and does not include refueling water storage tanks; condensate storage components are outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building, with no safety risk from their failure. The Instrumentation and Controls review per DSRS 7.0 applies to the DCA but lacks specific acceptance criteria. Gas accumulation is not expected to affect ECCS performance, and the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) interfaces only with the secondary system, not the RCS. Control room habitability system requirements (SRP 6.4, Rev 3) are met for the emergency zone, and pre-operational ECCS testing will be conducted to satisfy regulatory guidance.

results.question: Compare the primary coolant flow paths during normal operation vs DHRS operation.
results.expected_answer: During normal operation, primary coolant flows upward through the core and riser, then downward through the SG region where heat transfers to the secondary side. During DHRS operation, after reactor trip and secondary isolation, the primary still circulates by natural circulation but heat is removed via the DHRS condensers in the pool through the SG tubes.

During normal operation, primary coolant flows through the reactor core and steam generators in a closed loop driven by pumps; during DHRS operation, primary coolant circulates naturally through the reactor core and steam generators, with steam generated in the SGs routed to the DHRS condensers and condensed water returning to the SGs, forming a passive two-phase natural circulation loop.  
(Source: Section 5.4.3, Figure 5.4-10, and related context)

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) thermal-hydraulic performance analysis assessed the orifice loss coefficient under various initial conditions to confirm that the restriction orifice size ensures consistent heat transfer. Accumulation of noncondensible gas in the DHRS steam lines was identified as a factor that degrades system performance. As a two-phase natural circulation system, DHRS performance depends on factors such as Reactor Coolant System (RCS) temperature, with higher RCS temperatures increasing the driving temperature difference and thus DHRS heat transfer. The analysis, performed using NRELAP5, included three model categories: a Steady State Model (with constant primary side temperature, flow rate, reactor pool temperature, and DHRS inventory), Nominal Transient Models (to demonstrate DHRS cooling for typical initiating events), and Off-Normal Transient Models (to show adequate cooling under the most limiting conditions). The steady state model calculated the DHRS heat transfer rate and evaluated the loss coefficient in the DHRS steam line to ensure acceptable performance across a range of DHRS inventories, including scenarios with additional steam volume due to MSIV failure, DHRS and SG fouling, SG tube plugging, and the presence of noncondensible gas. The limiting mass of dissolved gas that could be released during DHRS operation was also considered. Nominal transient models analyzed DHRS cooldown capability for cases with and without containment isolation, affecting the timing of pressurizer heater isolation. Off-normal transient models assessed DHRS performance under limiting conditions, including heat transfer surface fouling, plugged SG tubes, reactor pool water temperature, and noncondensible gas presence, with conservative biasing of valve timings to simulate worst-case DHRS inventory conditions. The overall analysis confirmed the DHRS capability to remove heat over a range of loop inventories.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report describes the design, operation, and failure modes of key Reactor Coolant System components, with emphasis on the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and its actuation valves (DHR-HOV-0101A/B, DHR-HOV-0201A/B). These valves are normally closed and fail open upon actuation signal or loss of control power, using nitrogen accumulation pressure to ensure DHRS operation without electrical power or added inventory. They are torque-opened pneumatically and classified as Category B valves per OM Code ISTC-1300(b). Redundant parallel valves per train ensure continued DHRS function in case of single valve failure. Main steam isolation valves (MS-HOV-0101, MS-HOV-0201) and feedwater isolation valves (FW-HOV-0137, FW-HOV-0237) are normally open and fail closed to establish a closed DHRS cooling loop; their failure to close is mitigated by downstream valves or temporary inventory addition. These isolation valves are located outside the Nuclear Power Module (NPM) atop the containment vessel, and their actuation is automatic via the engineered safety features actuation system (ESFAS) or manually via the Module Protection System (MPS), though manual actuation is not credited for design basis events. DHRS actuation valves are designed to fully open or close within 30 seconds when the differential pressure is 50 psid or less. The DHRS operates by natural circulation, driven by density differences between steam and condensate in the DHRS and steam generator (SG). SG thermal relief valves (SG-RV-0102, SG-RV-0202) provide pressure boundary protection, but spurious opening with failure to close results in DHRS train inventory loss and inoperability. Failure modes analyzed include spurious opening/closure, failure to open/close, partial or slow operation, and leakage, with system redundancy and backup valves ensuring continued operation. Detection methods include valve position indication, steam and pressurizer pressures, passive condenser temperature and level, and periodic testing. Manual vent and drain valves are provided for maintenance, remaining closed and capped during operation. The system is designed to maintain safe shutdown without ECCS actuation using the remaining DHRS train, and overpressure protection is provided when the SGS is water solid. Minor leakage or mechanical failures in one train do not compromise overall DHRS function if redundancy is preserved.

results.question: Compare the NuScale RPV structural integrity program with conventional PWR vessel surveillance programs.
results.expected_answer: Both NuScale and conventional PWRs use material surveillance capsules to monitor irradiation effects on vessel material. NuScale benefits from lower neutron fluence due to smaller core power, resulting in less embrittlement. The NuScale adjusted reference temperatures at end of life remain well below PTS screening criteria, providing larger margins than many conventional plants.
results.generated_answer:
The NuScale RPV structural integrity program follows ASTM E185-82 with surveillance capsules using production materials, a 60-year design life, and a withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY, similar to conventional PWR programs, but positions capsules at mid-core with a lead factor of ~2.5 and includes a fourth capsule for license extension, aligning with but also providing flexibility beyond typical PWR surveillance practices.  
(Source: NuScale FSAR, Table 5.3-4, Table 5.3-5, ASTM E185-82 requirements)
retrieved_contexts: The passages detail the comprehensive inspection, testing, and fabrication requirements for the NuScale Nuclear Power Module (NPM) reactor vessel, reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), and associated systems, as specified in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report and in compliance with ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and NRC regulations. Reactor vessel and RCPB components are constructed from Type 3XX austenitic stainless steel (max 0.03 wt% C) to prevent sensitization and intergranular corrosion, with welding procedures qualified per ASME BPVC Section III (notably NB-4300, NB-4600, NB-4622), Section IX, and Regulatory Guides 1.43, 1.44, 1.50, and 1.71. Welders are qualified per ASME Section IX and RG 1.71. Austenitic stainless steel and Ni-Cr-Fe cladding (e.g., Alloy 52/152) are applied to low alloy steel surfaces, with specific layering (first layer 309L, subsequent 308L) and post-weld heat treatment at 1125°F ± 25°F; Alloy 600 and Alloy 82/182 are excluded. Nondestructive examination (NDE) of welds and tubular products follows ASME BPVC Section III and XI, with preservice ultrasonic and surface exams for Class 1 piping welds, and requirements varying by pipe size and location. Inspection and testing programs are based on ASME BPVC Section III (2013 Edition) for construction, Section XI for ISI, and ASME OM-2012 for operation and maintenance, as referenced in 10 CFR 50.55a and RGs 1.84, 1.147, and 1.192. ISI intervals, frequencies, and component selection are defined, with inspection elements for piping, supports, and valves detailed in Tables 5.2-6 through 5.2-9 and Figures 5.2-1, 5.2-2, 5.3-1, and 5.4-17. System leakage and VT-2 examinations are performed before startup, with continuous VT-2 monitoring of RPV leakage in containment. Valve position is verified every 24 months, set pressure every 5 years, and special seal verification tests are used for reactor pool valves. O-ring leakage and flange bolt inspections follow Section XI. Reactor vessel internals inspections, per MRP-227 and ASME BPVC Section XI, focus on core support blocks, plates, guide tubes, riser transitions, and fasteners, using VT-1 and VT-3, with augmented exams for critical components. The reactor vessel beltline is evaluated for Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (≥75 ft-lb initial, ≥50 ft-lb end-of-life), and plant procedures ensure compliance with pressure-temperature limits. The NuScale Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) Material Surveillance Program follows ASTM E185-82 requirements, as detailed in Table 5.3-4, using archived materials identical to those used for baseline and capsule specimens, but maintained as full-thickness sections for flexibility in future specimen fabrication. Archive materials are sourced from actual production forgings and weldments using the same weld wire heat and flux lot as production welds. The NuScale RPV is designed for a 60-year life, with the surveillance capsule withdrawal schedule for the first 40 years based on 32 effective full-power years (EFPY), per ASTM E185-82 Table 1. Three capsules suffice for the initial 40-year period, with a fourth capsule included for potential regulatory requirements at license extension. Capsules are housed in holders attached to the core barrel at mid-core height, positioned approximately 90 degrees apart to achieve a lead factor of ~2.5. The withdrawal schedule (Table 5.3-5) specifies: the 1st capsule at the first refuel outage (~2 EFPY or 1.55E+18 n/cm², E > 1 MeV), the 2nd at ~13 EFPY (when capsule fluence matches peak 32-EFPY RPV inside surface fluence), and the 3rd between ~13 and ~26 EFPY. The 4th capsule's withdrawal will be determined by regulations at license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT of 50°F for the limiting weld metal is reached at 4.27E+17 n/cm² (E > 1 MeV, 0.55 EFPY), but 28°F of this shift is a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F, likely causing overcompensation at low fluence. Therefore, the first capsule may be withdrawn at the end of the first fuel cycle rather than mid-cycle.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements, design parameters, material composition, and major components of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Table 5.2-6 specifies that RPV shell and head welds, including connections such as the lower RPV flange shell to RPV bottom head and steam plenum cap to integral steam plenum, are subject to B-A category volumetric examination, while internal welds like the core support block to RPV bottom head undergo B-N-2 category VT-3 visual testing; instrumentation and controls sleeve welds are part of the cladding and not separately examined. Table 5.3-1 lists key RPV parameters: design pressure is 2100 psia, design temperature is 650°F, overall height is 778 inches, and diameters range from 96.5 to 115.5 inches depending on section, with inner and outer clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches, respectively. Table 5.3-2 provides maximum allowable concentrations for RPV beltline forging material (e.g., sulfur 0.010 wt%, phosphorus 0.010 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%) and as-deposited weld metal (e.g., sulfur 0.015 wt%, phosphorus 0.012 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, manganese 1.80 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%). Figure 5.3-1 identifies major RPV components, including the lower and upper RPV sections, RPV head section, CRDM latch housing section with 16 latch housings, RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum with 4 access ports, upper and lower RPV SG shells, feed plenum access ports, lower RPV section flange shell, RPV bottom head, upper RPV flanged transition shell, CRDM seismic support structure, and 4 instrument seal assemblies.

The review of the NuScale design against relevant Standard Review Plan (SRP) criteria and regulatory requirements demonstrates broad conformance across multiple technical areas. For SRP 3.2.2, Rev 2 (System Quality Group Classification), the design meets GDC 1 and 10 CFR 50.55a with no exceptions, except for partial conformance regarding the use of RG 1.85 (withdrawn in 2004 and incorporated into RG 1.84) and some language in App.A and Table A-1 referencing SSCs not present in NuScale. Foundations testing, inservice surveillance (II.7), and specification of transients for mechanical components (SRP 3.9.1, Rev 3, II.1) fully conform. For SRP 3.9.4, Rev 3 (Control Rod Drive Systems), descriptive information is adequate, with a minor typographical error noted in seismic design references; construction codes, load combinations (ASME Code Section III, NB-3113), and material specifications (SRP 4.5.1, Rev 3) are met, with RG 1.85 withdrawal acknowledged. Requirements for austenitic stainless steel, cleaning, and the NuScale QAPD (ANSI/ASME NQA-1-2008, NQA-1a-2009, RG 1.28, Rev. 4) are satisfied. The Control Rod Drive Systems Operability Assurance Program (SRP 3.9.4, Rev 3, II.4), reactor pressure vessel internals (SRP 3.9.5, Rev 3, II.1, II.2, II.4), and reactor internal/core support structure materials and welding (SRP 4.5.2, Rev 3, II.1, II.2) all fully conform. Partial conformance is noted for asymmetric blowdown loads (SRP 3.9.5, Rev 3, II.5) and flow-induced vibration/acoustic resonance (App A, II.6) due to references to non-applicable LWR/SSC conditions and BWR-specific aspects. Fuel system and nuclear design aspects meet all requirements of SRP 4.2, Rev 3, SRP 4.3, Rev 0, and DSRS 4.4, Rev 0, including fuel rod failure, coolability, structural response, reactivity coefficients, control rod worth, analytical methods, and thermal margin, with no exceptions or deviations noted.

results.question: Compare the primary coolant chemistry control in NuScale with conventional PWR chemistry programs.
results.expected_answer: Both NuScale and conventional PWRs use similar chemistry programs: boron for reactivity control, hydrogen for radiolysis suppression, and strict limits on contaminants. NuScale's CVCS performs the same functions but is sized for the smaller RCS volume. The key difference is NuScale's Alloy 690 SG tubes replacing Alloy 600, reducing SCC susceptibility.
