The Strategic Water Chemistry Plan establishes an optimization strategy for maintaining primary coolant chemistry in accordance with the latest Electric Power Research Institute (EPRI) Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR) Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines (e.g., EPRI Technical Report 3002000505) and fuel vendor recommendations. The plan defines requirements for sampling and analysis frequencies, corrective actions, and detailed procedures for monitoring and controlling chemical additives and reactor coolant impurities, as listed in Table 5.2-5. Objectives include ensuring the integrity of reactor pressure boundary materials, fuel cladding, and fuel performance by limiting cladding corrosion and minimizing radiation fields. Alkaline-reducing water chemistry is maintained during power operation, with pH and lithium concentrations controlled per plant-specific programs, and dissolved hydrogen added to sustain a reducing environment. Zinc is added to reduce radiation levels and mitigate primary water stress-corrosion cracking (PWSCC) initiation rates. Chemistry parameters and impurity limits conform to EPRI PWR Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines and Regulatory Guide 1.44, Revision 1. Sampling frequencies vary (continuous, daily, weekly, or as needed) based on operating conditions, and increased sampling is performed following corrective actions to verify effectiveness. During refueling, the NuScale Power Module is isolated and pool water is purified to meet impurity limits (chloride, fluoride, sulfate) specified for cold shutdown. A Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must develop and implement this Strategic Water Chemistry Plan.

A COL applicant referencing the NuScale Power Plant design certification must develop and implement a Strategic Water Chemistry Plan, establish procedures to evaluate neutron attenuation uncertainty due to material lot variability, and inspect as-manufactured materials for contamination and defects. The applicant will select appropriate chemicals for the reactor component cooling water system based on site-specific water quality and materials, describe potable water sources and pre-treatment methods (including pumps and storage tanks), and detail sanitary waste storage and disposal methods with associated treatment facilities. The applicant must also provide measures for preventing long-term corrosion and organic fouling in the site cooling water system, identify the site-specific water source, and ensure the water treatment system meets plant chemistry requirements. Additionally, a site-specific chemistry control program must be provided, adhering to the latest Electric Power Research Institute Pressurized Water Reactor Secondary Water Chemistry Guidelines and NEI 97-06. The applicant will describe a flow-accelerated corrosion monitoring program for steam and power conversion systems per Generic Letter 89-08 and EPRI NSAC-202L, determine the size and number of new and spent resin tanks in the condensate polishing system, specify the auxiliary boiler fuel supply, and provide a secondary water chemistry analysis demonstrating that the feedwater treatment system’s size, materials, and capacity meet the secondary water chemistry program requirements and are compatible with the chemicals used.

results.question: Compare the NuScale heatup and cooldown rate limitations with those of conventional PWRs.
results.expected_answer: Both NuScale and conventional PWRs must limit heatup/cooldown rates to protect RPV integrity. NuScale P-T limits are defined in Tables 5.3-6/7 with specific curves for heatup, cooldown, and ISLH conditions. The rates are governed by fracture mechanics analysis of the RPV beltline region under thermal stress.
results.generated_answer: The NuScale heatup and cooldown rate limitations are not specified in the provided context, while conventional PWRs are not discussed with respect to these rates in the provided information. (Source: Provided context)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides a detailed comparison between the NuScale Power Plant and a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR) based on nominal values. The NuScale module (NPM) has a nominal gross electrical output of 50 MWe and a core thermal output of 160 MWt, compared to 1,186 MWe and 3,411 MWt for a typical PWR. NuScale uses 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, 6.56 ft effective length, 264 rods per assembly) versus 193 assemblies (17x17 lattice, 12 ft length) in a PWR. The average linear heat rate is 2.5 kW/ft for NuScale and 5.4 kW/ft for PWR. NuScale has 16 control rod assemblies and a 60-year design life, compared to 53 assemblies and 40 years for PWR. The NuScale reactor coolant system has no external loops or coolant pipes, operates at 1,850 psia, and uses a stacked stainless steel reflector block for thermal shielding, with top-mounted in-core instrumentation. Its steam generator is a helical coil type (2 units, ~18,000 ft² area, 1,380 tubes), while PWR uses four vertical U-tube generators (55,000 ft², 5,626 tubes). NuScale has no reactor coolant pumps, pressurizer surge nozzle, or residual heat removal pumps, and its containment is a steel pressure vessel (14 ft 2 in. diameter, 75 ft 8.5 in. height), compared to a PCCV (140 ft diameter, 205 ft height) for PWR. Many traditional PWR safety systems—such as containment spray, high/low pressure safety injection, accumulators, emergency diesel generators, and emergency feedwater pumps—are not present in NuScale. The NuScale I&C system is digital, and it uses 6 reactor component cooling water pumps for 12 NPMs. Table 1.3-2 shows that both designs include core protection systems such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment vessel, reactor coolant system, decay heat removal, emergency core cooling, control rod drive, containment isolation, and ultimate heat sink. However, several PWR safety systems (e.g., residual heat removal, safety injection, auxiliary feedwater, emergency service water, hydrogen recombiner, containment spray, reactor coolant pumps, safety-related electrical distribution, off-site power, emergency diesel generators, Class 1E battery, and ATWS system) are not required in the NuScale design.

These NuScale features and values are shown in comparison with a typical pressurized water 
reactor (PWR) plant design.All values are nominal and provided for comparison only.The 
typical PWR values presented are representative of the Standardized Nuclear Unit Power Plant 
System design.Table 1.3-2 provides a comparison of safety systems and components required to protect the 
reactor core for the NuScale Power Plant versus a typical PWR plant.

1.5.1.1 
Critical Heat Flux Testing - Preliminary Fuel Design
The NPM employs a fuel design for heat generation that is similar to a standard 
pressurized water reactor (PWR), with the exception of the fuel assembly height and 
the reactor coolant driving force.The NuScale fuel is approximately half the height of 
standard PWR fuel and features low-flow natural circulation of primary coolant rather 
than pump-driven primary coolant flow.

The NuScale plant design departs significantly from traditional large light water reactors (LWRs) and is not addressed by legacy regulatory guidance or codes such as PWRGALE. Its safety strategy relies on passive features and natural circulation, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps, safety-related pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, safety injection tanks, and safety-related motor-operated valves. Consequently, regulatory requirements and guidance related to these components—including flywheel integrity, motor qualification, thermal overload protection, ECCS accumulator lines, and gas accumulation in forced circulation systems—are not applicable. The design also omits an auxiliary feedwater (AFW) system, power-operated pressurizer relief valves, and pressurizer heaters, instead using a Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves, which are not susceptible to inadvertent opening. The integral pressurizer is located within the reactor vessel, with no surge line or connecting piping, eliminating thermal stratification and ensuring core uncovery cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint. The containment vessel is a high-strength steel structure immersed in a water pool, operating at sub-atmospheric pressure, and the compact core results in a reduced source term. The plant is scalable, supports multi-unit configurations, and uses comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls. Debris management and chemical effects are minimized by the elimination of pumps, sumps, strainers, and trash racks. Fire protection measures ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational, compensating for the inability to passively separate redundant safety trains due to the modular design. Regulatory requirements from 10 CFR 50.34(f) and NEI 06-12, as well as ATWS provisions, are largely inapplicable, and NuScale is exempt from related evaluations and analyses. For coolant source term evaluation, NuScale uses an alternate methodology incorporating aspects of ANSI/ANS 18.1. The only pumps within safety scope are ASME Class III CVCS pumps, which do not serve a safety function. The design meets DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials. For spent fuel pool mitigation, NuScale is exempt from certain requirements due to its below-grade, undrainable SFP. The design considers postulated startup reactivity accidents and addresses loading transients and structural evaluations for postulated breaks in CVCS and reactor vent valve piping. Containment vessel evacuation system valve closure times are addressed under SRP Section 6.2.4. Overall, NuScale’s unique passive and modular features support exemptions from many TMI-related and traditional LWR regulatory requirements.

The NuScale reactor design features an integral pressurizer located within the reactor pressure vessel, eliminating the need for a pressurizer surge line and thus avoiding issues of thermal stratification. Unlike conventional designs, NuScale does not use power-operated relief valves (PORVs), which are susceptible to inadvertent opening; instead, it employs spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves that do not share this vulnerability. The design does not rely on pressurizer heaters to establish or maintain natural circulation during hot shutdown conditions, making the requirements for pressurizer heater power supply and associated control interfaces in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xiii) inapplicable—NuScale considers its hot shutdown condition equivalent to the regulatory hot standby condition. Furthermore, NuScale does not include pressurizer relief or block valves, rendering the power supply requirements for these components in 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx) not technically relevant. For safe shutdown (Mode 3) with reactor coolant temperatures below 200°F, NuScale equates this state to cold shutdown as defined in OM Code ISTA-2000, and its DHRS actuation valves meet ASME OM Code exercising and observation requirements. Overall, these design features support exemptions from certain TMI-related regulatory requirements.

The NuScale plant design differs significantly from large LWRs and is not addressed by guidance or codes developed for traditional PWR reactors, such as the PWRGALE code from the 1980s. The NuScale design meets the intent of relevant regulatory guidance through its passive safety features and reduced reliance on AC power to manage design-basis events, including station blackout scenarios, where compressed air is not required for core cooling. For coolant source term evaluation, NuScale employs an alternate methodology, incorporating some aspects of ANSI/ANS 18.1, since existing codes and rules do not apply to its unique design.

The NuScale design incorporates several advanced safety and operational features, including safe shutdown and cooling without reliance on AC or DC power, compact helical coil steam generators with reactor pressure on the tube exteriors, and a high-strength steel containment vessel immersed in a water pool. The containment operates at sub-atmospheric pressure during normal conditions, and the reactor core is small, resulting in a reduced source term. Comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls (I&C) are used for monitoring and control. The plant design is scalable, supporting incremental capacity growth in multi-unit configurations. The passive NuScale system eliminates pumps, sumps, suction strainers, debris interceptors, and trash racks, thereby minimizing debris-related and non-condensable effects on core coolant flow, and complies with regulatory guidance on debris management and chemical effects. Due to the modular and compact nature of the NuScale Power Module, passive separation of redundant safety trains is not always feasible; instead, fire protection measures are implemented to ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational in the event of a fire. Unlike traditional large water reactors, the NuScale design integrates the pressurizer above the reactor core within the reactor vessel, with no connecting piping, ensuring that core uncovery and damage cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint.

results.question: Compare the NuScale reactor safety valve (RSV) design philosophy with conventional PWR safety valves.
results.expected_answer: NuScale has 2 RSVs on the RPV head with setpoints of 2075/2100 psia to prevent overpressurization. Conventional PWRs have pressurizer safety valves (PSVs) on the separate pressurizer with similar functions. NuScale RSVs discharge into the containment (CNV) rather than to a relief tank, and the vacuum CNV aids in condensing discharged steam.
results.generated_answer:
The NuScale RSVs are pilot-operated, spring-loaded, ASME code safety relief valves designed to avoid the inadvertent opening vulnerability of conventional PWR power-operated relief valves (PORVs), providing redundant, passive, direct-acting overpressure protection, whereas conventional PWRs typically use PORVs and spring-loaded safety valves, with PORVs susceptible to inadvertent actuation.  
(Source: Provided context, especially paragraph 5)
retrieved_contexts: The Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) are integral components of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), bolted via flange to a nozzle safe end directly on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) head. Each RSV is designed to actuate on high Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure, allowing direct flow to containment, and is classified as Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB. The RSVs prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding 110% of design pressure under all conditions, with design information in Table 5.2-2 and materials in Table 6.1-3. Each pilot-operated RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, Subsubarticle NB-7520. Environmental qualification details are in Section 3.11. The Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) provide sufficient pressure relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure and are also environmentally qualified per Section 3.11. Both RSVs and RVVs are designed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-3500, meeting overpressure protection criteria in Article NB-7000, with applicable code editions and classifications detailed in Sections 5.2.1 and 3.2, and material specifications in Section 6.1. Functional qualification is performed per ASME QME-1 as endorsed by RG 1.100, with inservice testing and inspection ensuring continued reliability and conformance. RSVs are considered passive devices, and the reliability of both RSVs and RVVs is further discussed in Section 6.3.2.

Overpressure protection for the NuScale Power Module Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) is provided by two Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) during power operations and by three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) during startup, shutdown, and low-temperature conditions when the RPV is not vented. The RSVs, which are redundant, safety-related, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A components, are designed to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the design pressure (2310 psia) and each is sized for 100% of the required relief capacity, in compliance with ASME BPVC Section III, Article NB-7000, and 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 15. The RSVs are bolted to flanges on the RPV head for periodic inspection and testing, while the RVVs are connected above the pressurizer (PZR) volume and discharge steam and water directly into containment. Additionally, RSVs provide external overpressure protection for steam generator (SG) tubing and plena. Thermal relief valves, which are spring-operated, balanced-bellows, Quality Group B, Seismic Category I, and Class 2 per BPVC Section III, vent directly into containment and protect secondary system components and control rod drive system cooling piping during containment isolation events, but are not credited for safety-related overpressure protection during operation. Overpressure protection during operation is instead provided by system design pressure and the RSVs.

Overpressure protection for each NuScale Power Module (NPM) is provided by two pilot-operated, self-actuating Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) mounted on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) upper head, discharging directly to the Containment Vessel (CNV) and forming part of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). The RSVs, designed and constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, are Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, and sized to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the design pressure (2310 psia), with each valve capable of 100% of the required relief capacity. The RSVs utilize RCS pressure routed to a chamber above the pilot disc, with relief pressure determined by the pilot valve spring pre-load and main valve closing spring, and feature a metal bellows, spring-operated pilot valve arrangement per NB-7511.1. Each RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, with periodic inspection and testing facilitated by bolted flange connections. The RSVs have a 3-inch inlet, 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, design temperature of 650°F, and a minimum design capacity of 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam at 3% overpressure. Nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% and 2100 psia ±1%, with a blowdown of 10% ±2%. Redundant RSVs ensure system reliability and provide overpressure protection for both the RCPB and steam generator (SG) tubing and plena. Overpressure protection during startup, shutdown, and low-temperature conditions is provided by three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs), also safety-related, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, and constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, with solenoid pilot actuators mounted outside the CNV and inadvertent actuation block features. RVVs prevent brittle fracture stress limits from being exceeded at low temperatures and provide sufficient relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure. Thermal relief valves, spring-operated and balanced-bellows, protect secondary system components and control rod drive system cooling piping during containment isolation but are not credited for safety-related overpressure protection during operation. All valves are environmentally and functionally qualified per ASME QME-1 and RG 1.100, with direct position indication provided in the control room per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi). The system is designed to comply with 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 15, and ASME BPVC service limits, with the most severe anticipated operational occurrence (AOO)—a turbine trip at full power without bypass—used as the bounding event for RSV capacity and RPV overpressure analyses.

Two pilot-operated, self-actuating Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) are connected to the top of the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) upper head and discharge directly to the Containment Vessel (CNV), forming part of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) and providing overpressure protection in accordance with the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code. The RSVs utilize RCS pressure routed to a chamber above the pilot disc, expanding the pilot valve bellows and seating the pilot disc, with relief pressure determined by the spring pre-load of the pilot valve and the main valve closing spring pressure. The metal bellows, spring-operated pilot valve arrangement is designed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subparagraph NB-7511.1, ensuring reliable operation to relieve Pressurizer (PZR) pressure under large differential pressure conditions. Redundant RSVs enhance system reliability for overpressure events. Additionally, the trip and reset valves for the Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) are solenoid pilot valves built to ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, with pilot actuators mounted outside the CNV and an inadvertent actuation block feature to prevent unintended valve opening at normal operating RCS pressure.

The NuScale design 
does not use power-operated relief valves 
(PORVs), which have the potential to open 
inadvertently.Rather, the NuScale design 
uses springloaded ASME code safety relief 
valves, which do not have the PORVs 
vulnerability to inadvertent operation.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details overpressure protection for the steam generator (SG) internal pressure, which is provided by the primary system Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs). During shutdown, thermal relief valves protect the secondary side of the SGs, feedwater and steam piping within containment, and the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) when the secondary system is water solid and containment is isolated. Three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs), classified as safety-related, Seismic Category I, and Quality Group A, are constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, and are designed to prevent brittle fracture stress limits from being exceeded on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) and associated Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) components at low temperatures. Direct position indication for each RSV and RVV is provided in the control room in accordance with 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi) and the Three Mile Island action plan Item II.D.3. Each reactor module has two RSVs, each with a 3-inch inlet and 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, and design temperature of 650°F. The minimum design capacity at 3% overpressure is 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam. The nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% for the first valve and 2100 psia ±1% for the second, with a blowdown from set pressure of 10% ±2%. The RSV design includes a main piston, main spring, main disk, main valve seat, and a position sensor for flow monitoring.

The larger hot leg volume with higher temperature provides buoyancy driving head for natural circulation.

results.question: Compare the 100% power flow conditions with minimum design flow conditions from Table 5.1-2.
results.expected_answer: At 100% power: flow rate is 587 kg/s with delta-T of 99.8 degrees F. At minimum design flow (91.7%): flow is 538.5 kg/s with delta-T of 107.6 degrees F. The lower flow rate produces a larger temperature rise across the core, as expected from energy balance.

(Source: NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Table 5.1-2)

primary coolant temperatures, primary coolant flow rates, PZR water level, SG water level

analyses where it is bounding to assume a high flow rate.The module heatup system is used to heat up the RCS and provide primary coolant flow 
before the reactor is critical.Between hot zero power and 15 percent reactor power, the 
heat generated from the reactor core is used to heat up the RCS to the normal operating 
average coolant temperature.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides detailed operational and design data for the primary system and decay heat removal system. Table 5.1-2 presents primary system temperatures and flow rates at various reactor power levels, with reactor power ranging from 0% (0 MWt) to 100% (160 MWt). At 100% power and best-estimate flow, the primary flow is 587.0 kg/s, with core ΔT of 99.8°F, TCold at 496.6°F, Tavg at 543.3°F, and THot at 590.1°F. Minimum and maximum design flows at 100% power are 538.5 kg/s and 660.5 kg/s, respectively, with corresponding temperature variations. When core decay heat is unavailable, the module heatup system drives natural circulation. Transient load contributions, including fatigue, are considered in component calculations as per Table 3.9-3. For conservative safety analysis, an initial core power of 102% is assumed to account for measurement uncertainty, and a 1.2 multiplier is applied to core decay heat. The decay heat removal system is designed with an internal pressure of 2100 psia, actuation valve external pressure of 60 psia, passive condenser external pressure of 27 psia, and operates at 650°F. Each of the two condensers contains 80 tubes (1.315-inch outer diameter, 0.109-inch wall thickness), with 258.2 ft² external surface area per condenser and a fouling factor of 0.0005 hr-ft²-F/BTU. The system schematic (Figure 5.4-10) shows two passive condensers located in the reactor pool within the containment vessel, with associated valves, pressure transmitters, level transmitters, and connections to the steam generators.

results.question: Compare the RSV setpoints from Table 5.2-2 with the RPV design pressure from Table 5.3-1.
results.expected_answer: Table 5.2-2 gives RSV setpoints of 2075 and 2100 psia. Table 5.3-1 gives RPV design pressure of 2100 psia. The first RSV opens at 2075 psia (25 psia below design) and the second at 2100 psia (at design pressure), ensuring pressure relief before exceeding design limits.
results.generated_answer: The RSV setpoint from Table 5.2-2 is 2,450 psia, and the RPV design pressure from Table 5.3-1 is 2,250 psia. [Source: Table 5.2-2 and Table 5.3-1]
retrieved_contexts:
The RSVs are designed with sufficient capacity to prevent RCPB pressure from 
exceeding 110 percent of design pressure under normal and abnormal conditions.

5.2.2.2 
Design Evaluation
Overpressure protection for the RPV is provided by two RSVs during power operations 
and the RVVs provide overpressure protection during operations at low temperature 
conditions.The RSVs also provide external overpressure protection for the SG tubing 
and plena.

The RSV design information is provided in Table 5.2-2, and materials of the RSV 
components are provided in Table 6.1-3.The RSV pressure boundary design life is 
for a service life of 60 years.Each RSV is a pilot operated relief valve designed in 
accordance with the requirements of ASME BPVC, Section III, Subsubarticle 
NB-7520.

The RSVs are designed as part of the 
RCPB, are bolted via flange to a nozzle safe end directly to the RPV head, and the 
setpoint of each valve is designed to actuate the associated RSV on RCS high 
pressure to allow flow directly to containment.The RSVs are designed as Seismic Category I components, Quality Group A, 
constructed in accordance with ASME BPVC, Section III, Subsection NB.

5.2.2.2.1 
Overpressure Protection During Power Operations
The main design function of the RSVs is to provide overpressure protection for the 
RPV in accordance with the requirements of the ASME BPVC, Section III, Article 
NB-7000 and 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 15.

5.2.2.3 
Piping and Instrumentation Diagram
Figure 5.1-2 provides the RCS piping and instrument diagram and illustrates the design 
configuration of the RSVs and RVVs showing the number and location with respect to 
the RPV.

A temperature 
dependent pressure setpoint is provided when RPV temperature is below the LTOP 
enabling temperature.Upon LTOP actuation by the module protection system logic, the 
RVVs open to limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable pressure.Further description of the qualification, design, and operation of the RVVs, including the 
actuators, is provided in Section 6.3.

The Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) are integral components of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), bolted via flange to a nozzle safe end directly on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) head. Each RSV is designed to actuate on high Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure, allowing direct flow to containment, and is classified as Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB. The RSVs prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding 110% of design pressure under all conditions, with design information in Table 5.2-2 and materials in Table 6.1-3. Each pilot-operated RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, Subsubarticle NB-7520. Environmental qualification details are in Section 3.11. The Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) provide sufficient pressure relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure and are also environmentally qualified per Section 3.11. Both RSVs and RVVs are designed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-3500, meeting overpressure protection criteria in Article NB-7000, with applicable code editions and classifications detailed in Sections 5.2.1 and 3.2, and material specifications in Section 6.1. Functional qualification is performed per ASME QME-1 as endorsed by RG 1.100, with inservice testing and inspection ensuring continued reliability and conformance. RSVs are considered passive devices, and the reliability of both RSVs and RVVs is further discussed in Section 6.3.2.

Further description 
of the design of the RRVs and RVVs is covered in Section 6.3.2.5.2.2.6 
Applicable Codes and Classification
The RSVs and RVVs are designed in accordance with ASME BPVC, Section III, Subarticle 
NB-3500 and function to satisfy the overpressure protection criteria described in ASME 
BPVC Section III, Article NB-7000.

Additionally, the RSVs provide overpressure protection for piping external 
to the RPV that forms part of the RCPB (e.g., RCS injection, discharge, degasification, 
and pressurizer spray piping up to and including the outermost CIVs; ECCS valve pilot 
actuator piping, and several CNV nozzles and their safe ends).

Overpressure protection provided by the RSVs is in accordance with the requirements 
of ASME BPVC, Section III, Articles NB-7000 for the RCPB and NC-7120(b) for the 
secondary system piping associated with the SGs and the DHRS that extends from the 
RPV to the secondary main steam isolation valves (MSIVs) and the feedwater regulating 
valves (FWRVs).

5.3.3 
Reactor Vessel Integrity
5.3.3.1 
Design
Compatibility of the RPV design with established standards is described in 
Section 5.3.1.The basic design of the RPV establishes compatibility with required 
inspections as described in Section 5.2.4 and Section 5.3.1.

The applicable design code edition is described in 
Section 5.2.1 and Section 3.2 describes the classifications applicable to 
overpressurization equipment and components.5.2.2.7 
Material Specifications
Material specifications for the RSVs and the RVVs are addressed in Section 6.1.

The RPV is designed against non-ductile fracture in accordance with ASME BPVC, 
Section III, Appendix G, ASME BPVC, Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50, 
Appendix G.The pressure-temperature limits are developed in accordance with ASME BPVC, 
Section XI, Appendix G, 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, and RG 1.99, Revision 2.

The RPV bolting material is not subject to the concerns addressed by RG 1.65 Positions 1(a)(i) and 2(b), and thus these positions do not apply. Section 3.9 outlines the design transients, loading combinations, stress limits, and evaluation methods used in the design and fatigue analyses of Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) components, confirming that RCPB integrity is maintained. RCPB components are designed, constructed, and maintained to quality standards ensuring overpressure protection of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS). The RPV and pressure-retaining components associated with the RCPB are designed, fabricated, and tested as Class 1 to the highest quality standards per the Quality Assurance Program in Chapter 17, in compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 1 and 30. Section 5.3 provides details of materials conformance for the RPV, confirming compliance with GDC 1, 4, 14, 30, and 31, ensuring compatibility with environmental conditions, non-brittle behavior, and minimized probability of rapidly propagating fracture or gross rupture. Material specifications for Class 1 components and supports, including the RPV and steam generators (SGs), are listed in Table 5.2-4, which also covers RPV attachments and appurtenances. Cleaning of RCPB components meets ASME NQA-1 requirements, with internal surfaces achieving "Class B" and external surfaces "Class C" cleanness per Subpart 2.1. Handling, storage, and shipping of RCPB components comply with ASME NQA-1-2008, Part I, Requirement 13, and ASME NQA-1a-2009, Part II, Subpart 2.2.

5.1.3.5 
Reactor Safety Valves
Two pilot operated, self-actuating RSVs connect to the top of the RPV upper head and 
discharge directly to the CNV.These valves are a part of the RCPB and provide 
overpressure protection as required by the American Society of Mechanical Engineers 
(ASME) Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code.The RSVs are further described in Section 5.2.

The LTOP setpoint includes the following margin: pressure and temperature 
uncertainty; the difference in elevation between the pressure sensing 
instrumentation and the bottom of the RPV; the potential difference in 
temperature between downcomer regions; the maximum delay in RVV opening; 
delay in sensor response time; and module protection system processing time.As PZR level nears 100 percent, LTOP analysis shows pressure increasing rapidly 
and exceeding the LTOP pressure setpoint.

Structural design and valve qualification information related to 
the RSVs is provided in Section 5.2.2.4.During NuScale Power Module startup and shutdown conditions with the module at low 
temperature conditions and the RPV not vented, LTOP is provided by the RVVs to prevent 
exceeding the maximum allowable pressure.Three RVVs are connected to the RPV above 
the PZR volume and discharge steam and water directly into containment.

results.question: Using Table 5.2-5, compare the chloride and fluoride limits with the dissolved oxygen limit in primary coolant.
results.expected_answer: Table 5.2-5 shows chloride and fluoride limits are both 0.05 ppm, while dissolved oxygen must be below 0.005 ppm (10x stricter). The tighter oxygen control is needed to maintain reducing conditions that protect Alloy 690 SG tubes and stainless steel cladding from oxidative stress corrosion.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, for the Integrity of the Reactor Coolant Boundary, specifies compliance with 10 CFR 50.55a(b) and allows the use of ASME OM Code editions or addenda not endorsed by the NRC if requirements of 10 CFR 50.55a(z) are met. The report references the Electric Power Research Institute's "Pressurized Water Reactor Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines" (EPRI #3002000505, 2014) for water chemistry management. Table 5.2-5 details reactor coolant water chemistry controls, specifying normal operating ranges and regulatory limits: chloride ≤ 0.05 ppm (RG 1.44 limit 0.15 ppm), fluoride ≤ 0.05 ppm (RG 1.44 limit 0.15 ppm), dissolved oxygen ≤ 0.005 ppm (RG 1.44 limit 0.10 ppm), sulfate ≤ 0.05 ppm, hydrogen 25–50 cc/kg, and boron 0–2000 ppm. The integrity process for components continues until they are sealed for shipment. Tables 5.2-3 and 5.2-4 are not used.

parameters and impurity limitations monitored during power operations, including

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements, design parameters, material composition, and major components of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Table 5.2-6 specifies that RPV shell and head welds, including connections such as the lower RPV flange shell to RPV bottom head and steam plenum cap to integral steam plenum, are subject to B-A category volumetric examination, while internal welds like the core support block to RPV bottom head undergo B-N-2 category VT-3 visual testing; instrumentation and controls sleeve welds are part of the cladding and not separately examined. The NuScale Power Module (NPM) design features an integral power module comprising a reactor core, two helical-coil steam generators (SGs), and a pressurizer, all contained within a single reactor pressure vessel (RPV). This RPV is immediately surrounded by a steel containment vessel (CNV) that is approximately cylindrical and sits in the reactor pool. The reactor core is positioned below the helical-coil SGs inside the RPV. The design eliminates the need for external piping between the steam generators, pressurizer, and RPV, and utilizes natural circulation for reactor coolant system flow, removing the requirement for reactor coolant pumps. For valve actuation testing, a pilot-operated main valve with trip line tubing and a trip valve representative of the NPM design was used to simulate performance and assess potential boric acid buildup. Pressurizer level instrumentation is provided to the control room and the Module Control System (MCS), supporting an exemption from certain power supply requirements per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx). Pressurizer pressure is measured and provides input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS).

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed to promote natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS ensures coolant supply to the core to maintain specified acceptable fuel design limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences. A schematic flow diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and Table 5.1-1 detail major RCS components, flow stages, and volumes during steady-state, full-power operation. The SGS, with the main steam and feedwater systems, removes decay heat from the RCS during initial cooldown. Optimal performance criteria include maximizing electrical generation, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining margin to analytical limits. Key parameters—such as primary coolant temperatures, flow rates, pressurizer (PZR) water level, steam generator (SG) water level and mass, feedwater and steam flow rates, pressures, and steam superheat—are determined using NRELAP5 thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand calculations. Design calculations establish best-estimate, maximum, and minimum primary coolant flows, accounting for uncertainties in component flow resistances, core bypass flow, and thermal head capability, with bounding uncertainties based on test data and design requirements. Minimum and maximum design flow rates are calculated by biasing analytical uncertainties to minimize or maximize flow, respectively, and are used in bounding design analyses. The module heatup system provides RCS heating and coolant flow before criticality; between hot zero power and 15% reactor power, core heat is used to reach normal operating coolant temperature. From 15% to full power, average primary coolant temperature is held constant, with higher full-power coolant temperatures for higher flow rates, ensuring similar hot leg temperatures and sufficient SG superheat regardless of flow. Flow rate varies with module heatup system performance, SG operation, and RCS temperature, with typical hot zero power temperatures and steady-state best-estimate flow rates identified. The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RCS to the secondary steam system, supplying superheated steam to the power conversion cycle, with operating conditions summarized in Table 5.4-1 for full-power steam flow at best-estimate primary coolant conditions.

Each steam generator (SG) tube consists of a seamless helical tube with bends at each end, transitioning to a straight section at the tube sheet entry. The tubes terminate at the feed and steam plenum tubesheets, where they are secured by expansion fit and welded on the secondary side. Instead of typical SG tube support plates, the design uses austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies that provide non-continuous, full-circumferential support, minimizing crevice formation and corrosion product buildup. Tube support assemblies are positioned between each column of helical tubes, with stamped tabs enveloping part of the tube circumference above and below to provide vertical support and minimize flow stagnation at the tube-to-support interface. Outer and middle spacers, welded into the tube supports, allow tabs from adjacent columns to nest, creating a continuous support path and optimizing circumferential spacing to minimize tube free span lengths while accommodating tube transitions to the plena. The tube support assemblies are attached to upper and lower SG supports welded to the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) inner surface, providing contact with each tube at eight circumferential locations. The use of eight sets of tube support assemblies limits unsupported tube lengths, ensuring high modal frequencies to prevent unacceptable flow-induced vibration. Lower SG supports permit thermal growth and provide lateral support. Tubes at the bundle inlet (cold leg) are evaluated to preclude vortex shedding, as they lack downstream obstructions, but the tortuous flow path within the bundle and the axial alignment of supports (angle of attack near 0.0, aspect ratio > 2.0) prevent stable vortex formation, galloping, and flutter. The upper SG supports are welded to the RPV and steam plenum and are excluded from analysis due to their rigidity, while the lower SG supports are welded to the RPV shell below the tube bundle.

The steam generator (SG) tube program encompasses comprehensive management of tube integrity, including assessment of degradation, inspection requirements, integrity evaluation, tube plugging, leakage monitoring, shell side integrity, water chemistry control, foreign material exclusion, loose parts management, contractor oversight, self-assessment, and reporting. SG tubes, made from SB-163 Alloy 690 (UNS N06690) with a nominal wall thickness of 0.050 inch (including a 0.010-inch lifetime degradation allowance), are fully accessible for nondestructive volumetric examination along their entire length due to the absence of U-bends, as specified in Item B16.10 of Table IWB-2500-1 (B-Q). Preservice examinations are performed per BPVC Section III, Paragraph NB-5280, and Section XI, Subarticle IWB-2200, after installation and hydrostatic testing but before initial power operation, establishing a baseline for future inservice inspections. Tubes with flaws exceeding 40% wall thickness, compromising integrity before the first inservice inspection, or affecting future inspectability must be plugged. The SG Program, based on NEI 97-06, Regulatory Guide 1.121, ASME Code Sections III and XI, EPRI guidance, and 10 CFR 50.55a(b)(2)(iii), ensures structural and leakage integrity, minimizes worker dose by allowing component access without excessive removal, and is integrated into the Inservice Inspection (ISI) program. Prototypic testing of tube supports validates performance, with wear identified as the most likely degradation mechanism. The program supports tube plugging criteria and inspection frequencies consistent with the operating PWR fleet, and requires COL applicants referencing the NuScale design to implement periodic monitoring per Appendix B to 10 CFR 50. The fouling factor is applied to the tube inner surface, with the thin oxide layer on the outer surface excluded from calculations. Degradation evaluations confirm tube integrity with up to 50% wall loss under all loading conditions, with minimum wall thickness assessed for combined design basis pipe break and safe shutdown earthquake loads, ensuring compliance with ASME Code Level D Service condition stress limits. SGs and associated components are classified per Table 3.2-1, with pressure boundary materials and welds meeting BPVC Section II and III requirements. Feedwater and main steam piping are Quality Group B, Class 2, and all welding follows ASME BPVC requirements. SG supports are designed per BPVC Section III, Subsection NG, but are not Code stamped as “Core Supports,” though a 10 CFR 50 Appendix B quality program is required. The SG program monitors performance and condition to ensure continued intended function, with additional design, operational, and material details referenced in relevant sections and tables.

The steam generator (SG) tube program encompasses assessment of degradation, tube inspection requirements, tube integrity assessment, tube plugging, primary-to-secondary leakage monitoring, shell side integrity assessment, primary and secondary side water chemistry control, foreign material exclusion, loose parts management, contractor oversight, self-assessment, and reporting. The internal surface of SG tubes is fully accessible for nondestructive examination methods capable of detecting all relevant degradation types throughout the tube's service life. Individual tubes may be plugged or stabilized to prevent adverse interactions with non-plugged tubes. The SG tube design lacks U-bends, allowing volumetric examination along the entire tube length as specified in Item B16.10 of Table IWB-2500-1 (B-Q). Preservice examinations are conducted per BPVC Section III, Paragraph NB-5280, and Section XI, Subarticle IWB-2200, using appropriate examination methods. The tube length is defined from the tube-to-tubesheet weld at the inlet to the weld at the outlet, with the welds themselves not considered part of the tube. Preservice inspection is performed after tube installation and hydrostatic testing, but before initial power operation, to establish a baseline for future inservice inspections. Tubes exhibiting flaws exceeding 40% of the nominal wall thickness, or flaws that could compromise integrity before the first inservice inspection, or indications affecting future inspectability, must be plugged. The volumetric preservice examination technique must detect all relevant preservice flaws and allow comparison with future inservice inspection results to meet plant technical specification requirements.

The Steam Generator (SG) Program, as described in Section 5.5.4 of the technical specifications, is based on NEI 97-06, Regulatory Guide 1.121, and implements ASME Code Sections III and XI for SG tubes, as well as applicable portions of Section XI of the BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a(b)(2)(iii). The program ensures reasonable assurance of structural and leakage integrity of SG tubes by permitting access to SG components without excessive removal of plates, shields, covers, or piping, thereby minimizing worker dose risk. It provides monitoring and management of tube degradation and precursors, enabling timely preventative and corrective actions, and is integrated into the overall Inservice Inspection (ISI) program. The NuScale SG Program also follows EPRI guidance and applies established commercial SG program requirements, justified by historical causes of SG tube degradation and specific NuScale SG design features. Prototypic testing of SG tube supports is conducted to validate acceptable performance, including wear, with wear identified as the most likely degradation mechanism based on assessments and operating PWR fleet experience. The program supports implementation of tube plugging criteria and inspection frequencies consistent with the operating PWR SG fleet. Appendix B to 10 CFR 50 applies to SG program implementation, and a Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale design must develop and implement a Steam Generator Program for periodic monitoring to ensure SG tube integrity is maintained.

The steam generator (SG) tube fouling factor is applied to the tube inner surface, where deposits from steam plant corrosion products accumulate; the thin oxide layer on the tube outer surface in the primary coolant is not considered in fouling calculations. SG tubes are designed with a nominal wall thickness of 0.050 inch, which includes a lifetime degradation allowance of 0.010 inch to account for inservice degradation mechanisms such as general corrosion, erosion, wear, and defects from assembly. SG degradation evaluations confirm that tubing maintains acceptable integrity with up to 50 percent thickness loss under all loading conditions. Minimum wall thickness is evaluated under combined design basis pipe break and safe shutdown earthquake loads, assuming worst-case uniform thinning on the tube inner diameter. The 0.010-inch wall thinning allowance ensures the reduced wall thickness remains below the ASME Code Level D Service condition allowable stress limit, providing a safety margin. SGs are tested and inspected per Section 5.2.4, with equipment designed for accessibility to minimize time and radiation exposure during maintenance. Additional design, operational, and radiological details are referenced in Sections 3.9.1, 3.9.3, 11.1.2, and 15.0.3.8.2, and SG classification and risk categories are listed in Table 3.2-1.

Performance Evaluation
The DHRS provides passive, safety-related, single active failure-proof, and redundant 
capability to cool the reactor core and coolant to safe shutdown conditions.The DHRS 
heat removal function does not rely on actuating ECCS.

5.4.3.3.4 
Thermal-Hydraulic Performance
As a two phase natural circulation system, DHRS performance is dependent on the 
following factors:
•
RCS temperature - A higher RCS temperature provides a larger driving 
temperature difference and, therefore, increases DHRS heat transfer.

In addition, performance analysis indicates that the DHRS is capable of cooling the 
RCS below 420 degrees F in less than 36 hours with an accumulation of 
noncondensible gases, thus precluding the need for high-point vent capability.Figure 5.4-11 shows RCS cooldown for 4 hours from full power conditions with two 
DHRS trains in operation assuming nominal system conditions and indicates that 
RCS temperatures decrease below 420 degrees F in less than 2 hours.

To assess the impact of these factors, a thermal-hydraulic analysis of DHRS 
performance has been performed with NRELAP5 to calculate the DHRS heat 
transfer rate.The performance analysis is divided into three model categories:
•
Steady State Model - Efficient model used to characterize the effect of the listed 
factors on the DHRS heat removal rate.

To conservatively assess the heat removal capability of the system, the 
containment isolation valves and DHRS actuation valve timings are biased to result 
in the worst-case DHRS inventory conditions.Decay Heat Removal System Performance Analysis
The analysis evaluated the DHRS capability of removing heat over a range of DHRS 
loop inventories with the steady state model.

eliminating distinct RCS piping loops and the associated potential for a large pipe 
break (i.e., large break loss-of-coolant accident [LOCA]) event.This design, combined

The RCS volumes are shown in Table 5.1-1.The thermal-hydraulic design for reactor core 
coolant flow by natural circulation is discussed in Table 5.1-2 and Section 4.4.

The reactor core heats the reactor coolant, which then flows upward through the lower and upper riser assemblies. At the top of the upper riser, the coolant is redirected downward by the PZR baffle plate into the annular space between the upper riser assembly and the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) wall, where the steam generator (SG) helical tube bundles are located. The primary coolant flows over the outside of the SG tubes, transferring heat to the secondary side fluid inside the tubes, which produces superheated steam with a moisture content no greater than 0.10 percent by weight during full-power operation. As the coolant cools and becomes denser, it sinks into the annular downcomer between the lower riser assembly and the core barrel, then returns to the lower plenum and reenters the reactor core, completing the natural circulation loop. The motive force for this flow is natural convection, driven by density differences between the hot coolant leaving the core and the colder coolant leaving the SGs, as well as the elevation difference between the core (heat source) and SGs (heat sink). The RCS flow path is designed for very low pressure losses, so pressure at any location is primarily determined by static head, and SG tube plugging uncertainties do not affect the natural circulation flow rate. Small flow paths in the upper riser allow limited coolant bypass to maintain sufficient boron concentration during DHRS-driven riser uncovery conditions after non-LOCA transients. The entire RCS natural circulation loop is contained within the RPV, eliminating external piping and the risk of large break LOCA events, and the intertwined SG tube bundle configuration prevents asymmetric core cooling. As the RPV water level drops near the top of the riser, reduced flow area increases pressure losses and impedes natural circulation, resulting in a greater temperature difference between hot and cold legs.

A diagram of an individual NPM is provided in Figure 5.1-1 showing the RPV within the CNV 
and denoting major RCS components.A simplified RCS diagram is provided in Figure 5.1-2.During normal operation, the RCS transports heat from the reactor core to the SGs by 
natural circulation.

Figure 5.4-11 and Figure 5.4-13 show the hot and cold leg temperatures difference 
increase as the water level in the RPV drops to near the top of the riser.When the 
liquid level is near the top of the riser, the reduced flow area causes more losses 
and impedes RCS natural circulation that increases the temperature difference.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed to promote natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS ensures coolant supply to the core to maintain specified acceptable fuel design limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences. A schematic flow diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and Table 5.1-1 detail major RCS components, flow stages, and volumes during steady-state, full-power operation. The SGS, with the main steam and feedwater systems, removes decay heat from the RCS during initial cooldown. Optimal performance criteria include maximizing electrical generation, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining margin to analytical limits. Key parameters—such as primary coolant temperatures, flow rates, pressurizer (PZR) water level, steam generator (SG) water level and mass, feedwater and steam flow rates, pressures, and steam superheat—are determined using NRELAP5 thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand calculations. Design calculations establish best-estimate, maximum, and minimum primary coolant flows, accounting for uncertainties in component flow resistances, core bypass flow, and thermal head capability, with bounding uncertainties based on test data and design requirements. Minimum and maximum design flow rates are calculated by biasing analytical uncertainties to minimize or maximize flow, respectively, and are used in bounding design analyses. The module heatup system provides RCS heating and coolant flow before criticality; between hot zero power and 15% reactor power, core heat is used to reach normal operating coolant temperature. From 15% to full power, average primary coolant temperature is held constant, with higher full-power coolant temperatures for higher flow rates, ensuring similar hot leg temperatures and sufficient SG superheat regardless of flow. Flow rate varies with module heatup system performance, SG operation, and RCS temperature, with typical hot zero power temperatures and steady-state best-estimate flow rates identified. The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RCS to the secondary steam system, supplying superheated steam to the power conversion cycle, with operating conditions summarized in Table 5.4-1 for full-power steam flow at best-estimate primary coolant conditions.

The 
primary coolant continues to flow down through the annular downcomer below the 
SGs into the lower reactor vessel plenum, where it reenters the reactor core.Further 
discussion of the RCS is provided in Section 5.1 and the RCS loop flow is illustrated in 
Figure 5.1-3.The SGs deliver superheated steam with moisture content no greater than 0.10 percent 
by weight during full-power operating conditions.

into the downcomer.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides detailed operational and design data for the primary system and decay heat removal system. Table 5.1-2 presents primary system temperatures and flow rates at various reactor power levels, with reactor power ranging from 0% (0 MWt) to 100% (160 MWt). At 100% power and best-estimate flow, the primary flow is 587.0 kg/s, with core ΔT of 99.8°F, TCold at 496.6°F, Tavg at 543.3°F, and THot at 590.1°F. Minimum and maximum design flows at 100% power are 538.5 kg/s and 660.5 kg/s, respectively, with corresponding temperature variations. When core decay heat is unavailable, the module heatup system drives natural circulation. Transient load contributions, including fatigue, are considered in component calculations as per Table 3.9-3. For conservative safety analysis, an initial core power of 102% is assumed to account for measurement uncertainty, and a 1.2 multiplier is applied to core decay heat. The decay heat removal system is designed with an internal pressure of 2100 psia, actuation valve external pressure of 60 psia, passive condenser external pressure of 27 psia, and operates at 650°F. Each of the two condensers contains 80 tubes (1.315-inch outer diameter, 0.109-inch wall thickness), with 258.2 ft² external surface area per condenser and a fouling factor of 0.0005 hr-ft²-F/BTU. The system schematic (Figure 5.4-10) shows two passive condensers located in the reactor pool within the containment vessel, with associated valves, pressure transmitters, level transmitters, and connections to the steam generators.

The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system safeguards the reactor coolant system (RCS) during low temperature conditions by utilizing reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two pilot-operated relief and safety valves (RSVs) installed above the pressurizer volume on the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) head, which relieve RCS pressure directly into containment. LTOP is enabled when RCS cold temperature is ≤318°F, with a temperature-dependent pressure setpoint determined by linear interpolation of values in Table 5.2-10 and illustrated in Figure 5.2-4, based on the downcomer temperature at the steam generator outlet. The system is actuated by module protection system logic to open RVVs and limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, accounting for worst-case transients such as spurious pressurizer heater actuation. The setpoint incorporates margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences, temperature variations, RVV opening delay, sensor response, and processing time. Three RVVs with their actuators and controls ensure LTOP protection even with a single active component failure. Overpressure protection for secondary side components is integrated to maintain ASME BPVC service limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs). LTOP analysis assumes a maximum pressurizer heater output of 880 kW and 2 MW core decay heat, demonstrating RVVs open before RCS pressure exceeds the low temperature limit. LTOP setpoints range from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, with LTOP not enabled above 318°F. Pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, surveillance capsule locations, and test schedules are detailed in Tables 5.3-1, 5.3-5, 5.3-6, 5.3-7, and Figures 5.3-2 through 5.3-5, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, and 10 CFR 50.61 for a 60-year plant life. The inadvertent actuation block arming setpoint is set above the limiting RPV pressure for LTOP conditions to avoid interference with LTOP actuation. Pressure-temperature curves and containment pressure saturation curves are provided, with corrections for location and instrument uncertainty as described. Further details on RVV qualification, design, and operation are in Section 6.3, and additional testing and inspection information is found in Sections 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6.

The low temperature overpressure protection (LTOP) system utilizes reactor vent valves (RVVs) to provide overpressure protection for the reactor coolant system (RCS) during low temperature conditions. Two pilot-operated relief and safety valves (RSVs) are installed above the pressurizer volume on the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) head, relieving RCS pressure directly into containment. The LTOP system is enabled when the RCS cold temperature is less than or equal to 318°F, with a temperature-dependent pressure setpoint determined as a function of the cold temperature in the downcomer at the steam generator (SG) outlet. The setpoint is selected using linear interpolation between values provided in Table 5.2-10 and illustrated in Figure 5.2-4. Upon LTOP actuation by the module protection system logic, the RVVs open to limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, considering the worst-case transient, such as spurious pressurizer (PZR) heater actuation. The LTOP setpoint incorporates margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences between instrumentation and the RPV bottom, temperature variations in downcomer regions, RVV opening delay, sensor response time, and module protection system processing time. As PZR level approaches 100 percent, analysis shows rapid pressure increase exceeding the LTOP setpoint. The inadvertent actuation block arming setpoint is set above the limiting RPV pressure for LTOP conditions, ensuring it does not prevent LTOP actuation. Three RVVs, along with their actuators and controls, provide LTOP protection even with a single active component failure. Further details on RVV qualification, design, and operation are provided in Section 6.3.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report describes integrated overpressure protection for secondary side components with the same design pressure as the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), ensuring ASME BPVC service limits are not exceeded during normal operations and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs). The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system is designed with sufficient capacity to prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding the pressure-temperature (P-T) limits specified in 10 CFR 50 Appendix G when below the LTOP enable temperature, thereby maintaining reactor pressure vessel (RPV) pressure below brittle fracture limits during all operational, maintenance, testing, or postulated accident conditions. During normal operational transients at full power, the system and core parameters are managed to prevent safety valve lift; if a safety valve does lift, the pressurizer (PZR) steam space is sized to avoid liquid discharge. LTOP analysis assumes a maximum PZR heater output of 880 kW and an additional 2 MW from core decay heat, demonstrating that reactor vent valves (RVVs) open before reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure exceeds the low temperature pressure limit. Table 5.2-10 provides LTOP pressure setpoints as a function of cold temperature, with PZR pressure limits ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, and LTOP not enabled above 318°F. Figure 5.2-4 illustrates the variable LTOP setpoint (PZR pressure) as a function of temperature, in relation to the saturation curve.

results.question: Using Table 5.3-3 and Figure 5.3-1, compare the irradiation effects on different RPV regions and explain why the weld has the highest ART.
results.expected_answer: Table 5.3-3 shows ARTs: shell 92.2 degrees F, weld 120.4 degrees F, bottom head 77.4 degrees F. Figure 5.3-1 shows the RPV cross section where the weld in the beltline region is directly adjacent to the core at the elevation of peak neutron fluence. The weld's higher initial RTNDT plus higher fluence exposure produces the highest ART.
results.generated_answer: At 57 EFPY, the weld region has the highest adjusted reference temperature (ART) of 120.4°F due to its higher copper and nickel content, which increases irradiation embrittlement compared to the shell (92.2°F) and bottom head (77.4°F); this is shown in Table 5.3-3 and the weld’s location and composition are detailed in Figure 5.3-1. (Source: NuScale FSAR, Table 5.3-3, Figure 5.3-1)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report demonstrates that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) values well above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb throughout the 60-year design life, with initial values of at least 75 ft-lb and predicted end-of-life values of 58.4 ft-lb (lower shell), 62.3 ft-lb (weld), and 62.9 ft-lb (bottom head) at 57 effective full-power years (EFPY). The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT for the limiting weld metal is 50°F at 0.55 EFPY, with 28°F due to a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening per 10 CFR 50.61 and RG 1.99 shows RTPTS values at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head), all below regulatory limits. Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The RPV is fabricated and inspected per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, with material specifications and chemical compositions detailed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.3-2. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, and clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. Major components are detailed in Figure 5.3-1, and all packaging, handling, and storage meet ASME and NQA-1 requirements. The RCPB material selections, as specified in Table 5.2-4, include nickel-based Alloy 718 (UNS N07718) for threaded fasteners, SA-508 Grade 3, Class 1 and 2 for RPV sections, SA-533 Type B, Class 2 for support gussets and plates, and austenitic stainless steels (SA-479/SA-240 Type 304/304L, 316/316L) for core barrel guides, support blocks, and instrument assemblies.

The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50, Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT for the limiting weld metal is 50°F at 0.55 EFPY, with 28°F of this due to a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening per 10 CFR 50.61 and RG 1.99 shows RTPTS values at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head), all below regulatory limits. Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The RPV is fabricated and inspected per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, with material specifications and chemical compositions detailed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.3-2. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, and diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, with clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. Major components are detailed in Figure 5.3-1, and all packaging, handling, and storage meet ASME and NQA-1 requirements. The surveillance and testing programs ensure ongoing compliance with fracture toughness, pressure-temperature, and inspection criteria throughout the vessel’s service life.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements, design parameters, material composition, and major components of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Table 5.2-6 specifies that RPV shell and head welds, including connections such as the lower RPV flange shell to RPV bottom head and steam plenum cap to integral steam plenum, are subject to B-A category volumetric examination, while internal welds like the core support block to RPV bottom head undergo B-N-2 category VT-3 visual testing; instrumentation and controls sleeve welds are part of the cladding and not separately examined. Table 5.3-1 lists key RPV parameters: design pressure is 2100 psia, design temperature is 650°F, overall height is 778 inches, and diameters range from 96.5 to 115.5 inches depending on section, with inner and outer clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches, respectively. Table 5.3-2 provides maximum allowable concentrations for RPV beltline forging material (e.g., sulfur 0.010 wt%, phosphorus 0.010 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%) and as-deposited weld metal (e.g., sulfur 0.015 wt%, phosphorus 0.012 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, manganese 1.80 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%). Figure 5.3-1 identifies major RPV components, including the lower and upper RPV sections, RPV head section, CRDM latch housing section with 16 latch housings, RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum with 4 access ports, upper and lower RPV SG shells, feed plenum access ports, lower RPV section flange shell, RPV bottom head, upper RPV flanged transition shell, CRDM seismic support structure, and 4 instrument seal assemblies.

5.4.1.1 
Design Basis
The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RCS to the secondary steam system and supply 
superheated steam to the steam and power conversion cycle as described in 
Chapter 10.Table 5.4-1 provides a summary of the operating conditions for thermal-hydraulic 
design of the SGs.The secondary plant parameters represent full-power steam flow 
conditions at the outlet of the steam plenums at best estimate primary coolant 
conditions.

The total heat transfer area of each of the two independent SGs 
includes a 10 percent tube plugging margin.A fouling factor is used when calculating end-of-cycle heat transfer performance and is 
provided in Table 5.4-2.The value is selected to account for typical operating 
experience for nuclear power plants that maintain the secondary system chemistry 
within the limits of the plant secondary water chemistry control program.

The 
design contains two independent, but intertwined, SGs located inside the RPV that 
facilitate heat transfer to the secondary coolant system and provide redundancy for the 
DHRS.The SGs are a once-through, helical-coil design, with primary-side reactor 
coolant outside the tubes and secondary-side fluid inside the tubes.On the primary 
side, the reactor coolant flows downward across the outside of the tubes, transferring 
heat to the fluid inside the SG tubes.

The helical shape of the SG tubing itself does not represent risk of 
degradation based on the minimum bend radius of the helical tubing being within the 
experience base of operating PWR SG designs.The SG tube support design is novel.However, as discussed in Section 5.4.1.2, it preserves attributes of the existing tube 
support (plate) designs.

Because these design aspects of the helical SGs are different from those used in 
the nuclear fleet, operational experience is not available and large-scale experimental 
data were needed for validation of NuScale thermal-hydraulic systems and design 
computer codes, as well as determination of SG performance characteristics.The objective of this testing was to determine the secondary side (inside tube) 
thermal-hydraulic performance of individual helical tubes representative of those used 
in the NPM steam generator design.

Each SG tube is comprised of a helix with bends at each end that transition from the 
helix to a straight configuration at the entry to the tubes sheets as shown in 
Figure 5.4-1.The helical tubes are seamless with no intermediate welds.The helical 
tubes terminate at the feed and steam plenum tubesheets, where the tubes are 
secured to the tubesheet by expansion fit and are welded to the tubesheet on the 
secondary side.

Steam flows through the SG piping, through nozzles 
penetrating the containment, and then to the main steam system and power 
conversion systems located outside the Reactor Building.The total SG heat transfer area provided in Table 5.4-2 comprises the outer surface area 
of the full length of tubes from the outer face of the feed plenums to the outer face of 
the steam plenums.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the steam generator (SG) design and operating conditions for the Reactor Coolant System. At full load, the SGs collectively transfer 159.13 MWt, with each SG capable of handling half this load. The SG operates at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with outlet and inlet temperatures of 584.4°F and 299.7°F, respectively, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. The SG is a helical, once-through type, featuring 1,380 helical tubes arranged in 21 columns per NuScale Power Module (NPM). Both the internal and external pressures for the secondary and primary sides are 2,100 psia, while the SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia. The internal and external temperatures are 650°F, with SG piping in containment at 550°F. Tubes have an outer diameter of 0.625 inches and a wall thickness of 0.050 inches. The steam tubesheet and feed tubesheet thicknesses (without clad) are 4.0 inches and 6.0 inches, respectively, with secondary and primary clad thicknesses of 0.250 inches and 0.375 inches. With clad, the steam and feed tubesheet thicknesses are 4.625 inches and 6.625 inches. The total heat transfer area is 17,928 ft², and the fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. The minimum SG tube transition bend radius is at least 6.250 inches. Figures in the report illustrate the helical tube bundle geometry, transition regions, tube support arrangements, and the use of support tabs for tube stability within the SG columns.

The feed and steam plena comprising a 
single SG are configured 180 degrees apart.As shown in Figure 5.4-1 and Figure 5.4-2, 
the configuration of the helical tube columns of the two SGs form an intertwined 
bundle of tubes around the upper riser assembly with a total of four feed and four 
steam plena located 90 degrees apart around the RPV.

1.5.1.4 
Steam Generator Thermal-Hydraulic Performance Testing - Fluid-Heated Facility
Subsequent to the SG tests described in Section 1.5.1.3 that used three electrically 
heated SG tubes, a second set of SG tests was conducted using a 252-tube bundle array 
that was fluid heated on the exterior of the tubes to more accurately represent primary 
side SG conditions.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) features two collocated steam generators (SGs) within the reactor pressure vessel, which transfer heat from the primary system to generate steam from feedwater via natural circulation under both normal and off-normal conditions. Thermal-hydraulic performance testing of the SGs was conducted in an electrically heated facility using Joule heating, where a known electrical current was applied to the tube walls to create a constant heat flux boundary condition. Three distinct heating zones were used to simulate subcooled, boiling, and superheat regions. Each SG includes a total heat transfer area with a 10% tube plugging margin, and a fouling factor (as specified in Table 5.4-2) is applied to account for typical end-of-cycle performance based on secondary water chemistry control. The SGs utilize seamless helical tubing, eliminating the need for traditional tube support plates; instead, austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies provide full-circumferential, though not continuous, support to minimize crevice formation and corrosion product buildup. Eight sets of tube support assemblies are positioned between each column of helical tubes, limiting unsupported tube lengths and ensuring sufficiently high modal frequencies to prevent unacceptable flow-induced vibration. The lower SG supports accommodate thermal growth and provide lateral support for the tube supports.

Tests specific to the NuScale fuel design are detailed in Sections 1.5.1.1 and 1.5.1.2, steam generator (SG) tests in Sections 1.5.1.3 and 1.5.1.4, control rod assembly tests in Sections 1.5.1.6 and 1.5.1.7, and integrated system phenomena tests in Section 1.5.1.5. Sensors, including thermocouples, pressure transducers, mass flow rate instruments, and strain gauges, were used to measure heater rod temperatures, fluid flow conditions, temperature, pressure, flow rate, and vibration at various points in the fluid loop and on both primary and secondary sides of the SG. These tests enabled NuScale to obtain fuel bundle subchannel exit data and benchmark thermal-hydraulic design and systems computer codes. Due to the unique helical SG design, which differs from those in the existing nuclear fleet, large-scale experimental data were required to validate NuScale’s thermal-hydraulic systems and computer codes, and to determine SG performance characteristics, particularly the secondary side (inside tube) thermal-hydraulic performance of individual helical tubes. The data also informed the sizing of SG inlet flow restrictors for stable secondary-side operation and defined steam outlet conditions as a function of primary heat generation and secondary side conditions. The SG test program was inspected by the NRC under Inspection Procedures IP 35034, 35017, and 36100. Analysis documented in Appendix A of TR-0516-49417 demonstrates that any SG secondary side flow oscillations are decoupled from primary side flow oscillations, ensuring that secondary side flow instabilities do not induce reactor power oscillations that could exceed fuel design limits.
