Preliminary critical heat flux (CHF) tests for the NuScale small modular reactor were conducted at Stern Laboratories in Ontario, Canada, using a high-pressure flow loop and custom 5x5 fuel assembly simulators with a 2-meter heated length, various axial power profiles, and configurations with and without simulated guide tubes. These tests examined the effects of shorter fuel length and low-flow natural circulation, generating data for the development and validation of the NuScale NSP2 CHF correlation. Additional CHF testing at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics (KATHY) facility in Germany, using prototypic fuel designs, provided further data to validate NSP2 and develop the NSP4 CHF correlation for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. CHF occurrence was identified by temperature excursions in the fuel simulators, and data collected included bundle subchannel exit temperatures for mixing coefficient determination and single-phase/two-phase pressure drop characteristics across various bundle powers and hydraulic conditions. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, prepared under 10 CFR 52.47(a), details the plant’s general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, including process effluent radiation monitoring (Section 11.5.1.2.2). The NuScale plant comprises 12 modules, each with a 160 MWth reactor and 50 MWe gross output, for a total net output of 570 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The core uses UO2 fuel enriched below 4.95% with a 24-month refueling interval. Safety features include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps. The containment is designed for high pressure to maintain integrity during worst-case accidents, minimizing radioactive releases. The FSAR references tables and figures detailing plant and module characteristics (Tables 1.2-1, 1.2-2), system boundaries (Figure 1.2-2), module schematics (Figure 1.2-3), multi-module layouts (Figures 1.2-4, 1.2-5), module cutaways (Figure 1.2-6), steam generator and reactor flow (Figure 1.2-7), technical reports (Table 1.6-2), and I&C diagrams (Table 1.7-1). The provided passages reference several tables and figures detailing the design and characteristics of the NuScale Power Plant and its modules. Table 1.2-1 presents the overall characteristics of a NuScale Power Plant, while Table 1.2-2 outlines specific design features of a NuScale Power Module. Figure 1.2-2 defines the functional boundaries of the NuScale system, and Figure 1.2-3 provides a schematic of a single NuScale Power Module with its associated secondary equipment. Figures 1.2-4 and 1.2-5 illustrate the layout of a multi-module NuScale plant and a cutaway of the 12-module configuration, respectively. Figure 1.2-6 offers a cutaway view of a NuScale Power Module, and Figure 1.2-7 depicts the steam generator and reactor flow. Table 1.6-2 lists referenced technical reports for NuScale, and Table 1.7-1 provides instrumentation and controls functional and electrical one-line diagrams. Additionally, NuScale Power, LLC’s report TR-0516-49417-P-A Revision 1 describes the evaluation methodology for stability analysis of the NuScale Power Module.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details several test programs and methodologies supporting the NuScale reactor design. Temperature measurements were used to determine mixing coefficients and to obtain single-phase and two-phase pressure drop characteristics across a range of bundle powers and hydraulic conditions, providing all necessary data for critical heat flux (CHF) correlation development and evaluation. The primary objective of the CHF testing program was to obtain CHF data for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology, thereby augmenting the existing database from the preliminary fuel design. Additionally, the NuScale integral system test program generated thermal-hydraulic data for system characterization and safety code validation using a scaled representation of the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design, also informing safety methodology development. NuScale specifically evaluates CHF, which is considered more applicable to its design than the departure from nucleate boiling ratio (DNBR). Reference is made to NuScale Power, LLC’s “NuScale Power Critical Heat Flux Correlations,” TR-0116-21012-P-A, Revision 1.

Preliminary critical heat flux (CHF) tests for the NuScale small modular reactor were conducted at Stern Laboratories in Ontario, Canada, using a high-pressure flow loop and custom 5x5 fuel assembly simulators with a 2-meter heated length, various axial power profiles, and configurations with and without simulated guide tubes. These tests examined the effects of shorter fuel length and low-flow natural circulation, generating data for the development and validation of the NuScale NSP2 CHF correlation. Additional CHF testing at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics (KATHY) facility in Germany, using prototypic fuel designs, provided further data to validate NSP2 and develop the NSP4 CHF correlation for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. CHF occurrence was identified by temperature excursions in the fuel simulators, and data collected included bundle subchannel exit temperatures for mixing coefficient determination and single-phase/two-phase pressure drop characteristics across various bundle powers and hydraulic conditions. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, prepared under 10 CFR 52.47(a), details the plant’s general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, including process effluent radiation monitoring (Section 11.5.1.2.2). The NuScale plant comprises 12 modules, each with a 160 MWth reactor and 50 MWe gross output, for a total net output of 570 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The core uses UO2 fuel enriched below 4.95% with a 24-month refueling interval. Safety features include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps. The containment is designed for high pressure to maintain integrity during worst-case accidents, minimizing radioactive releases. The FSAR references tables and figures detailing plant and module characteristics (Tables 1.2-1, 1.2-2), system boundaries (Figure 1.2-2), module schematics (Figure 1.2-3), multi-module layouts (Figures 1.2-4, 1.2-5), module cutaways (Figure 1.2-6), steam generator and reactor flow (Figure 1.2-7), technical reports (Table 1.6-2), and I&C diagrams (Table 1.7-1). The NuScale module (NPM) has a nominal gross electrical output of 50 MWe and a core thermal output of 160 MWt, compared to 1,

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details several test programs and methodologies supporting the NuScale reactor design. Temperature measurements were used to determine mixing coefficients and to obtain single-phase and two-phase pressure drop characteristics across a range of bundle powers and hydraulic conditions, providing all necessary data for critical heat flux (CHF) correlation development and evaluation. The primary objective of the CHF testing program was to obtain CHF data for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology, thereby augmenting the existing database from the preliminary fuel design. Additionally, the NuScale integral system test program generated thermal-hydraulic data for system characterization and safety code validation using a scaled representation of the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design, also informing safety methodology development. NuScale specifically evaluates CHF, which is considered more applicable to its design than the departure from nucleate boiling ratio (DNBR). Reference is made to NuScale Power, LLC’s “NuScale Power Critical Heat Flux Correlations,” TR-0116-21012-P-A, Revision 1.

For NuScale reactor events involving uncontrolled control rod assembly withdrawal from subcritical or low power startup conditions (SRP 15.4.1) and at power (SRP 15.4.2), as well as control rod misoperation (SRP 15.4.3), the use of critical heat flux (CHF) is considered more appropriate than departure from nucleate boiling ratio (DNBR) for assessing thermal margin limits. NuScale ensures fuel centerline melting limits are met during slower reactivity insertions by applying a heat generation rate limit. The uniform cladding strain criterion, specified in the SRP as applicable to BWRs, is not applicable to NuScale; instead, NuScale employs the 95/95 minimum critical heat flux ratio (MCHFR) approach to ensure no cladding or fuel failures. All relevant criteria for thermal margin limits and fuel centerline temperatures are met, with no additional notes for uniform cladding strain under control rod misoperation scenarios.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides detailed operational and design data for the primary system and decay heat removal system. Table 5.1-2 presents primary system temperatures and flow rates at various reactor power levels, with reactor power ranging from 0% (0 MWt) to 100% (160 MWt). At 100% power and best-estimate flow, the primary flow is 587.0 kg/s, with core ΔT of 99.8°F, TCold at 496.6°F, Tavg at 543.3°F, and THot at 590.1°F. Minimum and maximum design flows at 100% power are 538.5 kg/s and 660.5 kg/s, respectively, with corresponding temperature variations. When core decay heat is unavailable, the module heatup system drives natural circulation. Transient load contributions, including fatigue, are considered in component calculations as per Table 3.9-3. For conservative safety analysis, an initial core power of 102% is assumed to account for measurement uncertainty, and a 1.2 multiplier is applied to core decay heat. The decay heat removal system is designed with an internal pressure of 2100 psia, actuation valve external pressure of 60 psia, passive condenser external pressure of 27 psia, and operates at 650°F. Each of the two condensers contains 80 tubes (1.315-inch outer diameter, 0.109-inch wall thickness), with 258.2 ft² external surface area per condenser and a fouling factor of 0.0005 hr-ft²-F/BTU. The system schematic (Figure 5.4-10) shows two passive condensers located in the reactor pool within the containment vessel, with associated valves, pressure transmitters, level transmitters, and connections to the steam generators.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, details the design’s validation, regulatory conformance, and tailored application of industry standards for safety-related systems. NuScale partially conforms to several regulatory criteria, including accident monitoring instrumentation (IEEE Std 497-2002), use of computers in safety systems (RG 1.152), and software lifecycle processes (IEEE 1074-2006), adapting requirements to its I&C development lifecycle, which differs from the traditional waterfall model. Applicable tasks from IEEE standards such as 1012-2004, 1028-2008, 828-2005, 829-2008, 1008-1987, 830-1998, and 1074-2006 are mapped to NuScale’s processes, with exceptions to certain administrative requirements conflicting with established documentation practices. The design fully conforms to aircraft threat response strategies and environmental qualification for safety-related computer-based I&C systems in mild environments (RG 1.209), and addresses environmental effects on important-to-safety equipment. For electromagnetic and radio-frequency interference (RG 1.180) and environmental qualification (DSRS 3.11, RG 1.89), NuScale partially conforms, with some requirements designated for COL applicants. The Instrument Air Supply (IAS) system meets ANS/ISA S7.3-R1981 and Generic Letter 88-14, while setpoint methodology conforms to ISA-67.04.01-2006, addressing NRC RIS 2006-17. Safety classification of SSCs integrates deterministic and probabilistic methods, combining traditional definitions (10 CFR 50.2, RG 1.26, SRP 3.2.2) with an alternative risk-informed framework (10 CFR 50.69, RG 1.201, NEI 00-04), consistent with SECY-03-0047 and SECY-10-0034. The potable and sanitary water systems are designed to prevent flooding or adverse impacts on essential SSCs. NuScale’s electrical system partially conforms to NRC Generic Letter 88-15 and addresses SECY-91-078 by providing alternate power sources and direct offsite circuit connections to redundant safety divisions. The Fire Protection System contains no RTNSS equipment, and RTNSS guidance is partially incorporated into current regulations. Qualification of safety-related motor control centers and development of a risk-informed, performance-based inservice testing program (SRP 3.9.7) are not applicable, as these are either not used or are the responsibility of COL applicants.

The NuScale passive Advanced Light Water Reactor (ALWR) design adheres to the passive plant guidance outlined in SECY-93-087 and SECY-94-084, rather than SECY-90-016, as detailed in Table 1.9-8. SECY-91-078, which addresses evolutionary ALWR designs and Chapter 11 of the EPRI Requirements Document, is not directly applicable to passive plant designs like NuScale; however, Section C of SECY-91-078, concerning safe shutdown requirements for passive ALWRs, is relevant to the NuScale Power Plant. The design partially conforms to Section IV of SECY-96-128, which discusses policy and key technical issues for the Westinghouse AP600 standardized passive reactor. SECY-14-038, regarding a performance-based framework for nuclear power plant emergency preparedness oversight, is not applicable. The applicability of the SECY-90-016 Station Blackout (SBO) issue to passive ALWRs was deferred and addressed in SECY-94-084 and SECY-95-132, with NuScale conforming to the guidance in these documents. Additionally, fire protection positions focus on design configuration and features of the fire protection system and other management schemes to ensure safe reactor shutdown.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) details the verification and confirmation testing program for unique design features supporting the plant’s safety analysis (Section 1.5), and incorporates numerous topical and technical reports by reference (Section 1.6), as listed in Tables 1.6-1 and 1.6-2. These reports cover areas such as quality assurance (NP-TR-1010-859-NP-A, Rev. 5), risk significance, safety classification of passive electrical systems, protection system platforms, accident source term methodology, fuel methodology, nuclear analysis codes, stability analysis, LOCA evaluation, subchannel analysis, non-LOCA analysis, critical heat flux, rod ejection, and fuel assembly structural response, as well as technical topics like fluence calculation, advanced sensors, physical security, containment response, instrument setpoints, combustible gas control, vibration assessment, fuel storage, loss of large areas, and mitigation strategies for loss of all AC power. Section 1.9.5 and related tables (1.9-7, 1.9-8) provide a comprehensive conformance assessment with NRC regulatory criteria for advanced and evolutionary light-water reactor (ALWR) designs, referencing SECY-93-087, SECY-94-084, and other SECY documents, and noting partial or full conformance, exceptions, and applicability to passive plant designs. The FSAR follows the format of Regulatory Guide 1.206, with additional chapters on beyond-design-basis events (Ch. 20) and multi-module design (Ch. 21), and bases its technical content on the Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS) and Standard Review Plan (SRP), with conformance evaluations in Section 1.9.1 and Table 1.9-2. Sensitive information is withheld per 10 CFR 2.390 and RIS 2005-26, with public and non-public versions clearly marked. The NuScale design adheres to passive plant guidance from SECY-93-087 and SECY-94-084, with specific SECY documents (e.g., SECY-90-016, SECY-91-078, SECY-96-128, SECY-14-038) assessed for applicability and conformance, particularly regarding safe shutdown, station blackout, and fire protection. Referenced SECY documents addressing prototype decisions, regulatory treatment of non-safety systems, severe accident mitigation, and technical issue resolution have been incorporated into 10 CFR 52, NRC guidance, or are subject to rulemaking, with NuScale’s design conforming to these regulatory frameworks.

The provided passages detail the inapplicability of several regulatory guidance documents and technical requirements to the NuScale reactor design due to its reliance on passive safety features and natural circulation. Specifically, NuScale does not use reactor coolant pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, or safety-related motor-operated valves, making guidance on flywheel integrity, motor qualification, and thermal overload protection irrelevant. The design also does not employ safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, or safety injection tanks, so requirements for ECCS accumulator lines and related motor-operated valves do not apply. Additionally, NuScale does not use removal of electric power from safety-related valves to meet the single failure criterion, and guidance on gas accumulation in systems dependent on forced circulation pumps is not relevant. However, guidance remains applicable for evaluating loading transients and structural components for postulated breaks in CVCS piping and reactor vent valve piping. Containment vessel evacuation system valve closure times are addressed separately under SRP Section 6.2.4.

With respect to the NuScale design, compliance 
with and the applicability of 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, General Design Criteria (GDC) 55 and 57 is 
addressed in Section 3.1.

Tests specific to the NuScale fuel design are detailed in Sections 1.5.1.1 and 1.5.1.2, steam generator (SG) tests in Sections 1.5.1.3 and 1.5.1.4, control rod assembly tests in Sections 1.5.1.6 and 1.5.1.7, and integrated system phenomena tests in Section 1.5.1.5. Sensors, including thermocouples, pressure transducers, mass flow rate instruments, and strain gauges, were used to measure heater rod temperatures, fluid flow conditions, temperature, pressure, flow rate, and vibration at various points in the fluid loop and on both primary and secondary sides of the SG. These tests enabled NuScale to obtain fuel bundle subchannel exit data and benchmark thermal-hydraulic design and systems computer codes. Due to the unique helical SG design, which differs from those in the existing nuclear fleet, large-scale experimental data were required to validate NuScale’s thermal-hydraulic systems and computer codes, and to determine SG performance characteristics, particularly the secondary side (inside tube) thermal-hydraulic performance of individual helical tubes. The data also informed the sizing of SG inlet flow restrictors for stable secondary-side operation and defined steam outlet conditions as a function of primary heat generation and secondary side conditions. The SG test program was inspected by the NRC under Inspection Procedures IP 35034, 35017, and 36100. Analysis documented in Appendix A of TR-0516-49417 demonstrates that any SG secondary side flow oscillations are decoupled from primary side flow oscillations, ensuring that secondary side flow instabilities do not induce reactor power oscillations that could exceed fuel design limits.

Power, LLC design, including (1) the design bases and limits on its operation; (2) a safety 
analysis of the structures, systems, and components and of the facility as a whole; and (3) the

The NuScale Power, LLC design documentation includes the design bases, operational limits, a safety analysis of structures, systems, and components, and all information required by 10 CFR 52.47(a) relevant to the NuScale design. The design also considers conformance with the NRC's Design-Specific Review Standard (DSRS) for small modular reactors, as detailed in Table 1.9-3, and addresses NRC Interim Staff Guidance in Table 1.9-4. The NuScale system accommodates 10 CFR 50.54(o) and the test method frequencies of 10 CFR 50, Appendix J, Option A or B, with responsibility for Regulatory Guide (RG) compliance assigned to the COL applicant or licensee implementing Option B. Some referenced documents are not fully relevant to the NuScale design, but technically relevant features of Appendix K requirements are included in the analysis of small break LOCAs. While discussions influenced the development of NuScale Technical Specifications (TS), their specific content is not applicable to new generic TS in a Design Certification Application (DCA). Compliance with and applicability of 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, General Design Criteria (GDC) 55 and 57 for the NuScale design are addressed in Section 3.1.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the steam generator (SG) design and operating conditions for the Reactor Coolant System. At full load, the SGs collectively transfer 159.13 MWt, with each SG capable of handling half this load. The SG operates at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with outlet and inlet temperatures of 584.4°F and 299.7°F, respectively, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. The SG is a helical, once-through type, featuring 1,380 helical tubes arranged in 21 columns per NuScale Power Module (NPM). Both the internal and external pressures for the secondary and primary sides are 2,100 psia, while the SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia. The internal and external temperatures are 650°F, with SG piping in containment at 550°F. Tubes have an outer diameter of 0.625 inches and a wall thickness of 0.050 inches. The steam tubesheet and feed tubesheet thicknesses (without clad) are 4.0 inches and 6.0 inches, respectively, with secondary and primary clad thicknesses of 0.250 inches and 0.375 inches. With clad, the steam and feed tubesheet thicknesses are 4.625 inches and 6.625 inches. The total heat transfer area is 17,928 ft², and the fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. The minimum SG tube transition bend radius is at least 6.250 inches. Figures in the report illustrate the helical tube bundle geometry, transition regions, tube support arrangements, and the use of support tabs for tube stability within the SG columns.

Because these design aspects of the helical SGs are different from those used in 
the nuclear fleet, operational experience is not available and large-scale experimental 
data were needed for validation of NuScale thermal-hydraulic systems and design 
computer codes, as well as determination of SG performance characteristics.The objective of this testing was to determine the secondary side (inside tube) 
thermal-hydraulic performance of individual helical tubes representative of those used 
in the NPM steam generator design.

The NuScale SG tube wall thickness is thicker than 
existing designs (see Table 5.4-2) based on incorporation of a substantial degradation 
allowance (additional tube wall thickness above minimum required for design) as 
discussed in Section 5.4.1.2.The NPM reactor coolant flowrates are lower than the 
flowrates across the SG tubes in PWR recirculating steam generators as discussed in 
Section 5.1.

NuScale conducted a comprehensive suite of tests to validate its unique fuel and steam generator (SG) designs, employing detailed programs as outlined in Sections 1.5.1.1–1.5.1.7. Fuel-specific tests included two critical heat flux (CHF) programs—one for preliminary and one for final fuel designs—using vertical 5x5 electrically-heated simulated fuel bundles with AREVA HTP™/HMP™ grid technology, covering a wide range of hydraulic conditions, axial power shapes, and configurations (including guide tube substitutions). Instrumentation such as thermocouples, resistance temperature detectors (RTDs), pressure transducers, mass flow rate instruments, and strain gauges measured heater rod temperatures, fluid flow, pressure, and vibration on both primary and secondary SG sides. CHF was detected by excursions in fuel simulator thermocouples as bundle power was incrementally increased at specified flow, temperature, and pressure conditions, with all test execution and acceptance following industry standards. Large-scale SG tests, required due to the helical design’s departure from conventional nuclear fleet SGs, were performed at the SIET facility in Italy, using both electrically-heated isolated tubes and a 252-tube helical bundle fluid-heated on the exterior to model primary-side conditions. These tests characterized secondary-side thermal-hydraulic performance, informed SG inlet flow restrictor sizing, and defined steam outlet conditions. Dynamic pressure measurements supported evaluation of two-phase boiling-induced pressure fluctuations and flow-induced vibration risks. The SG test program was inspected by the NRC under IP 35034, 35017, and 36100, and analysis in Appendix A of TR-0516-49417 confirmed that secondary-side flow oscillations are decoupled from the primary side, preventing reactor power oscillations that could exceed fuel design limits. All heater bundles were verified to meet vendor acceptance criteria, with power ratings within 2% of nominal. Facility design ensured accessibility for inspection and maintenance to minimize technician dose and refueling time.

Tests specific to the NuScale fuel design 
are summarized in Section 1.5.1.1 and Section 1.5.1.2; tests specific to the steam generator 
(SG) are summarized in Section 1.5.1.3 and Section 1.5.1.4; tests specific to the control rod 
assemblies are summarized in Section 1.5.1.6 and Section 1.5.1.7; and tests involving 
integrated system phenomena are summarized in Section 1.5.1.5.

The safety-related Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature of the NPM design, with its reliability evaluated through the reliability assurance program (Section 17.4) and risk significance determined per Chapter 19 guidance; its classification and risk categories are detailed in Table 3.2-1. The DHRS meets 10 CFR 50 Appendix A General Design Criteria (GDC 1, 2, and 4), is classified as Quality Group B, and is designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected as Class 2 in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, adhering to the highest quality standards per the Quality Assurance Program (Chapter 17). The DHRS is engineered to withstand natural phenomena and remain functional under normal, maintenance, testing, and postulated accident conditions. DHRS actuation valves, piping, and passive condensers are Quality Group B, Class 2 per BPVC Section III, and remain operable after a design basis seismic event; supports are Class 2 per BPVC Section III, Subsection NF. Classification details and applicability are in Chapter 3, while Section 6.1 covers material selection, fabrication methods, and fluid compatibility, with Table 6.1-1 listing material specifications for DHRS components and supports. Welding follows ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NC-4300, and Section IX. The DHRS condenser, actuation valves, and piping are Seismic Category I, and the system is protected from natural phenomena by the reactor building. DHRS instrumentation and control components are seismically qualified per IEEE 344-2004. Table 5.4-8 provides the DHRS failure modes and effects analysis. Each NPM and its DHRS are housed within the Reactor Building for protection from external events (Section 3.8), with DHRS piping branching from the main steam lines outside and above the CNV, upstream of the MSIVs. Section 3.9 describes the vibration assessment program, identifying that DHRS components exposed to secondary side flow are susceptible to acoustic resonance, with no other credible flow-induced vibration mechanisms.

results.question: Compare the Ch.01 description of NuScale's eliminated RCPs with the Ch.05 analysis of natural circulation flow rates.
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 lists RCPs as eliminated, with 0 pumps compared to 4 in a typical PWR. Ch.05 quantifies the natural circulation flow: 587 kg/s at 100% power, adequate for heat removal at NuScale's power density. The absence of RCPs eliminates loss-of-flow accidents and RCP seal LOCAs.

retrieved_contexts: The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details several test programs and methodologies supporting the NuScale reactor design. Temperature measurements were used to determine mixing coefficients and to obtain single-phase and two-phase pressure drop characteristics across a range of bundle powers and hydraulic conditions, providing all necessary data for critical heat flux (CHF) correlation development and evaluation. The primary objective of the CHF testing program was to obtain CHF data for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology, thereby augmenting the existing database from the preliminary fuel design. Additionally, the NuScale integral system test program generated thermal-hydraulic data for system characterization and safety code validation using a scaled representation of the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design, also informing safety methodology development. NuScale specifically evaluates CHF, which is considered more applicable to its design than the departure from nucleate boiling ratio (DNBR). Reference is made to NuScale Power, LLC’s “NuScale Power Critical Heat Flux Correlations,” TR-0116-21012-P-A, Revision 1.

The NuScale reactor design and safety analysis methodologies are detailed in topical reports TR-0516-49416 and TR-0516-49422, with pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 EFPY calculated per ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects using Regulatory Guide 1.99 and ASTM E185-82 for surveillance. Fluence and pressure-temperature limits are further addressed in TR-0116-20781-P and TR-1015-18177-P. The design does not require certain systems post-accident and does not credit them in radiological consequence analyses, fully applying the single-failure criterion. NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, justifying departures in a topical report, and applies a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for Chapter 15 events, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models addressing only relevant features per Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30. Boric acid concentrations are selected to bound all operating conditions, with NRC-inspected testing per IP35034.1.5.2 and startup testing detailed in Section 14.2. The design does not use combustible gas control systems, supporting exemptions to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), 50.34(f)(2)(xiv), and GDC 27, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, for containment isolation valve closure times. Hydrogen control is not required, as combustion does not affect CNV integrity. Post-accident assessment relies on radiation monitors and core exit temperature, supporting exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding post-accident sampling. Additional exemptions include 10 CFR 50.46a, 50.34(f)(2)(vi) (high point venting), and portions of 50.34(f)(2)(xx) (pressurizer level indicators). The CVCS does not include holdup tanks under vacuum but provides required venting and draining. The RPV high-point degasification vent is not safety-significant, and high-point vents are not needed. The LTOP system is not connected to a low-pressure system, but guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation applies. NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, for LOCA mass and energy release analysis, and requires no additional ITAAC. The design ensures no core uncovery during LOCA, conforms to DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, and GDC 5 for sharing of SSCs, does

Preliminary critical heat flux (CHF) tests for the NuScale small modular reactor were conducted at Stern Laboratories in Ontario, Canada, using a high-pressure flow loop and custom 5x5 fuel assembly simulators with a 2-meter heated length, various axial power profiles, and configurations with and without simulated guide tubes. These tests examined the effects of shorter fuel length and low-flow natural circulation, generating data for the development and validation of the NuScale NSP2 CHF correlation. Additional CHF testing at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics (KATHY) facility in Germany, using prototypic fuel designs, provided further data to validate NSP2 and develop the NSP4 CHF correlation for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. CHF occurrence was identified by temperature excursions in the fuel simulators, and data collected included bundle subchannel exit temperatures for mixing coefficient determination and single-phase/two-phase pressure drop characteristics across various bundle powers and hydraulic conditions. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, prepared under 10 CFR 52.47(a), details the plant’s general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, including process effluent radiation monitoring (Section 11.5.1.2.2). The NuScale plant comprises 12 modules, each with a 160 MWth reactor and 50 MWe gross output, for a total net output of 570 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The core uses UO2 fuel enriched below 4.95% with a 24-month refueling interval. Safety features include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps. The containment is designed for high pressure to maintain integrity during worst-case accidents, minimizing radioactive releases. The FSAR references tables and figures detailing plant and module characteristics (Tables 1.2-1, 1.2-2), system boundaries (Figure 1.2-2), module schematics (Figure 1.2-3), multi-module layouts (Figures 1.2-4, 1.2-5), module cutaways (Figure 1.2-6), steam generator and reactor flow (Figure 1.2-7), technical reports (Table 1.6-2), and I&C diagrams (Table 1.7-1). The NuScale module (NPM) has a nominal gross electrical output of 50 MWe and a core thermal output of 160 MWt, compared to 1,

results.question: Compare the NuScale design life from Ch.01 with the RPV embrittlement projections in Ch.05.
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 specifies a 60-year design life. Ch.05 projects RPV embrittlement to 57 EFPY with adjusted reference temperatures (max 120.4 degrees F for welds) well below PTS screening criteria of 270/300 degrees F, confirming adequate margin for the 60-year life.
results.generated_answer:
The NuScale design life is 60 years (Ch.01), and RPV embrittlement projections in Ch.05 show Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy values at end-of-life (57 EFPY) of 58.4–62.9 ft-lb, all above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb, confirming material integrity throughout the design life (Ch.05).  
(Source: NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Ch.01 and Ch.05)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) Material Surveillance Program follows ASTM E185-82 requirements, as detailed in Table 5.3-4, using archived materials identical to those used for baseline and capsule specimens, but maintained as full-thickness sections for flexibility in future specimen fabrication. Archive materials are sourced from actual production forgings and weldments using the same weld wire heat and flux lot as production welds. The NuScale RPV is designed for a 60-year life, with the surveillance capsule withdrawal schedule for the first 40 years based on 32 effective full-power years (EFPY), per ASTM E185-82 Table 1. Three capsules suffice for the initial 40-year period, with a fourth capsule included for potential regulatory requirements at license extension. Capsules are housed in holders attached to the core barrel at mid-core height, positioned approximately 90 degrees apart to achieve a lead factor of ~2.5. The withdrawal schedule (Table 5.3-5) specifies: the 1st capsule at the first refuel outage (~2 EFPY or 1.55E+18 n/cm², E > 1 MeV), the 2nd at ~13 EFPY (when capsule fluence matches peak 32-EFPY RPV inside surface fluence), and the 3rd between ~13 and ~26 EFPY. The 4th capsule's withdrawal will be determined by regulations at license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT of 50°F for the limiting weld metal is reached at 4.27E+17 n/cm² (E > 1 MeV, 0.55 EFPY), but 28°F of this shift is a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F, likely causing overcompensation at low fluence. Therefore, the first capsule may be withdrawn at the end of the first fuel cycle rather than mid-cycle.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report and supporting documentation confirm that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) values well above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb throughout the 60-year design life, with initial values of at least 75 ft-lb and predicted end-of-life values, after irradiation and temperature corrections, of 58.4 ft-lb (lower shell), 62.3 ft-lb (weld), and 62.9 ft-lb (bottom head) at 57 effective full-power years (EFPY). The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50, Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT for the limiting weld metal is 50°F at 0.55 EFPY, with 28°F of this due to a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening per 10 CFR 50.61 and RG 1.99 shows RTPTS values at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head), all below regulatory limits. Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The RPV is fabricated and inspected per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, with material specifications and chemical compositions detailed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.3-2. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, and diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, with clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. Major components are detailed in Figure 5.3-1, and all packaging, handling, and storage meet ASME and NQA-1 requirements. The surveillance and testing programs ensure ongoing compliance with fracture toughness, pressure-temperature, and inspection criteria throughout the vessel’s service life.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report demonstrates that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) values well above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb throughout the 60-year design life, with initial values of at least 75 ft-lb and predicted end-of-life values of 58.4 ft-lb (lower shell), 62.3 ft-lb (weld), and 62.9 ft-lb (bottom head) at 57 effective full-power years (EFPY). The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT for the limiting weld metal is 50°F at 0.55 EFPY, with 28°F due to a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening per 10 CFR 50.61 and RG 1.99 shows RTPTS values at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head), all below regulatory limits. Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The RPV is fabricated and inspected per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, with material specifications and chemical compositions detailed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.3-2. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, and clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. Major components are detailed in Figure 5.3-1, and all packaging, handling, and storage meet ASME and NQA-1 requirements. The RCPB material selections, as specified in Table 5.2-4, include nickel-based Alloy 718 (UNS N07718) for threaded fasteners, SA-508 Grade 3, Class 1 and 2 for RPV sections, SA-533 Type B, Class 2 for support gussets and plates, and austenitic stainless steels (SA-479/SA-240 Type 304/304L, 316/316L) for core barrel guides, support blocks, and instrument assemblies.

The NuScale reactor vessel beltline materials are evaluated for Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) in accordance with Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.99, Rev. 2, to ensure compliance with the 10 CFR 50, Appendix G requirement of a minimum end-of-life USE of 50 ft-lb at 57 effective full power years (EFPY) fluence, which bounds the 60-year design life. The initial Charpy USE is 75 ft-lb. Table 5.3-9 summarizes the predicted 1/4-T Charpy USE decrease at 57-EFPY fluence: for the lower RPV shell beltline (0.10 wt% Cu, 1.37E+19 n/cm², E > 1 MeV), the USE decreases by 20.5% to 59.6 ft-lb; for the lower RPV weld (0.06 wt% Cu, 3.21E+18 n/cm²), the decrease is 15.3% to 63.5 ft-lb; and for the RPV bottom head (0.10 wt% Cu, 3.21E+18 n/cm²), the decrease is 14.5% to 64.1 ft-lb. Table 5.3-10 provides the final 1/4-T Charpy USE values after adjusting for NuScale reactor pressure vessel irradiation temperature, applying a -1.2 ft-lb correction: lower RPV shell beltline, 58.4 ft-lb; lower RPV weld, 62.3 ft-lb; and RPV bottom head, 62.9 ft-lb. These values meet the regulatory minimum, and the Material Surveillance program monitors changes in fracture toughness properties throughout the vessel’s service life.

results.question: Compare the Ch.01 overview of containment features with the Ch.05 detailed containment role in ECCS operation.
results.expected_answer: Ch.01 describes the CNV as a steel vessel under vacuum providing passive containment. Ch.05 explains the CNV's dual role in ECCS: it receives steam from RVVs which condenses on the cool CNV wall, and RRVs return condensed water to the RPV. The CNV effectively acts as both containment barrier and ECCS component.
results.generated_answer:
Ch.01 provides a high-level overview stating the containment is integral to the NPM, providing primary containment for the RCS, while Ch.05 details that during ECCS operation, the containment vessel (CNV) passively condenses steam vented from the RPV, collects water, and enables passive heat transfer to the reactor pool, ensuring long-term core cooling and containment integrity.  
(Source: Context sections 1.2.1.2.2 and detailed ECCS operation description)
retrieved_contexts:
1.2.1.2.2 
Containment Systems
The containment is an integral part of the NPM and provides primary containment 
for the RCS.Section 6.2 provides further information for the containment system.

Maintenance activity controls are implemented to ensure that emergency core cooling system (ECCS) function is not compromised by changes to analytical inputs, assumptions, or activities that could introduce debris or potential debris sources into containment. Additional controls limit the introduction of coating materials into containment, and an inspection program verifies containment vessel cleanliness prior to closure for normal power operation. For any in-containment event resulting in core damage or degradation, containment isolation is automatically actuated on low-low pressurizer level and high containment pressure, providing a reliable alternative means to prevent radiological release from the containment evacuation system (CES) to the environment, consistent with acceptance criteria. The containment high pressure analytical limit is subatmospheric, ensuring that any setpoint up to this limit will prevent environmental release. Design basis events meet all thermal and hydraulic acceptance criteria without relying on CES isolation via high radiation signals, and no design basis event results in core damage or degradation. Section 19.2 analyses confirm that severe accident conditions resulting in core damage also generate reliable containment isolation signals, independent of high containment radiation isolation. Calculated peak containment pressures for design basis events remain below the containment vessel (CNV) internal design pressure, and even under severe accident progression, peak pressures do not challenge containment integrity, as referenced in TR-0716-50424, Section 2.8.

The ECCS, including the design, operation, single failure capability, 
and protection from inadvertent actuation of the RVVs, is described in Section 6.3.The RCS does not include separate safety-related high-point vent capability.

Maintenance activity controls are implemented to ensure Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) functionality is not compromised by changes to analytical inputs, assumptions, or activities that could introduce debris or potential debris sources into containment. Additional controls limit the introduction of coating materials, and an inspection program verifies containment vessel (CNV) cleanliness before closure for power operation. Automatic containment isolation is actuated on low-low pressurizer level and high containment pressure during in-containment events with core damage, reliably preventing radiological release from the containment evacuation system (CES). The containment high pressure analytical limit is subatmospheric, ensuring setpoints prevent environmental release, and all design basis events meet thermal and hydraulic criteria without relying on CES isolation via high radiation signals; no design basis event results in core damage. Section 19.2 analyses confirm severe accident conditions also generate reliable containment isolation signals, independent of high radiation. Calculated peak containment pressures for design basis events remain below the CNV internal design pressure, and even under severe accident progression, peak pressures do not challenge containment integrity (TR-0716-50424, Section 2.8). The CNV is an ASME BPVC Class MC (steel) containment, designed, fabricated, and tested as a Class 1 pressure vessel, maintained at a vacuum during normal operations to eliminate insulation, minimize moisture and corrosion, facilitate leakage detection, limit oxygen for combustible gas control, and increase steam condensation rates during accidents. The CNV contains radioactive material post-accident, provides heat rejection to the reactor pool after ECCS actuation, and supports the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Manways and penetrations allow access and accommodate process piping, electrical, and instrumentation. Approximately 90% of the CNV is immersed in the reactor pool, which acts as a passive heat sink. During ECCS actuation, steam vented from the RPV causes a temporary spike in containment pressure and temperature, but passive cooling and condensation by the reactor pool rapidly reduce and maintain these parameters below design limits. Water lost from the RPV during an accident remains within containment and is passively returned, with no postulated small-break LOCA capable of uncovering fuel. The low-power core (160 MWt) reduces decay heat removal requirements and simplifies emergency planning. The absence of RPV insulation eliminates potential sump screen blockage and allows vessel exterior cooling. Passive safety systems ensure cooling and depressurization of the RPV/CNV even with loss of external power. When decay heat and RCS temperature are sufficiently low, the CNV is filled to the pressurizer baffle plate by the containment flooding system, and passive cooldown continues via heat transfer to the reactor pool. During ECCS operation, reactor vent valves (RVVs) vent steam and noncondensible gases from the RPV and pressurizer directly into the CNV, where steam condenses and collects at the bottom, while reactor recirculation valves (RRVs) allow water to reenter the RPV and circulate through

During Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) operation, reactor vent valves (RVVs) vent steam and noncondensible gases from the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) and pressurizer (PZR) directly into the containment vessel (CNV), where the steam condenses and collects at the bottom. Reactor recirculation valves (RRVs) allow water to reenter the RPV and circulate through the core. As reactor coolant temperature drops below boiling, core cooling continues via conduction into the reactor pool. The ECCS cooling function is entirely passive, relying on heat conduction through the CNV wall to the reactor pool, with further details provided in Section 6.3. Upon stable shutdown, long-term decay heat removal is achieved by conduction and convection through the flooded containment and CNV shell to the reactor pool. When Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure falls below the ECCS valve inadvertent actuation block threshold, the RVVs and RRVs may be opened to promote circulation between the RCS and flooded containment. The pressure-retaining portions of the RCS maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), serving as a barrier to radionuclide release. There are no mechanisms for accumulation of noncondensible gases in the RPV during ECCS operation, as the open RVVs provide a direct vent path to containment, eliminating the need for additional high-point venting and ensuring long-term cooling is not adversely affected. The Control Room Habitability System (CRHS) protects operators from accidental releases of toxic or radioactive gases.

The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) for the PWR, including the Boron Recovery System, partially conforms to DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0 requirements regarding functional performance during adverse environmental phenomena, pumping capacity, and defense-in-depth RCS makeup, as its only safety-related function is to preclude inadvertent boron dilution of the reactor coolant system. The system conforms to single failure criteria and GDC 5, which apply solely to the two safety-related demineralized water isolation valves designed to prevent inadvertent boron dilution. The CVCS also conforms to requirements for minimization of contamination, system design and arrangement, detection of reactor coolant leakage outside containment, and ITAAC, with no additional comments. However, it only partially conforms to criteria for prevention of CVCS holdup tank wall buckling/failure and CVCS venting and draining, as some acceptance criteria reference CVCS designs not applicable to the NuScale design.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, and supporting documentation emphasize that many regulatory criteria and guidance documents—especially those tailored to Boiling Water Reactors (BWRs) and large Pressurized Water Reactors (PWRs)—are inapplicable to NuScale due to its unique, passive design. The plant eliminates forced reactor coolant flow, coolant loops, pumps, and several auxiliary systems, rendering guidance such as RG 1.79.1, BTP 3-2, SRP 4.6 II.8, BTP 10-1, SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5, and requirements for core spray and low pressure coolant injection systems (10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(viii)) non-applicable. The Strategic Water Chemistry Plan aligns with EPRI PWR Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines (e.g., EPRI #3002000505) and fuel vendor recommendations, specifying impurity limits (chloride, fluoride, sulfate ≤ 0.05 ppm; hydrogen 25–50 cc/kg; boron 0–2000 ppm), sampling frequencies, corrective actions, and chemistry parameters per RG 1.44, Rev 1. The plan ensures reactor pressure boundary and fuel integrity through alkaline-reducing chemistry, controlled pH, lithium, dissolved hydrogen, and zinc addition to mitigate PWSCC and radiation fields. Effluent release and coolant source term calculations follow NUREG-0017, PWR-GALE86, ANSI/ANS 18.1-1999, and NuScale technical reports, with decontamination factors and failed fuel fractions well below 0.25%. Partial conformance is noted with RG 1.110, RG 1.112, and 10 CFR 50.34(b)(3), 50.34a, and 52.79(a)(3), while DC/COL-ISG-5 and guidance for CAOC, SG blowdown, and once-through/top-feed/preheat steam generators (BTP 4-1, SRP 10.4.8, SRP 10.4.6, BTP 10-2) are not applicable; only OTSGD B.2 test acceptance criteria for once-through SGs are relevant. Flow Accelerated Corrosion is addressed per DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0. For LOCA and recirculation cooling (RG 1.82), NuScale meets the intent but differs in system design, supporting exemptions for 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) and portions of Appendix K, and partially conforming to radiological consequences of main steam line failures. The design omits reactor coolant pumps, safety-related pumps, Class 1E motors, nonmetallic insulation, safety injection systems, auxiliary feedwater, power-operated pressurizer relief valves, and pressurizer heaters, instead

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents several departures and conformances with regulatory criteria. Positions C1.1.3 and C1.1.4 are not applicable because the NuScale design does not rely on operator action or active devices to mitigate or prevent debris accumulation. The design does not comply with regulatory position C1.3.12 (prototypical head loss testing), nor with position C.2 except for meeting the intent of chemical reaction effects (position 2.2), and does not comply with position C.3. For DSRS 5.4.7 (Decay Heat Removal System), the design partially conforms to GDC 54, featuring a closed-loop DHRS outside containment directly connected to the closed-loop SG system within the RPV, providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and the reactor pool outside the NPM. Regarding DSRS 6.2.2 (Containment Heat Removal Systems), the design supports an exemption from the power provisions of GDC 38. For DSRS 6.2.5 (Combustible Gas Control in Containment), GDC 42 and 43 are not applicable as the design lacks a containment atmospheric cleanup system. In DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, the design conforms to fuel cladding integrity requirements and does not include a steam pressure regulator. The exclusion zone and buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability review (DSRS 15.9.A) is not used in the NuScale design. Finally, for DC/COL-ISG-5, the NuScale design is similar to large PWRs in the existing fleet regarding effluent release calculations, rendering the specific interim staff guidance not applicable.

The passages summarize the evaluation of NuScale’s conformance with DSRS 15.1.1-15.1.4, Rev 0, which addresses events such as decrease in feedwater temperature, increase in feedwater flow, increase in steam flow, inadvertent opening of the turbine bypass system, and inadvertent operation of the decay heat removal system. The basic objectives are to identify limiting heat removal events and verify that fuel damage and system pressure criteria are met for these events. Specific criteria include maintaining the Minimum Critical Heat Flux Ratio (MCHFR) above the 95/95 limit, with NuScale noting that critical heat flux is a more accurate descriptor than departure from nucleate boiling. Anticipated Operational Occurrences (AOOs) should not escalate to more serious conditions without independent faults, and NuScale’s event classification aligns with SRP requirements. Instrument spans and setpoints conform to RG 1.105. Analytical parameters include an initial power level of 102%, use of conservative scram assumptions, and consideration of core burnup. For DSRS 15.2.7, Rev 0 (Loss of Normal Feedwater Flow), NuScale meets fuel and system pressure parameters and categorizes events as AOOs and Initiating Events (IEs), conforming to regulatory expectations.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements, design parameters, material composition, and major components of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Table 5.2-6 specifies that RPV shell and head welds, including connections such as the lower RPV flange shell to RPV bottom head and steam plenum cap to integral steam plenum, are subject to B-A category volumetric examination, while internal welds like the core support block to RPV bottom head undergo B-N-2 category VT-3 visual testing; instrumentation and controls sleeve welds are part of the cladding and not separately examined. Table 5.3-1 lists key RPV parameters: design pressure is 2100 psia, design temperature is 650°F, overall height is 778 inches, and diameters range from 96.5 to 115.5 inches depending on section, with inner and outer clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches, respectively. Table 5.3-2 provides maximum allowable concentrations for RPV beltline forging material (e.g., sulfur 0.010 wt%, phosphorus 0.010 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%) and as-deposited weld metal (e.g., sulfur 0.015 wt%, phosphorus 0.012 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, manganese 1.80 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%). Figure 5.3-1 identifies major RPV components, including the lower and upper RPV sections, RPV head section, CRDM latch housing section with 16 latch housings, RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum with 4 access ports, upper and lower RPV SG shells, feed plenum access ports, lower RPV section flange shell, RPV bottom head, upper RPV flanged transition shell, CRDM seismic support structure, and 4 instrument seal assemblies.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides a detailed comparison between the NuScale Power Plant and a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR) based on nominal values. The NuScale module (NPM) has a nominal gross electrical output of 50 MWe and a core thermal output of 160 MWt, compared to 1,186 MWe and 3,411 MWt for a typical PWR. NuScale uses 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, 6.56 ft effective length, 264 rods per assembly) versus 193 assemblies (17x17 lattice, 12 ft length) in a PWR. The average linear heat rate is 2.5 kW/ft for NuScale and 5.4 kW/ft for PWR. NuScale has 16 control rod assemblies and a 60-year design life, compared to 53 assemblies and 40 years for PWR. The NuScale reactor coolant system has no external loops or coolant pipes, operates at 1,850 psia, and uses a stacked stainless steel reflector block for thermal shielding, with top-mounted in-core instrumentation. Its steam generator is a helical coil type (2 units, ~18,000 ft² area, 1,380 tubes), while PWR uses four vertical U-tube generators (55,000 ft², 5,626 tubes). NuScale has no reactor coolant pumps, pressurizer surge nozzle, or residual heat removal pumps, and its containment is a steel pressure vessel (14 ft 2 in. diameter, 75 ft 8.5 in. height), compared to a PCCV (140 ft diameter, 205 ft height) for PWR. Many traditional PWR safety systems—such as containment spray, high/low pressure safety injection, accumulators, emergency diesel generators, and emergency feedwater pumps—are not present in NuScale. The NuScale I&C system is digital, and it uses 6 reactor component cooling water pumps for 12 NPMs. Table 1.3-2 shows that both designs include core protection systems such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment vessel, reactor coolant system, decay heat removal, emergency core cooling, control rod drive, containment isolation, and ultimate heat sink. However, several PWR safety systems (e.g., residual heat removal, safety injection, auxiliary feedwater, emergency service water, hydrogen recombiner, containment spray, reactor coolant pumps, safety-related electrical distribution, off-site power, emergency diesel generators, Class 1E battery, and ATWS system) are not required in the NuScale design.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report and supporting documentation confirm that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) values well above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb throughout the 60-year design life, with initial values of at least 75 ft-lb and predicted end-of-life values, after irradiation and temperature corrections, of 58.4 ft-lb (lower shell), 62.3 ft-lb (weld), and 62.9 ft-lb (bottom head) at 57 effective full-power years (EFPY). The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50, Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT for the limiting weld metal is 50°F at 0.55 EFPY, with 28°F of this due to a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening per 10 CFR 50.61 and RG 1.99 shows RTPTS values at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head), all below regulatory limits. Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The RPV is fabricated and inspected per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, with material specifications and chemical compositions detailed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.3-2. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, and diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, with clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. Major components are detailed in Figure 5.3-1, and all packaging, handling, and storage meet ASME and NQA-1 requirements. The surveillance and testing programs ensure ongoing compliance with fracture toughness, pressure-temperature, and inspection criteria throughout the vessel’s service life.

The NuScale design was evaluated against DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0, which covers initiating events such as Loss of External Load, Turbine Trip, Loss of Condenser Vacuum, Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve (MSIV), and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed). For the basic objectives regarding initiating events (II.1), the design conforms fully. For specific criteria for events of moderate frequency (II.2), the design partially conforms because NuScale does not have a steam pressure regulator. The design conforms to criteria for Reactor Coolant System and Main Steam System Pressures (II.2.A), Incidents of Moderate Frequency (II.2.C), Instrument Setpoints and their impact on plant response (II.2.D), Most Limiting Plant System Single Failure (II.2.E), and Performance of Nonsafety-Related Systems and Single Failures of Active and Passive Systems (II.2.F). The analyzed Anticipated Operational Occurrences (AOOs) that could lead to Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) overpressure include loss of load, turbine trip (with and without bypass), loss of condenser vacuum, inadvertent MSIV closure, steam pressure regulator failure closed, loss of normal AC power, loss of feedwater, and inadvertent operation of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS). These AOOs are detailed in DCD Chapter 15, including initial conditions, system parameters, analysis assumptions, systems and equipment assumed to operate, reactor trip signals, and sensitivity analyses.

The NuScale design does not utilize safety-related pumps. In the event of reactor coolant system overpressure, fluid is relieved directly to the containment vessel via the overpressure protection system. Table 1.9-3 documents conformance with NUREG-0800, Standard Review Plan (SRP), and Design Specific Review Standard (DSRS) across multiple sections, including 14.2, 15.1.1–15.1.4, 15.1.5, 15.1.6, 15.2.8, 15.4.2, 15.4.6, 15.6.1, and 15.6.6. The design is noted as conforming to the applicable SRP and DSRS requirements, with no additional comments provided for these sections.

The NuScale reactor design and safety analysis methodologies are detailed in topical reports TR-0516-49416 and TR-0516-49422, with pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 EFPY calculated per ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects using Regulatory Guide 1.99 and ASTM E185-82 for surveillance. Fluence and pressure-temperature limits are further addressed in TR-0116-20781-P and TR-1015-18177-P. The design does not require certain systems post-accident and does not credit them in radiological consequence analyses, fully applying the single-failure criterion. NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, justifying departures in a topical report, and applies a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for Chapter 15 events, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models addressing only relevant features per Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30. Boric acid concentrations are selected to bound all operating conditions, with NRC-inspected testing per IP35034.1.5.2 and startup testing detailed in Section 14.2. The design does not use combustible gas control systems, supporting exemptions to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), 50.34(f)(2)(xiv), and GDC 27, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, for containment isolation valve closure times. Hydrogen control is not required, as combustion does not affect CNV integrity. Post-accident assessment relies on radiation monitors and core exit temperature, supporting exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding post-accident sampling. Additional exemptions include 10 CFR 50.46a, 50.34(f)(2)(vi) (high point venting), and portions of 50.34(f)(2)(xx) (pressurizer level indicators). The CVCS does not include holdup tanks under vacuum but provides required venting and draining. The RPV high-point degasification vent is not safety-significant, and high-point vents are not needed. The LTOP system is not connected to a low-pressure system, but guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation applies. NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, for LOCA mass and energy release analysis, and requires no additional ITAAC. The design ensures no core uncovery during LOCA, conforms to DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, and GDC 5 for sharing of SSCs, does

The provided passages summarize NuScale's conformance with various NRC Regulatory Guides (RGs) as detailed in Table 1.9-2. For RG 1.68.1, "Initial Test Program of Condensate and Feedwater Systems for Light-Water Reactors" (Rev. 2), NuScale partially conforms, as the RG is applicable except for BWR-specific aspects or PWR design features not present in the NuScale design. RG 1.70, "Standard Format and Content of Safety Analysis Reports for Nuclear Power Plants (LWR Edition)" (Rev. 3), is not applicable since RG 1.206 and NuScale Design Specific Review Standards (DSRS) are used instead. For RG 1.79, "Preoperational Testing of Emergency Core Cooling Systems for Pressurized Water Reactors" (Rev. 2), NuScale partially conforms; the intent is applicable, but the literal language refers to SSC design features not in the NuScale design. RG 1.190, "Calculational and Dosimetry Methods for Determining Pressure Vessel Neutron Fluence," is also partially conformed to, with NuScale's neutron flux and fluence calculation methods consistent with RG 1.190 guidance except as described in Technical Report TR-0116-20781. RG 1.191, "Fire Protection Program for Nuclear Power Plants During Decommissioning and Permanent Shutdown," is not applicable, as it pertains only to license holders that have permanently ceased power operations.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed to promote natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS ensures coolant supply to the core to maintain specified acceptable fuel design limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences. A schematic flow diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and Table 5.1-1 detail major RCS components, flow stages, and volumes during steady-state, full-power operation. The SGS, with the main steam and feedwater systems, removes decay heat from the RCS during initial cooldown. Optimal performance criteria include maximizing electrical generation, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining margin to analytical limits. Key parameters—such as primary coolant temperatures, flow rates, pressurizer (PZR) water level, steam generator (SG) water level and mass, feedwater and steam flow rates, pressures, and steam superheat—are determined using NRELAP5 thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand calculations. Design calculations establish best-estimate, maximum, and minimum primary coolant flows, accounting for uncertainties in component flow resistances, core bypass flow, and thermal head capability, with bounding uncertainties based on test data and design requirements. Minimum and maximum design flow rates are calculated by biasing analytical uncertainties to minimize or maximize flow, respectively, and are used in bounding design analyses. The module heatup system provides RCS heating and coolant flow before criticality; between hot zero power and 15% reactor power, core heat is used to reach normal operating coolant temperature. From 15% to full power, average primary coolant temperature is held constant, with higher full-power coolant temperatures for higher flow rates, ensuring similar hot leg temperatures and sufficient SG superheat regardless of flow. Flow rate varies with module heatup system performance, SG operation, and RCS temperature, with typical hot zero power temperatures and steady-state best-estimate flow rates identified. The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RCS to the secondary steam system, supplying superheated steam to the power conversion cycle, with operating conditions summarized in Table 5.4-1 for full-power steam flow at best-estimate primary coolant conditions.

The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) is designed for natural circulation of the primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps or external piping. The RCS serves as the core neutron moderator and includes reflector blocks to improve neutron economy. It features a degasification line to remove noncondensible gases from the pressurizer (PZR) volume during normal operation. The reactor vessel internals (RVI) support and align the core, control rod assemblies, drive shafts, surveillance capsules, and instrument guide tubes. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core region—89 ft³, steam generator (SG) region—621 ft³, and PZR region—578 ft³ (with the cylindrical portion at 487 ft³). Each integrated NuScale Power Module (NPM) contains a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) housing the reactor, RVI, PZR, two SGs, reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, RCS injection and discharge lines, pressurizer spray and high-point degasification vent lines, and a decay heat removal system (DHRS). The system is depicted in Figure 5.1-2, showing key components and flow paths.

The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed for natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during both power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps and external piping, with all major components—including the core, pressurizer (PZR), two steam generators (SGs), reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, and decay heat removal system (DHRS)—integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). The core heats the coolant, which rises through the lower and upper riser assemblies, is redirected by the PZR baffle plate, and flows over the SG helical tube bundles, transferring heat to the secondary side and producing superheated steam with a moisture content ≤0.10% by weight at full power. The cooled, denser coolant descends through the annular downcomer and reenters the core, completing the natural circulation loop, with flow driven by density differences and elevation between the core and SGs. The RCS flow path is optimized for low pressure losses, so pressure is primarily determined by static head, and SG tube plugging does not affect natural circulation flow rate. The system includes small bypass flow paths in the upper riser to maintain boron concentration during DHRS-driven riser uncovery after non-LOCA transients. The pressurizer, integral to the RPV above the coolant and separated by a baffle plate, operates at 2100 psia and 650°F, with two 1-inch spray nozzles and heater bundles (each 400 kW, total ≥785 kW with 15% margin, 480 Vac, 60 Hz, 800°F sheath temperature) to maintain RCS pressure. Instrumentation provides control room indication of RCS pressure, pressurizer level, spray flow, and heater output, with the Monitoring and Control System (MCS) automating RCS level and pressure control. The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) maintains pressurizer level (normally 50–60%), adjusts RCS chemistry, and controls boron concentration, supplying continuous spray flow to minimize thermal stresses and maintain chemistry. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core—89 ft³, SG region—621 ft³, and PZR—578 ft³ (487 ft³ cylindrical). During off-normal transients, initial pool temperatures of 100°F or 140°F have minimal effect on RCS cooldown after 36 hours, even with reduced heat removal from fouling, tube plugging, or noncondensible gas accumulation. The system’s design, including intertwined SG tube bundles and elimination of external piping, prevents asymmetric core cooling and large break LOCA events. Key operational parameters—coolant temperatures, flow rates, press

5.4.1.3 
Performance Evaluation
A single RCS natural circulation flow loop is entirely contained within the RPV, thereby 
eliminating distinct RCS piping loops and the associated potential for a large pipe 
break (i.e., large break loss-of-coolant accident [LOCA]) event.This design, combined 
with the intertwined SGs tube bundle configuration, eliminates the potential for 
asymmetric core cooling and temperatures as a result of a loss of a single SG function.
