The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents several departures and conformances with regulatory criteria. Positions C1.1.3 and C1.1.4 are not applicable because the NuScale design does not rely on operator action or active devices to mitigate or prevent debris accumulation. The design does not comply with regulatory position C1.3.12 (prototypical head loss testing), nor with position C.2 except for meeting the intent of chemical reaction effects (position 2.2), and does not comply with position C.3. For DSRS 5.4.7 (Decay Heat Removal System), the design partially conforms to GDC 54, featuring a closed-loop DHRS outside containment directly connected to the closed-loop SG system within the RPV, providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and the reactor pool outside the NPM. Regarding DSRS 6.2.2 (Containment Heat Removal Systems), the design supports an exemption from the power provisions of GDC 38. For DSRS 6.2.5 (Combustible Gas Control in Containment), GDC 42 and 43 are not applicable as the design lacks a containment atmospheric cleanup system. In DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, the design conforms to fuel cladding integrity requirements and does not include a steam pressure regulator. The exclusion zone and buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability review (DSRS 15.9.A) is not used in the NuScale design. Finally, for DC/COL-ISG-5, the NuScale design is similar to large PWRs in the existing fleet regarding effluent release calculations, rendering the specific interim staff guidance not applicable.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents conformance with various regulatory criteria. For SRP 3.6.2 (Rev 2), regarding determination of rupture locations and dynamic effects of postulated piping ruptures inside containment, NuScale fully conforms. Similarly, SRP 3.6.3 (Rev 1) on leak-before-break evaluation procedures and DSRS 9.3.4 (Rev 0) concerning Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) components, quality, and seismic classification also show full conformance, with the CVCS located outside the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). DSRS 11.1 (Rev 0) on coolant source terms and DSRS 15.6.5 (Rev 0) on loss-of-coolant accidents from postulated piping breaks within the RCPB are also fully met. However, partial conformance is noted for BTP 6-1 (March 2007) regarding spray water pH and water chemistry for fission product removal, as some acceptance criteria reference structures, systems, and components (SSC) not present in the NuScale design. DSRS 15.0 (Rev 0) on transient and accident analyses is partially met, with exceptions for BWR-specific aspects. For 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(x), which requires a test program for qualifying reactor coolant system relief and safety valves and, for PWRs, PORV block valves, NuScale partially conforms, excluding PORV block valve testing and ATWS condition considerations.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents conformance with various regulatory criteria for safety-related systems. For NRC RG 1.180, addressing electromagnetic and radio-frequency interference (EMI/RFI) in safety-related instrumentation and control (I&C) systems, NuScale partially conforms, applying relevant design guidance for SSCs. Regarding DSRS 3.11 (Environmental Qualification of Mechanical and Electrical Equipment), NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.89 for the environmental qualification program per 10 CFR 50.49, with further details referenced in Table 1.9-2. Full conformance is achieved for RG 1.209, covering environmental design and qualification of safety-related computer-based I&C systems in mild environments, and for requirements on the effects of environmental conditions for all important-to-safety equipment. The requirement for design and procurement specifications (DSRS 3.11, II.25) is not applicable, as it is designated for the COL applicant. For setpoints (DSRS 7.2.7), NuScale’s methodology conforms to ISA-67.04.01-2006, addressing NRC RIS 2006-17. Additionally, the Instrument Air Supply (IAS) system conforms to Generic Letter 88-14, providing both instrument and service air to safety-related equipment.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) steam generator (SG) is an integral, once-through, helical tube design located within the reactor pressure vessel, with two SGs per module. Each SG contains 1,380 seamless helical tubes in 21 columns, providing a total heat transfer area of 17,928 ft² and a 10% tube plugging margin. The SG operates at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with inlet and outlet temperatures of 299.7°F and 584.4°F, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. Both primary and secondary sides are rated at 2,100 psia and 650°F, while SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia and 550°F. Tubes have a 0.625-inch outer diameter, 0.050-inch wall thickness, and a minimum bend radius of 6.250 inches. Steam and feed tubesheets are 4.0 and 6.0 inches thick (without clad), increasing to 4.625 and 6.625 inches with clad (0.250 inches secondary, 0.375 inches primary). Eight austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies per column provide full-circumferential, non-continuous support, minimizing crevice formation and corrosion, and ensuring high vibration resistance. The design eliminates traditional tube support plates and uses full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore to minimize crevices, with all tube-to-tubesheet contact in the primary coolant. The once-through design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet, preventing impurity accumulation and eliminating the need for blowdown systems; impurities are managed by chemistry control and periodic cleaning. Secondary side surfaces use corrosion-resistant materials (nickel alloy or stainless steel), allowing safe cleaning and inspection. System monitoring is performed at ≥500 Hz, and preservice inspections include 100% volumetric tube and weld examination per BPVC. The SG tube support structure is within the primary coolant, preventing secondary system corrosion product deposition. Chemistry control follows industry guidelines with NPM modifications, and secondary water quality adheres to NEI 97-06. The fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. SG tube failure is indicated by pressure changes, and actuation valves have position indication.

Secondary side instability evaluation shows no in-phase oscillations and that out-of-phase oscillations cancel, resulting in no net oscillating secondary flow. Acceptable instabilities are limited to tube mass flow fluctuations that do not induce reactor power oscillations exceeding fuel design limits and comply with ASME Code. The vibration assessment program (per Regulatory Guide 1.20, Rev. 3) covers SGs, main steam piping (including MSIV), SG supports, and tube supports within the RPV. Flow-induced vibration (FIV) screening criteria were developed, and low primary coolant flow rates result in low

5.4.4.2 
System Design
The NPM design comprises a reactor core, two SGs, and a pressurizer, contained within 
a single RPV, surrounded and contained within a steel CNV.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) steam generator (SG) is an integral, once-through, helical tube design located within the reactor pressure vessel, featuring two collocated SGs per module. Each SG contains 1,380 seamless helical tubes arranged in 21 columns, with a total heat transfer area of 17,928 ft² and a 10% tube plugging margin. The SGs operate at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with inlet and outlet temperatures of 299.7°F and 584.4°F, respectively, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. Both primary and secondary sides are rated at 2,100 psia and 650°F, while SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia and 550°F. Tubes have an outer diameter of 0.625 inches, a wall thickness of 0.050 inches, and a minimum transition bend radius of 6.250 inches. The steam and feed tubesheets are 4.0 and 6.0 inches thick (without clad), increasing to 4.625 and 6.625 inches with clad, which is 0.250 inches (secondary) and 0.375 inches (primary) thick. The SGs utilize austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies—eight sets per column—providing full-circumferential, non-continuous support to minimize crevice formation and corrosion product buildup, and to ensure high modal frequencies for vibration resistance. The design eliminates traditional tube support plates and minimizes crevices at tube-to-tubesheet interfaces by full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore, with all tube-to-tubesheet contact in the primary coolant environment. The once-through design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet, preventing impurity accumulation and negating the need for blowdown systems; impurities are managed by chemistry control and periodic cleaning using chemical or mechanical methods. Secondary side surfaces are constructed of corrosion-resistant materials such as nickel alloy or stainless steel, allowing for safe cleaning and inspection access to both sides of the tubesheets. System monitoring is performed at a minimum 500 Hz resolution, and preservice inspections include 100% volumetric, full-length tube examination and weld inspections per BPVC requirements. The SG tube support structure is located within the primary coolant, preventing secondary system corrosion product deposition on the SG shell. Chemistry control for both primary and secondary water follows industry guidelines, with modifications for the NPM, and the secondary water quality program adheres to NEI 97-06. The fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. SG tube failure is indicated by pressure changes, and actuation valves are equipped with position indication.

retrieved_contexts: The NuScale Power Module (NPM) design features an integral power module comprising a reactor core, two helical-coil steam generators (SGs), and a pressurizer, all contained within a single reactor pressure vessel (RPV). This RPV is immediately surrounded by a steel containment vessel (CNV) that is approximately cylindrical and sits in the reactor pool. The reactor core is positioned below the helical-coil SGs inside the RPV. The design eliminates the need for external piping between the steam generators, pressurizer, and RPV, and utilizes natural circulation for reactor coolant system flow, removing the requirement for reactor coolant pumps. For valve actuation testing, a pilot-operated main valve with trip line tubing and a trip valve representative of the NPM design was used to simulate performance and assess potential boric acid buildup. Pressurizer level instrumentation is provided to the control room and the Module Control System (MCS), supporting an exemption from certain power supply requirements per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xx). Pressurizer pressure is measured and provides input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS).

5.4.4.2

a single RPV, surrounded and contained within a steel CNV.

The NPM (NuScale Power Module) is designed for normal power maneuvers, allowing electric power adjustment via turbine bypass to the condenser, and core power maneuvering through control rods, soluble boron concentration changes, or a combination of both, as detailed in Sections 4.2 and 4.3. In multi-module plants, individual NPMs can be incrementally placed into service to match construction schedules and grid demand, and can be taken offline individually for refueling or maintenance. The refueling pool houses bolting tools for NPM disassembly and reassembly, with the reactor core remaining in the lower head of the RPV during refueling. A fuel handling machine transfers new and used fuel between the refueling pool and spent fuel pool (SFP) via a weir. The dry dock area, separated from the refueling pool by a gate, contains the module inspection rack and allows the water level to be lowered for maintenance on the upper NPM. Inspection and testing equipment are moved to the dry dock during refueling, which also serves for introducing new NPM components and preparing them for assembly. During refueling, the RBC (Reactor Building Crane) moves the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling pool, where it is set into the containment flange tool and the CNV flange is unbolted. The crane then separates the lower CNV from the upper CNV (with RPV attached), moves the upper CNV and RPV to the reactor vessel flange tool, and unbolts the RPV flange. The upper and lower RPV are separated, leaving the lower RPV with the reactor core in the flange tool, while the upper NPM (upper CNV with attached upper RPV) is moved to the module inspection rack in the dry dock for inspection, testing, and maintenance. After these activities and refueling, the upper NPM is returned to the refueling pool and reassembled in reverse order using the flange tools. The reassembled NPM is then moved back to its operating bay by the RBC, where startup tests are performed before the reactor is brought online for steam and power production.

The 
crane again lifts the NPM, this time separating the upper and lower RPV, leaving the 
lower RPV including the reactor core, in the reactor vessel flange tool.Finally, the 
crane transports the upper NPM (now consisting of just the upper CNV with 
attached upper RPV) to the module inspection rack in the dry dock.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) is designed to maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) throughout the plant’s design life. Each NPM complies with 10 CFR 50.55a, ensuring that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) and all pressure-retaining components associated with the RCPB are designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected as Class 1 in accordance with Section III of the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC), and meet the applicable conditions specified in 10 CFR 50.55a(b). The materials used for the RCPB, including weld materials, conform to the fabrication, construction, and testing requirements of ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, and comply with ASME BPVC Section II material requirements. Inspection requirements and conditions for Class 1 pressure-containing components and their supports are met as detailed in Section XI of the ASME BPVC. Additionally, the design utilizes specific ASME Code Cases, including N-759-2 (allowable external pressure and compressive stresses for Class 1, 2, and 3 components), N-62-7 (internal and external valve items for Classes 1, 2, and 3), and N-60-5 (material for core support structures).

These NuScale features and values are shown in comparison with a typical pressurized water 
reactor (PWR) plant design.All values are nominal and provided for comparison only.The 
typical PWR values presented are representative of the Standardized Nuclear Unit Power Plant 
System design.Table 1.3-2 provides a comparison of safety systems and components required to protect the 
reactor core for the NuScale Power Plant versus a typical PWR plant.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides a detailed comparison between the NuScale Power Plant and a typical pressurized water reactor (PWR) based on nominal values. The NuScale module (NPM) has a nominal gross electrical output of 50 MWe and a core thermal output of 160 MWt, compared to 1,186 MWe and 3,411 MWt for a typical PWR. NuScale uses 37 fuel assemblies (17x17 lattice, 6.56 ft effective length, 264 rods per assembly) versus 193 assemblies (17x17 lattice, 12 ft length) in a PWR. The average linear heat rate is 2.5 kW/ft for NuScale and 5.4 kW/ft for PWR. NuScale has 16 control rod assemblies and a 60-year design life, compared to 53 assemblies and 40 years for PWR. The NuScale reactor coolant system has no external loops or coolant pipes, operates at 1,850 psia, and uses a stacked stainless steel reflector block for thermal shielding, with top-mounted in-core instrumentation. Its steam generator is a helical coil type (2 units, ~18,000 ft² area, 1,380 tubes), while PWR uses four vertical U-tube generators (55,000 ft², 5,626 tubes). NuScale has no reactor coolant pumps, pressurizer surge nozzle, or residual heat removal pumps, and its containment is a steel pressure vessel (14 ft 2 in. diameter, 75 ft 8.5 in. height), compared to a PCCV (140 ft diameter, 205 ft height) for PWR. Many traditional PWR safety systems—such as containment spray, high/low pressure safety injection, accumulators, emergency diesel generators, and emergency feedwater pumps—are not present in NuScale. The NuScale I&C system is digital, and it uses 6 reactor component cooling water pumps for 12 NPMs. Table 1.3-2 shows that both designs include core protection systems such as the reactor pressure vessel, containment vessel, reactor coolant system, decay heat removal, emergency core cooling, control rod drive, containment isolation, and ultimate heat sink. However, several PWR safety systems (e.g., residual heat removal, safety injection, auxiliary feedwater, emergency service water, hydrogen recombiner, containment spray, reactor coolant pumps, safety-related electrical distribution, off-site power, emergency diesel generators, Class 1E battery, and ATWS system) are not required in the NuScale design.

The NuScale reactor design does not require or include reactor coolant pumps, making SRP 5.4.1.1, Rev 3: Pump Flywheel Integrity (PWR) and its acceptance criteria (II.1 through II.7) inapplicable. Similarly, SRP 5.4.11, Rev 4: Pressurizer Relief Tank and its criteria (II.1 and II.2) do not apply, as the NuScale design does not use a pressurizer relief tank. While much of the subtier guidance in DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0: Condensate and Feedwater System references large LWR designs and is not relevant to NuScale, the design conforms to II.3 regarding sharing of structures, systems, and components (GDC 5), with no exceptions noted. Additionally, SRP 10.4.9, Rev 3: Auxiliary Feedwater System (PWR) is not applicable, as NuScale does not require or use an auxiliary feedwater system; instead, its decay heat removal system (DHRS) performs some similar functions.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements, design parameters, material composition, and major components of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Table 5.2-6 specifies that RPV shell and head welds, including connections such as the lower RPV flange shell to RPV bottom head and steam plenum cap to integral steam plenum, are subject to B-A category volumetric examination, while internal welds like the core support block to RPV bottom head undergo B-N-2 category VT-3 visual testing; instrumentation and controls sleeve welds are part of the cladding and not separately examined. Table 5.3-1 lists key RPV parameters: design pressure is 2100 psia, design temperature is 650°F, overall height is 778 inches, and diameters range from 96.5 to 115.5 inches depending on section, with inner and outer clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches, respectively. Table 5.3-2 provides maximum allowable concentrations for RPV beltline forging material (e.g., sulfur 0.010 wt%, phosphorus 0.010 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%) and as-deposited weld metal (e.g., sulfur 0.015 wt%, phosphorus 0.012 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, manganese 1.80 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%). Figure 5.3-1 identifies major RPV components, including the lower and upper RPV sections, RPV head section, CRDM latch housing section with 16 latch housings, RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum with 4 access ports, upper and lower RPV SG shells, feed plenum access ports, lower RPV section flange shell, RPV bottom head, upper RPV flanged transition shell, CRDM seismic support structure, and 4 instrument seal assemblies.

results.question: How do the safety features in Table 1.2-2 relate to the DHRS performance shown in Ch.05 Figure 5.4-7 and DHRS-related text?
results.expected_answer: Table 1.2-2 lists passive systems and natural circulation as key safety enhancements. Ch.05 text confirms DHRS achieves safe cooldown using only natural circulation and gravity, validating the Ch.01 safety claims.
results.generated_answer: The safety features in Table 1.2-2, such as passive, redundant, single active failure-proof design and seismic qualification, directly support the DHRS performance shown in Ch.05 Figure 5.4-7 by ensuring reliable, automatic core cooling to safe shutdown conditions under all postulated events, as described in the DHRS-related text. retrieved_contexts:
The safety-related DHRS function is an engineered safety feature of the NPM design.Reliability of DHRS is evaluated using the reliability assurance program described in 
Section 17.4 and risk significance is determined using the guidance described in 
Chapter 19.The DHRS classification and risk categories are included in Table 3.2-1.

The DHRS is 
designed to withstand the effects of natural phenomena without loss of capability to 
perform its safety function.The DHRS is designed to accommodate the effects of, and 
be compatible with, the environmental conditions associated with normal operation, 
maintenance, testing, and postulated accidents.

It provides information regarding the design basis and qualification of 
structures, systems, and components based on these classifications and designations.Section 6.1 provides details regarding material selection and fabrication methods and 
discusses compatibility with fluids that the DHRS components may be exposed to 
during normal, accident, maintenance, and testing conditions.Table 6.1-1 provides a 
list of material and material specifications for engineered safety feature components, 
including the DHRS components and supports.

Table 5.4-8 provides the failure modes and effects analysis for the 
DHRS.Each NPM, including the associated DHRS, are located within the Reactor Building, 
which protects the system from physical damage by events and phenomena external 
to the facility as discussed in Section 3.8.The DHRS piping branches from the main steam lines outside and above the CNV and 
upstream of the MSIVs.

The safety-related Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is an engineered safety feature of the NPM design, with its reliability evaluated through the reliability assurance program (Section 17.4) and risk significance determined per Chapter 19 guidance; its classification and risk categories are detailed in Table 3.2-1. The DHRS meets 10 CFR 50 Appendix A General Design Criteria (GDC 1, 2, and 4), is classified as Quality Group B, and is designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected as Class 2 in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, adhering to the highest quality standards per the Quality Assurance Program (Chapter 17). The DHRS is engineered to withstand natural phenomena and remain functional under normal, maintenance, testing, and postulated accident conditions. DHRS actuation valves, piping, and passive condensers are Quality Group B, Class 2 per BPVC Section III, and remain operable after a design basis seismic event; supports are Class 2 per BPVC Section III, Subsection NF. Classification details and applicability are in Chapter 3, while Section 6.1 covers material selection, fabrication methods, and fluid compatibility, with Table 6.1-1 listing material specifications for DHRS components and supports. Welding follows ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NC-4300, and Section IX. The DHRS condenser, actuation valves, and piping are Seismic Category I, and the system is protected from natural phenomena by the reactor building. DHRS instrumentation and control components are seismically qualified per IEEE 344-2004. Table 5.4-8 provides the DHRS failure modes and effects analysis. Preliminary critical heat flux (CHF) tests for the NuScale small modular reactor were conducted at Stern Laboratories in Ontario, Canada, using a high-pressure flow loop and custom 5x5 fuel assembly simulators with a 2-meter heated length, various axial power profiles, and configurations with and without simulated guide tubes. These tests examined the effects of shorter fuel length and low-flow natural circulation, generating data for the development and validation of the NuScale NSP2 CHF correlation. Additional CHF testing at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics (KATHY) facility in Germany, using prototypic fuel designs, provided further data to validate NSP2 and develop the NSP4 CHF correlation for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. CHF occurrence was identified by temperature excursions in the fuel simulators, and data collected included bundle subchannel exit temperatures for mixing coefficient determination and single-phase/two-phase pressure drop characteristics across various bundle powers and hydraulic conditions. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, prepared under 10 CFR 52.47(a), details the plant’s general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, including process effluent radiation monitoring (Section 11.5.1.2.2). The NuScale plant comprises 12 modules, each with a 160 MWth reactor and 50 MWe gross output, for a total net output of 570 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The core uses UO2 fuel enriched below 4.95% with a 24-month refueling interval. Safety features include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps. The containment is designed for high pressure to maintain integrity during worst-case accidents, minimizing radioactive releases. The FSAR references tables and figures detailing plant and module characteristics (Tables 1.2-1, 1.2-2), system boundaries (Figure 1.2-2), module schematics (Figure 1.2-3), multi-module layouts (Figures 1.2-4, 1.2-5), module cutaways (Figure 1.2-6), steam generator and reactor flow (Figure 1.2-7), technical reports (Table 1.6-2), and I&C diagrams (Table 1.7-1). results.expected_answer: Ch.01 states the integral design eliminates LBLOCA and uses passive ECCS. Ch.05 Table 5.2-2 shows the ECCS consists of RSVs with 63,360 lbm/hr capacity and setpoints at 2075/2100 psia. Figure 5.1-3 shows the RCS schematic with the locations of these valves and their connections to the CNV, confirming the passive safety architecture.

The Ch.01 overview states NuScale’s safety relies on no large-break LOCA (LBLOCA) and a passive ECCS using three reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), all closed during normal operation; Ch.05 and Figure 5.1-3 specify these valves’ locations—RVVs on the RPV top venting the pressurizer steam space, RRVs above the main closure flange—confirming the passive, valve-based ECCS design without pumps or active injection.

retrieved_contexts: The NuScale design employs the chemical and volume control system as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to meet GDC 26 requirements. For containment pressure boundary fracture prevention (SRP 6.2.7, Rev 1) and most Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) criteria (DSRS 6.3, Rev 0), including ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46, single-failure consideration, design of non-safety-related ECCS portions, ECCS interfaces and shared systems, long-term cooling, ECCS outage times and unavailability reporting, and programmatic requirements, the design conforms to regulatory guidance. However, departures are noted for inservice inspection and operability testing, and for combined reactivity control system capability and actuation provisions; specifically, the NuScale ECCS does not perform poison addition or provide a makeup function as required by GDC 27, though actuation signal guidance is applied. Control room habitability system requirements (SRP 6.4, Rev 3) for the emergency zone are met. For inadvertent ECCS operation (DSRS 15.6.6, Rev 0), the reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure remains below 110 percent of design value, and minimum DNBR is maintained. The design provides high point venting capability for noncondensible gases from the RCS and other systems to ensure adequate core cooling (50.34(f)(2)(vi)), and instruments in the control room give unambiguous indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(xviii), with all relevant requirements conformed to unless otherwise noted.

The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) utilizes three reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), with the RVVs being larger than the RRVs. Both valve types are pilot operated by remote-mounted trip and reset solenoid valves at the reactor containment boundary, and each main valve includes an inadvertent actuation block (IAB) feature located inside containment. The three RVVs are mounted on the top of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), connecting to the upper head and discharging the pressurizer (PZR) steam space directly to the containment vessel (CNV), thereby venting noncondensible gases during ECCS actuation. The two RRVs connect to the RPV shell just above the main closure flange. The ECCS design, including protection from inadvertent actuation and single failure capability, is detailed in Section 6.3, while the design basis for the RSVs and ECCS valves is provided in Sections 5.2.2 and 6.3. The NuScale design does not require separate safety-related high-point venting in the reactor coolant system (RCS), as the RVVs fulfill this function during and after a loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA), ensuring noncondensible gases do not accumulate in the pressurizer space and core cooling is maintained. Mechanical failure of the reactor safety valve (RSV) is bounded by the analysis of inadvertent ECCS valve actuation in Section 15.6.6.

The NuScale reactor design employs the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to meet GDC 26, with its only safety-related function being the prevention of inadvertent boron dilution of the reactor coolant system. The CVCS, including the Boron Recovery System, partially conforms to DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0, meeting requirements for functional performance, pumping capacity, defense-in-depth RCS makeup, single failure criteria, GDC 5, minimization of contamination, system design, leakage detection, and ITAAC, but only partially meets criteria for holdup tank wall buckling/failure and venting/draining due to design differences. The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design diverges from traditional PWRs by omitting high- and low-pressure safety injection pumps, a containment spray system, and a sump; instead, it relies on steam condensation and natural recirculation of water that accumulates inside the containment vessel for core cooling, fulfilling ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46 and related guidance. The ECCS does not perform poison addition or provide a makeup function as required by GDC 27, and departures are noted for inservice inspection, operability testing, and combined reactivity control system capability and actuation provisions, though actuation signal guidance is applied. The design ensures RCS pressure remains below 110% of design value during inadvertent ECCS operation, maintains minimum DNBR, provides high point venting for noncondensible gases, and includes control room instrumentation for unambiguous indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(vi) and (xviii). The ECCS does not require personnel access, pumps, or containment pressure for recirculation, and does not include refueling water storage tanks; condensate storage components are outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building, with no safety risk from their failure. The Instrumentation and Controls review per DSRS 7.0 applies to the DCA but lacks specific acceptance criteria. Gas accumulation is not expected to affect ECCS performance, and the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) interfaces only with the secondary system, not the RCS. Control room habitability system requirements (SRP 6.4, Rev 3) are met for the emergency zone, and pre-operational ECCS testing will be conducted to satisfy regulatory guidance.

The provided passages summarize the NuScale design’s conformance with various regulatory guidance and standards. Guidance specific to Boiling Water Reactors (BWRs), such as assumptions for evaluating radiological consequences of a loss of coolant accident (Section 1.3) and ECCS suction concerns (Issue 193), is not applicable to NuScale. Instrument lines do not penetrate the NuScale Power Module (NPM) containment (Section 1.11). For water sources for long-term recirculation cooling following a loss-of-coolant accident (RG 1.82), the NuScale design partially conforms, meeting the intent of regulatory positions on design criteria, performance standards, and analysis methods, though its system design differs from those addressed in the guidance. The NuScale design supports exemptions from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding reactor coolant system and containment sampling (SRP 13.3, Rev 3, II.27) and from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K for ECCS performance evaluation (DSRS 15.6.5, II.1). For radiological consequences of main steam line failures outside containment (SRP 15.1.5.A, Rev 2), NuScale partially conforms to guidance on technical specifications for primary and secondary coolant iodine activity and leak rates. Issue 199, concerning updated probabilistic seismic hazard estimates, is not applicable as it pertains to currently-operating plants.

results.question: Is the NuScale natural circulation design adequate for removing core heat without reactor coolant pumps?
results.expected_answer: Yes. The natural circulation design is adequate because the NuScale core has a much lower power density than conventional PWRs, and the tall RPV with large height difference between heat source (core) and heat sink (SG) provides sufficient buoyancy-driven flow. Analysis shows 587 kg/s flow at 100% power is sufficient for heat removal.
results.generated_answer:
Yes, the NuScale natural circulation design is adequate for removing core heat without reactor coolant pumps, as it eliminates the need for such pumps by relying on passive natural circulation of the primary coolant.

The NuScale ECCS (Emergency Core Cooling System) design differs from traditional systems by not utilizing high-pressure or low-pressure safety injection pumps, a containment spray system, or a sump. Instead, core decay heat removal is achieved through steam condensation and natural reactor coolant recirculation. During ECCS operation, water accumulates inside the NuScale containment vessel and is recirculated back to the reactor core, fulfilling the intent of relevant acceptance criteria. The design does not require personnel access during reactor operation, does not include pumps, and does not rely on containment pressure for ECCS recirculation as in typical PWRs. Consequently, purge or vent capability as specified by 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xv) is not required or included. Pre-operational testing of the ECCS will be conducted to satisfy regulatory guidance.

The NuScale plant design departs significantly from traditional large light water reactors (LWRs) and is not addressed by legacy regulatory guidance or codes such as PWRGALE. Its safety strategy relies on passive features and natural circulation, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps, safety-related pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, safety injection tanks, and safety-related motor-operated valves. Consequently, regulatory requirements and guidance related to these components—including flywheel integrity, motor qualification, thermal overload protection, ECCS accumulator lines, and gas accumulation in forced circulation systems—are not applicable. The design also omits an auxiliary feedwater (AFW) system, power-operated pressurizer relief valves, and pressurizer heaters, instead using a Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) and spring-loaded ASME code safety relief valves, which are not susceptible to inadvertent opening. The integral pressurizer is located within the reactor vessel, with no surge line or connecting piping, eliminating thermal stratification and ensuring core uncovery cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint. The containment vessel is a high-strength steel structure immersed in a water pool, operating at sub-atmospheric pressure, and the compact core results in a reduced source term. The plant is scalable, supports multi-unit configurations, and uses comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls. Debris management and chemical effects are minimized by the elimination of pumps, sumps, strainers, and trash racks. Fire protection measures ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational, compensating for the inability to passively separate redundant safety trains due to the modular design. Regulatory requirements from 10 CFR 50.34(f) and NEI 06-12, as well as ATWS provisions, are largely inapplicable, and NuScale is exempt from related evaluations and analyses. For coolant source term evaluation, NuScale uses an alternate methodology incorporating aspects of ANSI/ANS 18.1. The only pumps within safety scope are ASME Class III CVCS pumps, which do not serve a safety function. The design meets DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials. For spent fuel pool mitigation, NuScale is exempt from certain requirements due to its below-grade, undrainable SFP. The design considers postulated startup reactivity accidents and addresses loading transients and structural evaluations for postulated breaks in CVCS and reactor vent valve piping. Containment vessel evacuation system valve closure times are addressed under SRP Section 6.2.4. Overall, NuScale’s unique passive and modular features support exemptions from many TMI-related and traditional LWR regulatory requirements.

The NuScale design incorporates several advanced safety and operational features, including safe shutdown and cooling without reliance on AC or DC power, compact helical coil steam generators with reactor pressure on the tube exteriors, and a high-strength steel containment vessel immersed in a water pool. The containment operates at sub-atmospheric pressure during normal conditions, and the reactor core is small, resulting in a reduced source term. Comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls (I&C) are used for monitoring and control. The plant design is scalable, supporting incremental capacity growth in multi-unit configurations. The passive NuScale system eliminates pumps, sumps, suction strainers, debris interceptors, and trash racks, thereby minimizing debris-related and non-condensable effects on core coolant flow, and complies with regulatory guidance on debris management and chemical effects. Due to the modular and compact nature of the NuScale Power Module, passive separation of redundant safety trains is not always feasible; instead, fire protection measures are implemented to ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational in the event of a fire. Unlike traditional large water reactors, the NuScale design integrates the pressurizer above the reactor core within the reactor vessel, with no connecting piping, ensuring that core uncovery and damage cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint.

results.question: Does the NuScale vacuum containment design provide adequate pressure suppression during a LOCA?
results.expected_answer: Yes. The vacuum CNV provides effective pressure suppression because steam released during a LOCA condenses on the cool CNV inner wall, which is maintained at reactor pool water temperature. The vacuum starting condition and condensation mechanism keep containment pressure well below design limits without active spray systems.
results.generated_answer:
Yes, the NuScale vacuum containment design provides adequate pressure suppression during a LOCA, with the peak containment pressure remaining below the design pressure for the limiting event.  
(Source: Section 6.2, NuScale FSAR; DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, Rev 0)

Boric acid concentrations for the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design were selected to bound both refueling and operating boron concentrations, with tests conducted across the full range of reactor operating pressures and temperatures; this test program was inspected by the NRC per Inspection Procedures IP35034.1.5.2, and further details on startup testing are provided in Section 14.2. The NuScale design does not utilize combustible gas control systems and supports an exemption to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), as detailed in DCA Part 7, section 2. Additionally, the design supports an exemption from 50.34(f)(2)(xiv) and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, regarding containment isolation valve closure times, with no deviations noted. Hydrogen control systems within containment are not required, as combustion does not impact containment vessel (CNV) integrity, further supporting the exemption to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2). The design also supports an exemption to GDC 27, complying instead with a NuScale-specific principal design criterion as described in Section 3.1.4. For post-accident assessment, the NuScale design relies on radiation monitors under the bioshield and core exit temperature indications, supporting an exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding the requirement to obtain and analyze post-accident samples of the reactor coolant system and containment without exceeding prescribed radiation dose limits.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) documents conformance with various regulatory criteria, noting that guidance for containment purge systems venting directly to the environment is not applicable, as the NuScale containment does not require or incorporate such systems due to its small size and lack of sub-compartments. The spent fuel pool (SFP) mitigating measure is also not required, as the NuScale SFP is below grade and cannot be drained. Radiation and shielding design reviews are performed for spaces that may contain accident source term radioactive materials, with adequate access provided, and the only vital areas per NUREG-0737 are those for combustible gas monitoring, the main control room, and the technical support center. The containment isolation system conforms to 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xiv)(A)-(D), ensuring automatic isolation of non-essential systems, two isolation barriers for non-essential penetrations, no reopening of isolation valves on signal reset, and a containment set point pressure compatible with normal operation; however, NuScale requests an exemption from (E), which requires automatic closure on high radiation signals for systems venting to the environment. The requirement for redundant dedicated containment penetrations for external hydrogen recombiners is not applicable, as the NuScale design does not use external hydrogen recombiners.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) features an integral containment system that provides primary containment for the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), as detailed in Section 6.2. The containment design conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, Rev 0 requirements, including design margin for containment pressure, heat removal capability, protection from external pressure, monitoring instrumentation, internal structures, and evaluation of accidents involving generated hydrogen or other modules. For the limiting event, the peak containment pressure remains below the design pressure. The NuScale design does not utilize an ice condenser containment, as specified in SRP 6.2.1.1.B, Draft Rev 3. Additionally, preliminary design information requirements under 10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(v) are not applicable to the design certification application per 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8) and 10 CFR 50.34(f). Further details on containment systems are provided in Section 6.2.

The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50 Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. results.question: Based on Figure 5.1-2, does the NuScale RCS flow path appear to provide adequate driving head for natural circulation?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Figure 5.1-2 shows a tall vertical loop with the heat source (core) at the bottom and heat sink (SG) near the top, with a long riser between them. This large elevation difference between the heat source and sink maximizes the buoyancy driving head for natural circulation, which is the primary design feature enabling pump-less operation.

The NuScale design employs passive natural circulation of the primary coolant, which eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps. This approach conforms to DSRS 5.4.2.1, Rev 0 requirements for steam generator materials, specifically regarding the selection, processing, testing, and inspection of materials, with no exceptions noted.

5.4.1.3 
Performance Evaluation
A single RCS natural circulation flow loop is entirely contained within the RPV, thereby 
eliminating distinct RCS piping loops and the associated potential for a large pipe 
break (i.e., large break loss-of-coolant accident [LOCA]) event.This design, combined 
with the intertwined SGs tube bundle configuration, eliminates the potential for 
asymmetric core cooling and temperatures as a result of a loss of a single SG function.

Natural circulation flow is based on maximizing the vertical 
elevation difference between the thermal centers of the reactor core and the SGs and 
minimizing the flow dependent loss coefficient of the RCS flow circuit.

The pressure losses through the RCS flow path are very low, to support the natural 
circulation design of the RCS.Since the pressure losses due to flow are small, the pressure 
at any location in the RCS flow path is primarily a function of the static head.The primary 
coolant flows over the outside of the helical SG tubes; therefore, SG tube plugging 
uncertainties are not applicable to the primary coolant natural circulation flow rate 
determination.

The reactor core heats the reactor coolant, which then flows upward through the lower and upper riser assemblies. At the top of the upper riser, the coolant is redirected downward by the PZR baffle plate into the annular space between the upper riser assembly and the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) wall, where the steam generator (SG) helical tube bundles are located. The primary coolant flows over the outside of the SG tubes, transferring heat to the secondary side fluid inside the tubes, which produces superheated steam with a moisture content no greater than 0.10 percent by weight during full-power operation. As the coolant cools and becomes denser, it sinks into the annular downcomer between the lower riser assembly and the core barrel, then returns to the lower plenum and reenters the reactor core, completing the natural circulation loop. The motive force for this flow is natural convection, driven by density differences between the hot coolant leaving the core and the colder coolant leaving the SGs, as well as the elevation difference between the core (heat source) and SGs (heat sink). The RCS flow path is designed for very low pressure losses, so pressure at any location is primarily determined by static head, and SG tube plugging uncertainties do not affect the natural circulation flow rate. Small flow paths in the upper riser allow limited coolant bypass to maintain sufficient boron concentration during DHRS-driven riser uncovery conditions after non-LOCA transients. The entire RCS natural circulation loop is contained within the RPV, eliminating external piping and the risk of large break LOCA events, and the intertwined SG tube bundle configuration prevents asymmetric core cooling. As the RPV water level drops near the top of the riser, reduced flow area increases pressure losses and impedes natural circulation, resulting in a greater temperature difference between hot and cold legs.

The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed for natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during both power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps and external piping, with all major components—including the core, pressurizer (PZR), two steam generators (SGs), reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, and decay heat removal system (DHRS)—integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). The core heats the coolant, which rises through the lower and upper riser assemblies, is redirected by the PZR baffle plate, and flows over the SG helical tube bundles, transferring heat to the secondary side and producing superheated steam with a moisture content ≤0.10% by weight at full power. The cooled, denser coolant descends through the annular downcomer and reenters the core, completing the natural circulation loop, with flow driven by density differences and elevation between the core and SGs. The RCS flow path is optimized for low pressure losses, so pressure is primarily determined by static head, and SG tube plugging does not affect natural circulation flow rate. The system includes small bypass flow paths in the upper riser to maintain boron concentration during DHRS-driven riser uncovery after non-LOCA transients. The pressurizer, integral to the RPV above the coolant and separated by a baffle plate, operates at 2100 psia and 650°F, with two 1-inch spray nozzles and heater bundles (each 400 kW, total ≥785 kW with 15% margin, 480 Vac, 60 Hz, 800°F sheath temperature) to maintain RCS pressure. Instrumentation provides control room indication of RCS pressure, pressurizer level, spray flow, and heater output, with the Monitoring and Control System (MCS) automating RCS level and pressure control. The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) maintains pressurizer level (normally 50–60%), adjusts RCS chemistry, and controls boron concentration, supplying continuous spray flow to minimize thermal stresses and maintain chemistry. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core—89 ft³, SG region—621 ft³, and PZR—578 ft³ (487 ft³ cylindrical). During off-normal transients, initial pool temperatures of 100°F or 140°F have minimal effect on RCS cooldown after 36 hours, even with reduced heat removal from fouling, tube plugging, or noncondensible gas accumulation. The system’s design, including intertwined SG tube bundles and elimination of external piping, prevents asymmetric core cooling and large break LOCA events. The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) is designed for natural circulation of the primary coolant, eliminating the need for reactor coolant pumps or external piping. The RCS serves as the core neutron moderator and includes reflector blocks to improve neutron economy. It features a degasification line to remove noncondensible gases from the pressurizer (PZR) volume during normal operation. The reactor vessel internals (RVI) support and align the core, control rod assemblies, drive shafts, surveillance capsules, and instrument guide tubes. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core region—89 ft³, steam generator (SG) region—621 ft³, and PZR region—578 ft³ (with the cylindrical portion at 487 ft³). Each integrated NuScale Power Module (NPM) contains a reactor pressure vessel (RPV) housing the reactor, RVI, PZR, two SGs, reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, RCS injection and discharge lines, pressurizer spray and high-point degasification vent lines, and a decay heat removal system (DHRS). The system is depicted in Figure 5.1-2, showing key components and flow paths.

The NuScale design complies with the intent of 
regulatory position C.1.3 (evaluation of long term 
recirculation capability as applicable to the design) 
with the exception of the following:
• Position C.1.3.1 (net positive suction head)
• Portions of position C.1.3.2 that are not consistent 
with the NuScale design
The NuScale design does not comply with 
regulatory positions C1.3.7 (upstream effects) or 
C.1.3.9 (strainer structural integrity).

results.expected_answer: Yes. Tables 5.3-9/10 show minimum Charpy upper shelf energy of 58.4 ft-lb after irradiation, exceeding the 10 CFR 50 Appendix G requirement of 50 ft-lb. The text confirms that the reduced neutron fluence from NuScale's lower power density results in less embrittlement, maintaining adequate toughness margins through the 60-year design life.

The initial Charpy USE is 75 ft-lb. Table 5.3-9 summarizes the predicted 1/4-T Charpy USE decrease at 57-EFPY fluence: for the lower RPV shell beltline (0.10 wt% Cu, 1.37E+19 n/cm², E > 1 MeV), the USE decreases by 20.5% to 59.6 ft-lb; for the lower RPV weld (0.06 wt% Cu, 3.21E+18 n/cm²), the decrease is 15.3% to 63.5 ft-lb; and for the RPV bottom head (0.10 wt% Cu, 3.21E+18 n/cm²), the decrease is 14.5% to 64.1 ft-lb. Table 5.3-10 provides the final 1/4-T Charpy USE values after adjusting for NuScale reactor pressure vessel irradiation temperature, applying a -1.2 ft-lb correction: lower RPV shell beltline, 58.4 ft-lb; lower RPV weld, 62.3 ft-lb; and RPV bottom head, 62.9 ft-lb. These values meet the regulatory minimum, and the Material Surveillance program monitors changes in fracture toughness properties throughout the vessel’s service life.

requirements of the RPV materials are provided in Section 5.3.

results.question: Is the DHRS design adequate for removing decay heat following a reactor trip and loss of normal feedwater?
results.expected_answer: Yes. The DHRS is designed with two independent trains, each capable of removing 100% of decay heat. The passive condensers in the reactor pool provide indefinite cooling capability (limited only by pool heat capacity). No AC power, operator action, or active components are required, exceeding the 72-hour coping time requirement.

The DHRS is designed 
to remove post-reactor trip residual and core decay heat from operating conditions 
and transition the NPM to safe shutdown conditions without reliance on external 
power.The DHRS is designed to cool the RCS at a rate such that specified acceptable 
fuel design limits and the design conditions of the reactor coolant pressure boundary 
are not exceeded during a return to power event as described in Section 15.0.6.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is a passive, closed-loop system designed to remove decay heat from the reactor coolant system (RCS) by utilizing the steam generators (SGs). Water from the DHRS condenser circulates through the SG tubes, where it absorbs decay heat and is converted to steam. This steam flows back to the DHRS condenser, where it releases heat to the reactor pool water and condenses, promoting natural circulation in both the RCS and DHRS. The DHRS is connected to the steam and feedwater piping between the main steam isolation valves (MSIVs) and feedwater isolation valves (FWIVs), forming a closed-loop outside the containment and providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and the reactor pool. Each DHRS train is associated with one of the two NPM SGs, with piping connecting to the main steam and feedwater lines upstream of the MSIV and downstream of the main feedwater isolation valve (MFIV). Two DHRS actuation valves, arranged in parallel per train, isolate the system during normal operation. An orifice moderates flow between the actuation valves and the passive condensers, which are located at a higher elevation than the SGs to promote natural circulation. The DHRS remains filled and pressurized via the feedwater system and is in standby during normal operation, with actuation dependent on closure of the MSIVs and FWIVs. The system differs from an auxiliary feedwater system in design, operation, and response to small break LOCA events. The SGs are designed for reliability, minimizing tube corrosion, vibration, and wear. System performance is optimized across power levels by establishing steady-state parameters as a function of reactor power. RCS temperature and pressure sensors confirm DHRS operation, indicated by decreasing values after reactor trip and DHRS actuation.

Decay Heat Removal System Performance Results
The system performance analysis indicates the DHRS is capable of removing 
appreciable amounts of heat over a wide range of inventories and it is insensitive to 
a wide range of reactor pool temperatures and to the failure of an MSIV to close.The analysis also shows the ability to accommodate fouling and SG tube plugging.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is a passive, closed-loop safety system designed to remove decay heat from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) using the steam generators (SGs) and natural circulation. The DHRS operates by circulating water from its condenser through the SG tubes, where it absorbs decay heat, converts to steam, and returns to the condenser to release heat to the reactor pool water. The system is connected to the steam and feedwater piping between the main steam isolation valves (MSIVs) and feedwater isolation valves (FWIVs), forming a closed loop outside containment and providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and reactor pool. Each DHRS train is associated with one of the two NPM SGs, with actuation valves and orifices arranged to promote natural circulation and moderate flow. The DHRS is designed to maintain the RCS average temperature below 420°F within 36 hours of an initiating event, as recommended by EPRI and accepted by the NRC, without challenging the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) or uncovering the core, and without requiring Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) actuation. Performance analyses, including steady-state and transient models using NRELAP5, confirm the DHRS can remove heat across a wide range of loop inventories, accommodate SG tube plugging, fouling, noncondensible gas accumulation, and MSIV failure, and is insensitive to reactor pool temperature variations. With two DHRS trains, RCS temperature drops below 420°F in less than 2 hours and stabilizes below 300°F within 36 hours; with one train and limiting conditions, RCS temperature remains below 420°F within 36 hours. Figures and tables referenced (e.g., Figures 1.2-8, 5.4-10 to 5.4-16; Tables 5.4-5, 5.4-6, 5.4-8) provide detailed design data, failure modes analysis, and temperature cooldown profiles for various DHRS operational scenarios, demonstrating robust decay heat removal capability under both nominal and off-normal conditions. System performance is monitored by RCS temperature and pressure sensors, and initial testing will verify DHRS operation against design basis requirements.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS), as described in DSRS 5.4.7 Rev 0, is connected to the secondary system and does not directly interface with the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), making GDC 14 not applicable. Leakage detection and isolation from containment are not considered important to safety due to this lack of direct interface. The DHRS has no other interfaces or shared components with the RCPB and conforms to requirements regarding system interfaces. DHRS operation relies on passive natural circulation flow, making direct flow measurement less useful for the NuScale design; instead, control room indication focuses on other system parameters to ensure adequate monitoring of DHRS operation and reactor core cooling. The DHRS is designed to remove post-reactor trip residual and core decay heat, transitioning the NuScale Power Module (NPM) to safe shutdown without external power, and to cool the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) at a rate that maintains fuel design limits and RCPB design conditions during return to power events. Following DHRS actuation, any subsequent ECCS actuation allows both systems to continue residual heat removal. The DHRS can be initiated from the control room or from outside the Main Control Room (MCR) in the MPS equipment rooms. It is a passive system with two independent trains, each capable of performing the safety function in the event of a single failure, and utilizes natural circulation from the steam generators (SGs) to dissipate heat to the reactor pool. The DHRS is designed with leakage detection and control to minimize worker and public exposure and ensure system availability, and is not directly connected to the RCS. In compliance with 10 CFR 50.63, the DHRS can maintain adequate core cooling for 72 hours during a station blackout. Radioactive contamination in the DHRS originates indirectly from the feedwater and main steam systems, and is minimized by an all-welded design, leakage detection provisions, and facility programs that limit contamination and radioactive waste generation.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) thermal-hydraulic performance analysis assessed the orifice loss coefficient under various initial conditions to confirm that the restriction orifice size ensures consistent heat transfer. Accumulation of noncondensible gas in the DHRS steam lines was identified as a factor that degrades system performance. As a two-phase natural circulation system, DHRS performance depends on factors such as Reactor Coolant System (RCS) temperature, with higher RCS temperatures increasing the driving temperature difference and thus DHRS heat transfer. The analysis, performed using NRELAP5, included three model categories: a Steady State Model (with constant primary side temperature, flow rate, reactor pool temperature, and DHRS inventory), Nominal Transient Models (to demonstrate DHRS cooling for typical initiating events), and Off-Normal Transient Models (to show adequate cooling under the most limiting conditions). The steady state model calculated the DHRS heat transfer rate and evaluated the loss coefficient in the DHRS steam line to ensure acceptable performance across a range of DHRS inventories, including scenarios with additional steam volume due to MSIV failure, DHRS and SG fouling, SG tube plugging, and the presence of noncondensible gas. The limiting mass of dissolved gas that could be released during DHRS operation was also considered. Nominal transient models analyzed DHRS cooldown capability for cases with and without containment isolation, affecting the timing of pressurizer heater isolation. Off-normal transient models assessed DHRS performance under limiting conditions, including heat transfer surface fouling, plugged SG tubes, reactor pool water temperature, and noncondensible gas presence, with conservative biasing of valve timings to simulate worst-case DHRS inventory conditions. The overall analysis confirmed the DHRS capability to remove heat over a range of loop inventories.

design basis events that result in DHRS actuation.

conditions to below 420 degrees F within 36 hours assuming one DHRS train actuates 
and one of two associated actuation valves functions.

results.question: Does the NuScale ECCS design provide adequate core cooling during a loss-of-coolant accident?
results.expected_answer: Yes. The ECCS provides adequate cooling through passive valve operation. RVVs vent steam from RPV to CNV where it condenses, and RRVs allow condensed water to return to RPV by gravity. The IAB prevents spurious operation, and fail-open design ensures operation even without power. The combined RPV-CNV system maintains long-term coolant inventory.
results.generated_answer:
Yes, the NuScale ECCS design provides adequate core cooling during a loss-of-coolant accident, ensuring that core uncovery does not occur and all relevant ECCS acceptance criteria are met.  
(Source: Context, NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, DSRS 6.3, Rev 0, 10 CFR 50.46)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale ECCS (Emergency Core Cooling System) design differs from traditional systems by not utilizing high-pressure or low-pressure safety injection pumps, a containment spray system, or a sump. Instead, core decay heat removal is achieved through steam condensation and natural reactor coolant recirculation. During ECCS operation, water accumulates inside the NuScale containment vessel and is recirculated back to the reactor core, fulfilling the intent of relevant acceptance criteria. The design does not require personnel access during reactor operation, does not include pumps, and does not rely on containment pressure for ECCS recirculation as in typical PWRs. Consequently, purge or vent capability as specified by 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xv) is not required or included. Pre-operational testing of the ECCS will be conducted to satisfy regulatory guidance.

The NuScale design employs the chemical and volume control system as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to meet GDC 26 requirements. For containment pressure boundary fracture prevention (SRP 6.2.7, Rev 1) and most Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) criteria (DSRS 6.3, Rev 0), including ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46, single-failure consideration, design of non-safety-related ECCS portions, ECCS interfaces and shared systems, long-term cooling, ECCS outage times and unavailability reporting, and programmatic requirements, the design conforms to regulatory guidance. However, departures are noted for inservice inspection and operability testing, and for combined reactivity control system capability and actuation provisions; specifically, the NuScale ECCS does not perform poison addition or provide a makeup function as required by GDC 27, though actuation signal guidance is applied. Control room habitability system requirements (SRP 6.4, Rev 3) for the emergency zone are met. For inadvertent ECCS operation (DSRS 15.6.6, Rev 0), the reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure remains below 110 percent of design value, and minimum DNBR is maintained. The design provides high point venting capability for noncondensible gases from the RCS and other systems to ensure adequate core cooling (50.34(f)(2)(vi)), and instruments in the control room give unambiguous indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(xviii), with all relevant requirements conformed to unless otherwise noted.

The NuScale reactor design omits a containment spray system and sump, but during Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) operation, water accumulates inside the containment vessel for recirculation to the reactor core, making the pH guideline in the acceptance criterion applicable. The ECCS design does not utilize pumps or refueling water storage tanks, and operates via natural circulation without the need for pumps. The condensate storage systems and components are located outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building, and failure of these facilities poses no reasonable risk to safety-related systems or plant operation. The Instrumentation and Controls (I&C) review process described in DSRS 7.0 applies to the Design Certification Application (DCA), but lacks specific acceptance criteria. Regarding gas accumulation, NuScale has determined that gas buildup will not affect ECCS performance under accident conditions; the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) does not interface with the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) and is instead connected to the secondary system.

The provided passages summarize the NuScale design’s conformance with various regulatory guidance and standards. Guidance specific to Boiling Water Reactors (BWRs), such as assumptions for evaluating radiological consequences of a loss of coolant accident (Section 1.3) and ECCS suction concerns (Issue 193), is not applicable to NuScale. Instrument lines do not penetrate the NuScale Power Module (NPM) containment (Section 1.11). For water sources for long-term recirculation cooling following a loss-of-coolant accident (RG 1.82), the NuScale design partially conforms, meeting the intent of regulatory positions on design criteria, performance standards, and analysis methods, though its system design differs from those addressed in the guidance. The NuScale design supports exemptions from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding reactor coolant system and containment sampling (SRP 13.3, Rev 3, II.27) and from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K for ECCS performance evaluation (DSRS 15.6.5, II.1). For radiological consequences of main steam line failures outside containment (SRP 15.1.5.A, Rev 2), NuScale partially conforms to guidance on technical specifications for primary and secondary coolant iodine activity and leak rates. The NuScale reactor design features an LTOP (Low Temperature Overpressure Protection) system that is not connected to a low-pressure system, but the guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation from the primary system remains applicable. NuScale supports an exemption from selected portions of 10 CFR 50, Appendix K, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, regarding Mass and Energy Release Analysis for Postulated Loss-of-Coolant Accidents (LOCAs), with no additional inspections, tests, analyses, or acceptance criteria (ITAAC) required. In the event of a LOCA, the NuScale design ensures that core uncovery does not occur, and it conforms to DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, and GDC 5 regarding the sharing of structures, systems, and components, with no exceptions. The design does not credit or utilize a suppression pool and conforms to DSRS 6.6, Rev 0, for inservice inspection and testing of Class 2 and 3 components, with all relevant components subject to inspection and no additional requirements.

The NuScale Power Plant design does not require certain systems to function following an accident, and these systems are not credited in the determination of radiological consequences. The application of the single-failure criterion to safety systems is confirmed, with no exceptions noted. For radiological consequence analyses, NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, as its safety analysis demonstrates that core damage does not occur during any design basis event, making some RG 1.183 guidance only partially applicable. Departures from RG 1.183 for limiting dose consequence analysis are justified in a topical report, and NuScale applies the alternative source term (AST) methodology, specifically the non-LOCA or transient-specific guidance of RG 1.183, for Chapter 15 events. The design uses a modified AST methodology to evaluate radiological consequences of accidents, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis methods (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models for LOCAs addressing only the technically relevant features required by Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30.

results.question: Is the NuScale water chemistry program adequate for preventing stress corrosion cracking of Alloy 690 SG tubes?
results.expected_answer: Yes. The chemistry program maintains low chloride (<=0.05 ppm), fluoride (<=0.05 ppm), and dissolved oxygen (<=0.005 ppm) with dissolved hydrogen at 25-50 cc/kg. These limits, combined with Alloy 690's inherent SCC resistance (thermally treated condition), provide adequate protection. Alloy 690 has demonstrated superior SCC resistance compared to the previous Alloy 600.
results.generated_answer: Yes, the NuScale water chemistry program is adequate for preventing stress corrosion cracking of Alloy 690 SG tubes, as it aligns with EPRI PWR Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines and Alloy 690 has proven resistant to PWSCC in both operating PWRs and laboratory tests (EPRI #1019086; NuScale FSAR Rev. 5).
retrieved_contexts: Alloy 690 tubing in the NuScale steam generator (SG) design exhibits little-to-no corrosion from either primary or secondary-side fluids, with primary and secondary chemistries equivalent to existing PWR SG designs. However, secondary-side flow conditions, including boiling, differ from traditional PWRs, leading to the assumption of uniform thinning on the tube inner diameter. The NuScale design incorporates improvements to restrict SG tube degradation, addressing historical issues in PWRs such as wastage and stress corrosion cracking. Key enhancements include thicker SG tube walls, providing a substantial degradation allowance beyond minimum design requirements, and lower reactor coolant flowrates compared to PWR recirculating SGs. The risk of foreign object introduction is comparable to existing designs, requiring no changes to SG program guidelines. Notable design differences are the helical shape of the SG tubing—whose minimum bend radius remains within the operational experience of PWRs—and a novel tube support structure that retains essential attributes of traditional plate supports. Preliminary assessments identified potential secondary-side corrosion mechanisms, such as under deposit pitting and intergranular attack, due to the once-through design and internal tube boiling. However, estimated defect growth rates are low, and the SG tube plugging criterion remains at a 40 percent through-wall defect, consistent with the existing PWR SG fleet.

The evaluation of secondary side instabilities in the NuScale steam generator (SG) design demonstrates that in-phase oscillations of secondary flow do not occur, and out-of-phase oscillatory flows in individual tubes cancel out, resulting in no net oscillating secondary flow. Acceptable instabilities are limited to tube mass flow fluctuations that do not induce reactor power oscillations exceeding fuel design limits and comply with ASME Code criteria. The comprehensive vibration assessment program, in accordance with Regulatory Guide 1.20, Revision 3, covers the SGs, main steam piping up to and including the main steam isolation valve (MSIV), SG supports, and SG tube supports within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) fluid volume. Flow-induced vibration (FIV) screening criteria were developed, and the low primary coolant flow rates in the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design result in low flow energy available to excite tube vibration. The analysis identified fluid elastic instability (affecting SG tubes), vortex shedding (affecting SG tubes and lower SG supports), and turbulent buffeting (affecting SG tubes, SG tube supports, lower SG supports, and SG inlet flow restrictors) as mechanisms requiring evaluation, considering both beam and lower frequency helical "breathing" vibration modes. Testing shows that the onset of fluid elastic instability in helical tube arrays is governed by the same parameters as straight tubes, with Connor's constants applied, and effective cross-flow velocities provide at least a 33% margin to instability onset. Vortex shedding is precluded for downstream tubes due to turbulence, and turbulent flow analysis yields a 20.8% fatigue usage factor and a maximum fretting wear thickness ratio of 34.2% over the 60-year design life, below the 40% allowable tube wall wear depth. The vibration analysis confirms that FIV is either not predicted or is acceptable for SG pressure retaining components, including main steam piping, MSIVs, and SG tube support assemblies. SG reliability and risk significance are evaluated per Sections 17.4 and Chapter 19, with low flow rates reducing FIV wear degradation risk and additional tube wall and FIV turbulent buffeting wear margins supporting the application of existing PWR SG Program requirements to the NuScale design. results.question: Is the NuScale RPV structural integrity program adequate for a 60-year design life?
results.expected_answer: Yes. The program includes material surveillance capsules, beltline chemistry restrictions, fracture toughness monitoring, and P-T limit management. The lower neutron fluence due to reduced core power results in adjusted reference temperatures well below regulatory screening criteria, providing substantial margin for the full 60-year life.

retrieved_contexts: The NuScale Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) Material Surveillance Program follows ASTM E185-82 requirements, as detailed in Table 5.3-4, using archived materials identical to those used for baseline and capsule specimens, but maintained as full-thickness sections for flexibility in future specimen fabrication. Archive materials are sourced from actual production forgings and weldments using the same weld wire heat and flux lot as production welds. The NuScale RPV is designed for a 60-year life, with the surveillance capsule withdrawal schedule for the first 40 years based on 32 effective full-power years (EFPY), per ASTM E185-82 Table 1. Three capsules suffice for the initial 40-year period, with a fourth capsule included for potential regulatory requirements at license extension. Capsules are housed in holders attached to the core barrel at mid-core height, positioned approximately 90 degrees apart to achieve a lead factor of ~2.5. The withdrawal schedule (Table 5.3-5) specifies: the 1st capsule at the first refuel outage (~2 EFPY or 1.55E+18 n/cm², E > 1 MeV), the 2nd at ~13 EFPY (when capsule fluence matches peak 32-EFPY RPV inside surface fluence), and the 3rd between ~13 and ~26 EFPY. The 4th capsule's withdrawal will be determined by regulations at license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT of 50°F for the limiting weld metal is reached at 4.27E+17 n/cm² (E > 1 MeV, 0.55 EFPY), but 28°F of this shift is a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F, likely causing overcompensation at low fluence. Therefore, the first capsule may be withdrawn at the end of the first fuel cycle rather than mid-cycle.

Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, and clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. results.question: Does the NuScale LTOP system provide adequate protection during low-temperature heatup operations?
results.expected_answer: Yes. The LTOP system provides temperature-dependent setpoints that track the vessel's fracture toughness capability. Multiple setpoint ranges ensure protection across all low-temperature conditions. The relief capacity is adequate to prevent exceeding P-T limits during credible overpressure events including inadvertent safety injection.

In the event of a LOCA, the NuScale design ensures that core uncovery does not occur, and it conforms to DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, and GDC 5 regarding the sharing of structures, systems, and components, with no exceptions.
