The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report describes integrated overpressure protection for secondary side components with the same design pressure as the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), ensuring ASME BPVC service limits are not exceeded during normal operations and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs). The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system is designed with sufficient capacity to prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding the pressure-temperature (P-T) limits specified in 10 CFR 50 Appendix G when below the LTOP enable temperature, thereby maintaining reactor pressure vessel (RPV) pressure below brittle fracture limits during all operational, maintenance, testing, or postulated accident conditions. During normal operational transients at full power, the system and core parameters are managed to prevent safety valve lift; if a safety valve does lift, the pressurizer (PZR) steam space is sized to avoid liquid discharge. LTOP analysis assumes a maximum PZR heater output of 880 kW and an additional 2 MW from core decay heat, demonstrating that reactor vent valves (RVVs) open before reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure exceeds the low temperature pressure limit. Table 5.2-10 provides LTOP pressure setpoints as a function of cold temperature, with PZR pressure limits ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, and LTOP not enabled above 318°F. Figure 5.2-4 illustrates the variable LTOP setpoint (PZR pressure) as a function of temperature, in relation to the saturation curve.

The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system in the NuScale Power Module (NPM) safeguards the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) during low temperature conditions by using three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) and two pilot-operated Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) mounted on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) upper head, all constructed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A standards. The RSVs, each with a 3-inch inlet, 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, and design temperature of 650°F, are sized to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the 2310 psia design pressure, with a minimum relief capacity of 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam at 3% overpressure. Nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% and 2100 psia ±1%, with a blowdown of 10% ±2%. LTOP is enabled when RCS cold temperature is ≤318°F, with pressure setpoints ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, determined by linear interpolation based on downcomer temperature at the steam generator outlet. The system is actuated by module protection system logic to open RVVs and limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, accounting for worst-case transients such as spurious pressurizer heater actuation (up to 880 kW) and 2 MW core decay heat. The setpoints include margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences, temperature variations, RVV opening delay, sensor response, and processing time. Three RVVs ensure LTOP protection even with a single active component failure, and overpressure protection for secondary side components is integrated to maintain ASME BPVC service limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs), with the most severe AOO being a turbine trip at full power without bypass. RVVs and RSVs are environmentally and functionally qualified per ASME QME-1 and RG 1.100, with direct position indication in the control room per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi). Pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, surveillance capsule locations, and test schedules are detailed in referenced tables and figures, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, 10 CFR 50.61, and GDC 15 for a 60-year plant life. Additional details on valve qualification, design, operation, and testing are provided in Sections 6.3, 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6.

The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system safeguards the reactor coolant system (RCS) during low temperature conditions by utilizing reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two pilot-operated relief and safety valves (RSVs) installed above the pressurizer volume on the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) head, which relieve RCS pressure directly into containment. LTOP is enabled when RCS cold temperature is ≤318°F, with a temperature-dependent pressure setpoint determined by linear interpolation of values in Table 5.2-10 and illustrated in Figure 5.2-4, based on the downcomer temperature at the steam generator outlet. The system is actuated by module protection system logic to open RVVs and limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, accounting for worst-case transients such as spurious pressurizer heater actuation. The setpoint incorporates margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences, temperature variations, RVV opening delay, sensor response, and processing time. Three RVVs with their actuators and controls ensure LTOP protection even with a single active component failure. Overpressure protection for secondary side components is integrated to maintain ASME BPVC service limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs). LTOP analysis assumes a maximum pressurizer heater output of 880 kW and 2 MW core decay heat, demonstrating RVVs open before RCS pressure exceeds the low temperature limit. LTOP setpoints range from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, with LTOP not enabled above 318°F. Pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, surveillance capsule locations, and test schedules are detailed in Tables 5.3-1, 5.3-5, 5.3-6, 5.3-7, and Figures 5.3-2 through 5.3-5, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, and 10 CFR 50.61 for a 60-year plant life. The inadvertent actuation block arming setpoint is set above the limiting RPV pressure for LTOP conditions to avoid interference with LTOP actuation. Pressure-temperature curves and containment pressure saturation curves are provided, with corrections for location and instrument uncertainty as described. Further details on RVV qualification, design, and operation are in Section 6.3, and additional testing and inspection information is found in Sections 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6.

results.question: Is the NuScale integral pressurizer design adequate for managing RCS pressure transients?
results.expected_answer: Yes. The integral pressurizer in the upper RPV head provides adequate pressure control using electric heaters and spray. The pressurizer volume accommodates RCS inventory changes during load following and transients. The integral design eliminates the surge line and associated LOCAs while maintaining the same pressure control functions as conventional designs.

The NuScale reactor facility features site-specific cooling tower location, orientation, equipment design, and operation, with the reactor core housed in a core support assembly seated in the lower reactor pressure vessel (RPV) assembly and connected to a central hot leg riser. Each Reactor Coolant System (RCS) line enters containment through welded penetrations on the containment upper head, with two containment isolation valves mounted externally, and a single pressurizer spray supply line also entering through the containment head. The pressurizer is integral to the reactor vessel, comprising the upper region above the naturally circulating coolant, separated by a baffle plate. The pressurizer includes the RPV upper shell and head, baffle plate, heater bundles, and two spray nozzles (each 1 inch in diameter). Instrumentation measures the steam-water interface level, providing input to the safety-related Module Protection System (MPS), including low water level protection for the heaters. The pressurizer heaters maintain RCS pressure during normal operation, supplying at least 785 kW (with a 15% margin), divided into three control groups per bundle, with two heater bundles per pressurizer, each bundle rated at 400 kW. Heater parameters include 480 Vac voltage and 60 Hz frequency, with heater sheath temperature at 800°F. The pressurizer operates at an internal pressure of 2100 psia, external pressure of 1000 psia, and temperature of 650°F. Pressurizer level is maintained within its operating band by operator action to add or reduce coolant inventory via the LRWS.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) features an integral containment system that provides primary containment for the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), as detailed in Section 6.2. The containment design conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, Rev 0 requirements, including design margin for containment pressure, heat removal capability, protection from external pressure, monitoring instrumentation, internal structures, and evaluation of accidents involving generated hydrogen or other modules. For the limiting event, the peak containment pressure remains below the design pressure. The NuScale design does not utilize an ice condenser containment, as specified in SRP 6.2.1.1.B, Draft Rev 3. Additionally, preliminary design information requirements under 10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(v) are not applicable to the design certification application per 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8) and 10 CFR 50.34(f). Further details on containment systems are provided in Section 6.2.

The NuScale design was evaluated against DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, Rev 0, which covers initiating events such as Loss of External Load, Turbine Trip, Loss of Condenser Vacuum, Closure of Main Steam Isolation Valve (MSIV), and Steam Pressure Regulator Failure (Closed). For the basic objectives regarding initiating events (II.1), the design conforms fully. For specific criteria for events of moderate frequency (II.2), the design partially conforms because NuScale does not have a steam pressure regulator. The design conforms to criteria for Reactor Coolant System and Main Steam System Pressures (II.2.A), Incidents of Moderate Frequency (II.2.C), Instrument Setpoints and their impact on plant response (II.2.D), Most Limiting Plant System Single Failure (II.2.E), and Performance of Nonsafety-Related Systems and Single Failures of Active and Passive Systems (II.2.F). The analyzed Anticipated Operational Occurrences (AOOs) that could lead to Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) overpressure include loss of load, turbine trip (with and without bypass), loss of condenser vacuum, inadvertent MSIV closure, steam pressure regulator failure closed, loss of normal AC power, loss of feedwater, and inadvertent operation of the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS). These AOOs are detailed in DCD Chapter 15, including initial conditions, system parameters, analysis assumptions, systems and equipment assumed to operate, reactor trip signals, and sensitivity analyses.

The NuScale design employs the chemical and volume control system as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to meet GDC 26 requirements. For containment pressure boundary fracture prevention (SRP 6.2.7, Rev 1) and most Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) criteria (DSRS 6.3, Rev 0), including ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46, single-failure consideration, design of non-safety-related ECCS portions, ECCS interfaces and shared systems, long-term cooling, ECCS outage times and unavailability reporting, and programmatic requirements, the design conforms to regulatory guidance. However, departures are noted for inservice inspection and operability testing, and for combined reactivity control system capability and actuation provisions; specifically, the NuScale ECCS does not perform poison addition or provide a makeup function as required by GDC 27, though actuation signal guidance is applied. Control room habitability system requirements (SRP 6.4, Rev 3) for the emergency zone are met. For inadvertent ECCS operation (DSRS 15.6.6, Rev 0), the reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure remains below 110 percent of design value, and minimum DNBR is maintained. The design provides high point venting capability for noncondensible gases from the RCS and other systems to ensure adequate core cooling (50.34(f)(2)(vi)), and instruments in the control room give unambiguous indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(xviii), with all relevant requirements conformed to unless otherwise noted.

results.question: Does the elimination of large-bore RCS piping adequately address LOCA concerns?
results.expected_answer: Yes. By integrating all RCS components within the RPV, NuScale eliminates large-bore piping that could fail and cause a LBLOCA. The remaining small-bore connections (CVCS, instrument lines) limit break size to small-break LOCAs, which are within ECCS and DHRS mitigation capability. This is a fundamental safety improvement over conventional loop-type PWRs.

The criticality analysis adheres to DSS-ISG-2010-01 by using a single fuel assembly design without credit for burnup, zoning, loading patterns, or lumped fission products; depletion analysis, axial burnup profile, and interface considerations all explicitly state that burnup credit is not applied. The rack model is appropriate for the analyzed conditions, accounting for an additional adjacent assembly under normal conditions. Code validation includes area of applicability, trend analysis, bias, and uncertainty, with code-to-code comparisons performed and no reliance on precedents; all references are publicly available and cited in SFP criticality analyses, and assumptions are conservative or justified. The reactor design conforms to DSRS 4.4, Rev 0 and DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0, meeting requirements for subchannel hydraulic analysis, core oscillations, thermal-hydraulic instabilities, CHF calculations, process monitoring, and core cooling instrumentation, with no exceptions except for the loose parts monitoring system, which is not required due to low primary system flow. Core stability during ATWS is not applicable due to diverse RTS signals that prevent ATWS and flow instabilities; DSRS 15.9.A II.1 has no requirements, II.2 is met via GDC 12, and II.3 and trip-before-CHF criteria are not applicable as reactor trip signals act before instabilities or CHF violations. For II.5, partial conformance is achieved as existing trip signals provide a more limiting exclusion zone than required for flow instability and SAFDL protection. The review of DSRS 15.1.6, Rev 0, "Loss of Containment Vacuum," confirms full conformance with all criteria and analytical parameters: reactor coolant pressure and cladding integrity are maintained, AOOs do not worsen conditions, instrument spans and setpoints follow RG 1.105, the limiting single failure is identified, a conservative scram is used, core burnup is considered, RCS-to-containment/pool heat transfer is maximized, and setpoint inaccuracies are addressed per RG 1.105, with no exceptions or comments. Containment heat removal capability and LOCA design margin (DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, Rev 0, II.3) meet requirements with no exceptions; mass and energy release analysis for postulated LOCAs (DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, II.3) and its ITAAC are not applicable to this design. Containment heat removal systems (DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, II.5 and II.6) comply with 10 CFR 50.46(b)(5) for long-term cooling and water level margin in the presence of LOCA-generated and latent debris. The LOCA acceptance criteria use a temperature limit more restrictive than 2,200°F, conforming to DSRS 15.0, Rev 0, I.

results.question: Is the NuScale SG tube material selection (Alloy 690) adequate for the expected service conditions?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Alloy 690 thermally treated provides excellent resistance to primary water SCC, which was the primary degradation mechanism for the previously used Alloy 600. Combined with strict water chemistry controls (low oxygen, controlled hydrogen), the material selection is adequate for the 60-year design life under NuScale operating conditions.
results.generated_answer: Yes, the NuScale SG tube material selection of Alloy 690 is adequate for the expected service conditions, as it exhibits little-to-no corrosion from either primary or secondary-side fluids and retains acceptable tube integrity with up to 50% wall thickness degradation under all loading conditions (Source: provided context).
retrieved_contexts: Alloy 690 tubing in the NuScale steam generator (SG) design exhibits little-to-no corrosion from either primary or secondary-side fluids, with primary and secondary chemistries equivalent to existing PWR SG designs. However, secondary-side flow conditions, including boiling, differ from traditional PWRs, leading to the assumption of uniform thinning on the tube inner diameter. The NuScale design incorporates improvements to restrict SG tube degradation, addressing historical issues in PWRs such as wastage and stress corrosion cracking. Key enhancements include thicker SG tube walls, providing a substantial degradation allowance beyond minimum design requirements, and lower reactor coolant flowrates compared to PWR recirculating SGs. The risk of foreign object introduction is comparable to existing designs, requiring no changes to SG program guidelines. Notable design differences are the helical shape of the SG tubing—whose minimum bend radius remains within the operational experience of PWRs—and a novel tube support structure that retains essential attributes of traditional plate supports. Preliminary assessments identified potential secondary-side corrosion mechanisms, such as under deposit pitting and intergranular attack, due to the once-through design and internal tube boiling. However, estimated defect growth rates are low, and the SG tube plugging criterion remains at a 40 percent through-wall defect, consistent with the existing PWR SG fleet.

The evaluation of secondary side instabilities in the NuScale steam generator (SG) design demonstrates that in-phase oscillations of secondary flow do not occur, and out-of-phase oscillatory flows in individual tubes cancel out, resulting in no net oscillating secondary flow. Acceptable instabilities are limited to tube mass flow fluctuations that do not induce reactor power oscillations exceeding fuel design limits and comply with ASME Code criteria. The comprehensive vibration assessment program, in accordance with Regulatory Guide 1.20, Revision 3, covers the SGs, main steam piping up to and including the main steam isolation valve (MSIV), SG supports, and SG tube supports within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) fluid volume. Flow-induced vibration (FIV) screening criteria were developed, and the low primary coolant flow rates in the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design result in low flow energy available to excite tube vibration. The analysis identified fluid elastic instability (affecting SG tubes), vortex shedding (affecting SG tubes and lower SG supports), and turbulent buffeting (affecting SG tubes, SG tube supports, lower SG supports, and SG inlet flow restrictors) as mechanisms requiring evaluation, considering both beam and lower frequency helical "breathing" vibration modes. Testing shows that the onset of fluid elastic instability in helical tube arrays is governed by the same parameters as straight tubes, with Connor's constants applied, and effective cross-flow velocities provide at least a 33% margin to instability onset. Vortex shedding is precluded for downstream tubes due to turbulence, and turbulent flow analysis yields a 20.8% fatigue usage factor and a maximum fretting wear thickness ratio of 34.2% over the 60-year design life, below the 40% allowable tube wall wear depth. The vibration analysis confirms that FIV is either not predicted or is acceptable for SG pressure retaining components, including main steam piping, MSIVs, and SG tube support assemblies. SG reliability and risk significance are evaluated per Sections 17.4 and Chapter 19, with low flow rates reducing FIV wear degradation risk and additional tube wall and FIV turbulent buffeting wear margins supporting the application of existing PWR SG Program requirements to the NuScale design. Alloy 690 tubing in the NuScale SG exhibits little-to-no corrosion from either primary or secondary-side fluids, with chemistries equivalent to existing PWR SGs, though secondary-side boiling conditions differ, leading to the assumption of uniform inner diameter thinning. The design incorporates thicker SG tube walls for greater degradation allowance and lower reactor coolant flowrates, addressing historical PWR issues such as wastage and stress corrosion cracking. The risk of foreign object introduction is comparable to existing designs, requiring no changes to SG program guidelines. Notable differences include the helical tube shape—within PWR operational experience—and a novel tube support structure retaining essential attributes of traditional plate supports. Preliminary assessments identified potential secondary-side corrosion mechanisms, such as under deposit p

The steam generator (SG) tube program encompasses comprehensive management of tube integrity, including assessment of degradation, inspection requirements, integrity evaluation, tube plugging, leakage monitoring, shell side integrity, water chemistry control, foreign material exclusion, loose parts management, contractor oversight, self-assessment, and reporting. SG tubes, made from SB-163 Alloy 690 (UNS N06690) with a nominal wall thickness of 0.050 inch (including a 0.010-inch lifetime degradation allowance), are fully accessible for nondestructive volumetric examination along their entire length due to the absence of U-bends, as specified in Item B16.10 of Table IWB-2500-1 (B-Q). Preservice examinations are performed per BPVC Section III, Paragraph NB-5280, and Section XI, Subarticle IWB-2200, after installation and hydrostatic testing but before initial power operation, establishing a baseline for future inservice inspections. Tubes with flaws exceeding 40% wall thickness, compromising integrity before the first inservice inspection, or affecting future inspectability must be plugged. The SG Program, based on NEI 97-06, Regulatory Guide 1.121, ASME Code Sections III and XI, EPRI guidance, and 10 CFR 50.55a(b)(2)(iii), ensures structural and leakage integrity, minimizes worker dose by allowing component access without excessive removal, and is integrated into the Inservice Inspection (ISI) program. Prototypic testing of tube supports validates performance, with wear identified as the most likely degradation mechanism. The program supports tube plugging criteria and inspection frequencies consistent with the operating PWR fleet, and requires COL applicants referencing the NuScale design to implement periodic monitoring per Appendix B to 10 CFR 50. The fouling factor is applied to the tube inner surface, with the thin oxide layer on the outer surface excluded from calculations. Degradation evaluations confirm tube integrity with up to 50% wall loss under all loading conditions, with minimum wall thickness assessed for combined design basis pipe break and safe shutdown earthquake loads, ensuring compliance with ASME Code Level D Service condition stress limits. SGs and associated components are classified per Table 3.2-1, with pressure boundary materials and welds meeting BPVC Section II and III requirements. Feedwater and main steam piping are Quality Group B, Class 2, and all welding follows ASME BPVC requirements. SG supports are designed per BPVC Section III, Subsection NG, but are not Code stamped as “Core Supports,” though a 10 CFR 50 Appendix B quality program is required. The SG program monitors performance and condition to ensure continued intended function, with additional design, operational, and material details referenced in relevant sections and tables.

results.question: Does the NuScale CVCS design provide adequate boron control for reactivity management throughout the fuel cycle?
results.expected_answer: Yes. The CVCS provides boration and dilution capability to adjust boron concentration between 0 and 2000 ppm over the fuel cycle. This range covers the full reactivity swing from beginning to end of cycle. The CVCS also provides emergency boration capability for safe shutdown.

The NuScale design does not utilize combustible gas control systems and supports an exemption to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), as detailed in DCA Part 7, section 2. Additionally, the design supports an exemption from 50.34(f)(2)(xiv) and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, regarding containment isolation valve closure times, with no deviations noted. Hydrogen control systems within containment are not required, as combustion does not impact containment vessel (CNV) integrity, further supporting the exemption to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2). The design also supports an exemption to GDC 27, complying instead with a NuScale-specific principal design criterion as described in Section 3.1.4. For post-accident assessment, the NuScale design relies on radiation monitors under the bioshield and core exit temperature indications, supporting an exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding the requirement to obtain and analyze post-accident samples of the reactor coolant system and containment without exceeding prescribed radiation dose limits.

The design does not require certain systems post-accident and does not credit them in radiological consequence analyses, fully applying the single-failure criterion. NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, justifying departures in a topical report, and applies a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for Chapter 15 events, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models addressing only relevant features per Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30. The design does not use combustible gas control systems, supporting exemptions to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), 50.34(f)(2)(xiv), and GDC 27, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, for containment isolation valve closure times. Hydrogen control is not required, as combustion does not affect CNV integrity. Additional exemptions include 10 CFR 50.46a, 50.34(f)(2)(vi) (high point venting), and portions of 50.34(f)(2)(xx) (pressurizer level indicators). The CVCS does not include holdup tanks under vacuum but provides required venting and draining. The RPV high-point degasification vent is not safety-significant, and high-point vents are not needed. The LTOP system is not connected to a low-pressure system, but guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation applies. The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) for the PWR, including the Boron Recovery System, partially conforms to DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0 requirements regarding functional performance during adverse environmental phenomena, pumping capacity, and defense-in-depth RCS makeup, as its only safety-related function is to preclude inadvertent boron dilution of the reactor coolant system. The system conforms to single failure criteria and GDC 5, which apply solely to the two safety-related demineralized water isolation valves designed to prevent inadvertent boron dilution. The CVCS also conforms to requirements for minimization of contamination, system design and arrangement, detection of reactor coolant leakage outside containment, and ITAAC, with no additional comments. However, it only partially conforms to criteria for prevention of CVCS holdup tank wall buckling/failure and CVCS venting and draining, as some acceptance criteria reference CVCS designs not applicable to the NuScale design.

The NuScale reactor design employs the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) as an independent, non-safety-related reactivity control system to meet GDC 26, with its only safety-related function being the prevention of inadvertent boron dilution of the reactor coolant system. The CVCS, including the Boron Recovery System, partially conforms to DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0, meeting requirements for functional performance, pumping capacity, defense-in-depth RCS makeup, single failure criteria, GDC 5, minimization of contamination, system design, leakage detection, and ITAAC, but only partially meets criteria for holdup tank wall buckling/failure and venting/draining due to design differences. The Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) design diverges from traditional PWRs by omitting high- and low-pressure safety injection pumps, a containment spray system, and a sump; instead, it relies on steam condensation and natural recirculation of water that accumulates inside the containment vessel for core cooling, fulfilling ECCS acceptance criteria per 10 CFR 50.46 and related guidance. The ECCS does not perform poison addition or provide a makeup function as required by GDC 27, and departures are noted for inservice inspection, operability testing, and combined reactivity control system capability and actuation provisions, though actuation signal guidance is applied. The design ensures RCS pressure remains below 110% of design value during inadvertent ECCS operation, maintains minimum DNBR, provides high point venting for noncondensible gases, and includes control room instrumentation for unambiguous indication of inadequate core cooling per 50.34(f)(2)(vi) and (xviii). The ECCS does not require personnel access, pumps, or containment pressure for recirculation, and does not include refueling water storage tanks; condensate storage components are outside the Seismic Category I Reactor Building, with no safety risk from their failure. The Instrumentation and Controls review per DSRS 7.0 applies to the DCA but lacks specific acceptance criteria. Gas accumulation is not expected to affect ECCS performance, and the Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) interfaces only with the secondary system, not the RCS. Control room habitability system requirements (SRP 6.4, Rev 3) are met for the emergency zone, and pre-operational ECCS testing will be conducted to satisfy regulatory guidance.

results.question: Is the NuScale inservice inspection program adequate for monitoring RPV integrity?
results.expected_answer: Yes. The ISI program follows ASME Section XI requirements with volumetric and surface examinations of the vessel shell, welds, and nozzles. Inspections during refueling outages when the module is removed from the pool allow access to external surfaces. The 24-month refueling cycle provides regular inspection opportunities.
results.generated_answer:
Yes, the NuScale inservice inspection program is adequate for monitoring RPV integrity, as it complies with ASME BPVC Section XI requirements, includes comprehensive volumetric and visual examinations, and continuously monitors RPV leakage in containment.  
(Source: NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Sections 5.2.4, 5.3.1, Tables 5.2-6, 5.3-1, and referenced ASME BPVC requirements)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) Material Surveillance Program follows ASTM E185-82 requirements, as detailed in Table 5.3-4, using archived materials identical to those used for baseline and capsule specimens, but maintained as full-thickness sections for flexibility in future specimen fabrication. Archive materials are sourced from actual production forgings and weldments using the same weld wire heat and flux lot as production welds. The NuScale RPV is designed for a 60-year life, with the surveillance capsule withdrawal schedule for the first 40 years based on 32 effective full-power years (EFPY), per ASTM E185-82 Table 1. Three capsules suffice for the initial 40-year period, with a fourth capsule included for potential regulatory requirements at license extension. Capsules are housed in holders attached to the core barrel at mid-core height, positioned approximately 90 degrees apart to achieve a lead factor of ~2.5. The withdrawal schedule (Table 5.3-5) specifies: the 1st capsule at the first refuel outage (~2 EFPY or 1.55E+18 n/cm², E > 1 MeV), the 2nd at ~13 EFPY (when capsule fluence matches peak 32-EFPY RPV inside surface fluence), and the 3rd between ~13 and ~26 EFPY. The 4th capsule's withdrawal will be determined by regulations at license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT of 50°F for the limiting weld metal is reached at 4.27E+17 n/cm² (E > 1 MeV, 0.55 EFPY), but 28°F of this shift is a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F, likely causing overcompensation at low fluence. Therefore, the first capsule may be withdrawn at the end of the first fuel cycle rather than mid-cycle.

The passages detail the comprehensive inspection, testing, and fabrication requirements for the NuScale Nuclear Power Module (NPM) reactor vessel, reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), and associated systems, as specified in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report and in compliance with ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and NRC regulations. Reactor vessel and RCPB components are constructed from Type 3XX austenitic stainless steel (max 0.03 wt% C) to prevent sensitization and intergranular corrosion, with welding procedures qualified per ASME BPVC Section III (notably NB-4300, NB-4600, NB-4622), Section IX, and Regulatory Guides 1.43, 1.44, 1.50, and 1.71. Welders are qualified per ASME Section IX and RG 1.71. Austenitic stainless steel and Ni-Cr-Fe cladding (e.g., Alloy 52/152) are applied to low alloy steel surfaces, with specific layering (first layer 309L, subsequent 308L) and post-weld heat treatment at 1125°F ± 25°F; Alloy 600 and Alloy 82/182 are excluded. Nondestructive examination (NDE) of welds and tubular products follows ASME BPVC Section III and XI, with preservice ultrasonic and surface exams for Class 1 piping welds, and requirements varying by pipe size and location. Inspection and testing programs are based on ASME BPVC Section III (2013 Edition) for construction, Section XI for ISI, and ASME OM-2012 for operation and maintenance, as referenced in 10 CFR 50.55a and RGs 1.84, 1.147, and 1.192. ISI intervals, frequencies, and component selection are defined, with inspection elements for piping, supports, and valves detailed in Tables 5.2-6 through 5.2-9 and Figures 5.2-1, 5.2-2, 5.3-1, and 5.4-17. System leakage and VT-2 examinations are performed before startup, with continuous VT-2 monitoring of RPV leakage in containment. Valve position is verified every 24 months, set pressure every 5 years, and special seal verification tests are used for reactor pool valves. O-ring leakage and flange bolt inspections follow Section XI. Reactor vessel internals inspections, per MRP-227 and ASME BPVC Section XI, focus on core support blocks, plates, guide tubes, riser transitions, and fasteners, using VT-1 and VT-3, with augmented exams for critical components. The reactor vessel beltline is evaluated for Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (≥75 ft-lb initial, ≥50 ft-lb end-of-life), and plant procedures ensure compliance with pressure-temperature limits. The

Leakage testing is conducted after each installation of O-rings to ensure proper sealing, and the RPV and containment main flange bolts are inspected per ASME BPVC Section XI, Table IWB-2500-1 (B-G-1). Visual (VT-3) and volumetric examinations are performed on various RPV components, including the flow diverter to RPV lower head, interior surfaces, attachment welds (B-N-1), external welds, support plate to gussets, support plate to upper RPV SG shell (F-A), support gussets to upper RPV SG shell, and lateral support lug (B-K). RPV nozzle to shell and head welds, reactor recirc valve flange, feedwater nozzles, and RCS discharge (B-D) undergo volumetric inspection. All RVV and RRV flange threaded fasteners are subject to augmented volumetric examination per B-G-2, following B-G-1 guidance, with no sampling permitted; all are inspected during the interval, and RPV bolting two inches or less in diameter is examined by VT-1 if removed. Assembled RPV leakage is continuously monitored in the containment vessel (CNV) as a VT-2 examination per Section XI IWA-5241(c). RPV pressure-retaining and integrally attached materials are examined per ASME BPVC Section III, using methods from Section V as modified by Section III and additional requirements. Threaded insert fabrication inspections follow ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB-2580, using the outer diameter for sizing, and lock plates are used to hold the RPV flange nut during stud removal or installation. Inservice surveillance and threaded fastener requirements are detailed in Sections 5.2.4, 5.3.1, and 3.13.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements, design parameters, material composition, and major components of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Table 5.2-6 specifies that RPV shell and head welds, including connections such as the lower RPV flange shell to RPV bottom head and steam plenum cap to integral steam plenum, are subject to B-A category volumetric examination, while internal welds like the core support block to RPV bottom head undergo B-N-2 category VT-3 visual testing; instrumentation and controls sleeve welds are part of the cladding and not separately examined. Table 5.3-1 lists key RPV parameters: design pressure is 2100 psia, design temperature is 650°F, overall height is 778 inches, and diameters range from 96.5 to 115.5 inches depending on section, with inner and outer clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches, respectively. Table 5.3-2 provides maximum allowable concentrations for RPV beltline forging material (e.g., sulfur 0.010 wt%, phosphorus 0.010 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%) and as-deposited weld metal (e.g., sulfur 0.015 wt%, phosphorus 0.012 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, manganese 1.80 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%). Figure 5.3-1 identifies major RPV components, including the lower and upper RPV sections, RPV head section, CRDM latch housing section with 16 latch housings, RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum with 4 access ports, upper and lower RPV SG shells, feed plenum access ports, lower RPV section flange shell, RPV bottom head, upper RPV flanged transition shell, CRDM seismic support structure, and 4 instrument seal assemblies.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed to promote natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS ensures coolant supply to the core to maintain specified acceptable fuel design limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences. A schematic flow diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and Table 5.1-1 detail major RCS components, flow stages, and volumes during steady-state, full-power operation. The SGS, with the main steam and feedwater systems, removes decay heat from the RCS during initial cooldown. Optimal performance criteria include maximizing electrical generation, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining margin to analytical limits. Key parameters—such as primary coolant temperatures, flow rates, pressurizer (PZR) water level, steam generator (SG) water level and mass, feedwater and steam flow rates, pressures, and steam superheat—are determined using NRELAP5 thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand calculations. Design calculations establish best-estimate, maximum, and minimum primary coolant flows, accounting for uncertainties in component flow resistances, core bypass flow, and thermal head capability, with bounding uncertainties based on test data and design requirements. Minimum and maximum design flow rates are calculated by biasing analytical uncertainties to minimize or maximize flow, respectively, and are used in bounding design analyses. The module heatup system provides RCS heating and coolant flow before criticality; between hot zero power and 15% reactor power, core heat is used to reach normal operating coolant temperature. From 15% to full power, average primary coolant temperature is held constant, with higher full-power coolant temperatures for higher flow rates, ensuring similar hot leg temperatures and sufficient SG superheat regardless of flow. Flow rate varies with module heatup system performance, SG operation, and RCS temperature, with typical hot zero power temperatures and steady-state best-estimate flow rates identified. The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RCS to the secondary steam system, supplying superheated steam to the power conversion cycle, with operating conditions summarized in Table 5.4-1 for full-power steam flow at best-estimate primary coolant conditions.

An RCS average temperature of 420 degrees F was chosen based on the safe shutdown 
temperature proposed by EPRI for passive plant designs in the EPRI Advanced Light 
Water Reactor Utility Requirements Document (Reference 5.4-3) and determined to be 
acceptable by the Nuclear Regulatory Commission as documented in SECY-94-084.The DHRS heat removal function does not rely on actuating ECCS.

Performance Evaluation
A single RCS natural circulation flow loop is entirely contained within the RPV, thereby 
eliminating distinct RCS piping loops and the associated potential for a large pipe 
break (i.e., large break loss-of-coolant accident [LOCA]) event.This design, combined 
with the intertwined SGs tube bundle configuration, eliminates the potential for 
asymmetric core cooling and temperatures as a result of a loss of a single SG function.

The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed for natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during both power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps and external piping, with all major components—including the core, pressurizer (PZR), two steam generators (SGs), reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, and decay heat removal system (DHRS)—integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). The core heats the coolant, which rises through the lower and upper riser assemblies, is redirected by the PZR baffle plate, and flows over the SG helical tube bundles, transferring heat to the secondary side and producing superheated steam with a moisture content ≤0.10% by weight at full power. The cooled, denser coolant descends through the annular downcomer and reenters the core, completing the natural circulation loop, with flow driven by density differences and elevation between the core and SGs. The RCS flow path is optimized for low pressure losses, so pressure is primarily determined by static head, and SG tube plugging does not affect natural circulation flow rate. The system includes small bypass flow paths in the upper riser to maintain boron concentration during DHRS-driven riser uncovery after non-LOCA transients. The pressurizer, integral to the RPV above the coolant and separated by a baffle plate, operates at 2100 psia and 650°F, with two 1-inch spray nozzles and heater bundles (each 400 kW, total ≥785 kW with 15% margin, 480 Vac, 60 Hz, 800°F sheath temperature) to maintain RCS pressure. Instrumentation provides control room indication of RCS pressure, pressurizer level, spray flow, and heater output, with the Monitoring and Control System (MCS) automating RCS level and pressure control. The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) maintains pressurizer level (normally 50–60%), adjusts RCS chemistry, and controls boron concentration, supplying continuous spray flow to minimize thermal stresses and maintain chemistry. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core—89 ft³, SG region—621 ft³, and PZR—578 ft³ (487 ft³ cylindrical). During off-normal transients, initial pool temperatures of 100°F or 140°F have minimal effect on RCS cooldown after 36 hours, even with reduced heat removal from fouling, tube plugging, or noncondensible gas accumulation. The system’s design, including intertwined SG tube bundles and elimination of external piping, prevents asymmetric core cooling and large break LOCA events. The RSVs and RVVs are designed in accordance with ASME BPVC, Section III, Subarticle 
NB-3500 and function to satisfy the overpressure protection criteria described in ASME 
BPVC Section III, Article NB-7000.

Additionally, the RSVs provide overpressure protection for piping external 
to the RPV that forms part of the RCPB (e.g., RCS injection, discharge, degasification, 
and pressurizer spray piping up to and including the outermost CIVs; ECCS valve pilot 
actuator piping, and several CNV nozzles and their safe ends).

The RSVs are designed as part of the 
RCPB, are bolted via flange to a nozzle safe end directly to the RPV head, and the 
setpoint of each valve is designed to actuate the associated RSV on RCS high 
pressure to allow flow directly to containment.The RSVs are designed as Seismic Category I components, Quality Group A, 
constructed in accordance with ASME BPVC, Section III, Subsection NB.

Overpressure protection for each NuScale Power Module (NPM) is provided by two pilot-operated, self-actuating Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) mounted on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) upper head, discharging directly to the Containment Vessel (CNV) and forming part of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). The RSVs, designed and constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, are Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, and sized to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the design pressure (2310 psia), with each valve capable of 100% of the required relief capacity. The RSVs utilize RCS pressure routed to a chamber above the pilot disc, with relief pressure determined by the pilot valve spring pre-load and main valve closing spring, and feature a metal bellows, spring-operated pilot valve arrangement per NB-7511.1. Each RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, with periodic inspection and testing facilitated by bolted flange connections. The RSVs have a 3-inch inlet, 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, design temperature of 650°F, and a minimum design capacity of 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam at 3% overpressure. Nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% and 2100 psia ±1%, with a blowdown of 10% ±2%. Redundant RSVs ensure system reliability and provide overpressure protection for both the RCPB and steam generator (SG) tubing and plena. Overpressure protection during startup, shutdown, and low-temperature conditions is provided by three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs), also safety-related, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, and constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, with solenoid pilot actuators mounted outside the CNV and inadvertent actuation block features. RVVs prevent brittle fracture stress limits from being exceeded at low temperatures and provide sufficient relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure. Thermal relief valves, spring-operated and balanced-bellows, protect secondary system components and control rod drive system cooling piping during containment isolation but are not credited for safety-related overpressure protection during operation. All valves are environmentally and functionally qualified per ASME QME-1 and RG 1.100, with direct position indication provided in the control room per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi). The system is designed to comply with 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 15, and ASME BPVC service limits, with the most severe anticipated operational occurrence (AOO)—a turbine trip at full power without bypass—used as the bounding event for RSV capacity and RPV overpressure analyses.

5.2.2.4 
Equipment and Component Description
5.2.2.4.1 
Reactor Safety Valves
The RSVs are redundant, safety-related, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A 
components designed to maintain pressure below 110 percent of design pressure, 
2310 psia.Each valve is sized to provide 100 percent of the required relief capacity.

The Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) are integral components of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), bolted via flange to a nozzle safe end directly on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) head. Each RSV is designed to actuate on high Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure, allowing direct flow to containment, and is classified as Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB. The RSVs prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding 110% of design pressure under all conditions, with design information in Table 5.2-2 and materials in Table 6.1-3. Each pilot-operated RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, Subsubarticle NB-7520. Environmental qualification details are in Section 3.11. The Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) provide sufficient pressure relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure and are also environmentally qualified per Section 3.11. Both RSVs and RVVs are designed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-3500, meeting overpressure protection criteria in Article NB-7000, with applicable code editions and classifications detailed in Sections 5.2.1 and 3.2, and material specifications in Section 6.1. Functional qualification is performed per ASME QME-1 as endorsed by RG 1.100, with inservice testing and inspection ensuring continued reliability and conformance. RSVs are considered passive devices, and the reliability of both RSVs and RVVs is further discussed in Section 6.3.2.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, documents the design’s conformance with a range of regulatory criteria, noting both full and partial compliance depending on the applicability of specific systems and components. For BTP 5-2, Rev 3, NuScale’s low-temperature overpressurization protection (LTOP) system aligns with intent by not depending on offsite power. Partial conformance is noted for BTP 6-1 (emergency coolant water pH) and SRP 9.2.4 (potable and sanitary water), as some referenced SSCs are not present or systems do not interface with radioactive systems. Full conformance is achieved for SRP 9.2.1 (station service water) and SRP 9.2.6 (condensate storage), as these systems are neither safety-related nor risk-significant. Several SRPs, including 9.2.7 (chilled water), 9.5.4 (emergency diesel engine fuel oil storage), 10.2 (turbine generator inservice inspection), DSRS 10.2.3 (turbine rotor integrity), and 10.3.6 (steam and feedwater system materials), are not applicable due to the absence of relevant systems or reliance on design barriers. The design does not use safety-related pumps; overpressure is relieved directly to containment. The LTOP setpoint includes the following margin: pressure and temperature 
uncertainty; the difference in elevation between the pressure sensing 
instrumentation and the bottom of the RPV; the potential difference in 
temperature between downcomer regions; the maximum delay in RVV opening; 
delay in sensor response time; and module protection system processing time.As PZR level nears 100 percent, LTOP analysis shows pressure increasing rapidly 
and exceeding the LTOP pressure setpoint.

The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system safeguards the reactor coolant system (RCS) during low temperature conditions by utilizing reactor vent valves (RVVs) and two pilot-operated relief and safety valves (RSVs) installed above the pressurizer volume on the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) head, which relieve RCS pressure directly into containment. LTOP is enabled when RCS cold temperature is ≤318°F, with a temperature-dependent pressure setpoint determined by linear interpolation of values in Table 5.2-10 and illustrated in Figure 5.2-4, based on the downcomer temperature at the steam generator outlet. The system is actuated by module protection system logic to open RVVs and limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, accounting for worst-case transients such as spurious pressurizer heater actuation. The setpoint incorporates margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences, temperature variations, RVV opening delay, sensor response, and processing time. Three RVVs with their actuators and controls ensure LTOP protection even with a single active component failure. Overpressure protection for secondary side components is integrated to maintain ASME BPVC service limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs). LTOP analysis assumes a maximum pressurizer heater output of 880 kW and 2 MW core decay heat, demonstrating RVVs open before RCS pressure exceeds the low temperature limit. LTOP setpoints range from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, with LTOP not enabled above 318°F. Pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, surveillance capsule locations, and test schedules are detailed in Tables 5.3-1, 5.3-5, 5.3-6, 5.3-7, and Figures 5.3-2 through 5.3-5, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, and 10 CFR 50.61 for a 60-year plant life. The inadvertent actuation block arming setpoint is set above the limiting RPV pressure for LTOP conditions to avoid interference with LTOP actuation. Pressure-temperature curves and containment pressure saturation curves are provided, with corrections for location and instrument uncertainty as described. Further details on RVV qualification, design, and operation are in Section 6.3, and additional testing and inspection information is found in Sections 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report describes integrated overpressure protection for secondary side components with the same design pressure as the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), ensuring ASME BPVC service limits are not exceeded during normal operations and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs). The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system is designed with sufficient capacity to prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding the pressure-temperature (P-T) limits specified in 10 CFR 50 Appendix G when below the LTOP enable temperature, thereby maintaining reactor pressure vessel (RPV) pressure below brittle fracture limits during all operational, maintenance, testing, or postulated accident conditions. During normal operational transients at full power, the system and core parameters are managed to prevent safety valve lift; if a safety valve does lift, the pressurizer (PZR) steam space is sized to avoid liquid discharge. LTOP analysis assumes a maximum PZR heater output of 880 kW and an additional 2 MW from core decay heat, demonstrating that reactor vent valves (RVVs) open before reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure exceeds the low temperature pressure limit. Table 5.2-10 provides LTOP pressure setpoints as a function of cold temperature, with PZR pressure limits ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, and LTOP not enabled above 318°F. Figure 5.2-4 illustrates the variable LTOP setpoint (PZR pressure) as a function of temperature, in relation to the saturation curve.

The low temperature overpressure protection (LTOP) system utilizes reactor vent valves (RVVs) to provide overpressure protection for the reactor coolant system (RCS) during low temperature conditions. Two pilot-operated relief and safety valves (RSVs) are installed above the pressurizer volume on the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) head, relieving RCS pressure directly into containment. The LTOP system is enabled when the RCS cold temperature is less than or equal to 318°F, with a temperature-dependent pressure setpoint determined as a function of the cold temperature in the downcomer at the steam generator (SG) outlet. The setpoint is selected using linear interpolation between values provided in Table 5.2-10 and illustrated in Figure 5.2-4. Upon LTOP actuation by the module protection system logic, the RVVs open to limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, considering the worst-case transient, such as spurious pressurizer (PZR) heater actuation. Three RVVs, along with their actuators and controls, provide LTOP protection even with a single active component failure. Further details on RVV qualification, design, and operation are provided in Section 6.3.

Reactor vessel beltline materials are evaluated to ensure a minimum end-of-life Charpy upper shelf energy of 50 ft-lb. The ΔRTNDT at the 1/4-T location and the methodology for pressure and temperature limits are detailed in NuScale Technical Report TR-1015-18177. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening follows 10 CFR 50.61, using RG 1.99 equations for fluence and temperature factor calculations. At both 32 and 57 effective full-power years (EFPY), the predicted RTPTS values for the lower RPV shell, weld, and bottom head remain below the regulatory screening criteria (270°F for shell and head, 300°F for weld), with maximum adjusted reference temperatures at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head). The material specimen program matrix meets ASTM E185-82 requirements, with specified numbers of Charpy and tension specimens for both unirradiated and irradiated conditions. Operational restrictions are in place, such as prohibiting reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transient conditions and below 185°F during steady-state conditions. The data tables provide detailed fluence, RTNDT, and margin values for key vessel locations, confirming compliance with regulatory safety margins over the 60-year design life.

The NuScale reactor vessel beltline materials are evaluated for Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) in accordance with Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.99, Rev. 2, to ensure compliance with the 10 CFR 50, Appendix G requirement of a minimum end-of-life USE of 50 ft-lb at 57 effective full power years (EFPY) fluence, which bounds the 60-year design life. The initial Charpy USE is 75 ft-lb. Table 5.3-9 summarizes the predicted 1/4-T Charpy USE decrease at 57-EFPY fluence: for the lower RPV shell beltline (0.10 wt% Cu, 1.37E+19 n/cm², E > 1 MeV), the USE decreases by 20.5% to 59.6 ft-lb; for the lower RPV weld (0.06 wt% Cu, 3.21E+18 n/cm²), the decrease is 15.3% to 63.5 ft-lb; and for the RPV bottom head (0.10 wt% Cu, 3.21E+18 n/cm²), the decrease is 14.5% to 64.1 ft-lb. Table 5.3-10 provides the final 1/4-T Charpy USE values after adjusting for NuScale reactor pressure vessel irradiation temperature, applying a -1.2 ft-lb correction: lower RPV shell beltline, 58.4 ft-lb; lower RPV weld, 62.3 ft-lb; and RPV bottom head, 62.9 ft-lb. These values meet the regulatory minimum, and the Material Surveillance program monitors changes in fracture toughness properties throughout the vessel’s service life.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) thermal-hydraulic performance analysis assessed the orifice loss coefficient under various initial conditions to confirm that the restriction orifice size ensures consistent heat transfer. Accumulation of noncondensible gas in the DHRS steam lines was identified as a factor that degrades system performance. As a two-phase natural circulation system, DHRS performance depends on factors such as Reactor Coolant System (RCS) temperature, with higher RCS temperatures increasing the driving temperature difference and thus DHRS heat transfer. The analysis, performed using NRELAP5, included three model categories: a Steady State Model (with constant primary side temperature, flow rate, reactor pool temperature, and DHRS inventory), Nominal Transient Models (to demonstrate DHRS cooling for typical initiating events), and Off-Normal Transient Models (to show adequate cooling under the most limiting conditions). The steady state model calculated the DHRS heat transfer rate and evaluated the loss coefficient in the DHRS steam line to ensure acceptable performance across a range of DHRS inventories, including scenarios with additional steam volume due to MSIV failure, DHRS and SG fouling, SG tube plugging, and the presence of noncondensible gas. The limiting mass of dissolved gas that could be released during DHRS operation was also considered. Nominal transient models analyzed DHRS cooldown capability for cases with and without containment isolation, affecting the timing of pressurizer heater isolation. Off-normal transient models assessed DHRS performance under limiting conditions, including heat transfer surface fouling, plugged SG tubes, reactor pool water temperature, and noncondensible gas presence, with conservative biasing of valve timings to simulate worst-case DHRS inventory conditions. The overall analysis confirmed the DHRS capability to remove heat over a range of loop inventories.

To conservatively assess the heat removal capability of the system, the 
containment isolation valves and DHRS actuation valve timings are biased to result 
in the worst-case DHRS inventory conditions.Decay Heat Removal System Performance Analysis
The analysis evaluated the DHRS capability of removing heat over a range of DHRS 
loop inventories with the steady state model.

The NuScale reactor design and safety analysis methodologies are detailed in topical reports TR-0516-49416 and TR-0516-49422, with pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 EFPY calculated per ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects using Regulatory Guide 1.99 and ASTM E185-82 for surveillance. Fluence and pressure-temperature limits are further addressed in TR-0116-20781-P and TR-1015-18177-P. The design does not require certain systems post-accident and does not credit them in radiological consequence analyses, fully applying the single-failure criterion. NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, justifying departures in a topical report, and applies a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for Chapter 15 events, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models addressing only relevant features per Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30. Boric acid concentrations are selected to bound all operating conditions, with NRC-inspected testing per IP35034.1.5.2 and startup testing detailed in Section 14.2. Hydrogen control is not required, as combustion does not affect CNV integrity. Post-accident assessment relies on radiation monitors and core exit temperature, supporting exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding post-accident sampling. Additional exemptions include 10 CFR 50.46a, 50.34(f)(2)(vi) (high point venting), and portions of 50.34(f)(2)(xx) (pressurizer level indicators). The CVCS does not include holdup tanks under vacuum but provides required venting and draining. The RPV high-point degasification vent is not safety-significant, and high-point vents are not needed. The LTOP system is not connected to a low-pressure system, but guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation applies. NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, for LOCA mass and energy release analysis, and requires no additional ITAAC. The NuScale steam generator (SG) design minimizes crevices between SG tubes, tube supports, and tubesheets to limit corrosion product buildup, with tube-to-tubesheet contact fully within the primary coolant environment. Full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore eliminates crevices at the tube-to-tubesheet face, and tubes are expanded into both steam and feed plenum tubesheets. The once-through SG design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet plena, preventing accumulation or concentration of materials and making traditional SG blowdown systems unnecessary and ineffective for removing corrosion products or impurities. Instead, any secondary coolant impurities or corrosion products may deposit as scale or film on interior tube surfaces or be removed by carryover, with low concentrations ensured by material selection and chemistry control. Periodic cleaning during outages, using proven chemical or mechanical methods, removes unacceptable corrosion product films. Secondary side SG surfaces are constructed of corrosion-resistant materials such as nickel alloy, stainless steel, or stainless steel cladding, eliminating concerns about degradation from cleaning solutions and negating the need for invasive sludge removal techniques. The design provides access to both internal (secondary) and external (reactor coolant) sides of tubesheets for inspection and foreign object removal. The tube support structure is located within the primary coolant environment, preventing secondary system corrosion products from depositing on the SG shell. Chemistry control for both primary and secondary water is maintained per industry guidelines, with modifications for the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design, ensuring corrosion protection for stainless steels and nickel alloys in SG components, as detailed in Sections 5.2.3, 6.1, and 10.3.5, with the secondary water quality control program following NEI 97-06.

The NuScale design incorporates several unique features and regulatory approaches. The use of a single-failure-proof crane eliminates the need for load drop accident analyses for spent fuel casks or Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs). Containment atmosphere management minimizes oxygen during normal operations and employs continuous hydrogen and oxygen monitoring post-accident, conforming to the intent of RG 1.7 but without active combustible gas control systems; the design partially meets DSRS 6.2.5 for gas concentration analysis but fully meets requirements for equipment survivability, containment integrity, and atmosphere mixing, supporting exemptions from containment leakage rate testing (GDC 52, 10 CFR 50 Appendix J Type A). The Decay Heat Removal (DHR) System generally conforms to GDC 5 and related requirements, with exemptions for GDC 19 and 34, and supports NuScale-specific principal design criteria; for containment heat removal (DSRS 6.2.2), the design meets GDC 39 but departs from GDC 40, and for combustible gas control (DSRS 6.2.5), it supports an exemption from GDC 41 power provisions. Equipment survivability ensures radiation protection, and the design does not rely on prompt sampling for accident source term assessment (10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii)). The Control Rod Drive System (CRDS) conforms to GDCs 4, 23, 25, 28, and 29, clarifies GDC 26 does not require two safety-related reactivity controls, and departs from GDC 27 in favor of a design-specific PDC. Testing confirmed CRA drop time and impact force, with partial conformance to RG 1.77, SRP 6.5.3, BTP 6-1, SRP 9.2.4, and SRP 9.3.2, and full conformance to SRP 4.2, 9.2.6, 15.4.2, and 15.4.7; the condensate storage system is non-safety-related, and potable/sanitary water systems do not interface with radioactive systems. The FSAR documents non-applicability or non-conformance with several regulatory positions (e.g., C1.1.3, C1.1.4, C1.3.12, C.2 except 2.2, C.3), partial conformance to GDC 54 for DHRS, and exemptions from GDC 38, 42, and 43 due to design features. The design does not use exclusion zone/buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability and is similar to large PWRs for effluent release calculations, making certain interim guidance inapplicable. Containment purge systems venting directly to the environment and SFP mitigating measures are not required due to design features (small containment, below-grade SFP

System Description
The RCS is a subsystem of the NPM and is located inside the CNV.The RCS includes the RPV, 
the integral PZR, the RVI, the RSVs, reactor coolant piping inside the CNV and the RCS 
instruments and controls.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) interfaces with several systems, including the chemical and volume control system (CVCS) via the containment system, the emergency core cooling system (ECCS) through reactor vent valves (RVVs) and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), the containment vessel (CNV), control rod drive system, steam generator system (SGS), and the primary sampling system (via the CVCS). The RCS piping external to the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) comprises two pressurizer (PZR) spray line branches from a common spray header, an RPV high point degasification line, an RCS injection line, and an RCS discharge line. The RCS injection line includes branch lines connecting to each ECCS valve reset valve. The reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) encompasses the RCS injection and discharge piping interfacing with the CVCS up to the outermost containment isolation valve (CIV) on the top of each nuclear power module (NPM). This piping, including associated isolation valves, is designed as ASME BPVC Section III, Class 1, while the piping from the outermost CIVs to the NPM flange connections is not part of the RCPB and is designed as Class 3. Pressure-retaining piping that penetrates the RCS and forms part of the RCPB includes the PZR spray supply, RCS injection, RCS discharge, and RPV high-point degasification piping, as defined in 10 CFR 50.2. Figures 5.1-2 and 6.6-1 illustrate the RCS piping configuration, including the location and number of RSVs and RVVs relative to the RPV and the routing from the CNV upper head to RPV penetrations. Design, fabrication, and operational provisions to mitigate stress-corrosion cracking are detailed in Sections 3.9, 3.12, and 5.2. The RCS piping supports CVCS functions (see Section 9.3.4), and preservice and inservice inspection (ISI) requirements for ASME Class 1 components, including RCS piping, are summarized in Section 5.2.

The NuScale Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed for natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during both power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS eliminates the need for reactor coolant pumps and external piping, with all major components—including the core, pressurizer (PZR), two steam generators (SGs), reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) valves, and decay heat removal system (DHRS)—integrated within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). The core heats the coolant, which rises through the lower and upper riser assemblies, is redirected by the PZR baffle plate, and flows over the SG helical tube bundles, transferring heat to the secondary side and producing superheated steam with a moisture content ≤0.10% by weight at full power. The cooled, denser coolant descends through the annular downcomer and reenters the core, completing the natural circulation loop, with flow driven by density differences and elevation between the core and SGs. The RCS flow path is optimized for low pressure losses, so pressure is primarily determined by static head, and SG tube plugging does not affect natural circulation flow rate. The system includes small bypass flow paths in the upper riser to maintain boron concentration during DHRS-driven riser uncovery after non-LOCA transients. The pressurizer, integral to the RPV above the coolant and separated by a baffle plate, operates at 2100 psia and 650°F, with two 1-inch spray nozzles and heater bundles (each 400 kW, total ≥785 kW with 15% margin, 480 Vac, 60 Hz, 800°F sheath temperature) to maintain RCS pressure. Instrumentation provides control room indication of RCS pressure, pressurizer level, spray flow, and heater output, with the Monitoring and Control System (MCS) automating RCS level and pressure control. The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) maintains pressurizer level (normally 50–60%), adjusts RCS chemistry, and controls boron concentration, supplying continuous spray flow to minimize thermal stresses and maintain chemistry. The RCS volumes are: hot leg—635 ft³, cold leg—578 ft³, core—89 ft³, SG region—621 ft³, and PZR—578 ft³ (487 ft³ cylindrical). During off-normal transients, initial pool temperatures of 100°F or 140°F have minimal effect on RCS cooldown after 36 hours, even with reduced heat removal from fouling, tube plugging, or noncondensible gas accumulation. The system’s design, including intertwined SG tube bundles and elimination of external piping, prevents asymmetric core cooling and large break LOCA events. Table 5.3-1 lists key RPV parameters: design pressure is 2100 psia, design temperature is 650°F, overall height is 778 inches, and diameters range from 96.5 to 115.5 inches depending on section, with inner and outer clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches, respectively. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides detailed operational and design data for the primary system and decay heat removal system. Table 5.1-2 presents primary system temperatures and flow rates at various reactor power levels, with reactor power ranging from 0% (0 MWt) to 100% (160 MWt). At 100% power and best-estimate flow, the primary flow is 587.0 kg/s, with core ΔT of 99.8°F, TCold at 496.6°F, Tavg at 543.3°F, and THot at 590.1°F. Minimum and maximum design flows at 100% power are 538.5 kg/s and 660.5 kg/s, respectively, with corresponding temperature variations. When core decay heat is unavailable, the module heatup system drives natural circulation. Transient load contributions, including fatigue, are considered in component calculations as per Table 3.9-3. For conservative safety analysis, an initial core power of 102% is assumed to account for measurement uncertainty, and a 1.2 multiplier is applied to core decay heat. The decay heat removal system is designed with an internal pressure of 2100 psia, actuation valve external pressure of 60 psia, passive condenser external pressure of 27 psia, and operates at 650°F. Each of the two condensers contains 80 tubes (1.315-inch outer diameter, 0.109-inch wall thickness), with 258.2 ft² external surface area per condenser and a fouling factor of 0.0005 hr-ft²-F/BTU. The system schematic (Figure 5.4-10) shows two passive condensers located in the reactor pool within the containment vessel, with associated valves, pressure transmitters, level transmitters, and connections to the steam generators.

The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) is designed to promote natural circulation, transferring approximately 160 MW of thermal power from the reactor core to the Steam Generator System (SGS) during power operation and subcritical conditions, including initial plant cooldown. The RCS ensures coolant supply to the core to maintain specified acceptable fuel design limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences. A schematic flow diagram (Figure 5.1-3) and Table 5.1-1 detail major RCS components, flow stages, and volumes during steady-state, full-power operation. The SGS, with the main steam and feedwater systems, removes decay heat from the RCS during initial cooldown. Optimal performance criteria include maximizing electrical generation, efficient use of support systems, and maintaining margin to analytical limits. Key parameters—such as primary coolant temperatures, flow rates, pressurizer (PZR) water level, steam generator (SG) water level and mass, feedwater and steam flow rates, pressures, and steam superheat—are determined using NRELAP5 thermal hydraulic software and verified by hand calculations. Design calculations establish best-estimate, maximum, and minimum primary coolant flows, accounting for uncertainties in component flow resistances, core bypass flow, and thermal head capability, with bounding uncertainties based on test data and design requirements. Minimum and maximum design flow rates are calculated by biasing analytical uncertainties to minimize or maximize flow, respectively, and are used in bounding design analyses. The module heatup system provides RCS heating and coolant flow before criticality; between hot zero power and 15% reactor power, core heat is used to reach normal operating coolant temperature. From 15% to full power, average primary coolant temperature is held constant, with higher full-power coolant temperatures for higher flow rates, ensuring similar hot leg temperatures and sufficient SG superheat regardless of flow. Flow rate varies with module heatup system performance, SG operation, and RCS temperature, with typical hot zero power temperatures and steady-state best-estimate flow rates identified. The SGs transfer sensible heat from the RCS to the secondary steam system, supplying superheated steam to the power conversion cycle, with operating conditions summarized in Table 5.4-1 for full-power steam flow at best-estimate primary coolant conditions.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the steam generator (SG) design and operating conditions for the Reactor Coolant System. At full load, the SGs collectively transfer 159.13 MWt, with each SG capable of handling half this load. The SG operates at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with outlet and inlet temperatures of 584.4°F and 299.7°F, respectively, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. The SG is a helical, once-through type, featuring 1,380 helical tubes arranged in 21 columns per NuScale Power Module (NPM). Both the internal and external pressures for the secondary and primary sides are 2,100 psia, while the SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia. The internal and external temperatures are 650°F, with SG piping in containment at 550°F. Tubes have an outer diameter of 0.625 inches and a wall thickness of 0.050 inches. The steam tubesheet and feed tubesheet thicknesses (without clad) are 4.0 inches and 6.0 inches, respectively, with secondary and primary clad thicknesses of 0.250 inches and 0.375 inches. With clad, the steam and feed tubesheet thicknesses are 4.625 inches and 6.625 inches. The total heat transfer area is 17,928 ft², and the fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. The minimum SG tube transition bend radius is at least 6.250 inches. Figures in the report illustrate the helical tube bundle geometry, transition regions, tube support arrangements, and the use of support tabs for tube stability within the SG columns.
