The NuScale Power Module (NPM) features two collocated steam generators (SGs) within the reactor pressure vessel, which transfer heat from the primary system to generate steam from feedwater via natural circulation under both normal and off-normal conditions. Thermal-hydraulic performance testing of the SGs was conducted in an electrically heated facility using Joule heating, where a known electrical current was applied to the tube walls to create a constant heat flux boundary condition. Three distinct heating zones were used to simulate subcooled, boiling, and superheat regions. Each SG includes a total heat transfer area with a 10% tube plugging margin, and a fouling factor (as specified in Table 5.4-2) is applied to account for typical end-of-cycle performance based on secondary water chemistry control. The SGs utilize seamless helical tubing, eliminating the need for traditional tube support plates; instead, austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies provide full-circumferential, though not continuous, support to minimize crevice formation and corrosion product buildup. Eight sets of tube support assemblies are positioned between each column of helical tubes, limiting unsupported tube lengths and ensuring sufficiently high modal frequencies to prevent unacceptable flow-induced vibration. The lower SG supports accommodate thermal growth and provide lateral support for the tube supports.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) steam generator (SG) is an integral, once-through, helical tube design located within the reactor pressure vessel, with two SGs per module. Each SG contains 1,380 seamless helical tubes in 21 columns, providing a total heat transfer area of 17,928 ft² and a 10% tube plugging margin. The SG operates at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with inlet and outlet temperatures of 299.7°F and 584.4°F, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. Both primary and secondary sides are rated at 2,100 psia and 650°F, while SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia and 550°F. Tubes have a 0.625-inch outer diameter, 0.050-inch wall thickness, and a minimum bend radius of 6.250 inches. Steam and feed tubesheets are 4.0 and 6.0 inches thick (without clad), increasing to 4.625 and 6.625 inches with clad (0.250 inches secondary, 0.375 inches primary). Eight austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies per column provide full-circumferential, non-continuous support, minimizing crevice formation and corrosion, and ensuring high vibration resistance. The design eliminates traditional tube support plates and uses full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore to minimize crevices, with all tube-to-tubesheet contact in the primary coolant. The once-through design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet, preventing impurity accumulation and eliminating the need for blowdown systems; impurities are managed by chemistry control and periodic cleaning. Secondary side surfaces use corrosion-resistant materials (nickel alloy or stainless steel), allowing safe cleaning and inspection. System monitoring is performed at ≥500 Hz, and preservice inspections include 100% volumetric tube and weld examination per BPVC. The SG tube support structure is within the primary coolant, preventing secondary system corrosion product deposition. Chemistry control follows industry guidelines with NPM modifications, and secondary water quality adheres to NEI 97-06. The fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. SG tube failure is indicated by pressure changes, and actuation valves have position indication.

Secondary side instability evaluation shows no in-phase oscillations and that out-of-phase oscillations cancel, resulting in no net oscillating secondary flow. Acceptable instabilities are limited to tube mass flow fluctuations that do not induce reactor power oscillations exceeding fuel design limits and comply with ASME Code. The vibration assessment program (per Regulatory Guide 1.20, Rev. 3) covers SGs, main steam piping (including MSIV), SG supports, and tube supports within the RPV. Flow-induced vibration (FIV) screening criteria were developed, and low primary coolant flow rates result in low

The NuScale Power Plant is designed for 1 to 12 NPMs with the associated primary and 
secondary systems and components necessary to produce power and maintain the facility.This includes main steam systems, turbine generator sets, condensate and feedwater 
systems, and shared external cooling water systems (Figure 1.2-3), plus module assembly 
equipment, fuel handling equipment, turbine maintenance equipment, and radioactive

The NuScale steam generator (SG) design minimizes crevices between SG tubes, tube supports, and tubesheets to limit corrosion product buildup, with tube-to-tubesheet contact fully within the primary coolant environment. Full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore eliminates crevices at the tube-to-tubesheet face, and tubes are expanded into both steam and feed plenum tubesheets. The once-through SG design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet plena, preventing accumulation or concentration of materials and making traditional SG blowdown systems unnecessary and ineffective for removing corrosion products or impurities. Instead, any secondary coolant impurities or corrosion products may deposit as scale or film on interior tube surfaces or be removed by carryover, with low concentrations ensured by material selection and chemistry control. Periodic cleaning during outages, using proven chemical or mechanical methods, removes unacceptable corrosion product films. Secondary side SG surfaces are constructed of corrosion-resistant materials such as nickel alloy, stainless steel, or stainless steel cladding, eliminating concerns about degradation from cleaning solutions and negating the need for invasive sludge removal techniques. The design provides access to both internal (secondary) and external (reactor coolant) sides of tubesheets for inspection and foreign object removal. The tube support structure is located within the primary coolant environment, preventing secondary system corrosion products from depositing on the SG shell. Chemistry control for both primary and secondary water is maintained per industry guidelines, with modifications for the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design, ensuring corrosion protection for stainless steels and nickel alloys in SG components, as detailed in Sections 5.2.3, 6.1, and 10.3.5, with the secondary water quality control program following NEI 97-06.

The RCPB encompasses all pressure-retaining components of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) up to and including the outermost containment isolation valve (CIV), reactor safety valves (RSVs), emergency core cooling system (ECCS) reactor vent valves (RVVs), and reactor recirculation valves (RRVs), as defined in 10 CFR 50.2. results.question: Based on Table 5.2-5 chemistry limits and the CVCS text description, does the NuScale chemistry program adequately prevent corrosion degradation?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Table 5.2-5 limits halides (Cl/F<=0.05 ppm), oxygen (<=0.005 ppm), and maintains hydrogen (25-50 cc/kg). This comprehensive approach prevents SCC, pitting, and general corrosion of RCS materials for the 60-year life.

The Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) for the PWR, including the Boron Recovery System, partially conforms to DSRS 9.3.4, Rev 0 requirements regarding functional performance during adverse environmental phenomena, pumping capacity, and defense-in-depth RCS makeup, as its only safety-related function is to preclude inadvertent boron dilution of the reactor coolant system. The system conforms to single failure criteria and GDC 5, which apply solely to the two safety-related demineralized water isolation valves designed to prevent inadvertent boron dilution. The CVCS also conforms to requirements for minimization of contamination, system design and arrangement, detection of reactor coolant leakage outside containment, and ITAAC, with no additional comments. However, it only partially conforms to criteria for prevention of CVCS holdup tank wall buckling/failure and CVCS venting and draining, as some acceptance criteria reference CVCS designs not applicable to the NuScale design.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, and associated documentation detail the inapplicability of numerous regulatory criteria and guidance documents—primarily those specific to Boiling Water Reactors (BWRs) and large Pressurized Water Reactors (PWRs)—due to NuScale’s unique design features, such as the absence of forced reactor coolant flow, coolant loops, pumps, and certain auxiliary systems. Regulatory guides and standards such as RG 1.79.1, BTP 3-2, SRP 4.6 II.8, BTP 10-1, and sections of SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5 are not applicable, nor are requirements for core spray and low pressure coolant injection systems (10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(viii)). The Strategic Water Chemistry Plan aligns with EPRI PWR Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines (e.g., EPRI #3002000505) and fuel vendor recommendations, specifying sampling and analysis frequencies, corrective actions, and impurity limits (e.g., chloride, fluoride, sulfate ≤ 0.05 ppm, hydrogen 25–50 cc/kg, boron 0–2000 ppm), with chemistry parameters conforming to RG 1.44, Rev 1. The plan ensures reactor pressure boundary and fuel integrity, with alkaline-reducing chemistry, controlled pH and lithium, dissolved hydrogen, and zinc addition to mitigate PWSCC and radiation fields. Effluent release calculations and coolant source terms follow methodologies from NUREG-0017, PWR-GALE86, ANSI/ANS 18.1-1999, and are consistent with NuScale’s technical reports, with decontamination factors and failed fuel fractions significantly less than 0.25%. NuScale’s design does not use CAOC, SG blowdown, or once-through/top-feed/preheat steam generators, rendering related guidance (BTP 4-1, SRP 10.4.8, SRP 10.4.6, BTP 10-2) inapplicable; only OTSGD B.2 test acceptance criteria for once-through SGs are relevant. Flow Accelerated Corrosion is addressed per DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0. For loss-of-coolant accident and recirculation cooling (RG 1.82), NuScale partially conforms, meeting intent but differing in system design. Overpressure protection for each NuScale Power Module (NPM) is provided by two pilot-operated, self-actuating Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) mounted on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) upper head, discharging directly to the Containment Vessel (CNV) and forming part of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB). The RSVs, designed and constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, are Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, and sized to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the design pressure (2310 psia), with each valve capable of 100% of the required relief capacity. The RSVs utilize RCS pressure routed to a chamber above the pilot disc, with relief pressure determined by the pilot valve spring pre-load and main valve closing spring, and feature a metal bellows, spring-operated pilot valve arrangement per NB-7511.1. Each RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, with periodic inspection and testing facilitated by bolted flange connections. The RSVs have a 3-inch inlet, 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, design temperature of 650°F, and a minimum design capacity of 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam at 3% overpressure. Nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% and 2100 psia ±1%, with a blowdown of 10% ±2%. Redundant RSVs ensure system reliability and provide overpressure protection for both the RCPB and steam generator (SG) tubing and plena. Overpressure protection during startup, shutdown, and low-temperature conditions is provided by three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs), also safety-related, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, and constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, with solenoid pilot actuators mounted outside the CNV and inadvertent actuation block features. RVVs prevent brittle fracture stress limits from being exceeded at low temperatures and provide sufficient relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure. Thermal relief valves, spring-operated and balanced-bellows, protect secondary system components and control rod drive system cooling piping during containment isolation but are not credited for safety-related overpressure protection during operation. All valves are environmentally and functionally qualified per ASME QME-1 and RG 1.100, with direct position indication provided in the control room per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi). The system is designed to comply with 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 15, and ASME BPVC service limits, with the most severe anticipated operational occurrence (AOO)—a turbine trip at full power without bypass—used as the bounding event for RSV capacity and RPV overpressure analyses.

Further description 
of the design of the RRVs and RVVs is covered in Section 6.3.2.5.2.2.6

NB-3500 and function to satisfy the overpressure protection criteria described in ASME 
BPVC Section III, Article NB-7000.

The Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) are integral components of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB), bolted via flange to a nozzle safe end directly on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) head. Each RSV is designed to actuate on high Reactor Coolant System (RCS) pressure, allowing direct flow to containment, and is classified as Seismic Category I, Quality Group A, constructed per ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB. The RSVs prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding 110% of design pressure under all conditions, with design information in Table 5.2-2 and materials in Table 6.1-3. Each pilot-operated RSV is designed for a 60-year service life and 300 cycles, in accordance with ASME BPVC Section III, Subsubarticle NB-7520. Environmental qualification details are in Section 3.11. The Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) provide sufficient pressure relief capacity for the most limiting single active failure and are also environmentally qualified per Section 3.11. Both RSVs and RVVs are designed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-3500, meeting overpressure protection criteria in Article NB-7000, with applicable code editions and classifications detailed in Sections 5.2.1 and 3.2, and material specifications in Section 6.1. Functional qualification is performed per ASME QME-1 as endorsed by RG 1.100, with inservice testing and inspection ensuring continued reliability and conformance. RSVs are considered passive devices, and the reliability of both RSVs and RVVs is further discussed in Section 6.3.2.

components designed to maintain pressure below 110 percent of design pressure,

results.question: Given the Ch.01 claim that NuScale eliminates LBLOCA, does Ch.05 provide adequate technical basis for this claim?

Ch.05's break spectrum analysis covers only small-break LOCAs, validating the Ch.01 claim.

The NuScale Power Plant design does not require certain systems to function following an accident, and these systems are not credited in the determination of radiological consequences. The application of the single-failure criterion to safety systems is confirmed, with no exceptions noted. For radiological consequence analyses, NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, as its safety analysis demonstrates that core damage does not occur during any design basis event, making some RG 1.183 guidance only partially applicable. Departures from RG 1.183 for limiting dose consequence analysis are justified in a topical report, and NuScale applies the alternative source term (AST) methodology, specifically the non-LOCA or transient-specific guidance of RG 1.183, for Chapter 15 events. The design uses a modified AST methodology to evaluate radiological consequences of accidents, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis methods (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models for LOCAs addressing only the technically relevant features required by Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30.

The NuScale design does not utilize combustible gas control systems and supports an exemption to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), as detailed in DCA Part 7, section 2. Additionally, the design supports an exemption from 50.34(f)(2)(xiv) and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, regarding containment isolation valve closure times, with no deviations noted. Hydrogen control systems within containment are not required, as combustion does not impact containment vessel (CNV) integrity, further supporting the exemption to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2). The design also supports an exemption to GDC 27, complying instead with a NuScale-specific principal design criterion as described in Section 3.1.4. For post-accident assessment, the NuScale design relies on radiation monitors under the bioshield and core exit temperature indications, supporting an exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding the requirement to obtain and analyze post-accident samples of the reactor coolant system and containment without exceeding prescribed radiation dose limits.

Safety Considerations
NuScale has achieved an improvement in safety over existing plants through 
simplicity of design, reliance on passive safety systems, and small fuel inventory.The integral design of the NPM eliminates external coolant loop piping, which 
eliminates large-break LOCA scenarios.

results.question: Does the Ch.05 DHRS analysis adequately support the Ch.01 claim of passive safety without AC power?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 claims NuScale safety systems require no AC power. Ch.05 DHRS analysis demonstrates adequate decay heat removal using only natural circulation through passive condensers in the reactor pool. No pumps, motors, or electrical actuation (beyond initial valve opening) is required, fully supporting the passive safety claim.

results.question: Is the NuScale 60-year design life claim from Ch.01 adequately supported by the Ch.05 RPV integrity analysis?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 claims 60-year design life. Ch.05 analyzes RPV embrittlement to 57 EFPY, showing all ART values well below PTS screening criteria and Charpy USE above 50 ft-lb requirements. The beltline chemistry restrictions and material surveillance program provide the technical basis for 60-year vessel integrity.
results.generated_answer: Yes, the NuScale 60-year design life claim is adequately supported by the Ch.05 RPV integrity analysis, which demonstrates compliance with fracture toughness, embrittlement, surveillance, and inspection requirements throughout the vessel’s service life (FSAR Ch.05, Tables 5.2-4, 5.3-2, 5.3-4, 5.3-5).
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report and supporting documentation confirm that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) values well above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb throughout the 60-year design life, with initial values of at least 75 ft-lb and predicted end-of-life values, after irradiation and temperature corrections, of 58.4 ft-lb (lower shell), 62.3 ft-lb (weld), and 62.9 ft-lb (bottom head) at 57 effective full-power years (EFPY). The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50, Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT for the limiting weld metal is 50°F at 0.55 EFPY, with 28°F of this due to a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening per 10 CFR 50.61 and RG 1.99 shows RTPTS values at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head), all below regulatory limits. Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The RPV is fabricated and inspected per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, with material specifications and chemical compositions detailed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.3-2. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, and diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, with clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. Major components are detailed in Figure 5.3-1, and all packaging, handling, and storage meet ASME and NQA-1 requirements. The surveillance and testing programs ensure ongoing compliance with fracture toughness, pressure-temperature, and inspection criteria throughout the vessel’s service life.

The NuScale Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) Material Surveillance Program follows ASTM E185-82 requirements, as detailed in Table 5.3-4, using archived materials identical to those used for baseline and capsule specimens, but maintained as full-thickness sections for flexibility in future specimen fabrication. Archive materials are sourced from actual production forgings and weldments using the same weld wire heat and flux lot as production welds. The NuScale RPV is designed for a 60-year life, with the surveillance capsule withdrawal schedule for the first 40 years based on 32 effective full-power years (EFPY), per ASTM E185-82 Table 1. Three capsules suffice for the initial 40-year period, with a fourth capsule included for potential regulatory requirements at license extension. Capsules are housed in holders attached to the core barrel at mid-core height, positioned approximately 90 degrees apart to achieve a lead factor of ~2.5. The withdrawal schedule (Table 5.3-5) specifies: the 1st capsule at the first refuel outage (~2 EFPY or 1.55E+18 n/cm², E > 1 MeV), the 2nd at ~13 EFPY (when capsule fluence matches peak 32-EFPY RPV inside surface fluence), and the 3rd between ~13 and ~26 EFPY. The 4th capsule's withdrawal will be determined by regulations at license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT of 50°F for the limiting weld metal is reached at 4.27E+17 n/cm² (E > 1 MeV, 0.55 EFPY), but 28°F of this shift is a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F, likely causing overcompensation at low fluence. Therefore, the first capsule may be withdrawn at the end of the first fuel cycle rather than mid-cycle.

The NuScale reactor design and safety analysis methodologies are detailed in topical reports TR-0516-49416 and TR-0516-49422, with pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 EFPY calculated per ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects using Regulatory Guide 1.99 and ASTM E185-82 for surveillance. Fluence and pressure-temperature limits are further addressed in TR-0116-20781-P and TR-1015-18177-P. The design does not require certain systems post-accident and does not credit them in radiological consequence analyses, fully applying the single-failure criterion. NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, justifying departures in a topical report, and applies a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for Chapter 15 events, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models addressing only relevant features per Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30. Boric acid concentrations are selected to bound all operating conditions, with NRC-inspected testing per IP35034.1.5.2 and startup testing detailed in Section 14.2. The design does not use combustible gas control systems, supporting exemptions to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), 50.34(f)(2)(xiv), and GDC 27, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, for containment isolation valve closure times. Hydrogen control is not required, as combustion does not affect CNV integrity. Post-accident assessment relies on radiation monitors and core exit temperature, supporting exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding post-accident sampling. Additional exemptions include 10 CFR 50.46a, 50.34(f)(2)(vi) (high point venting), and portions of 50.34(f)(2)(xx) (pressurizer level indicators). The CVCS does not include holdup tanks under vacuum but provides required venting and draining. The RPV high-point degasification vent is not safety-significant, and high-point vents are not needed. The LTOP system is not connected to a low-pressure system, but guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation applies. NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, for LOCA mass and energy release analysis, and requires no additional ITAAC. The design ensures no core uncovery during LOCA, conforms to DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, and GDC 5 for sharing of SSCs, does

results.question: Does the shared reactor pool described in Ch.01 present adequate heat sink capacity for the DHRS functions described in Ch.05?
results.expected_answer: Yes, but with constraints. Ch.01 describes a shared pool for 12 modules. Ch.05 shows DHRS heat rejection to this pool with a 140 degrees F temperature limit. The pool must absorb decay heat from potentially all 12 modules simultaneously. The pool design provides adequate capacity for this worst case, but the temperature limit constrains how long all modules can operate on DHRS.
results.generated_answer: Yes, the shared reactor pool provides adequate heat sink capacity for the DHRS functions, as analyses confirm the DHRS can remove heat in excess of decay heat after 36 hours with the RCS at 420°F and the pool at boiling conditions (see Ch.05).
retrieved_contexts:
The DHRS function is dependent on the presence of the reactor pool to remove heat 
from the DHRS passive condensers.The safety-related ultimate heat sink provided by 
the reactor pool is described in Section 9.2.5.The safety-related passive system is designed to cool the RCS from normal operating 
conditions to below 420 degrees F within 36 hours assuming one DHRS train actuates 
and one of two associated actuation valves functions.

The NPMs share the 
reactor pool as the ultimate heat sink for removal of decay heat from the DHRS passive 
condensers.The shared Reactor Building and other structures are described in 
Chapters 1 and 3 and the reactor pool is described in Section 9.2.5.DHRS active 
components fail-safe on a loss of power.Therefore, shared power supplies between 
NPMs do not impact the capability of performing the DHRS safety functions.

Results show that the potential oscillations do not affect the 
ability of the DHRS to remove heat.The DHRS has been shown to be capable of 
removing heat in excess of decay heat after 36 hours with the RCS at 420 degrees 
Fahrenheit and the pool at boiling conditions.Refer to Chapter 15 for plant initial conditions, assumptions, and response to 
design basis events that result in DHRS actuation.

Decay Heat Removal System Performance Results
The system performance analysis indicates the DHRS is capable of removing 
appreciable amounts of heat over a wide range of inventories and it is insensitive to 
a wide range of reactor pool temperatures and to the failure of an MSIV to close.The analysis also shows the ability to accommodate fouling and SG tube plugging.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) thermal-hydraulic performance analysis assessed the orifice loss coefficient under various initial conditions to confirm that the restriction orifice size ensures consistent heat transfer. Accumulation of noncondensible gas in the DHRS steam lines was identified as a factor that degrades system performance. As a two-phase natural circulation system, DHRS performance depends on factors such as Reactor Coolant System (RCS) temperature, with higher RCS temperatures increasing the driving temperature difference and thus DHRS heat transfer. The analysis, performed using NRELAP5, included three model categories: a Steady State Model (with constant primary side temperature, flow rate, reactor pool temperature, and DHRS inventory), Nominal Transient Models (to demonstrate DHRS cooling for typical initiating events), and Off-Normal Transient Models (to show adequate cooling under the most limiting conditions). The steady state model calculated the DHRS heat transfer rate and evaluated the loss coefficient in the DHRS steam line to ensure acceptable performance across a range of DHRS inventories, including scenarios with additional steam volume due to MSIV failure, DHRS and SG fouling, SG tube plugging, and the presence of noncondensible gas. The limiting mass of dissolved gas that could be released during DHRS operation was also considered. Nominal transient models analyzed DHRS cooldown capability for cases with and without containment isolation, affecting the timing of pressurizer heater isolation. Off-normal transient models assessed DHRS performance under limiting conditions, including heat transfer surface fouling, plugged SG tubes, reactor pool water temperature, and noncondensible gas presence, with conservative biasing of valve timings to simulate worst-case DHRS inventory conditions. The overall analysis confirmed the DHRS capability to remove heat over a range of loop inventories.

The DHRS is designed 
to remove post-reactor trip residual and core decay heat from operating conditions 
and transition the NPM to safe shutdown conditions without reliance on external 
power.The DHRS is designed to cool the RCS at a rate such that specified acceptable 
fuel design limits and the design conditions of the reactor coolant pressure boundary 
are not exceeded during a return to power event as described in Section 15.0.6.

An RCS average temperature of 420 degrees F was chosen based on the safe shutdown 
temperature proposed by EPRI for passive plant designs in the EPRI Advanced Light 
Water Reactor Utility Requirements Document (Reference 5.4-3) and determined to be 
acceptable by the Nuclear Regulatory Commission as documented in SECY-94-084.The DHRS heat removal function does not rely on actuating ECCS.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is a passive, closed-loop safety system designed to remove decay heat from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) using the steam generators (SGs) and natural circulation. The DHRS operates by circulating water from its condenser through the SG tubes, where it absorbs decay heat, converts to steam, and returns to the condenser to release heat to the reactor pool water. The system is connected to the steam and feedwater piping between the main steam isolation valves (MSIVs) and feedwater isolation valves (FWIVs), forming a closed loop outside containment and providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and reactor pool. Each DHRS train is associated with one of the two NPM SGs, with actuation valves and orifices arranged to promote natural circulation and moderate flow. The DHRS is designed to maintain the RCS average temperature below 420°F within 36 hours of an initiating event, as recommended by EPRI and accepted by the NRC, without challenging the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) or uncovering the core, and without requiring Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) actuation. Performance analyses, including steady-state and transient models using NRELAP5, confirm the DHRS can remove heat across a wide range of loop inventories, accommodate SG tube plugging, fouling, noncondensible gas accumulation, and MSIV failure, and is insensitive to reactor pool temperature variations. With two DHRS trains, RCS temperature drops below 420°F in less than 2 hours and stabilizes below 300°F within 36 hours; with one train and limiting conditions, RCS temperature remains below 420°F within 36 hours. Figures and tables referenced (e.g., Figures 1.2-8, 5.4-10 to 5.4-16; Tables 5.4-5, 5.4-6, 5.4-8) provide detailed design data, failure modes analysis, and temperature cooldown profiles for various DHRS operational scenarios, demonstrating robust decay heat removal capability under both nominal and off-normal conditions. System performance is monitored by RCS temperature and pressure sensors, and initial testing will verify DHRS operation against design basis requirements.

The Decay Heat Removal System (DHRS) is designed to ensure the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) average temperature remains below 420°F within 36 hours following an initiating event, without challenging the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) or uncovering the core. The 420°F threshold is based on the safe shutdown temperature recommended by EPRI for passive plant designs and accepted by the NRC. The DHRS does not require actuation of the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) and is shown to be insensitive to a wide range of reactor pool temperatures and to the failure of a Main Steam Isolation Valve (MSIV) to close. Performance analyses demonstrate that the DHRS can remove significant heat across a broad range of loop inventories and accommodate fouling and steam generator (SG) tube plugging. With two DHRS trains operating under nominal conditions, the RCS temperature drops below 420°F in less than 2 hours and stabilizes below 300°F within 36 hours. In low and high DHRS inventory cases, the RCS temperature stabilizes below 350°F and 400°F, respectively, within 36 hours, even with an initial reactor pool temperature of 140°F, consistent with Technical Specifications. The DHRS is also capable of cooling the RCS below 420°F in less than 36 hours with one train operating under limiting off-normal conditions and a single active MSIV failure. The system can remove heat in excess of decay heat after 36 hours, even with the RCS at 420°F and the pool at boiling. Initial testing will use the MHS to bring the RCS to near-normal conditions, then actuate the DHRS and monitor RCS temperature to verify performance against design basis requirements.

reactor pool water temperature - Pool water temperature affects the mode of 
heat transfer on the exterior of the DHRS condenser tubes.•
pressure losses – The DHRS loop pressure losses are dominated by the 
restriction orifice that limits the mass flow rate and heat removal.•
driving head – The DHRS loop driving head is provided by the elevation 
difference between the bottom of the DHRS condenser and the bottom of the 
SG.

results.question: Is the NuScale approach of eliminating emergency diesel generators (Ch.01) adequately justified by the passive safety systems in Ch.05?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01's Table 1.3-2 lists EDGs as eliminated. Ch.05 demonstrates that all safety functions (core cooling via natural circulation, decay heat removal via DHRS, ECCS via fail-open valves, containment via passive vacuum CNV) operate without AC power. The fail-safe design of ECCS valves and gravity-driven DHRS eliminates the need for backup power.
results.generated_answer: Yes, the NuScale approach of eliminating emergency diesel generators is adequately justified by its passive safety systems, as all safety-related functions are maintained for 72 hours post-accident without AC power, and the design does not require or include safety-related emergency diesel generators or their support systems (NuScale FSAR Rev. 5, Tier 2, 1.9-132; context).
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale Power Plant design omits emergency diesel generators, ESF rooms, and pressurizer power operated relief valves, rendering several Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections and Branch Technical Positions (BTPs)—including BTP 8-5, BTP 8-7, and SRP 9.5.5 through 9.5.8—inapplicable, as NuScale’s electric power systems are not engineered safety features and do not require safety-related emergency diesel generators or their support systems. The plant includes nonsafety-related Turbine Generator Buildings (TGBs), each with six turbine generator sets and auxiliaries, and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs), each with a single backup diesel generator and support equipment, none of which house safety-related systems or support ESFs. The Central Utility Building (CUB) provides common utility services, and the Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) supports plant auxiliaries. The design does not utilize or credit ESF filter or ventilation systems for accident mitigation; instead, nonsafety-related HVAC systems, such as the Reactor Building HVAC, provide atmosphere cleanup per RG 1.140 but are not credited in dose analyses or for offsite dose limits. Control room habitability is ensured by compressed air tanks supplying clean air during accidents, eliminating reliance on emergency filtration systems. The in-core instrumentation system monitors reactor and RCS parameters, transmitting data to the Monitoring and Control System (MCS). The plant’s safety systems do not depend on AC or DC power for actuation, and no onsite or offsite AC power is required to cope with design-basis events, making alternate AC power sources not applicable. Onsite backup diesel generators, housed in DGBs, are nonsafety-related and provide standby power at 480V AC and 13.8kV AC but are not required for safety functions during the first 72 hours post-accident and are not interconnected with other AC sources except for brief load testing. The electrical distribution system includes 13.8kV and switchyard systems, 4,160V AC medium voltage, 120V/480V AC low voltage, and a highly reliable 125V DC system for critical loads such as emergency lighting, MPS, PPS, and post-accident monitoring, with a separate normal DC system for nonsafety loads. The plant emphasizes fail-safe design, centralized multi-modular control, advanced automation, digital displays, soft control technology, and optimized workload management to minimize human error.

The NuScale plant design does not include emergency diesel generators, ESF rooms, or pressurizer power operated relief valves. Several Standard Review Plan (SRP) sections and Branch Technical Positions (BTPs) are not applicable to NuScale, including BTP 8-5 and BTP 8-7, as NuScale electric power systems are not engineered safety features and do not require safety-related emergency diesel generators. SRP sections 9.5.5 through 9.5.8, which address emergency diesel engine cooling water, starting, lubrication, and combustion air intake and exhaust systems, are also not applicable since these systems are not present in the NuScale design. SRP 9.4.4, concerning the turbine area ventilation system, does not apply because NuScale does not rely on this system for safety-related or risk-significant functions. For SRP 10.4.4, the NuScale design departs from the power provisions of GDC 34, instead complying with a NuScale-specific principal design criterion for residual heat removal. Additionally, DC/COL-ISG-21 guidance for gas turbine-driven standby emergency AC power systems is not applicable, as NuScale does not use such systems. For wind loadings (SRP 3.3.1), NuScale partially conforms by establishing bounding parameters.

Offsite power system requirements for Class 1E loads and grid stability studies are not applicable, with grid study responsibility assigned to the COL applicant. For DSRS 8.4, partial conformance with 10 CFR 50.63 and RG 1.155 is noted; all safety-related functions are maintained for 72 hours post-SBO without AC power, with RTNSS applied and EDSS independence addressed. The plant omits emergency diesel generators, ESF rooms, and pressurizer power operated relief valves, rendering several SRP sections and BTPs inapplicable. Nonsafety-related Turbine Generator Buildings (TGBs) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs) house turbine generators and backup diesel generators, none of which support safety-related systems. The Central Utility Building (CUB) and Site Cooling Water System (SCWS) provide common utility services and auxiliary support. The design does not credit ESF filter or ventilation systems for accident mitigation; nonsafety-related HVAC systems provide atmosphere cleanup per RG 1.140 but are not credited in dose analyses. Control room habitability is ensured by compressed air tanks supplying clean air during accidents, eliminating reliance on emergency filtration. results.question: Does the Ch.05 natural circulation analysis adequately justify Ch.01's claim that NuScale does not need reactor coolant pumps?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 lists 0 RCPs with natural circulation as the driving force. Ch.05 demonstrates that the tall RPV geometry, low power density, and temperature-driven buoyancy produce 587 kg/s at 100% power, adequate for heat removal. The self-regulating nature (lower flow increases delta-T, increasing driving force) provides inherent stability without pumps.

The NuScale reactor design does not require or include reactor coolant pumps, and therefore, safety-related pumps are not used in the design. The only pumps within the scope of safety criteria are the Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS) pumps, which are classified as ASME Class III because they contain reactor coolant during normal operation, but they do not serve any safety function. Additionally, the potential for a postulated startup reactivity accident, such as one initiated by an abnormal startup sequence, has been identified as an event requiring consideration in the NuScale reactor design.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report, Revision 5, and supporting documentation emphasize that many regulatory criteria and guidance documents—especially those tailored to Boiling Water Reactors (BWRs) and large Pressurized Water Reactors (PWRs)—are inapplicable to NuScale due to its unique, passive design. The plant eliminates forced reactor coolant flow, coolant loops, pumps, and several auxiliary systems, rendering guidance such as RG 1.79.1, BTP 3-2, SRP 4.6 II.8, BTP 10-1, SRP 15.4.4-15.4.5, and requirements for core spray and low pressure coolant injection systems (10 CFR 50.34(f)(1)(viii)) non-applicable. The Strategic Water Chemistry Plan aligns with EPRI PWR Primary Water Chemistry Guidelines (e.g., EPRI #3002000505) and fuel vendor recommendations, specifying impurity limits (chloride, fluoride, sulfate ≤ 0.05 ppm; hydrogen 25–50 cc/kg; boron 0–2000 ppm), sampling frequencies, corrective actions, and chemistry parameters per RG 1.44, Rev 1. The plan ensures reactor pressure boundary and fuel integrity through alkaline-reducing chemistry, controlled pH, lithium, dissolved hydrogen, and zinc addition to mitigate PWSCC and radiation fields. Effluent release and coolant source term calculations follow NUREG-0017, PWR-GALE86, ANSI/ANS 18.1-1999, and NuScale technical reports, with decontamination factors and failed fuel fractions well below 0.25%. Partial conformance is noted with RG 1.110, RG 1.112, and 10 CFR 50.34(b)(3), 50.34a, and 52.79(a)(3), while DC/COL-ISG-5 and guidance for CAOC, SG blowdown, and once-through/top-feed/preheat steam generators (BTP 4-1, SRP 10.4.8, SRP 10.4.6, BTP 10-2) are not applicable; only OTSGD B.2 test acceptance criteria for once-through SGs are relevant. Flow Accelerated Corrosion is addressed per DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0. For LOCA and recirculation cooling (RG 1.82), NuScale meets the intent but differs in system design, supporting exemptions for 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) and portions of Appendix K, and partially conforming to radiological consequences of main steam line failures. The design omits reactor coolant pumps, safety-related pumps, Class 1E motors, nonmetallic insulation, safety injection systems, auxiliary feedwater, power-operated pressurizer relief valves, and pressurizer heaters, instead

The provided passages detail the inapplicability of several regulatory guidance documents and technical requirements to the NuScale reactor design due to its reliance on passive safety features and natural circulation. Specifically, NuScale does not use reactor coolant pumps, continuous duty Class 1E motors, nonmetallic thermal insulation on RCPB or CNV components, or safety-related motor-operated valves, making guidance on flywheel integrity, motor qualification, and thermal overload protection irrelevant. The design also does not employ safety injection pumps, refueling or borated water storage tanks, or safety injection tanks, so requirements for ECCS accumulator lines and related motor-operated valves do not apply. Additionally, NuScale does not use removal of electric power from safety-related valves to meet the single failure criterion, and guidance on gas accumulation in systems dependent on forced circulation pumps is not relevant. However, guidance remains applicable for evaluating loading transients and structural components for postulated breaks in CVCS piping and reactor vent valve piping. Containment vessel evacuation system valve closure times are addressed separately under SRP Section 6.2.4.

The NuScale reactor design does not require or include reactor coolant pumps, making SRP 5.4.1.1, Rev 3: Pump Flywheel Integrity (PWR) and its acceptance criteria (II.1 through II.7) inapplicable. Similarly, SRP 5.4.11, Rev 4: Pressurizer Relief Tank and its criteria (II.1 and II.2) do not apply, as the NuScale design does not use a pressurizer relief tank. While much of the subtier guidance in DSRS 10.4.7, Rev 0: Condensate and Feedwater System references large LWR designs and is not relevant to NuScale, the design conforms to II.3 regarding sharing of structures, systems, and components (GDC 5), with no exceptions noted. Additionally, SRP 10.4.9, Rev 3: Auxiliary Feedwater System (PWR) is not applicable, as NuScale does not require or use an auxiliary feedwater system; instead, its decay heat removal system (DHRS) performs some similar functions.

results.question: Is the NuScale approach to seismic design (below-grade pool, Ch.01) consistent with the Ch.05 Seismic Category I classification of RCS components?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01's below-grade reactor pool provides inherent seismic protection by reducing amplification of ground motion. Ch.05's Seismic Category I classification of all RCS pressure boundary components per ASME Section III ensures structural integrity during design basis earthquakes. Together, the site design and component qualification provide a consistent seismic protection approach.
results.generated_answer:
Yes, the NuScale approach to seismic design for the below-grade pool (Ch.01) is consistent with the Ch.05 Seismic Category I classification of RCS components, as both conform to DSRS 3.7.1, 3.8.4, and 3.8.5 requirements for Seismic Category I structures and components, with no exceptions noted.  
(Source: Provided context, especially passages referencing DSRS 3.7.1, 3.8.4, 3.8.5 conformance and Seismic Category I classification.)
retrieved_contexts: The seismic subsystem analysis for the NuScale design conforms to the requirements of DSRS 3.7.3, Rev. 0, across multiple technical areas. These include seismic analysis methods, determination of the number of earthquake cycles, procedures for analytical modeling, and the basis for selection of frequencies. The analysis also addresses the combination of modal responses, interaction of non-seismic Category I subsystems with seismic Category I SSCs, multiply-supported equipment and components with distinct inputs, and the use of equivalent vertical static factors. Additional considerations include torsional effects of eccentric masses and the seismic analysis of Category I buried piping, conduits, and tunnels. The methods for seismic analysis of above-ground tanks are also addressed and conform to the standard. However, methods for seismic analysis of Category I concrete dams are not applicable, as the NuScale design does not utilize dams.

The provided passages detail the NuScale nuclear power plant’s conformance with seismic design and analysis requirements as specified in various regulatory standards and guidance documents. Site-specific ratios of peak ground acceleration (A), velocity (V), and displacement (D)—specifically V/A and AD/V²—are confirmed to be consistent with the controlling seismic events for the site-specific uniform hazard response spectra. Seismic design parameters, including ground motion and damping values, fully conform to DSRS 3.7.1, 3.7.2, and 3.7.3, Rev. 0, with no exceptions. The seismic system and subsystem analyses address analytical modeling, soil-structure interaction, modal response combination, interaction of non-seismic and seismic Category I SSCs, overturning moments, sliding forces, and frictional forces, all in compliance with DSRS 3.7.2 and 3.7.3. The Certified Seismic Design Response Spectra (CSDRS), though not developed using RG 1.60, envelopes the RG 1.60 spectrum anchored to 0.1g and serves as the Safe-Shutdown Earthquake (SSE); the Operating-Basis Earthquake (OBE) is set at one-third of the SSE, decoupling OBE from safety system design evaluation. Seismic design classification generally follows RG 1.29 and SRP 3.2.1, with a noted deviation: SSCs under Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.i are classified as Seismic Category II instead of I. Structural analysis for Seismic Category I structures, including foundations, conforms to DSRS 3.8.4 and 3.8.5, Rev. 0, with no exceptions; masonry walls and concrete dams are not used in the design. Requirements for winter precipitation loads on roofs are met per DC/COL-ISG-7. Seismic instrumentation partially conforms to RG 1.12 and SRP 3.7.4, as detectors cannot be installed inside containment and are instead located in the Reactor Building, with full compliance deferred to the COL applicant. Regulatory Guide 1.93 and draft JLD-ISG-12-04 are not applicable. Overall, the NuScale design demonstrates comprehensive compliance with seismic regulatory requirements, with minor exceptions and COL items noted.

The seismic design classification for nuclear power plants, as described in the referenced passages, partially conforms to regulatory requirements. Structures, systems, and components (SSCs) identified in Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.a through C.1.h are designated as Seismic Category I, in accordance with Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.29. However, SSCs that meet the criteria of Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.i are classified as Seismic Category II instead of Seismic Category I. The specific seismic classification for each component, as determined by RG 1.29, is detailed throughout the Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR). This approach is consistent with the acceptance criteria of SRP 3.2.1, Rev 2, which implements the requirements of GDC 2, 10 CFR 100 Appendix A, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix S, except for the noted deviation regarding SSCs under C.1.i.

results.question: Does the single control room concept (Ch.01) present regulatory concerns given the RCS complexity described in Ch.05?
results.expected_answer: No significant concerns. Ch.01 describes a single control room for all 12 modules. Ch.05 shows the RCS is greatly simplified with passive safety systems requiring no operator action for safety functions. The reduced operator workload per module (no RCPs, no active ECCS injection, passive DHRS) supports the single control room approach.

results.question: Is the 24-month refueling cycle (Ch.01) consistent with the Ch.05 RCS design for cooldown and depressurization?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 specifies 24-month refueling. Ch.05 describes the DHRS capability for controlled cooldown and the LTOP system for safe depressurization at low temperatures. The module can be safely cooled, depressurized, and disconnected for movement to the refueling area within the planned outage window.

results.question: Does the NuScale approach of having multiple small modules (Ch.01) provide safety advantages over a single large reactor, considering the Ch.05 RCS design?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Multiple small modules provide: (1) smaller radioactive source term per module, (2) ability to shut down and cool individual modules independently, (3) shared pool provides backup heat sink, and (4) the simplified passive RCS (Ch.05) means each module is self-contained with no dependencies on other modules for safety.
results.generated_answer:
Yes, the NuScale approach of multiple small modules provides safety advantages over a single large reactor by eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, reducing radioactive source term, and ensuring containment integrity even in worst-case accidents due to the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel and the use of passive safety systems.  
(Source: NuScale FSAR Rev. 5, Tier 2, Sections 1.2, Table 1.2-2)

1.2.1.1.3 
Safety Considerations
NuScale has achieved an improvement in safety over existing plants through 
simplicity of design, reliance on passive safety systems, and small fuel inventory.The integral design of the NPM eliminates external coolant loop piping, which 
eliminates large-break LOCA scenarios.

The NuScale design incorporates several advanced safety and operational features, including safe shutdown and cooling without reliance on AC or DC power, compact helical coil steam generators with reactor pressure on the tube exteriors, and a high-strength steel containment vessel immersed in a water pool. The containment operates at sub-atmospheric pressure during normal conditions, and the reactor core is small, resulting in a reduced source term. Comprehensive digital instrumentation and controls (I&C) are used for monitoring and control. The plant design is scalable, supporting incremental capacity growth in multi-unit configurations. The passive NuScale system eliminates pumps, sumps, suction strainers, debris interceptors, and trash racks, thereby minimizing debris-related and non-condensable effects on core coolant flow, and complies with regulatory guidance on debris management and chemical effects. Due to the modular and compact nature of the NuScale Power Module, passive separation of redundant safety trains is not always feasible; instead, fire protection measures are implemented to ensure at least one shutdown division remains operational in the event of a fire. Unlike traditional large water reactors, the NuScale design integrates the pressurizer above the reactor core within the reactor vessel, with no connecting piping, ensuring that core uncovery and damage cannot occur without first reaching the low low pressurizer level containment isolation setpoint.

Preliminary critical heat flux (CHF) tests for the NuScale small modular reactor were conducted at Stern Laboratories in Ontario, Canada, using a high-pressure flow loop and custom 5x5 fuel assembly simulators with a 2-meter heated length, various axial power profiles, and configurations with and without simulated guide tubes. These tests examined the effects of shorter fuel length and low-flow natural circulation, generating data for the development and validation of the NuScale NSP2 CHF correlation. Additional CHF testing at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics (KATHY) facility in Germany, using prototypic fuel designs, provided further data to validate NSP2 and develop the NSP4 CHF correlation for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. CHF occurrence was identified by temperature excursions in the fuel simulators, and data collected included bundle subchannel exit temperatures for mixing coefficient determination and single-phase/two-phase pressure drop characteristics across various bundle powers and hydraulic conditions. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, prepared under 10 CFR 52.47(a), details the plant’s general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, including process effluent radiation monitoring (Section 11.5.1.2.2). The NuScale plant comprises 12 modules, each with a 160 MWth reactor and 50 MWe gross output, for a total net output of 570 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The core uses UO2 fuel enriched below 4.95% with a 24-month refueling interval. Safety features include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps. The containment is designed for high pressure to maintain integrity during worst-case accidents, minimizing radioactive releases. The FSAR references tables and figures detailing plant and module characteristics (Tables 1.2-1, 1.2-2), system boundaries (Figure 1.2-2), module schematics (Figure 1.2-3), multi-module layouts (Figures 1.2-4, 1.2-5), module cutaways (Figure 1.2-6), steam generator and reactor flow (Figure 1.2-7), technical reports (Table 1.6-2), and I&C diagrams (Table 1.7-1). The NuScale module (NPM) has a nominal gross electrical output of 50 MWe and a core thermal output of 160 MWt, compared to 1,

The NuScale Power Plant features a compact nuclear island and is capable of operating in "island mode." It is designed for sites with characteristics (seismology, hydrology, meteorology, geology, etc.) within the bounds specified in Chapter 2, Site Characteristics. The plant includes two sets of cooling towers for 12 NuScale Power Modules (NPMs). Principal design criteria emphasize a simple, safe reactor with reliable, passive safety systems that do not rely on electrical power, and safety features that ensure a core damage frequency significantly lower than current light water reactor fleets. Key NPM features include a small, modular design; an integral pressurized water reactor (PWR) nuclear steam supply system (NSSS) that combines the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating external piping; natural circulation for reactor coolant flow, removing the need for reactor coolant pumps; an RPV housed in a steel containment partially immersed in water for passive heat removal; and a steel containment operated at a vacuum, eliminating the need for RPV insulation. The integral design eliminates external coolant loop piping, removing large-break loss-of-coolant accident (LOCA) scenarios. Passive safety systems provide decay heat removal, emergency core cooling, and control room habitability without external power, ensuring that small-break LOCAs do not challenge plant safety. The design results in a lower core damage frequency and a smaller radioactive source term compared to conventional 1,000 MWe reactors, reducing the amount of radioactive material available for release during postulated accidents.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, is prepared in accordance with 10 CFR 52.47(a) as part of the standard design certification application under 10 CFR 52, Subpart B. The report includes detailed descriptions of the plant's general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, with process effluent radiation monitoring and sampling systems addressed in Section 11.5.1.2.2. The NuScale Power Plant features a nominal net output of 570 MWe, comprising 12 power modules, each with a single reactor rated at 160 MWth thermal power and a nominal gross electrical output of 50 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at a normal pressure of 1,850 psia and utilizes two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, operating on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine with extraction. The reactor core uses UO2 fuel with enrichment below 4.95% and has a 24-month refueling interval. Key safety enhancements include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps, thereby eliminating pump-related accident scenarios. The high containment design pressure ensures that even in the worst-case design-basis accident, containment integrity is maintained, minimizing the potential for radioactive releases. Figures in the report illustrate the functional boundaries, schematic of a single power module, and the layout of a multi-module plant, with certain structures such as the administration and training building and warehouse excluded from the FSAR scope.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) features an integral containment system that provides primary containment for the Reactor Coolant System (RCS), as detailed in Section 6.2. The containment design conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.1.A, Rev 0 requirements, including design margin for containment pressure, heat removal capability, protection from external pressure, monitoring instrumentation, internal structures, and evaluation of accidents involving generated hydrogen or other modules. For the limiting event, the peak containment pressure remains below the design pressure. The NuScale design does not utilize an ice condenser containment, as specified in SRP 6.2.1.1.B, Draft Rev 3. Additionally, preliminary design information requirements under 10 CFR 50.34(f)(3)(v) are not applicable to the design certification application per 10 CFR 52.47(a)(8) and 10 CFR 50.34(f). Further details on containment systems are provided in Section 6.2.

results.question: Is the integral SG design described in both Ch.01 and Ch.05 adequate for addressing steam generator tube rupture (SGTR) concerns?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Ch.01 describes the integral SG within the RPV. Ch.05 confirms that because the SG is inside the RPV/containment, an SGTR does not release radioactive coolant outside containment (unlike conventional PWRs where SGTR bypasses containment). The Alloy 690 tube material further reduces SGTR likelihood.
results.generated_answer:
Yes, the integral SG design described in both Ch.01 and Ch.05 is adequate for addressing steam generator tube rupture (SGTR) concerns, as it ensures tube integrity under all loading conditions, implements comprehensive inspection and plugging criteria, and maintains compliance with ASME Code and regulatory requirements.  
(Source: Context, Sections 5.4, 5.5.4, and referenced technical specifications)
retrieved_contexts: The steam generator (SG) tube program encompasses comprehensive management of tube integrity, including assessment of degradation, inspection requirements, integrity evaluation, tube plugging, leakage monitoring, shell side integrity, water chemistry control, foreign material exclusion, loose parts management, contractor oversight, self-assessment, and reporting. SG tubes, made from SB-163 Alloy 690 (UNS N06690) with a nominal wall thickness of 0.050 inch (including a 0.010-inch lifetime degradation allowance), are fully accessible for nondestructive volumetric examination along their entire length due to the absence of U-bends, as specified in Item B16.10 of Table IWB-2500-1 (B-Q). Preservice examinations are performed per BPVC Section III, Paragraph NB-5280, and Section XI, Subarticle IWB-2200, after installation and hydrostatic testing but before initial power operation, establishing a baseline for future inservice inspections. Tubes with flaws exceeding 40% wall thickness, compromising integrity before the first inservice inspection, or affecting future inspectability must be plugged. The SG Program, based on NEI 97-06, Regulatory Guide 1.121, ASME Code Sections III and XI, EPRI guidance, and 10 CFR 50.55a(b)(2)(iii), ensures structural and leakage integrity, minimizes worker dose by allowing component access without excessive removal, and is integrated into the Inservice Inspection (ISI) program. Prototypic testing of tube supports validates performance, with wear identified as the most likely degradation mechanism. The program supports tube plugging criteria and inspection frequencies consistent with the operating PWR fleet, and requires COL applicants referencing the NuScale design to implement periodic monitoring per Appendix B to 10 CFR 50. The fouling factor is applied to the tube inner surface, with the thin oxide layer on the outer surface excluded from calculations. Degradation evaluations confirm tube integrity with up to 50% wall loss under all loading conditions, with minimum wall thickness assessed for combined design basis pipe break and safe shutdown earthquake loads, ensuring compliance with ASME Code Level D Service condition stress limits. SGs and associated components are classified per Table 3.2-1, with pressure boundary materials and welds meeting BPVC Section II and III requirements. Feedwater and main steam piping are Quality Group B, Class 2, and all welding follows ASME BPVC requirements. SG supports are designed per BPVC Section III, Subsection NG, but are not Code stamped as “Core Supports,” though a 10 CFR 50 Appendix B quality program is required. The SG program monitors performance and condition to ensure continued intended function, with additional design, operational, and material details referenced in relevant sections and tables.

The Steam Generator (SG) Program, as described in Section 5.5.4 of the technical specifications, is based on NEI 97-06, Regulatory Guide 1.121, and implements ASME Code Sections III and XI for SG tubes, as well as applicable portions of Section XI of the BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a(b)(2)(iii). The program ensures reasonable assurance of structural and leakage integrity of SG tubes by permitting access to SG components without excessive removal of plates, shields, covers, or piping, thereby minimizing worker dose risk. It provides monitoring and management of tube degradation and precursors, enabling timely preventative and corrective actions, and is integrated into the overall Inservice Inspection (ISI) program. The NuScale SG Program also follows EPRI guidance and applies established commercial SG program requirements, justified by historical causes of SG tube degradation and specific NuScale SG design features. Prototypic testing of SG tube supports is conducted to validate acceptable performance, including wear, with wear identified as the most likely degradation mechanism based on assessments and operating PWR fleet experience. The program supports implementation of tube plugging criteria and inspection frequencies consistent with the operating PWR SG fleet. Appendix B to 10 CFR 50 applies to SG program implementation, and a Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale design must develop and implement a Steam Generator Program for periodic monitoring to ensure SG tube integrity is maintained.

The steam generator (SG) tube fouling factor is applied to the tube inner surface, where deposits from steam plant corrosion products accumulate; the thin oxide layer on the tube outer surface in the primary coolant is not considered in fouling calculations. SG tubes are designed with a nominal wall thickness of 0.050 inch, which includes a lifetime degradation allowance of 0.010 inch to account for inservice degradation mechanisms such as general corrosion, erosion, wear, and defects from assembly. SG degradation evaluations confirm that tubing maintains acceptable integrity with up to 50 percent thickness loss under all loading conditions. Minimum wall thickness is evaluated under combined design basis pipe break and safe shutdown earthquake loads, assuming worst-case uniform thinning on the tube inner diameter. The 0.010-inch wall thinning allowance ensures the reduced wall thickness remains below the ASME Code Level D Service condition allowable stress limit, providing a safety margin. SGs are tested and inspected per Section 5.2.4, with equipment designed for accessibility to minimize time and radiation exposure during maintenance. Additional design, operational, and radiological details are referenced in Sections 3.9.1, 3.9.3, 11.1.2, and 15.0.3.8.2, and SG classification and risk categories are listed in Table 3.2-1.

The steam generator (SG) classification designations and their applicability are detailed in Table 3.2-1, with Chapter 3 providing comprehensive information on the design basis and qualification of structures, systems, and components according to these classifications. Figure 6.6-1 illustrates the BPVC Section III, Class 1 and 2 boundaries for the SG system (SGS). Pressure boundary materials for the SGs and associated components are selected and fabricated per BPVC Section III and Section II requirements, as outlined in Section 5.2.3, with specific materials listed in Table 5.2-4. All fabrication materials conform to BPVC Section II specifications and Section III, Article NB-2000 requirements. SG tubes are made from SB-163 Alloy 690 (UNS N06690), and all SGS materials forming the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) comply with BPVC Section II and Section III, Article NB-2000. Feedwater (FW) and main steam (MS) piping from the CNTS to the SGs, including the thermal relief valve, are classified as Quality Group B and designed, fabricated, constructed, tested, and inspected as Class 2 per BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a(d), with all weld materials meeting BPVC requirements. Welding of RCPB portions of the SGS follows ASME BPVC Section III, Subarticle NB-4300 and Section IX, while secondary side welding follows Subarticle NC-4300 and Section IX. SG weld filler metals, listed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.4-3, comply with BPVC Section II, Part C. SG supports and tube supports are designed per BPVC Section III, Subsection NG, except they are not Code stamped as “Core Supports” per NG-8000 and do not require an N-Certificate holder, though a 10 CFR 50 Appendix B quality program is mandatory. SG piping structural supports, including weld materials, meet BPVC Section III, Subsection NF requirements, with materials specified in Table 5.4-3. Additional details on material compatibility, fabrication, process controls, and welding controls for ASME Class 2 components are in Section 6.6, and threaded fasteners are described in Section 3.13. The SG program monitors SG performance and condition to ensure intended function is maintained.

results.question: Does the NuScale design adequately address station blackout (SBO) given the Ch.01 passive design philosophy and Ch.05 safety system details?
results.expected_answer: Yes. NuScale's passive safety systems are inherently SBO-resistant. Ch.01 states no AC power is needed for safety functions. Ch.05 confirms ECCS valves fail open, DHRS operates by natural circulation, and containment pressure suppression is passive. An SBO does not prevent any safety function from performing, eliminating SBO as a severe accident precursor.

Yes, the NuScale design adequately addresses station blackout (SBO) by maintaining all safety-related functions for 72 hours without reliance on AC power, consistent with its passive design philosophy and safety system features.  
(Source: NuScale FSAR Rev. 5, DSRS 8.4, Section 8.4, and Ch.01/Ch.05 context)

Safety Considerations
NuScale has achieved an improvement in safety over existing plants through 
simplicity of design, reliance on passive safety systems, and small fuel inventory.The integral design of the NPM eliminates external coolant loop piping, which 
eliminates large-break LOCA scenarios.

The NuScale Power Plant design does not require certain systems to function following an accident, and these systems are not credited in the determination of radiological consequences. The application of the single-failure criterion to safety systems is confirmed, with no exceptions noted. For radiological consequence analyses, NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, as its safety analysis demonstrates that core damage does not occur during any design basis event, making some RG 1.183 guidance only partially applicable. Departures from RG 1.183 for limiting dose consequence analysis are justified in a topical report, and NuScale applies the alternative source term (AST) methodology, specifically the non-LOCA or transient-specific guidance of RG 1.183, for Chapter 15 events. The design uses a modified AST methodology to evaluate radiological consequences of accidents, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis methods (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models for LOCAs addressing only the technically relevant features required by Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30.

The review of DSRS 8.4, Rev 0: Station Blackout indicates partial conformance with regulatory requirements. Compliance with 10 CFR 50.63 and the guidelines of RG 1.155 is only partially achieved. For passive plant designs, all safety-related functions can be performed without reliance on AC power for 72 hours following a station blackout (SBO) event, as detailed in Section 8.4, and an RTNSS (Regulatory Treatment of Non-Safety Systems) process has been implemented per Section 19.3. While DC power supplies are not required to meet the SBO mitigation requirements of 10 CFR 50.63, the independence of SBO-related power supplies, specifically EDSS (Emergency Direct Current Supply System), is addressed in Section 8.3.

Ch.05 specifies the SG produces superheated steam at ~575 degrees F from ~300 degrees F feedwater, typical conditions for a Rankine cycle with moderate superheat. The conditions are well-matched for efficient turbine operation at the 50 MWe per module scale.

Tests specific to the NuScale fuel design are detailed in Sections 1.5.1.1 and 1.5.1.2, steam generator (SG) tests in Sections 1.5.1.3 and 1.5.1.4, control rod assembly tests in Sections 1.5.1.6 and 1.5.1.7, and integrated system phenomena tests in Section 1.5.1.5. Sensors, including thermocouples, pressure transducers, mass flow rate instruments, and strain gauges, were used to measure heater rod temperatures, fluid flow conditions, temperature, pressure, flow rate, and vibration at various points in the fluid loop and on both primary and secondary sides of the SG. These tests enabled NuScale to obtain fuel bundle subchannel exit data and benchmark thermal-hydraulic design and systems computer codes. Due to the unique helical SG design, which differs from those in the existing nuclear fleet, large-scale experimental data were required to validate NuScale’s thermal-hydraulic systems and computer codes, and to determine SG performance characteristics, particularly the secondary side (inside tube) thermal-hydraulic performance of individual helical tubes. The data also informed the sizing of SG inlet flow restrictors for stable secondary-side operation and defined steam outlet conditions as a function of primary heat generation and secondary side conditions. The SG test program was inspected by the NRC under Inspection Procedures IP 35034, 35017, and 36100. Analysis documented in Appendix A of TR-0516-49417 demonstrates that any SG secondary side flow oscillations are decoupled from primary side flow oscillations, ensuring that secondary side flow instabilities do not induce reactor power oscillations that could exceed fuel design limits.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the steam generator (SG) design and operating conditions for the Reactor Coolant System. At full load, the SGs collectively transfer 159.13 MWt, with each SG capable of handling half this load. The SG operates at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with outlet and inlet temperatures of 584.4°F and 299.7°F, respectively, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. The SG is a helical, once-through type, featuring 1,380 helical tubes arranged in 21 columns per NuScale Power Module (NPM). Both the internal and external pressures for the secondary and primary sides are 2,100 psia, while the SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia. The internal and external temperatures are 650°F, with SG piping in containment at 550°F. Tubes have an outer diameter of 0.625 inches and a wall thickness of 0.050 inches. The steam tubesheet and feed tubesheet thicknesses (without clad) are 4.0 inches and 6.0 inches, respectively, with secondary and primary clad thicknesses of 0.250 inches and 0.375 inches. With clad, the steam and feed tubesheet thicknesses are 4.625 inches and 6.625 inches. The total heat transfer area is 17,928 ft², and the fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. The minimum SG tube transition bend radius is at least 6.250 inches. Figures in the report illustrate the helical tube bundle geometry, transition regions, tube support arrangements, and the use of support tabs for tube stability within the SG columns.

The NuScale design ensures that the nonsafety-related Secondary Component Water System (SCWS) is constructed to meet the provisions of Regulatory Guide (RG) 1.29, Staff Regulatory Guidance C.1.i. For the Reactor Component Cooling Water (RCCW) system, which is not safety-related, NuScale applies RG 1.29 position C.2, ensuring that structures, systems, and components (SSCs) whose structural failure could impact the operability of safety-related SSCs are designed as Seismic Category II. No portion of the NuScale condensate storage system requires design and construction to withstand the safe-shutdown earthquake to prevent adverse effects on Seismic Category I SSCs, consistent with RG 1.29 C.1.i. The plant’s passive design and reduced reliance on AC power align with regulatory guidance for coping with design basis events. The NuScale main steam supply system conforms to RG 1.117, Appendix Positions 2 and 4, for tornado missile protection of important-to-safety SSCs. Unlike B&W reactor designs, which were sensitive to secondary system transients per NUREG-0933, the NuScale design does not exhibit such sensitivity.

The NuScale design's approach to coolant source terms, as described in DSRS 11.1, Rev 0, partially conforms to several regulatory guides, including RG 1.110 and RG 1.112 (referenced in Table 1.9-2), and follows methodologies consistent with NUREG-0017, PWR-GALE86, and ANSI/ANS 18.1-1999 for effluent release calculations. The design is similar to large PWRs in the existing fleet regarding effluent release calculations, and decontamination factors for both gaseous and liquid effluent releases are consistent with the NuScale Technical Report, Effluent Release (GALE Replacement) Methodology and Results, Revision 0 (TR-1116-52065). RWMS system augmentations used in cost-benefit calculations are also partially consistent with RG 1.110. The primary and secondary coolant source terms used for characterizing liquid and gaseous effluents fully conform to requirements. The design basis coolant source term is partially based on a failed fuel fraction significantly less than 0.25 percent, as detailed in the referenced NuScale technical report. Compliance with 10 CFR 50.34(b)(3), 10 CFR 50.34a, and 10 CFR 52.79(a)(3) is partial, reflecting the similarities to large PWRs in effluent release calculations. DC/COL-ISG-5 is deemed not applicable due to these similarities.
