The Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) is an approximately cylindrical steel vessel with an inside diameter of about 9 ft and an overall height of 58 ft, designed for an operating pressure of 1,850 psia. It consists of three main sections: the head, upper, and lower sections. The torispherical RPV head is welded to the top of the upper section, while the upper and lower sections are flanged together with bolts. The upper section is cylindrical, with thicker areas at the feedwater inlet and steam outlet, and contains penetrations ranging from 2.25” to 25” in diameter for main steam piping nozzles and access ports, pressurizer heaters, feedwater piping and access ports, reactor recirculation valves, Chemical and Volume Control System (CVCS), and pressure instrumentation. The lower section is also cylindrical with a welded torispherical lower head and contains no penetrations. An integral steel pressurizer baffle plate separates the saturated water in the pressurizer from the Reactor Coolant System (RCS). The RPV provides steam and feedwater plenums for the steam generators and RSV penetrations, and supports or attaches to the control rod drive mechanisms (CRDMs), CRDM seismic support structure, pressurizer heater bundles, in-core instrumentation, steam generator system piping, RCS piping, reactor safety valves (RSVs), reactor vent valves, and reactor recirculation valves. The RPV, including the steam generators, is certified and stamped per Article NCA-8000 of ASME BPVC, Section III. Pressure-temperature curves for the RPV comply with 10 CFR 50, Appendix G, ensuring operation below the pressure-temperature limits to prevent non-ductile failure. Two pressurizer heater bundles, each with a nominal capacity of 400 kW, are mounted through the RPV side, 180 degrees apart around the integral steam plenum assembly. The pressurizer baffle plate and its support structure are subject to BPVC and ANSI/ASME NQA-1 requirements, with inspections and testing conducted per BPVC standards. Material specifications for the RPV upper shell and head, as well as details on pressurizer spray nozzles and safe ends, are provided in referenced sections.

The RPV bolting material is not subject to the concerns addressed by RG 1.65 Positions 1(a)(i) and 2(b), and thus these positions do not apply. Section 3.9 outlines the design transients, loading combinations, stress limits, and evaluation methods used in the design and fatigue analyses of Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary (RCPB) components, confirming that RCPB integrity is maintained. RCPB components are designed, constructed, and maintained to quality standards ensuring overpressure protection of the Reactor Coolant System (RCS). The RPV and pressure-retaining components associated with the RCPB are designed, fabricated, and tested as Class 1 to the highest quality standards per the Quality Assurance Program in Chapter 17, in compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendix A, GDC 1 and 30. Section 5.3 provides details of materials conformance for the RPV, confirming compliance with GDC 1, 4, 14, 30, and 31, ensuring compatibility with environmental conditions, non-brittle behavior, and minimized probability of rapidly propagating fracture or gross rupture. Material specifications for Class 1 components and supports, including the RPV and steam generators (SGs), are listed in Table 5.2-4, which also covers RPV attachments and appurtenances. Cleaning of RCPB components meets ASME NQA-1 requirements, with internal surfaces achieving "Class B" and external surfaces "Class C" cleanness per Subpart 2.1. Handling, storage, and shipping of RCPB components comply with ASME NQA-1-2008, Part I, Requirement 13, and ASME NQA-1a-2009, Part II, Subpart 2.2.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the inspection elements, design parameters, material composition, and major components of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV). Table 5.2-6 specifies that RPV shell and head welds, including connections such as the lower RPV flange shell to RPV bottom head and steam plenum cap to integral steam plenum, are subject to B-A category volumetric examination, while internal welds like the core support block to RPV bottom head undergo B-N-2 category VT-3 visual testing; instrumentation and controls sleeve welds are part of the cladding and not separately examined. Table 5.3-1 lists key RPV parameters: design pressure is 2100 psia, design temperature is 650°F, overall height is 778 inches, and diameters range from 96.5 to 115.5 inches depending on section, with inner and outer clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches, respectively. Table 5.3-2 provides maximum allowable concentrations for RPV beltline forging material (e.g., sulfur 0.010 wt%, phosphorus 0.010 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%) and as-deposited weld metal (e.g., sulfur 0.015 wt%, phosphorus 0.012 wt%, copper 0.06 wt%, cobalt 0.05 wt%, manganese 1.80 wt%, nickel 0.85 wt%). Figure 5.3-1 identifies major RPV components, including the lower and upper RPV sections, RPV head section, CRDM latch housing section with 16 latch housings, RPV top head, PZR shell, integral steam plenum with 4 access ports, upper and lower RPV SG shells, feed plenum access ports, lower RPV section flange shell, RPV bottom head, upper RPV flanged transition shell, CRDM seismic support structure, and 4 instrument seal assemblies.

results.question: Based on Table 1.3-1 comparison, Table 5.2-4 materials, and Figure 5.4-1 SG design, does the NuScale SG offer safety advantages over conventional PWR steam generators?
results.expected_answer: Yes. Table 1.3-1 shows the integral design vs conventional external SGs. Table 5.2-4 specifies Alloy 690 tubes (superior to Alloy 600). Figure 5.4-1 shows the helical coil design within the RPV. The integral location means SGTR does not bypass containment, Alloy 690 reduces tube degradation, and the compact design eliminates external SG piping failures.
results.generated_answer: Yes, the NuScale SG offers safety advantages over conventional PWR steam generators, including thicker Alloy 690 tubes, minimized crevices, corrosion-resistant materials (nickel alloy/stainless steel), a once-through helical design eliminating bulk water reservoirs, and enhanced tube support structures, as shown in Table 1.3-1, Table 5.2-4, and Figure 5.4-1. (NuScale FSAR, referenced sections and figures)
retrieved_contexts: The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report details the steam generator (SG) design and operating conditions for the Reactor Coolant System. At full load, the SGs collectively transfer 159.13 MWt, with each SG capable of handling half this load. The SG operates at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with outlet and inlet temperatures of 584.4°F and 299.7°F, respectively, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. The SG is a helical, once-through type, featuring 1,380 helical tubes arranged in 21 columns per NuScale Power Module (NPM). Both the internal and external pressures for the secondary and primary sides are 2,100 psia, while the SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia. The internal and external temperatures are 650°F, with SG piping in containment at 550°F. Tubes have an outer diameter of 0.625 inches and a wall thickness of 0.050 inches. The steam tubesheet and feed tubesheet thicknesses (without clad) are 4.0 inches and 6.0 inches, respectively, with secondary and primary clad thicknesses of 0.250 inches and 0.375 inches. With clad, the steam and feed tubesheet thicknesses are 4.625 inches and 6.625 inches. The total heat transfer area is 17,928 ft², and the fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. The minimum SG tube transition bend radius is at least 6.250 inches. Figures in the report illustrate the helical tube bundle geometry, transition regions, tube support arrangements, and the use of support tabs for tube stability within the SG columns.

The NuScale design incorporates design improvements 
necessary to restrict SG tube degradation and has additional design features that 
reduce the risk of SG tube degradation compared to existing PWR designs.Historically, significant SG tube degradation has occurred in the operating PWR SG fleet 
due to various corrosion mechanisms, including wastage and both primary and 
secondary side stress corrosion cracking.

The NuScale steam generator (SG) design minimizes crevices between SG tubes, tube supports, and tubesheets to limit corrosion product buildup, with tube-to-tubesheet contact fully within the primary coolant environment. Full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore eliminates crevices at the tube-to-tubesheet face, and tubes are expanded into both steam and feed plenum tubesheets. The once-through SG design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet plena, preventing accumulation or concentration of materials and making traditional SG blowdown systems unnecessary and ineffective for removing corrosion products or impurities. Instead, any secondary coolant impurities or corrosion products may deposit as scale or film on interior tube surfaces or be removed by carryover, with low concentrations ensured by material selection and chemistry control. Periodic cleaning during outages, using proven chemical or mechanical methods, removes unacceptable corrosion product films. Secondary side SG surfaces are constructed of corrosion-resistant materials such as nickel alloy, stainless steel, or stainless steel cladding, eliminating concerns about degradation from cleaning solutions and negating the need for invasive sludge removal techniques. The design provides access to both internal (secondary) and external (reactor coolant) sides of tubesheets for inspection and foreign object removal. The tube support structure is located within the primary coolant environment, preventing secondary system corrosion products from depositing on the SG shell. Chemistry control for both primary and secondary water is maintained per industry guidelines, with modifications for the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design, ensuring corrosion protection for stainless steels and nickel alloys in SG components, as detailed in Sections 5.2.3, 6.1, and 10.3.5, with the secondary water quality control program following NEI 97-06.

Alloy 690 tubing in the NuScale steam generator (SG) design exhibits little-to-no corrosion from either primary or secondary-side fluids, with primary and secondary chemistries equivalent to existing PWR SG designs. However, secondary-side flow conditions, including boiling, differ from traditional PWRs, leading to the assumption of uniform thinning on the tube inner diameter. The NuScale design incorporates improvements to restrict SG tube degradation, addressing historical issues in PWRs such as wastage and stress corrosion cracking. Key enhancements include thicker SG tube walls, providing a substantial degradation allowance beyond minimum design requirements, and lower reactor coolant flowrates compared to PWR recirculating SGs. The risk of foreign object introduction is comparable to existing designs, requiring no changes to SG program guidelines. Notable design differences are the helical shape of the SG tubing—whose minimum bend radius remains within the operational experience of PWRs—and a novel tube support structure that retains essential attributes of traditional plate supports. Preliminary assessments identified potential secondary-side corrosion mechanisms, such as under deposit pitting and intergranular attack, due to the once-through design and internal tube boiling. However, estimated defect growth rates are low, and the SG tube plugging criterion remains at a 40 percent through-wall defect, consistent with the existing PWR SG fleet.

The NuScale Power Module (NPM) steam generator (SG) is an integral, once-through, helical tube design located within the reactor pressure vessel, with two SGs per module. Each SG contains 1,380 seamless helical tubes in 21 columns, providing a total heat transfer area of 17,928 ft² and a 10% tube plugging margin. The SG operates at an outlet pressure of 500.1 psia, with inlet and outlet temperatures of 299.7°F and 584.4°F, and a flow rate of 532,298 lbm/hr. Both primary and secondary sides are rated at 2,100 psia and 650°F, while SG piping in containment is rated at 1,000 psia and 550°F. Tubes have a 0.625-inch outer diameter, 0.050-inch wall thickness, and a minimum bend radius of 6.250 inches. Steam and feed tubesheets are 4.0 and 6.0 inches thick (without clad), increasing to 4.625 and 6.625 inches with clad (0.250 inches secondary, 0.375 inches primary). Eight austenitic stainless steel tube support assemblies per column provide full-circumferential, non-continuous support, minimizing crevice formation and corrosion, and ensuring high vibration resistance. The design eliminates traditional tube support plates and uses full-length tube expansion within the tubesheet bore to minimize crevices, with all tube-to-tubesheet contact in the primary coolant. The once-through design lacks a bulk water reservoir at the inlet, preventing impurity accumulation and eliminating the need for blowdown systems; impurities are managed by chemistry control and periodic cleaning. Secondary side surfaces use corrosion-resistant materials (nickel alloy or stainless steel), allowing safe cleaning and inspection. System monitoring is performed at ≥500 Hz, and preservice inspections include 100% volumetric tube and weld examination per BPVC. The SG tube support structure is within the primary coolant, preventing secondary system corrosion product deposition. Chemistry control follows industry guidelines with NPM modifications, and secondary water quality adheres to NEI 97-06. The fouling factor is 0.0001 hr-ft²-°F/BTU. SG tube failure is indicated by pressure changes, and actuation valves have position indication.

Secondary side instability evaluation shows no in-phase oscillations and that out-of-phase oscillations cancel, resulting in no net oscillating secondary flow. Acceptable instabilities are limited to tube mass flow fluctuations that do not induce reactor power oscillations exceeding fuel design limits and comply with ASME Code. The vibration assessment program (per Regulatory Guide 1.20, Rev. 3) covers SGs, main steam piping (including MSIV), SG supports, and tube supports within the RPV. Flow-induced vibration (FIV) screening criteria were developed, and low primary coolant flow rates result in low

The Steam Generator (SG) Program, as described in Section 5.5.4 of the technical specifications, is based on NEI 97-06, Regulatory Guide 1.121, and implements ASME Code Sections III and XI for SG tubes, as well as applicable portions of Section XI of the BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a(b)(2)(iii). The program ensures reasonable assurance of structural and leakage integrity of SG tubes by permitting access to SG components without excessive removal of plates, shields, covers, or piping, thereby minimizing worker dose risk. It provides monitoring and management of tube degradation and precursors, enabling timely preventative and corrective actions, and is integrated into the overall Inservice Inspection (ISI) program. The NuScale SG Program also follows EPRI guidance and applies established commercial SG program requirements, justified by historical causes of SG tube degradation and specific NuScale SG design features. Prototypic testing of SG tube supports is conducted to validate acceptable performance, including wear, with wear identified as the most likely degradation mechanism based on assessments and operating PWR fleet experience. The program supports implementation of tube plugging criteria and inspection frequencies consistent with the operating PWR SG fleet. Appendix B to 10 CFR 50 applies to SG program implementation, and a Combined License (COL) applicant referencing the NuScale design must develop and implement a Steam Generator Program for periodic monitoring to ensure SG tube integrity is maintained.

The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system is designed with sufficient capacity to prevent RCPB pressure from exceeding the pressure-temperature (P-T) limits specified in 10 CFR 50 Appendix G when below the LTOP enable temperature, thereby maintaining reactor pressure vessel (RPV) pressure below brittle fracture limits during all operational, maintenance, testing, or postulated accident conditions. During normal operational transients at full power, the system and core parameters are managed to prevent safety valve lift; if a safety valve does lift, the pressurizer (PZR) steam space is sized to avoid liquid discharge. LTOP analysis assumes a maximum PZR heater output of 880 kW and an additional 2 MW from core decay heat, demonstrating that reactor vent valves (RVVs) open before reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure exceeds the low temperature pressure limit. Table 5.2-10 provides LTOP pressure setpoints as a function of cold temperature, with PZR pressure limits ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, and LTOP not enabled above 318°F. The Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) system in the NuScale Power Module (NPM) safeguards the Reactor Coolant System (RCS) during low temperature conditions by using three Reactor Vent Valves (RVVs) and two pilot-operated Reactor Safety Valves (RSVs) mounted on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) upper head, all constructed to ASME BPVC Section III, Subsection NB, Seismic Category I, Quality Group A standards. The RSVs, each with a 3-inch inlet, 4-inch outlet, internal design pressure of 2100 psia, external design pressure of 1000 psia, and design temperature of 650°F, are sized to maintain RPV pressure below 110% of the 2310 psia design pressure, with a minimum relief capacity of 63,360 lbm/hr of saturated steam at 3% overpressure. Nominal setpoints are 2075 psia ±1% and 2100 psia ±1%, with a blowdown of 10% ±2%. LTOP is enabled when RCS cold temperature is ≤318°F, with pressure setpoints ranging from 380.0 psia below 193°F to 850.0 psia at and above 277°F, determined by linear interpolation based on downcomer temperature at the steam generator outlet. The system is actuated by module protection system logic to open RVVs and limit RCS pressure below the maximum allowable value, accounting for worst-case transients such as spurious pressurizer heater actuation (up to 880 kW) and 2 MW core decay heat. The setpoints include margins for pressure and temperature uncertainty, elevation differences, temperature variations, RVV opening delay, sensor response, and processing time. Three RVVs ensure LTOP protection even with a single active component failure, and overpressure protection for secondary side components is integrated to maintain ASME BPVC service limits during normal and anticipated operational occurrences (AOOs), with the most severe AOO being a turbine trip at full power without bypass. RVVs and RSVs are environmentally and functionally qualified per ASME QME-1 and RG 1.100, with direct position indication in the control room per 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(xi). Pressure-temperature (P-T) limits, surveillance capsule locations, and test schedules are detailed in referenced tables and figures, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, 10 CFR 50.61, and GDC 15 for a 60-year plant life. Additional details on valve qualification, design, operation, and testing are provided in Sections 6.3, 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6.

Section 5.2.2 addresses overpressure protection, with additional details on testing and inspection of overpressure protection components found in Sections 6.6, 14.2, and 3.9.6. Table 5.2-10 presents the Low Temperature Overpressure Protection (LTOP) pressure setpoint as a function of cold temperature. Section 5.3.2 details the analyses and data supporting the establishment of pressure-temperature limits, pressurized thermal shock (PTS) evaluations, and Charpy upper-shelf energy requirements, ensuring compliance with 10 CFR 50, Appendices G and H, and 10 CFR 50.61 for the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB) over a 60-year plant life. Table 5.3-1 lists reactor vessel parameters, while Table 5.3-5 provides the surveillance capsule withdrawal schedule and Table 5.3-6 and Table 5.3-7 specify pressure-temperature limits for normal heatup, cooldown, inservice leak, and hydrostatic tests. Table 5.3-8 presents the PTS screening result. Figures 5.3-2 through 5.3-5 illustrate the location of surveillance capsules and graphical pressure-temperature limits for various operational and test conditions.

The NuScale reactor design and safety analysis methodologies are detailed in topical reports TR-0516-49416 and TR-0516-49422, with pressure-temperature limit curves at 57 EFPY calculated per ASME BPVC Section XI, Appendix G, and 10 CFR 50 Appendix G, incorporating vessel embrittlement effects using Regulatory Guide 1.99 and ASTM E185-82 for surveillance. Fluence and pressure-temperature limits are further addressed in TR-0116-20781-P and TR-1015-18177-P. The design does not require certain systems post-accident and does not credit them in radiological consequence analyses, fully applying the single-failure criterion. NuScale partially conforms to RG 1.183, justifying departures in a topical report, and applies a modified alternative source term (AST) methodology for Chapter 15 events, as referenced in SRP 15.0.1, Rev 0. For transient and accident analysis (SRP 15.0.2), NuScale supports exemptions from portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, with ECCS evaluation models addressing only relevant features per Appendix K and TMI Action Item II.K3.30. Boric acid concentrations are selected to bound all operating conditions, with NRC-inspected testing per IP35034.1.5.2 and startup testing detailed in Section 14.2. The design does not use combustible gas control systems, supporting exemptions to 10 CFR 50.44(c)(2), 50.34(f)(2)(xiv), and GDC 27, and conforms to DSRS 6.2.4, Rev 0, for containment isolation valve closure times. Hydrogen control is not required, as combustion does not affect CNV integrity. Post-accident assessment relies on radiation monitors and core exit temperature, supporting exemption from 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii) regarding post-accident sampling. Additional exemptions include 10 CFR 50.46a, 50.34(f)(2)(vi) (high point venting), and portions of 50.34(f)(2)(xx) (pressurizer level indicators). The CVCS does not include holdup tanks under vacuum but provides required venting and draining. The RPV high-point degasification vent is not safety-significant, and high-point vents are not needed. The LTOP system is not connected to a low-pressure system, but guidance to prevent inadvertent isolation applies. NuScale supports exemptions from selected portions of 10 CFR 50 Appendix K, conforms to DSRS 6.2.1.3, Rev 0, for LOCA mass and energy release analysis, and requires no additional ITAAC. The design ensures no core uncovery during LOCA, conforms to DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0, and GDC 5 for sharing of SSCs, does

The design does not comply with regulatory position C1.3.12 (prototypical head loss testing), nor with position C.2 except for meeting the intent of chemical reaction effects (position 2.2), and does not comply with position C.3. For DSRS 5.4.7 (Decay Heat Removal System), the design partially conforms to GDC 54, featuring a closed-loop DHRS outside containment directly connected to the closed-loop SG system within the RPV, providing dual passive barriers between the RCS and the reactor pool outside the NPM. Regarding DSRS 6.2.2 (Containment Heat Removal Systems), the design supports an exemption from the power provisions of GDC 38. For DSRS 6.2.5 (Combustible Gas Control in Containment), GDC 42 and 43 are not applicable as the design lacks a containment atmospheric cleanup system. In DSRS 15.2.1-15.2.5, the design conforms to fuel cladding integrity requirements and does not include a steam pressure regulator. The exclusion zone and buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability review (DSRS 15.9.A) is not used in the NuScale design. Finally, for DC/COL-ISG-5, the NuScale design is similar to large PWRs in the existing fleet regarding effluent release calculations, rendering the specific interim staff guidance not applicable.

The NuScale reactor design incorporates several unique features and regulatory approaches, including a single-failure-proof crane that eliminates the need for load drop accident analyses for spent fuel casks or Nuclear Power Modules (NPMs). Containment atmosphere management minimizes oxygen during normal operations and uses continuous hydrogen and oxygen monitoring post-accident, meeting the intent of RG 1.7 without active combustible gas control systems; the design partially meets DSRS 6.2.5 for gas concentration analysis but fully meets requirements for equipment survivability, containment integrity, and atmosphere mixing, supporting exemptions from containment leakage rate testing (GDC 52, 10 CFR 50 Appendix J Type A). The Decay Heat Removal (DHR) System generally conforms to GDC 5 and related requirements, with exemptions for GDC 19 and 34, and supports NuScale-specific principal design criteria; for containment heat removal (DSRS 6.2.2), the design meets GDC 39 but departs from GDC 40, and for combustible gas control (DSRS 6.2.5), it supports an exemption from GDC 41 power provisions. Equipment survivability ensures radiation protection, and the design does not rely on prompt sampling for accident source term assessment (10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii)). The Control Rod Drive System (CRDS) conforms to GDCs 4, 23, 25, 28, and 29, clarifies GDC 26 does not require two safety-related reactivity controls, and departs from GDC 27 in favor of a design-specific PDC. Testing confirmed CRA drop time and impact force, with partial conformance to RG 1.77, SRP 6.5.3, BTP 6-1, SRP 9.2.4, and SRP 9.3.2, and full conformance to SRP 4.2, 9.2.6, 15.4.2, and 15.4.7; the condensate storage system is non-safety-related, and potable/sanitary water systems do not interface with radioactive systems. The FSAR documents non-applicability or non-conformance with several regulatory positions (e.g., C1.1.3, C1.1.4, C1.3.12, C.2 except 2.2, C.3), partial conformance to GDC 54 for DHRS, and exemptions from GDC 38, 42, and 43 due to design features. The design does not use exclusion zone/buffer region methodology for thermal hydraulic stability and is similar to large PWRs for effluent release calculations, making certain interim guidance inapplicable. Containment purge systems vent directly to the environment, and SFP mitigating measures are not required due to design features (small containment, below-grade SFP). The Ultimate

The NuScale design's Decay Heat Removal (DHR) System, as described in DSRS 5.4.7, Rev 0, generally conforms to applicable requirements, including GDC 5, with no departures noted for responsibilities II.1 through II.4. The design ensures that sharing of the site cooling water system between units does not adversely affect essential SSCs or safety functions. However, departures are identified for GDC 19 (II.6) and GDC 34 (II.7), where the design supports exemptions and instead complies with NuScale-specific principal design criteria as detailed in Sections 3.1.2 and 3.1.4, respectively. For containment heat removal systems (DSRS 6.2.2, Rev 0), the design conforms to GDC 39 regarding inspection (II.3) but departs from GDC 40 on testing (II.4), supporting an exemption. Similarly, for combustible gas control in containment (DSRS 6.2.5, Rev 0, II.4), the design supports an exemption from the power provisions of GDC 41, again relying on a NuScale-specific principal design criterion. Equipment survivability is used to assure protection from radiation. Additionally, regarding 10 CFR 50.34(f)(2)(viii), the NuScale design departs from the requirement to provide prompt sampling and analysis of reactor coolant and containment for accident source term radioactive materials, as it does not rely on such samples to assess potential core damage.

The NuScale Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV) Material Surveillance Program follows ASTM E185-82 requirements, as detailed in Table 5.3-4, using archived materials identical to those used for baseline and capsule specimens, but maintained as full-thickness sections for flexibility in future specimen fabrication. Archive materials are sourced from actual production forgings and weldments using the same weld wire heat and flux lot as production welds. The NuScale RPV is designed for a 60-year life, with the surveillance capsule withdrawal schedule for the first 40 years based on 32 effective full-power years (EFPY), per ASTM E185-82 Table 1. Three capsules suffice for the initial 40-year period, with a fourth capsule included for potential regulatory requirements at license extension. Capsules are housed in holders attached to the core barrel at mid-core height, positioned approximately 90 degrees apart to achieve a lead factor of ~2.5. The withdrawal schedule (Table 5.3-5) specifies: the 1st capsule at the first refuel outage (~2 EFPY or 1.55E+18 n/cm², E > 1 MeV), the 2nd at ~13 EFPY (when capsule fluence matches peak 32-EFPY RPV inside surface fluence), and the 3rd between ~13 and ~26 EFPY. The 4th capsule's withdrawal will be determined by regulations at license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT of 50°F for the limiting weld metal is reached at 4.27E+17 n/cm² (E > 1 MeV, 0.55 EFPY), but 28°F of this shift is a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F, likely causing overcompensation at low fluence. Therefore, the first capsule may be withdrawn at the end of the first fuel cycle rather than mid-cycle.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report and supporting documentation confirm that the reactor pressure vessel (RPV) materials maintain Charpy V-notch upper shelf energy (USE) values well above the regulatory minimum of 50 ft-lb throughout the 60-year design life, with initial values of at least 75 ft-lb and predicted end-of-life values, after irradiation and temperature corrections, of 58.4 ft-lb (lower shell), 62.3 ft-lb (weld), and 62.9 ft-lb (bottom head) at 57 effective full-power years (EFPY). The RPV Material Surveillance Program, compliant with ASTM E185-82 and 10 CFR 50, Appendix H, uses archived production materials and a capsule withdrawal schedule based on 32 EFPY for the first 40 years, with three capsules withdrawn at specified fluence intervals and a fourth for license extension. The highest predicted ΔRTNDT for the limiting weld metal is 50°F at 0.55 EFPY, with 28°F of this due to a fixed adjustment for irradiation temperature below 525°F. Pressurized thermal shock (PTS) screening per 10 CFR 50.61 and RG 1.99 shows RTPTS values at 57 EFPY of 92.2°F (shell), 120.4°F (weld), and 77.4°F (bottom head), all below regulatory limits. Operational restrictions prohibit reactor criticality below 287°F during ISLH testing in transients and below 185°F in steady-state. The RPV is fabricated and inspected per ASME BPVC Sections III and XI, with material specifications and chemical compositions detailed in Tables 5.2-4 and 5.3-2. The limiting base metal is typically the lower RPV shell forging, and the limiting weld is the lower shell to bottom head circumferential weld. Inspection requirements include B-A category volumetric and B-N-2 category VT-3 visual examinations for key welds. The RPV design parameters include a design pressure of 2100 psia, design temperature of 650°F, height of 778 inches, and diameters from 96.5 to 115.5 inches, with clad thicknesses of 0.25 and 0.125 inches. Major components are detailed in Figure 5.3-1, and all packaging, handling, and storage meet ASME and NQA-1 requirements. The surveillance and testing programs ensure ongoing compliance with fracture toughness, pressure-temperature, and inspection criteria throughout the vessel’s service life.

The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report provides detailed operational and design data for the primary system and decay heat removal system. Table 5.1-2 presents primary system temperatures and flow rates at various reactor power levels, with reactor power ranging from 0% (0 MWt) to 100% (160 MWt). At 100% power and best-estimate flow, the primary flow is 587.0 kg/s, with core ΔT of 99.8°F, TCold at 496.6°F, Tavg at 543.3°F, and THot at 590.1°F. Minimum and maximum design flows at 100% power are 538.5 kg/s and 660.5 kg/s, respectively, with corresponding temperature variations. When core decay heat is unavailable, the module heatup system drives natural circulation. Transient load contributions, including fatigue, are considered in component calculations as per Table 3.9-3. For conservative safety analysis, an initial core power of 102% is assumed to account for measurement uncertainty, and a 1.2 multiplier is applied to core decay heat. The decay heat removal system is designed with an internal pressure of 2100 psia, actuation valve external pressure of 60 psia, passive condenser external pressure of 27 psia, and operates at 650°F. Each of the two condensers contains 80 tubes (1.315-inch outer diameter, 0.109-inch wall thickness), with 258.2 ft² external surface area per condenser and a fouling factor of 0.0005 hr-ft²-F/BTU. The system schematic (Figure 5.4-10) shows two passive condensers located in the reactor pool within the containment vessel, with associated valves, pressure transmitters, level transmitters, and connections to the steam generators.

Preliminary critical heat flux (CHF) tests for the NuScale small modular reactor were conducted at Stern Laboratories in Ontario, Canada, using a high-pressure flow loop and custom 5x5 fuel assembly simulators with a 2-meter heated length, various axial power profiles, and configurations with and without simulated guide tubes. These tests examined the effects of shorter fuel length and low-flow natural circulation, generating data for the development and validation of the NuScale NSP2 CHF correlation. Additional CHF testing at the AREVA Karlstein Thermal Hydraulics (KATHY) facility in Germany, using prototypic fuel designs, provided further data to validate NSP2 and develop the NSP4 CHF correlation for the NuFuel HTP2™ fuel design, which uses AREVA HMP™/HTP™ spacer grid technology. CHF occurrence was identified by temperature excursions in the fuel simulators, and data collected included bundle subchannel exit temperatures for mixing coefficient determination and single-phase/two-phase pressure drop characteristics across various bundle powers and hydraulic conditions. The NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR), Revision 5, prepared under 10 CFR 52.47(a), details the plant’s general arrangement, major structures, and equipment, including process effluent radiation monitoring (Section 11.5.1.2.2). The NuScale plant comprises 12 modules, each with a 160 MWth reactor and 50 MWe gross output, for a total net output of 570 MWe. The reactor coolant system (RCS) operates at 1,850 psia, uses two vertical helical tube steam generators per module, and runs on a Rankine-subcritical regenerative cycle with superheat and a 3,600 rpm condensing turbine. The core uses UO2 fuel enriched below 4.95% with a 24-month refueling interval. Safety features include the RCS being fully contained within the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), eliminating large-diameter primary coolant piping and associated large-break LOCA accidents, and a natural-convection-cooled core that removes the need for reactor coolant pumps. The containment is designed for high pressure to maintain integrity during worst-case accidents, minimizing radioactive releases. The FSAR references tables and figures detailing plant and module characteristics (Tables 1.2-1, 1.2-2), system boundaries (Figure 1.2-2), module schematics (Figure 1.2-3), multi-module layouts (Figures 1.2-4, 1.2-5), module cutaways (Figure 1.2-6), steam generator and reactor flow (Figure 1.2-7), technical reports (Table 1.6-2), and I&C diagrams (Table 1.7-1).
